item.title: Iran oil shock prompts ECB hawks to seek 2021/22 rematch
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - European Central Bank hawks are itching to rewrite the record. This month's energy price shock may not match the fallout from Russia's invasion of Ukraine , but officials are clearly wary of repeating the slow post-pandemic policy tightening that left them scrambling in 2022.
The debate within the ECB Governing Council will surely be intense over whether, how or when to respond to this potentially inflationary oil and gas price spike . Uncertainty ‚Äãabout the course of the Iran conflict and the length of the related energy hiatus means few will want to decide much as soon as next week's policy meeting .
But financial markets ‚Äåaren't hanging about to find out who's going to win the argument. Money markets shifted on Tuesday to fully price an ECB interest rate rise by July - a seismic shift from the flat policy horizon seen only a few weeks ago, when markets were even flirting with the chance of one more cut earlier this year.
And hawks have been out in force - perhaps listened to by markets more closely now after recent speculation that ECB President Christine Lagarde may stand down early, as soon as this year.
One name ‚Äãtouted as a possible successor, Bundesbank boss and well-known policy hawk Joachim Nagel , made his view clear on Wednesday.
"We must be very vigilant," Nagel told Reuters, adding that the debate about a possible ‚Äãinflation undershoot and further easing had ended.
"If it becomes apparent that the current energy price increases will translate into broad consumer price inflation in the medium ‚ÅÝterm, the Governing Council of the ECB will act decisively in a timely manner."
"Timely manner" is doing a lot of heavy lifting in that comment.
That's likely because there's still a bruise at the ECB - and at ‚Äãmany other major central banks - from the attempt to see through what was considered a "transitory" post-pandemic inflation surge, stoked mainly by economies rebooting from COVID-19 lockdowns and huge fiscal rescue spending.
Leaving ECB policy rates in negative territory ‚Äãeven as headline euro zone inflation soared from 2% to almost 6% in the eight months through February 2022 probably hurts in hindsight.
ECB board member Isabel Schnabel went one further by saying on Wednesday there are "scars from this high-inflation episode."
NOT RELIANT ON LUCK
It may even have worked out okay back then too, but it left the whole picture very vulnerable to another left-field event.
Once Russia invaded Ukraine in February 2022 and sent natural gas and oil prices soaring - an event the central bank couldn't ‚Äãreasonably have predicted - euro zone inflation shot above 10% later that year.
The ECB eventually scrambled into action by July, lifting rates from below zero to 4% in just 14 months and returning inflation to target a year later. The price ‚Äãwas steep: higher energy bills, general inflation and the sharp rate shock for businesses, households and the wider economy.
For many, that sequence of events may have been just unlucky - but it also displayed the risks of not moving quickly to head off above-target inflation, rising ‚Äåinflation expectations and ‚ÅÝsecond-round effects including business margin-padding and wage bargaining pressure.
The delay, they argue, meant the ECB eventually had to stamp on the brakes harder than would otherwise have been necessary.
Former Dutch central banker Klaas Knot, another name widely touted as a possible successor to Lagarde, made that very point late last week in a podcast with Brussels think tank Bruegel , opens new tab .
"The lessons (from 2021/22) are that while standard theory prescribes that you should look through a negative supply shock like an energy shock, you have to be very, very wary of non-linearities and second-round effects," Knot said.
"If necessary, if a similar response was warranted, then the ECB would look back to ‚Äãthe playbook of 2022/23," he said.
There's plenty of argument ‚Äãagainst that still, of course - the most ‚ÅÝobvious one being that we don't know the extent and length of this Gulf energy hit yet, and overreaction may prove unnecessarily damaging.
ECB Vice President Luis de Guindos spoke on Wednesday of a need for cool heads and warned that stoked financial market volatility could cause its own problems.
For others the comparison with 2021/2022 is ‚Äãunfair because both fiscal and monetary policy were much looser back then than they are now - so the starting points are different.
Even Schnabel argued that ‚Äãthe energy supply shocks are ‚ÅÝdifferent now than four years ago - the earlier one being a more permanent cut of Russian gas and oil supplies and this month's likely a somewhat more temporary halt to Gulf exports.
For now, however, markets seem to favor the idea that the ECB will move more quickly to limit the need for subsequent harsher action down the road.
If oil and gas prices don't fall back very soon, an early shot across the bow to price-setters may well ‚ÅÝbe in store.
(The ‚Äãopinions expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new ‚Äãsource for global financial commentary.

And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: It‚Äôs time for short sellers to make a comeback
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 12 (Reuters) - The last three years have been a wonderful time to own U.S. stocks. The S&P 500 returned about 22% a year between 2022 and 2025. For short sellers - those betting on falling prices - the environment has been far more punishing.
Some of the best-known short-focused firms have either retreated or ‚Äãshut down. Jim Chanos' Kynikos Associates closed its main hedge funds in 2023, citing pressure on the long/short equity model. By then, Pershing Square's Bill Ackman had already sworn off ‚Äåactivist short campaigns. And last year Hindenburg Research founder Nathan Anderson announced that he was disbanding his firm.
Short sellers have historically been cast as the ‚Äúbad guys‚Äù, those rooting for companies to fail so that the ‚Äúshorts‚Äù can benefit at the expense of long-only investors - the vast majority of market participants.
All the way back in 1929, short sellers were widely blamed for deepening the stock market crash. President Herbert Hoover excoriated them for supposedly helping to plunge the country into distress by pummelling security prices.
More ‚Äãrecently, in March 2025, Kyndryl Holdings (KD.N) , opens new tab blasted a bearish report on its business by short‚Äëfocused Gotham City Research. The IT services provider rejected Gotham's claim that it was using deceptive accounting to ‚Äãcreate an illusion of profitability, calling the allegations ‚Äúdeliberately misleading‚Äù and ‚Äúdesigned to manipulate the company's stock.‚Äù
However, it turned out that investors would have benefited from heeding the ‚ÅÝshort-oriented research shop‚Äôs warning that Kyndryl appeared to be grossly overvalued. On February 9 , 2026, Kyndryl's chief financial officer and general counsel both resigned amid news of a Securities and Exchange Commission inquiry into its ‚Äãaccounting practices. That day, the share price plummeted from $23.49 to $10.59.
NORMAL SELLING ALONE CAN'T PREVENT BUBBLES
The instinctive dislike of short sellers is understandable. But their disappearance would not necessarily make life better for long-only investors. Going ‚Äãlong a stock when it‚Äôs severely overvalued can prove extremely costly. Short sellers can reduce investors‚Äô risk by helping push prices down to a more reasonable level.
Critics might argue that short sellers are unnecessary because markets already have a natural check on overvaluation. After all, for every buyer there is a seller, and the seller, by definition, must think the stock is no longer worth holding at the current price. Doesn‚Äôt that prevent a stock from becoming objectively overvalued?
The flaw in ‚Äãthis argument is that value isn‚Äôt always the primary consideration behind a sale. Some investors take profits for tax reasons or to raise cash for personal needs. Others follow short-term trading strategies designed to capitalize ‚Äãon tiny price moves, with little or no regard to intrinsic value.
Such investors won‚Äôt drive down a stock that‚Äôs unreasonably inflated by the latest investment fad, especially if buyers are ignoring telltale signs that reported profits have been ‚Äåexaggerated through accounting ‚ÅÝgimmickry.
Correcting an overvaluation under those conditions requires proactive selling, which isn‚Äôt feasible if short sales are prohibited. Under such a ban, a trader aiming to push the stock lower by selling would first have to buy it, which would defeat the purpose.
The popular image of short sellers as wealth destroyers is therefore mistaken. In reality, they discourage the bandwagon effect, whereby masses of enthusiastic buyers jump into a stock at a wildly excessive price and subsequently suffer a massive wipeout.
Of course, short sellers are not selfless heroes working for the common good. They are seeking to make a profit, just like any investor, and heavy shorting can ‚Äãpotentially worsen market stress. That may warrant reasonable regulation, ‚Äãbut not the driving out of informed ‚ÅÝskeptics who help keep prices tied to reality.
A COMEBACK FOR SKEPTICS?
Could we soon see a reversal of the recent contraction in short sellers‚Äô ranks?
There are certainly signs that investor optimism about frothy valuations is being tested.
As of March 9, the previously high-flying Magnificent Seven tech stocks were down by an average of 11% from ‚Äãtheir 2026 highs.
Meanwhile, shares of leading lenders in the private-credit market have also lost their luster, as investors worry about these firms‚Äô substantial exposure to software producers ‚Äãthreatened by advances in artificial ‚ÅÝintelligence.
At the iConnections Global Alts conference late last month, Dan Loeb, the CEO of hedge fund Third Point, proclaimed, ‚ÄúThe lost art of short selling has come back and it‚Äôs absolutely critical this year.‚Äù
If he is right, long-only investors may experience a bit more volatility in the coming months. But they may also get help avoiding devastating losses in stocks that temporarily detach from their fundamentals before nose-diving back to fair value.
(The views expressed here ‚ÅÝare those of Marty ‚ÄãFridson , the publisher of Income Securities Advisor. He is a past governor of the CFA Institute, consultant to the Federal ‚ÄãReserve Board of Governors, and Special Assistant to the Director for Deferred Compensation, Office of Management and the Budget, The City of New York.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen ‚Äãto the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Energean to acquire Chevron's Angola oil stakes for $260 million
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - Energean (ENOG.L) , opens new tab on Thursday said it will acquire Chevron's (CVX.N) , opens new tab interests in two offshore ‚ÄãAngola oil blocks for a base consideration ‚Äåof $260 million, as the Mediterranean-focused gas producer follows through on its plan to build out a ‚Äãhub in West Africa.
Energean has been ‚Äãramping up spending to boost output amid ‚ÅÝgeopolitical disruptions and assessing new merger and ‚Äãacquisition opportunities, particularly in West Africa, as ‚Äãit aims to expand its business.
Here are some key details about the deal:

item.title: Russia's Tatneft says 2025 net profit falls 48% y/y to $2 bln
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - Russian ‚Äãmid-sized oil ‚Äåproducer Tatneft on ‚ÄãThursday posted ‚Äãa 48.1% fall ‚ÅÝin ‚Äã2025 full-year ‚Äãnet profit to 158.62 ‚Äãbillion ‚Äãroubles ($2 billion).
Its 2025 ‚Äårevenue ‚ÅÝfell 10% to 1.8 trillion ‚Äãroubles, ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝcompany said.
($1 = ‚Äã79.1500 ‚Äãroubles)

item.title: Tesla's energy business wins approval to supply electricity in UK
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - Britain's energy regulator Ofgem ‚Äãsaid on Thursday ‚Äåit had approved an electricity supply ‚Äãlicence for ‚ÄãTesla Energy Ventures Limited, ‚ÅÝa subsidiary ‚Äãof Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab , allowing ‚Äãthe company to provide power to households and ‚Äãbusinesses across ‚ÄãBritain.
The authorisation took effect ‚Äåon ‚ÅÝWednesday after a months-long assessment process.
Ofgem said the ‚Äãlicence covers ‚Äãelectricity ‚ÅÝsupply activities only and ‚Äãis separate ‚Äãfrom ‚ÅÝan existing generation licence held by ‚ÅÝTesla ‚ÄãMotors ‚ÄãLimited.

item.title: Explainer: How persistently high oil prices could impact India's vulnerable economy
item.description:
MUMBAI, March 12 (Reuters) - India's external balance and government finances could be hit if oil prices stay high for an extended period, economists have said, as the Iran war pushes up oil import costs and the subsidies needed to keep key commodities affordable.
India is considered to be ‚Äãamong the most vulnerable to a global oil shock as it imports nearly 90% of its crude requirements and about ‚Äå50% of its gas requirements. Over half of its crude is from the Middle East, where export flows have been disrupted by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran , and India's current oil stocks are only enough to cover 20 to 25 days.
Gas supply shortages have already begun hitting industries and consumers , and Iran has threatened a protracted conflict and $200 ‚Äãper barrel for oil .
If oil prices average $100 a barrel for close to 12 months, the South Asian nation could also see ‚Äãgrowth fall sharply and inflation rise.
A prolonged crisis could widen the country's current account deficit, weaken the rupee ‚ÅÝand stoke inflation, the government said in its monthly report last week.
The most immediate impact would be on India's current account deficit. ‚ÄãThis concern has pushed the rupee to a record low and forced the central bank to sell dollars from its reserves.
An average price ‚Äãof $100 a barrel would widen the current account deficit to 1.9%-2.2% of GDP for the 2026/27 financial year, from a projected 0.7%-0.8% of GDP, rating agency ICRA said in a note.
India's current account deficit was last at 2% in 2022. Its financial year runs from April 1 to March 31.
The federal government's annual expenditure ‚Äãcould also rise by 3.6 trillion rupees ($39 billion) in the next financial year if oil prices hold at an average of $100 per barrel, ‚Äãaccording to Mumbai-based Elara Securities.
The government's total estimated expenditure for the next financial year is 53.5 trillion rupees, according to the annual budget presented in February.
A ‚Äåkey expense ‚ÅÝwould be higher subsidies for the fertiliser sector to ensure farmers have the key input at affordable costs.
At an average price of $100 a barrel, fertiliser subsidies could rise by 200 billion rupees, Elara Securities said, and the government might also need to compensate oil marketing companies if they are asked to keep retail petrol and diesel prices low.
While retail fuel costs are technically deregulated in India, oil companies tend to delay ‚Äãprice adjustments in times of economic ‚Äãstrain.
The government is targeting a ‚ÅÝfiscal deficit of 4.3% of GDP for the 2026/27 year.
If it chooses to maintain that deficit, it may have to cut long-term infrastructure spending being used to boost growth and jobs, Elara Securities said.
The Indian economy is expected to grow at more than 7% in the next financial year, on ‚Äãtop of growth ‚ÅÝof 7.6% forecast for the current year.
If oil prices hold near $100 per barrel through the next financial year, GDP growth could fall to 6.6% and inflation could rise to 4.1%, the research department at the State Bank of India said in a report on March 7. If oil prices average $130 ‚ÅÝper barrel, ‚ÄãGDP growth could plummet to 6%, it said.
India's economy has been in a "Goldilocks" phase, ‚ÄãReserve Bank of India Governor Sanjay Malhotra said in December.
While growth has been strong, inflation is low - coming in at 2.75% in January, close to the lower end of ‚Äãthe central bank's comfort band of 2%-6%.
($1 = 92.0000 Indian rupees)

item.title: Taiwan parties agree government can sign stalled agreements on U.S. arms deals
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 12 (Reuters) - Taiwan's three main political parties agreed on Thursday to authorise its government to sign U.S. agreements for four arms sales packages, after ‚Äãofficials warned that Taipei would go to the back of the ‚Äåline if it missed the deadline.
The back and forth on Taiwan's defence spending has provoked concern in the United States, as it is the most important international backer and ‚Äãarms supplier of the Chinese-claimed island, despite a lack of formal ‚Äãdiplomatic ties.
President Lai Ching-te's government has tried to get parliament ‚ÅÝto pass $40 billion in extra defence spending but the opposition, which controls ‚Äãthe most seats, says the proposals are unclear, and it cannot be expected ‚Äãto pass "blank cheques" despite supporting defence.
Both opposition parties have come up with their own, less expensive proposals, but the defence ministry has said the letters of offer and acceptance ‚Äãfor the weapons with the United States have to be signed or ‚ÄãTaiwan would lose its place in the production and delivery queue.
Lawmakers from both sides agreed ‚Äåduring ‚ÅÝa meeting of parliament's foreign affairs and defence committee that the government can still sign the agreements in advance, even if the reviews of the spending proposals are not approved in time.
The weapons to be signed for include ‚ÄãTOW anti-tank missiles, M109A7 ‚Äãself-propelled howitzers, ‚ÅÝLockheed Martin-made (LMT.N) , opens new tab Javelin missiles and the HIMARS multiple launch rocket system.
On Tuesday, Defence Minister Wellington Koo told reporters the ‚ÄãHIMARS letter expired on March 26, for 82 systems the ‚ÄãU.S. announced ‚ÅÝas part of an $11-billion arms sale package for Taiwan.
Sunday is the deadline to sign for the other weapons systems, the ministry says.
Last month, a bipartisan group ‚ÅÝof ‚Äã37 U.S. lawmakers voiced concern to senior Taiwan lawmakers ‚Äãabout the stalled plans.
The Trump administration has pressed allies to increase defence spending, a plank Lai ‚Äãand his government have enthusiastically embraced.

item.title: China teapots maintain refinery runs to cash in on lucrative fuel sales
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 12 (Reuters) - China's smaller independent refiners are maintaining output to cash in on a surge in domestic fuel prices, but may be forced to trim runs ‚Äãfrom late April as cheap feedstocks from inventories run thin and new deliveries price ‚Äåin the war-induced rally, industry and trade sources said.
Plants in Shandong province, China's hub for smaller refineries known as teapots that make up roughly a quarter of national output, are keeping run rates steady to maximise fuel sales at ‚Äãprices 20%-30% higher since Israel and the U.S. attacked Iran on February 28.
"Runs are ‚Äãstable. We're focusing on lifting the refined fuel prices and maximising sales at higher prices, ‚ÅÝaiming to reap profits in the month of March for the whole of 2026," said a ‚Äãteapot official.
Many refiners in Asia have been cutting runs to preserve feedstock.
Teapots are capitalising on deeply discounted Iranian ‚Äãand Russian crude bought before the war, when benchmark Brent was around $73 a barrel. Brent is now around $100 after touching nearly $120 on Monday.
"We built some inventories earlier, so the pressure is not that big for the near ‚Äãterm," said another Shandong teapot executive.
Ex-factory wholesale prices for 0#diesel stood at 7,840 yuan ($1,142.19) per metric ‚Äãton on Wednesday, up 37% from February 28, while that of 92-octane gasoline rose 26% during the same period, ‚Äåaccording ‚ÅÝto postings by Shandong Dongming Petrochemical, a large independent plant.
Hengli Petrochemical, a large independent in Dalian in the northeast, is maintaining processing rates at around 105% of its nameplate capacity of 400,000 barrels per day plant, a company representative told Reuters on Wednesday.
Consultancy Oilchem last assessed that Shandong teapots ‚Äãwere operating at 54.58% ‚Äãof capacity in the ‚ÅÝweek ended March 5, up 2.89 percentage points on the week, adding that runs may inch up further this week on improving margins.
Crude oil bought and ‚Äãdelivered before February 28 should last one and half months for many ‚ÄãShandong-based plants, according ‚ÅÝto traders dealing with teapots, but operators may soon feel the pinch of soaring crude costs as the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively shut.
Meanwhile, Chinese demand for refined gasoline and diesel has been declining ‚ÅÝas ‚Äãits cars, buses, trucks and motorcycles are rapidly becoming electricity-powered, ‚Äãand Beijing's move to ban fuel exports to pre-empt a war-induced shortage will keep a lid on margins, traders said.
($1 = 6.8640 ‚ÄãChinese yuan renminbi)

item.title: Shell CEO Wael Sawan's pay jumps 60% to $19 mln for 2025 -annual report
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - The pay package ‚Äãfor Shell < SHEL.L , opens new tab Chief ‚ÄåExecutive Wael Sawan was 13.8 million ‚Äãpounds ($18.47 million) ‚Äãfor 2025 compared with ‚ÅÝ8.6 million ‚Äãpounds in the ‚Äãprevious year, Shell's annual report showed on Thursday.
Shell's ‚Äãannual profit of ‚Äãaround $18.5 billion last year ‚Äåwas ‚ÅÝmore than a fifth below 2024's amid weaker ‚Äãoil ‚Äãprices ‚ÅÝand chemicals results, but ‚Äãit kept the ‚Äãpace ‚ÅÝof its bumper share buyback ‚ÅÝprogramme.
($1 = ‚Äã0.7473 ‚Äãpounds)

item.title: Shell's emissions broadly stable at around 1.1 bln tons CO2 equivalent
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - Shell's (SHEL.L) , opens new tab emissions were largely stable in 2025 at ‚Äãaround 1.1 billion metric tons ‚Äåof CO2 equivalent, according to its annual report published on Thursday and Reuters ‚Äãcalculations.
The bulk of the energy ‚Äãmajor's emissions come from so-called Scope ‚ÅÝ3 emissions based mainly on the ‚Äãcombustion of the fuel a company ‚Äãsells. In comparison, Britain's emissions stood at around 480 million tons of CO2 equivalent ‚Äãin 2024.
Net carbon intensity (NCI), the main ‚Äãmeasure the group uses for its energy transition ‚Äåstrategy, ‚ÅÝstood at 71 grams of CO2 equivalent per megajoule, unchanged from 2024, said Shell, which wants to ‚Äãreduce this ‚Äãmeasure to ‚ÅÝzero by 2050.
Measuring emissions performance by intensity means a ‚Äãcompany can technically increase its ‚Äãfossil ‚ÅÝfuel output and overall emissions while using offsets or adding renewable energy ‚ÅÝor ‚Äãbiofuels to its ‚Äãproduct mix to bring the headline figure down.

item.title: Generali posts record 2025 profit, unveils 500-million-euro share buyback
item.description:
MILAN, March 12 (Reuters) - Italy's top insurer Generali on Thursday posted a record profit for 2025, driven by all business segments, ‚Äãand said it was preparing a new 500-million-euro ($577.1 million) share ‚Äåbuyback, highlighting CEO Philippe Donnet's focus on shareholder returns.
In its first full-year results since Donnet's critics gained influence, Generali posted record operating profit of 8 billion euros, up ‚Äã9.7%, while adjusted net profit rose 14.5% to an all-time high ‚Äãof 4.3 billion euros.
Both figures were broadly in line with ‚ÅÝa company-provided analyst consensus.
Lower natural catastrophe claims helped lift non-life ‚Äãearnings, while life business held up on broad-based growth and fewer surrenders, Generali ‚Äãsaid in a statement.
State-backed Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena (BMPS.MI) , opens new tab , with the backing of Italian tycoon Francesco Gaetano Caltagirone and Delfin, the holding company of late billionaire Leonardo Del ‚ÄãVecchio, gained control of Mediobanca, Generali's largest investor, last year.
Caltagirone and ‚ÄãDelfin, two major Generali investors, tried unsuccessfully to oust Donnet, who was backed by ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝformer Mediobanca CEO Alberto Nagel, in 2022, blaming him for failing to grow the insurer fast enough and have since criticized several of his decisions.
MPS have not yet outlined its plans for Generali or the future of Donnet.
Generali's board ‚Äãapproved a dividend ‚Äãof 1.64 euros ‚ÅÝper share, above the highest analyst estimate in the company's consensus, for a total payout of up to 2.5 ‚Äãbillion euros.
Under its strategy 2025-2027, Generali pledged at least ‚Äã500 million ‚ÅÝeuros a year in share buybacks and more than 7 billion euros in cumulative dividends.
Despite warning that recent U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran would ‚ÅÝweigh ‚Äãon the global economy, Generali said it ‚Äãremained committed to the targets of its 2025-2027 plan, including 8%-10% annual growth in earnings per ‚Äãshare.
($1 = 0.8664 euros)

item.title: First train to Pyongyang in six years leaves Beijing
item.description:
BEIJING, March 12 (Reuters) - The first passenger train service between the Chinese and North Korean capitals left Beijing ‚ÄãRailway Station on Thursday, ending a six-year gap, as China moves to ‚Äåshore up cross-border infrastructure and rebuild ties with its neighbour.
Train K27 will arrive in the North Korean capital at 6:07 p.m. (0907 GMT) on Friday, after a journey of ‚Äã24 hours and 41 minutes skirting north of the Bohai Sea ‚Äãwith a stopover in the border city of Dandong , China's railway ‚ÅÝauthority said.
China and North Korea are "friendly neighbours" and a cross-border passenger ‚Äãtrain service facilitates people-to-people exchanges, a foreign ministry spokesperson told reporters on Thursday.
China ‚Äãalso backs stronger communication between both sides to ease such exchanges, the spokesperson added.
The service was suspended when the COVID-19 pandemic broke out in 2020.
North Korea is largely ‚Äãclosed to foreign tourism, with few exceptions, largely for Russian tour ‚Äãgroups under restricted arrangements, say travel agencies organising trips to the country.
The service linking ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝcapitals will operate four days a week in both directions, running on Mondays, Wednesdays, Thursdays and Saturdays, China's railway authority said in a notice.
Tickets, restricted to business visa holders, were sold out for Thursday's trip, but those for ‚ÄãMarch 18 were ‚Äãstill available, a ‚ÅÝBeijing travel agency said.
The shorter Dandong-Pyongyang link will operate daily in both directions, with the first service leaving ‚ÄãChina's northeastern city of Dandong at 10 a.m. on ‚ÄãThursday to ‚ÅÝarrive in Pyongyang at 6:07 p.m., the official news agency Xinhua said.
Cross-border flights were also halted during the pandemic.
North Korea's state carrier Air Koryo resumed flights ‚ÅÝto China ‚Äãin 2023 and now offers services between ‚Äãthe capitals twice weekly on Tuesdays and Saturdays, its website booking service showed.

item.title: Kenya arrests man trying to smuggle over 2,000 live ants in his luggage
item.description:
NAIROBI, March 12 (Reuters) - A man was arrested with more than 2,200 live garden ants in his luggage at Nairobi's main airport ‚Äãthis week amid a rise in cases of smuggling ‚Äåof the insects in Kenya.
Chinese national Zhang Kequn, 27, was arrested at Jomo Kenyatta International Airport on Tuesday while he was trying to ‚Äãleave the country, court filings seen by Reuters on ‚ÄãThursday showed. Immigration officials flagged a "stop order" on ‚ÅÝZhang's passport after he evaded arrest in Kenya last year.
Ant ‚Äãaficionados pay large sums to maintain colonies in large transparent vessels known ‚Äãas formicariums, which offer a literal window into the species' complex social structures and behaviours.
Last year four men were fined $7,700 each for trying to traffic ‚Äãthousands of ants valuable to Kenya's ecosystem in a ‚Äãcase that experts said signalled a shift in biopiracy from trophies like elephant ‚Äåivory ‚ÅÝto lesser-known species.
Investigators said a search of Zhang's luggage recovered 2,238 ants, including 1,948 packed in test tubes and the rest in three rolls of "soft tissue papers".
They said Zhang had ‚Äãbeen in Kenya ‚Äãfor two weeks ‚ÅÝand had mentioned three accomplices who supplied him with the ants.
The Kenya Wildlife Service told ‚Äãthe court that it needed more time to ‚Äãcomplete ‚ÅÝinvestigations, including examining an iPhone and a MacBook recovered from Zhang.
The wildlife service said a similar consignment of ants had been seized ‚ÅÝin ‚ÄãBangkok on Tuesday that originated from ‚ÄãKenya, indicating the existence of a widespread and organised ant-smuggling network.

item.title: Japan PM Takaichi cancels official duties over suspected cold symptoms, Jiji reports
item.description: TOKYO, March 12 (Reuters) - Japan's ‚ÄãPrime Minister ‚ÄåSanae Takaichi ‚Äãhas ‚Äãcut short her ‚ÅÝofficial ‚Äãduties and ‚Äãreturned to her official ‚Äãresidence ‚Äãafter suffering suspected ‚Äåcold ‚ÅÝsymptoms, the Jiji ‚Äãnews ‚Äãagency ‚ÅÝreported on ‚ÄãThursday.

item.title: World faces largest-ever oil supply disruption on Middle East war,¬ÝIEA says
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - The war in the Middle East ‚Äãis creating the biggest ‚Äåoil supply disruption in the history of the global ‚Äãmarket, the International ‚ÄãEnergy Agency said on Thursday, ‚ÅÝa day after the ‚Äãagency agreed to release ‚Äãa record volume of oil from strategic stockpiles.
Middle East Gulf ‚Äãcountries have cut total ‚Äãoil production by at least 10 ‚Äåmillion ‚ÅÝbpd - a volume equal to almost 10% of world demand - as a ‚Äãresult ‚Äãof ‚ÅÝthe conflict, the agency said in ‚Äãits latest monthly oil ‚Äãmarket ‚ÅÝreport. Without a rapid restart of shipping flows, ‚ÅÝthese ‚Äãlosses are set ‚Äãto increase, IEA said.

item.title: Russia jails for life Islamic State-linked Tajiks over concert hall attack that killed 149
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - A Russian military court handed down life sentences on Thursday to four ‚ÄãTajik men and 11 accomplices over ‚Äåtheir involvement in a mass shooting that killed 149 people at a concert hall near Moscow two ‚Äãyears ago, Russian state news agency ‚ÄãTASS reported.
Prosecutors charged 19 people in connection ‚ÅÝwith the shooting spree at the Crocus ‚ÄãCity Hall on March 22, 2024 - the deadliest ‚Äãsuch attack in Russia in two decades. The Islamic State-Khorasan, or ISIS-K, claimed responsibility.
The four other defendants ‚Äãwere given sentences ranging from 19 to ‚Äã22 years in prison, Russia's RIA state news agency ‚Äåreported.
It ‚ÅÝwas not immediately clear how the defendants pleaded or whether they would appeal. Their trial was held behind closed doors, as ‚Äãis customary with ‚Äãsuch ‚ÅÝcases in Russia.
ISIS-K is the Afghan offshoot of the militant group ‚Äãthat once sought control over swathes ‚Äãof ‚ÅÝIraq and Syria. It has been linked to several other plotted attacks in southern Russia and Azerbaijan ‚ÅÝin ‚Äãrecent years, a trend that ‚Äãhas alarmed intelligence services.

item.title: Oil jumps 6% as Iran escalates attacks on Gulf shipping
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - Oil prices rose sharply on Thursday as Iran stepped up attacks ‚Äåon oil and transport facilities across the Middle East, fuelling concerns of a prolonged conflict and potential disruptions to oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz.
Brent futures climbed $5.95, or 6.47%, to $97.93 a barrel at 0915 GMT, having hit $100 per barrel in earlier trading, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate ‚Äãcrude was up $5.25, or 6%, to $92.50.
Brent hit $119.50 a barrel on Monday, its highest since mid-2022, then dropped after ‚ÄãU.S. President Donald Trump said the Iran war could be over soon.
The war in the Middle ‚ÅÝEast is causing the biggest oil-supply disruption in the history of global markets, the International Energy Agency said ‚Äãon Thursday, a day after approving the release of a record volume of 400 million barrels of oil from strategic stockpiles.
Middle ‚ÄãEast Gulf countries have cut total oil production by at least 10 million barrels per day - a volume equalling almost 10% of world demand - as a result of the conflict, the agency said in its latest monthly oil market report.
The market pricing is taking ‚Äãthe IEA release with a pinch of salt, as its timeline is not established, PVM analyst John Evans said, ‚Äãadding that it is presumed to span 90 days, equating to approximately 4.5 million barrels per day.
Goldman Sachs forecasts Brent crude to ‚Äåaverage $98 ‚ÅÝper barrel in March and April before declining to $71 by the fourth quarter, but warns that in an upside-risk scenario, where flows through the strait are disrupted for a month, the March and April average could surge to $110.
"The only way to see oil prices trade lower on a sustained basis is by getting oil flowing through the Strait of ‚ÄãHormuz," ING analysts said. "Failing to ‚Äãdo so means that ‚ÅÝthe market highs are still ahead of us."
Explosives-laden Iranian boats appear to have attacked two fuel tankers in Iraqi waters, setting them ablaze and killing one crew member on Wednesday ‚Äãafter projectiles struck four vessels in Gulf waters, port, maritime security and risk firms ‚Äãsaid.
Lebanon's Hezbollah launched its ‚ÅÝbiggest rocket salvo of the current war on Wednesday night, prompting Israeli strikes that shook Beirut. Hezbollah's attack also raised fears about Yemen's Houthis joining the war alongside Iran, to further disrupt shipping in the Middle East.
Also on the supply side, ‚ÅÝChina has ordered ‚Äãan immediate ban on refined fuel exports in March, a further ‚Äãstep to preempt a potential domestic fuel shortage caused by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, sources said on Thursday.

item.title: Landslide in southern Ethiopia kills 52, communications office says
item.description: NAIROBI, March 12 (Reuters) - A landslide ‚Äåin the ‚ÄãGamo ‚ÄãZone of ‚ÅÝsouthern ‚ÄãEthiopia ‚Äãearlier this week ‚Äãhas ‚Äãkilled at least ‚Äå52 ‚ÅÝpeople, the ‚ÄãGamo ‚ÄãZone ‚ÅÝcommunications office ‚Äãsaid ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝThursday.

item.title: ASEAN ministers to hold meetings to address Middle East crisis
item.description:
MANILA, March 12 (Reuters) - The widening conflict in the Middle East is expected to dominate discussions at Friday's ASEAN economic ministers' retreat, with foreign ministers also holding a virtual meeting on the same day to tackle a deepening ‚Äãcrisis that has upended global markets.
The Philippines, this year's ASEAN chair, is ‚Äåhosting the meetings as officials weigh impacts and responses to surging oil prices as well as disruptions to shipping, logistics, and trade flows across the region's export-reliant economies.
"The concern is a given," Philippine ‚Äãtrade undersecretary Allan Gepty told reporters, saying the region could not ignore the ‚Äãeffects of the crisis on inflation and jobs.
Joint U.S. and Israeli strikes ‚ÅÝon Iran launched almost two weeks ago have so far killed around 2,000 people and have ‚Äãthrown global energy markets and transport into chaos.
The conflict has effectively shut the Strait of ‚ÄãHormuz, the chokepoint that funnels about a fifth of the world's oil and LNG to Asia, sending crude surging to above $100 a barrel.
The crisis has forced ASEAN foreign ministers to call a special virtual meeting to assess ‚Äãthe implications for Southeast Asia, where several economies rely heavily on crude and LNG ‚Äãshipments from the Gulf.
The Philippines sources much of its oil needs from the Middle East and Qatar's ‚ÄåLNG ‚ÅÝhalt has also tightened supplies.
Several ASEAN countries have already taken concrete steps to cope with the impact of the Middle East crisis. The Philippines has shortened the government work week to conserve fuel, and its president has asked Congress for authority to suspend excise taxes ‚Äãon fuel to help ‚Äãstabilise costs.
Vietnam on Thursday cut its ‚ÅÝretail fuel prices overnight following the easing in global crude oil prices from earlier highs, but the government warned prices will ‚Äãremain volatile, with more supply disruptions anticipated.
Earlier this month, Thailand also halted energy ‚Äãexports to ‚ÅÝall countries other than Laos and Myanmar.
"It is important that our actions and responses to the ongoing conflicts must be synchronised," Gepty said.
ASEAN foreign ministers have said that the escalation in ‚ÅÝconflict ‚Äãwas " particularly regrettable , opens new tab ," and urged an immediate cessation of hostilities, ‚Äãcalling on all sides to exercise maximum restraint, protect civilians, and resolve differences through dialogue in line with ‚Äãinternational law.

item.title: Israeli military says it targeted site used in Iran's nuclear programme
item.description:
DUBAI, March 12 (Reuters) - The Israeli ‚Äãmilitary said ‚Äåon Thursday it ‚Äãhad in ‚Äãrecent days targeted ‚ÅÝa ‚Äãsite used ‚Äãin the Iranian nuclear programme ‚Äãwhich ‚Äãis identified as "Taleghan".
Taleghan is ‚Äåa ‚ÅÝfacility at the Parchin ‚Äãmilitary ‚Äãcomplex ‚ÅÝwhich Western officials suspect ‚Äãis ‚Äãlinked ‚ÅÝto Tehran's nuclear programme.

item.title: Up to 3.2 million people displaced inside Iran by conflict, UN says
item.description:
GENEVA, March 12 (Reuters) - The U.N. refugee ‚Äãagency said ‚Äåon Thursday that up to ‚Äã3.2 million ‚Äãpeople have been displaced ‚ÅÝinside Iran ‚Äãsince the ‚Äãconflict began on February 28.
"This figure ‚Äãis likely ‚Äãto continue rising as ‚Äåhostilities ‚ÅÝpersist, marking a worrying escalation in humanitarian ‚Äãneeds," ‚ÄãUNHCR ‚ÅÝsaid in a statement, ‚Äãciting ‚Äãpreliminary ‚ÅÝassessments based on the number ‚ÅÝof ‚Äãuprooted ‚Äãhouseholds.

item.title: Poland says foiled cyberattack on nuclear centre may have come from Iran
item.description:
WARSAW, March 12 (Reuters) - Poland has foiled a cyberattack on its nuclear research centre and is examining signs that Iran may be behind it, ‚Äãthe government said on Thursday, cautioning the indicators might ‚Äåbe a deliberate misdirection to hide the attackers‚Äô true location.
Poland says it has been the target of numerous cyberattacks since Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in ‚Äã2022. Moscow has repeatedly denied involvement.
Minister for Digital Affairs ‚ÄãKrzysztof Gawkowski told private broadcaster TVN24+ that the attack on ‚ÅÝPoland's National Centre for Nuclear Research had taken place "in the ‚Äãpast few days".
"The attack may not have been on a huge ‚Äãscale, but there was an attempt to break through the security that was stopped. Appropriate services are already working", Gawkowski said, adding that the centre ‚Äãwas safe.
"The first identifications of the entry vectors, i.e. those places ‚Äãfrom which (the centre) was attacked, are related to Iran," he said. "When there is ‚Äåfinal ‚ÅÝinformation and the services will check it, we will verify it, but there are many indications that it took place on the territory of Iran."
The Iranian embassy in Warsaw did not immediately ‚Äãreply to an ‚Äãemailed request for ‚ÅÝcomment.
The centre conducts research into nuclear energy, subatomic physics and related fields. Poland has no nuclear ‚Äãweapons and is building its first nuclear power plant.
The ‚ÄãU.S. ‚ÅÝand Israel unleashed coordinated airstrikes on Iran on February 28, killing its Supreme Leader , Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
Tehran responded by hitting Israel and Gulf states hosting ‚ÅÝU.S. ‚Äãmilitary installations, effectively halting oil and gas ‚Äãshipments through the Strait of Hormuz -- a conduit for roughly a fifth of the ‚Äãworld‚Äôs LNG and petroleum.

item.title: Explainer: Who might the US be talking to in Cuba?
item.description:
HAVANA, March 12 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump has said repeatedly the United States is in talks with high-level ‚ÄãCuban representatives, and that Cuba is eager to reach a deal to defuse tensions between the two neighbors that have deepened the island's economic crisis .
The Cuban government ‚Äåhas denied any official talks are under way, but has yet to explicitly deny press reports that U.S. officials were in talks with Raul Guillermo Rodriguez Castro, the grandson of former Cuban President Raul Castro, who is 94 and still wields great influence.
If talks are taking place, they come at a time when Washington has tightened its economic sanctions on Cuba by imposing a near-total oil blockade, and after the U.S. ‚Äãcaptured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro on January 3, removing a crucial Cuban ally from power.
Raul Castro fought alongside his older brother Fidel in ‚Äãthe revolution that toppled a U.S. ally in 1959 and served as his brother's loyal defense minister for decades. He ascended to the ‚ÅÝpresidency, first on an interim basis when Fidel got sick in 2006, then took over definitively when Fidel retired in 2008. With Fidel's death in 2016, Raul assumed the mantle ‚Äãas the single unifying leader among those loyal to the Cuban revolution.
He still wields influence even after stepping down as president in 2018 and as leader of the Communist Party in 2021, ‚Äãsince then carrying the honorific title army general.
At his 2018 swearing-in ceremony, current President Miguel Diaz-Canel said Raul Castro "will lead the most important decisions for the present and future of the nation."
That was on public display as recently as December 2025. As Cuba's Communist Party prepared to choose Diaz-Canel's successor at an all-important party congress this year, Raul Castro proposed postponing the congress indefinitely, given Cuba's economic crisis. The party's ‚ÄãCentral Committee approved the proposal unanimously.
Known as "El Cangrejo," or "The Crab," because of his hand, Rodriguez Castro, 41, is widely seen as one of his grandfather's closest ‚Äãconfidants. As former bodyguard to Raul Castro, he was at his grandfather's side, sometimes dressed in the military uniform of the Interior Ministry, throughout Raul Castro's presidency. He has been reported to hold the ‚Äårank of ‚ÅÝlieutenant colonel.
The Cuban government did not immediately respond to a query about Rodriguez Castro's biography or possible role in negotiations.
Axios, citing three unnamed sources, reported on February 18 that Rodriguez Castro was in secret talks with U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio. The Miami Herald, citing unnamed sources, reported on February 26 that officials close to Rubio met with Rodriguez Castro on the sidelines of a regional Caribbean Community conference in St. Kitts and Nevis.
The Trump administration has been having secret, high-level conversations with several people in Ra√∫l Castro‚Äôs inner circle, similar to the ‚Äãdiscussions held in Venezuela before the U.S. captured ‚ÄãMaduro in a military raid earlier this ‚ÅÝyear, U.S. Representative Mario D√≠az-Balart told the Miami Herald.
Rodriguez Castro is the son of Raul's daughter Deborah Castro Espin and
That lineage places Rodriguez Castro at the intersection of Cuba's political leadership and its most powerful economic institution, making him a potentially significant go-between for Washington.
Rodriguez Castro has never spoken publicly nor given interviews.
If "El Cangrejo" is acting as a back-channel connection between Havana and Washington, he would be playing a similar role to ‚ÅÝthat of his ‚Äãuncle and Raul Castro's son, Alejandro Castro Espin, who conducted then-secret negotiations marking a major shift by Washington ‚Äãtoward Cuba during Barack Obama's presidency.
Family ties appear to matter. Neither of the younger Castros held a diplomatic job. Raul Castro in turn had been a confidant and adviser to older brother Fidel.
Alejandro Castro Espin, 60, is believed to ‚Äãhave been sidelined after his father left office, though the Interior Ministry colonel may have ascended to one-star general.

item.title: Russia's Rosatom says it will stay in Iran despite war
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - Russia‚Äôs state nuclear corporation Rosatom will stay in Iran despite the conflict in the Middle East and ‚Äãis committed to its agreement to build two ‚Äåmore units at the Bushehr nuclear plant, its head Alexei Likhachev said on Thursday.
Rosatom, which built the first 1‚Äëgigawatt unit of ‚ÄãIran‚Äôs sole nuclear power plant at Bushehr, evacuated ‚Äãsome of its staff and suspended construction work on ‚ÅÝthe new units after the United States and Israel launched ‚Äãstrikes against Iran on February 28.
Around 450 Rosatom employees remain ‚Äãat the site, Likhachev said, after 150 returned to Russia via Armenia this week.
"The construction of the second and third units ‚Äãremains among the corporation‚Äôs priorities. It is definitely not ‚Äãthe time to leave. What is happening in the Middle East is ‚Äåonly ‚ÅÝpart of a global mosaic," Rosatom quoted Likhachev as saying.
On Monday, Likhachev said that the situation around Bushehr remained tense but that there had been no strikes on ‚Äãeither the plant ‚Äãor the ‚ÅÝconstruction site.
A bilateral agreement between Russia and Iran allows for the construction of up ‚Äãto eight nuclear units, four of them at ‚ÄãBushehr.
Last ‚ÅÝautumn, Iran said it had signed a $25 billion agreement with Rosatom to build four nuclear power units with a capacity ‚ÅÝof ‚Äã5 gigawatts at another site in the country‚Äôs ‚Äãsoutheast. The parties also signed a memorandum on developing small nuclear power ‚Äãplants in Iran.

item.title: Morning Bid: Triple digit crude
item.description:
March 12 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
Crude oil is testing $100 per barrel once again, briefly topping that important threshold overnight after rising nearly 5% on Wednesday, as more ‚Äãattacks on ships in the Gulf outweighed the impact of the International Energy Agency‚Äôs plan for a record release of ‚Äåcrude reserves. Oil market volatility by one gauge recently hit levels not seen since 2020.
Iran, clearly aware that an oil shock is one of its biggest defensive weapons, warned yesterday that the conflict could send oil to $200 per barrel - and the Islamic Republic seems in no hurry to free Gulf shipping as long as the war rages.
I‚Äôll ‚Äãget into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why the lessons of the Ukraine war may now embolden ‚Äãhawks on the ECB.
And listen to today's episode of the Morning Bid podcast , where I discuss the longer-term implications of ‚ÅÝthis oil shock. Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.
Stock markets ‚Äãare down across the world again on the latest jump on oil, with major U.S. indexes finishing flat to lower on Wednesday and Asian indexes giving up ‚Äãsome recent gains on Thursday. European and U.S. stock futures were down before the bell.
The oil market‚Äôs shrug at the record 400-million-barrel reserve release is worrying and an indication of the timeline traders are now contemplating.
The 400 million barrels - more than twice the release seen after the Ukraine invasion in 2022 - would cover about 2-4 weeks of global ‚Äãdemand. If the war is still shutting down the Gulf after that, markets could tighten further.
With that timeline in mind, financial markets are already ‚Äãturning attention to potential inflation impacts and central bank responses.
A second U.S. interest rate cut has all but disappeared from the Fed futures curve, with markets now ‚Äåpricing in ‚ÅÝbarely one for 2026. February CPI inflation numbers came in as expected , but that data is from before this oil shock.
Looking ahead, there‚Äôs a sweep of central bank decisions next week. The Reserve Bank of Australia is expected to raise interest rates again. Markets are already fully priced for a European Central Bank rate rise by July, and mortgage rates are rising again in Britain. No central bank is likely to be brave enough to ‚Äãcut in this environment.
Meantime, U.S. Treasury ‚Äãyields hit their highest in almost ‚ÅÝsix months heading into Thursday. Soft debt auctions haven‚Äôt helped restore confidence this week.
And the dollar continued to firm on the dwindling rate cut expectations, holding near its strongest levels of the year so far, weighing down gold ‚Äãin turn.
Chart of the day
The IEA has coordinated emergency stock releases only four times since it was created ‚Äãby OECD members ‚ÅÝin the mid‚Äë1970s in response to the 1973 OPEC oil embargo. The infrequency underscores the gravity of the current situation, writes ROI Energy Columnist Ron Bousso .
Today's events to watch
* U.S. January trade balance (8:30 AM EDT), January housing starts (8:30 AM EDT), weekly jobless claims (8:30 AM EDT)
* U.S. 30-year note auction
* Fed's Michelle Bowman speaks
* ‚ÅÝInternational Energy ‚ÄãAgency releases its March 2026 Oil Market Report
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your ‚Äãinbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They ‚Äãdo not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Brazil's dreams for industrial-scale cocoa farms fading after price crash
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 12 (Reuters) - Cocoa farmers in Brazil have slammed the brakes on new planting projects following a 70% plunge in cocoa prices from their 2024 record high, stalling growth that investors had expected would make the country a ‚Äãmajor supplier of the main ingredient in chocolate.
At current prices of around $3,000 per metric ton , farmers and analysts told Reuters they expected around half the projects in Brazil to ‚Äågrow cocoa on an industrial scale could be canceled.
The projects, centered in Northeastern Brazil, would have added at least some 75,000 hectares of growing area, according to an estimate by supply chain services provider Czarnikow, enough to supply nearly 5% of the global demand for cocoa.
"I think Brazil expansion plans have had a massive cold shower," said Paulo Torres, a London-based cocoa industry advisor and cocoa farmer in Brazil's Bahia state. Torres himself canceled a plan for an additional 30 hectares of cocoa at his farm ‚Äãin Bahia.
Current prices do not cover the investment or production costs of new fields, making the Brazilian projects unfeasible, he said.
The farmers and investors that planned the giant cocoa farms in Brazil , backed ‚Äãby big players in the industry such as Cargill (CARG.UL) and Barry Callebaut (BARN.S) , opens new tab , saw them as a solution to years of supply shortages that led to skyrocketing prices.
The planned farms would ‚ÅÝhave provided an alternative to the main growing region in West Africa. Ghana and Ivory Coast produce nearly 50% of global cocoa, which has left the market vulnerable to production problems there.
Unfavorable weather, illegal mining and diseases ‚Äãreduced West Africa production in 2023 and 2024, causing prices to spike from an average $2,500 per ton to more than $11,000 per ton and generating panic in the industry.
Cocoa traders scrambled to source beans. Smuggling rose sharply in Africa ‚Äãwith people bypassing official government buying to sell across the border at higher prices. The chocolate industry raised prices to offset spiraling production costs.
African production has since recovered, and other geographies such as Ecuador have increased production. At the same time, consumers have cut back on buying expensive chocolate, and small cocoa farmers in Brazil have not been making money. A group of them last month blocked a road leading to the Ilheus port in Bahia, setting old tires on fire, to protest ‚Äãthe arrival of imported cocoa from Africa.
After that, Brazil's food supply agency Conab suspended any imports of cocoa from Ivory Coast.
Weight-loss drugs have further curbed demand, and the industry has reduced package sizes and turned to non-cocoa ingredients, ‚Äãsuch as artificially flavored palm oil butter, hitting demand for the beans and exacerbating the cocoa price crash.
Moises Schmidt, one of the biggest cocoa farmers in Northeast Brazil, said the current price of cocoa would not cover the investments in irrigation ‚Äåand machines for ‚ÅÝcrop care and post-harvest processing typical of the region's farms.
"If the market remains below $5,000 per ton, more than 50% of the projects are gone," he said.
Schmidt had expected to oversee as much as 10,000 hectares (24,710 acres) of cocoa plantations, roughly the size of Manhattan. He declined to comment on what the price crash means for his own plans.
Two sources, one a large farmer in the region and the other an executive at an agricultural equipment supplier, told Reuters that a large project by Switzerland-based investment company NewAg Partners , opens new tab , for up to 22,000 acres (8,900 hectares) of cocoa fields, had been suspended.
"At this stage we prefer not to comment," said NewAg chief executive Detlef ‚ÄãSchoen.
There are other initiatives in the area whose future ‚Äãis uncertain.
Copa Investimentos, a Sao Paulo-based asset manager ‚ÅÝwith investments in agriculture and forestry, was also planning an industrial-scale cocoa farm, but one of its partners, Apolonio Sales, said it is now "evaluating" the investment.
Sales said he visited some of the new cocoa farms and also held talks with cocoa traders and chocolate companies, but the company has not yet reached a conclusion ‚Äãabout whether to go forward.
Brazil cocoa production could still grow, experts said, but at a much slower rate, mostly as a diversification strategy in farms ‚Äãwhere the trees would grow ‚ÅÝalongside other crops.
The big Brazilian farms, however, had intended to deliver quick and massive production growth. An additional area of 75,000 hectares of irrigated fields could deliver 225,000 tons of beans in four years, or 4.5% of global production.
"If some big farm had plans for 400, 500 hectares, now it will do 80 or 100, only to start learning about the crop," said Emerson Silva, a sales executive at irrigation equipment maker Netafim.
Brazilian co-op Cooabriel, which announced a cocoa initiative ‚ÅÝwith Cargill last ‚Äãyear , said it would continue developing its small-scale project.
Projects from Sao Paulo's state government, which include using cocoa trees to reforest ‚Äãdegraded parts of farms, will also go ahead, said the coordinator Ricardo Pereira.
"In some areas, we already have seedlings almost ready to go into the field," he said, adding that as with any commodity market, cocoa prices would eventually rebound.

item.title: Iran war unsettles India's packaged water makers as bottles, caps get pricey
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 12 (Reuters) - The Iran war is rattling India's $5 billion packaged water market just ahead of the sweltering ‚Äåsummer season.
One of the world's fastest growing bottled water markets is seeing some manufacturers hike prices for distributors, as supply disruptions linked to the war fuel higher costs in everything from plastic bottles to caps, labels and cardboard boxes.
Though retail prices are yet to feel the heat and bigger ‚Äãcompanies are absorbing the pain, about 2,000 smaller bottled water makers have increased rates for their resellers by around ‚Äã1 rupee per bottle, a 5% hike, which will rise by a further 10% in coming ‚ÅÝdays, according to the Federation of All India Packaged Drinking Water Manufacturers' Association.
Consumers usually pay less than 20 rupees, or ‚Äãaround 20 U.S. cents, for a one-litre bottle.
"There is chaos and within the next 4-5 days, this will start impacting customer prices," ‚Äãsaid Apurva Doshi, the federation's secretary general.
Rising oil prices have increased the cost of polymer, which is made from crude oil and is a key material for the industry's plastic bottles.
The cost of material used in making plastic bottles has risen by 50% to 170 rupees per kilogram, ‚Äãwhile the price of the caps has more than doubled to 0.45 rupees apiece. Even corrugated boxes, labels and adhesive tape ‚Äãare costing much more, industry letters showed.
Clean water is a privilege in the country of 1.4 billion people where researchers say 70% of the ‚Äågroundwater ‚ÅÝis contaminated, leaving people reliant on bottled water. Companies including Bisleri, Coca-Cola's (KO.N) , opens new tab Kinley, Pepsi's (PEP.O) , opens new tab Aquafina, billionaire Mukesh Ambani's Reliance (RELI.NS) , opens new tab and Tata all compete for a share of the $5 billion market.
The companies did not respond to Reuters request for comment.
Within the broad bottled water market, natural mineral water is a $400 million business in India and a new, fast-growing wellness product for India's wealthy.
The ‚Äãpremium water segment accounted for ‚Äã8% of the bottled water ‚ÅÝmarket last year in India, compared to just 1% in 2021, Euromonitor says.
Aava, which sells mineral water sourced from the foothills of the Aravalli mountains, has increased prices of its water bottles ‚Äãby 18% for resellers, Shiroy Mehta, CEO of the company, told Reuters.
"Most manufacturers are absorbing ‚Äã40-50% of the ‚ÅÝcost to ensure that they don't lose clients. It's a poor situation for the beverage industry ahead of the summer season," he said.
The mass market, however, is dominated by companies that produce "drinking water" to be sold in 1-litre bottles to customers.
Clear Premium Water, a ‚ÅÝbrand of ‚ÄãIndia's Energy Beverages, said in a notice to its distributors there had been ‚Äãan "unprecedented and continuous surge" in prices of key raw materials used in packaging and production.
"It is no longer possible for us to absorb the escalating costs ‚Äãwhile maintaining existing product prices," the notice said.
(This story has been refiled to fix the date in the dateline to March 12)

item.title: China's mediation eases fighting between Pakistan, Afghanistan, sources say
item.description:
ISLAMABAD/BEIJING, March 12 (Reuters) - Chinese mediation efforts, including ‚Äãa message from President Xi Jinping, have helped ease the worst fighting between Pakistan and Afghanistan since ‚Äåthe Taliban returned to power in 2021, three Pakistani government officials said.
The officials said a meeting between the Chinese ambassador to Pakistan, Jiang Zaidong, and Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif late last month included a message from Xi to cease hostilities.
Neither side has reported any Pakistani air strikes on ‚ÄãAfghanistan in recent days and ground fighting along the 2,600-km (1,600-mile) border has tapered off, although daily clashes continue ‚Äãto be reported.
China has said it is in contact with both countries about ending hostilities but ‚ÅÝMosharraf Zaidi, a spokesman for Sharif who has previously said there would not be any talks with the Taliban, did ‚Äãnot respond to questions about Beijing's efforts.
Pakistani security officials have said the military campaign will continue until desired goals were achieved, ‚Äãwhich was to prevent militant attacks in Pakistan launched from Afghan soil.
Pakistan's foreign ministry and military did not respond to Reuters requests for comment.
Islamabad launched air strikes on Afghanistan on February 26, saying the Taliban were providing a safe haven to militants carrying out attacks in Pakistan. ‚ÄãKabul denies the charge and says militancy in Pakistan is an internal problem.
The Chinese efforts came as Qatar, Saudi Arabia ‚Äãand Turkey, who hosted talks between Pakistan and Afghanistan during previous clashes in October, have been embroiled in the war in the Middle ‚ÄåEast following ‚ÅÝthe U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran .
"China's Special Envoy for Afghanistan Affairs is currently shuttling between the two countries to mediate, while Chinese embassies in both nations maintain close communication with the respective parties," the Chinese foreign ministry told Reuters in an e-mail.
"The most urgent task is to prevent the fighting from expanding and for the two countries to return to the negotiating ‚Äãtable as soon as possible."
The ‚Äãforeign ministry added that Foreign ‚ÅÝMinister Wang Yi held telephone talks with Pakistan's Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar on Tuesday to discuss the conflict.
China's ambassador to Kabul, Zhao Xing, and the special envoy ‚ÄãYue Xiaoyong met Afghanistan's acting Foreign Minister Amir Khan Muttaqi this week, the Afghan foreign ‚Äãministry said in ‚ÅÝa statement.
Afghanistan and Pakistan have said they inflicted heavy damage on the other in the conflict and killed hundreds of opposition troops, without providing evidence. Reuters has not been able to verify the reports.
Beijing, a longtime Pakistani ally, has invested heavily in mines ‚ÅÝand minerals ‚Äãin both nations.
The investments include over $65 billion in road, rail and other ‚Äãdevelopment projects in Pakistan, part of Beijing's Belt and Road Initiative to expand land and sea trade routes to Europe and Africa.

item.title: Thailand seeks apology from Iran after vessel hit in Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
BANGKOK, March 12 (Reuters) - Thailand's Foreign Ministry has demanded ‚Äãan apology from Iran ‚Äåover damage caused to a Thai vessel that ‚Äãwas hit by ‚Äãa projectile in the ‚ÅÝStrait of Hormuz ‚Äãon Wednesday, which led ‚Äãto a fire and forced the crew to abandon ‚Äãship.
The ministry said ‚Äãit was seeking clarity from Iran's ‚Äåambassador ‚ÅÝon Thursday on the facts surrounding the incident.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards ‚Äãsaid in ‚Äãa ‚ÅÝstatement carried by the Tasnim ‚Äãnews agency that ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝship was "fired upon by Iranian fighters".

item.title: Russia calls on Israel and US to end the Iran war
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - Russia on Thursday called ‚Äãon Israel and the ‚ÄåUnited States to end their attacks on Iran ‚Äãand come to ‚Äãthe negotiating table.
"Russia will continue ‚ÅÝto take steps ‚Äãto end the escalation ‚Äãin the Middle East as soon as possible and ‚Äãresolve any contradictions ‚Äãby peaceful means," Foreign Ministry spokeswoman ‚ÄåMaria ‚ÅÝZakharova told reporters.
"The number of victims of the illegal military ‚Äãaction of ‚ÄãWashington ‚ÅÝand Tel Aviv among the civilian ‚Äãpopulation of Iran, ‚Äãaccording ‚ÅÝto the authorities, is in the thousands," ‚ÅÝshe ‚Äãsaid.

item.title: Russia‚Äôs Iran setback comes with consolation prize
item.description:
BERLIN, March 12 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Russia is reaping a hefty reward from the loss of its influence in the Middle East. The bombing of Iran by the U.S. ‚Äãand Israel has boosted oil prices just as Moscow‚Äôs finances are crumbling. That will give President Vladimir Putin some ‚Äåfinancial firepower to fund his war in Ukraine and postpone painful fiscal decisions.
The Russian government built its 2026 budget based on Urals crude trading at $59 a barrel. That was a realistic forecast ‚Äî and just about the price its oil was trading at the end of February. But in the ‚Äã10 days since the war in the Middle East erupted it has shot up to more than $80. That ‚Äãhands Russian President Vladimir Putin a helpful windfall given taxes and levies on oil and gas account ‚ÅÝfor about 20% , opens new tab of the central government‚Äôs revenue.
The boost is also timely. Oil and gas receipts were down 47% , opens new tab in the first ‚Äãtwo months of 2026 compared to the same period in 2025. The Kremlin had already exhausted its planned annual deficit by ‚Äãthe end of February.
If the Russian government abides by its own fiscal rules, the windfall won't flow into the budget. Anything above the $59 threshold must be saved in the country‚Äôs National Wealth Fund. But the fund acts as a rainy-day facility that can cover budget shortfalls ‚Äî the situation Russia ‚Äãis in now. The liquid assets of the fund (held in foreign currency or gold) have already been depleted from $150 billion to $50 ‚Äãbillion since the war started.
If the prices of oil and gas remain at their current level for a few months, Russia can expect ‚Äåa significant ‚ÅÝfinancial boost. According to an estimate , opens new tab from investment banker Yevgeniy Kogan, a $1 change in the Urals price triggers a variation of up to $1.9 billion in government revenue. Maintained over a whole year, higher oil prices could then deliver some $40 billion in extra revenue to Moscow.
And Putin may also sell more oil and gas. Donald Trump last week granted India a 30-day waiver from Russian oil sanctions, ‚Äãand the U.S. president hinted ‚Äãhe could go further to ‚ÅÝmake up for the impact of the Strait of Hormuz blockade on world oil markets.
Moscow refrained from condemning the U.S. when it launched its recent Iran bombing campaign. It already lost a ‚Äãkey ally in 2024 in the region with the end of Syria‚Äôs Assad regime. Many ‚Äãgovernments in the ‚ÅÝMiddle East and beyond may soon wonder what is the point of having Russia as a supposed ally. But Putin has his consolation prize ‚Äì an unexpected windfall to fund his war.
Follow Pierre Briancon on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Indian source says Iran to allow India-flagged tankers through Hormuz as first tanker arrives
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 12 (Reuters) - Iran will allow Indian-flagged tankers ‚Äãto transit the Strait of Hormuz, a conduit for 40% of the South Asian nation's crude imports, an Indian government source said ‚Äåon Thursday, but an Iranian source outside the country denied any such deal was reached.
India, the world's No. 3 oil consumer, said on Thursday the foreign ministers of the two countries have had three conversations in recent days and the latest one this week focused on "issues pertaining to the safety of shipping and India's energy security".
"Beyond that, it would be premature ‚Äãfor me to say anything," foreign ministry spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal told a weekly media briefing.
The Indian source, who was briefed on the matter, ‚Äãdeclined to be identified, citing a lack of authorisation to speak to media, while the Iranian source said the matter was ‚ÅÝa sensitive one.
On Thursday, the Suezmax tanker Shenlong, carrying Saudi crude, arrived at a port in Mumbai after transiting the strait. The Liberia-flagged vessel was ‚Äãthe first crude carrier to reach India from the Middle East since the war between Iran and the United States and Israel broke out in late February, ‚Äãaccording to LSEG data. The customer is state-run Bharat Petroleum Corp (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab , said a source with direct knowledge of the matter.
The company did not immediately respond to a request seeking comment.
The Indian source said two other foreign-flagged tankers believed to be bound for India had recently transited the Strait, and that Iran had given assurances of safe passage for Indian-flagged vessels after ‚Äãthe foreign ministers of both countries spoke by telephone late on Tuesday.
The source said the situation remained fluid, with limited clarity on how instructions were being ‚Äãrelayed across different layers of Iran‚Äôs administration.
In a statement after the talks between India's S. Jaishankar and his counterpart, Seyed Abbas Araghchi, Iran's foreign ministry said the United States ‚Äåshould be ‚ÅÝheld accountable for the "insecure situation and problems arising for shipping in the Persian Gulf".
Neither side mentioned any agreement on allowing safe passage for Indian vessels.
On Wednesday, India said 28 Indian-flagged vessels were operating west and east of the Strait with 778 Indian sailors aboard.
"Authorities, ship managers and recruitment agencies are coordinating closely with Indian embassies and local authorities to ensure safety and provide assistance to Indian seafarers," the Indian petroleum ministry said in a statement.
India has given ‚Äãsafe harbour to 183 Iranian sailors ‚Äãfrom a vessel that docked after ‚ÅÝthe war broke out between Iran and the United States and Israel.
New Delhi had allowed three Iranian ships that departed following a naval exercise in the Bay of Bengal to dock, but one was later sunk by a U.S. submarine ‚Äãin international waters, and another sought assistance from Sri Lanka.
Iran has attacked at least 16 ships in the ‚ÄãStrait since the war ‚ÅÝbegan in late February. Tehran has warned that oil prices could nearly double to $200 a barrel as it retaliates.
A Thai vessel bound for India's western port of Kandla was attacked in the Strait on Wednesday, prompting criticism from New Delhi.
"India deplores the fact that commercial shipping is being made a target of military attacks in the ongoing ‚ÅÝconflict," the ‚Äãforeign ministry said on Wednesday, adding that its citizens had also died.
The near-closure of the Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz has compelled India to scramble for alternatives, such as buying more from Russia.

item.title: Bombing won't overthrow ruling clerics, Iran dissidents say
item.description:
PARIS, March 11 (Reuters) - A senior official from ‚Äãa Paris-based Iranian opposition group said on Thursday that the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran ‚Äåwould not topple the clerical leadership, arguing that only a popular uprising backed by internal resistance could do so.
Almost two weeks of bombing have killed around 2,000 people in Iran including supreme leader Ayatollah ‚ÄãAli Khamenei, and damaged much of its military and security apparatus.
Iran has responded in ‚Äãkind, throwing global energy markets and transport into chaos and spreading the ‚ÅÝconflict across the Middle East, while the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps has tightened its grip ‚Äãon power and threatened to crush any unrest.
"The 12-day war in June, and the current ‚Äãwar, now in its 12th day, proved that bombings cannot overthrow the regime," Mohammad Mohaddesin, head of foreign policy at the National Council of Resistance of Iran (NCRI), told a news conference.
"Even if you have 50,000 ‚Äãarmed soldiers on the ground, you need the support of Iranian people. You need a ‚Äãpopular uprising. The combination of this 50,000 or 20,000 or any other number with a popular uprising, ‚Äåthen ‚ÅÝyou have this power to overthrow the regime."
Mohaddesin said he did not consider a deployment of U.S. ground troops realistic.
The NCRI, also known by its Farsi name Mujahideen-e-Khalq, was listed as a terrorist organisation by the United States until 2012.
It is banned in Iran, and it ‚Äãis unclear how much ‚Äãsupport it has there. ‚ÅÝHowever, along with its bitter rival , the monarchists backing Reza Pahlavi, exiled son of the toppled shah, it is one of the few ‚Äãopposition groups able to rally supporters.
Mohaddesin acknowledged that his group alone could ‚Äãnot bring ‚ÅÝdown the system. But he said mass protests, like those that raged in January until they were bloodily quashed, would resume once bombing stopped, and could eventually shift the balance.
"I cannot say ‚ÅÝhow ‚Äãmany months or a year, but ... this is the ‚Äãtrack of overthrowing the regime," he said.
Israeli officials have said that one of their objectives is to weaken the ‚Äãsecurity apparatus so that Iran's people can take control of their own destiny.

item.title: In Norwegian Arctic, civilian doctors, volunteer 'casualties' rehearse for war
item.description:
NARVIK, Norway, March 12 (Reuters) - In Narvik harbour in the Norwegian Arctic, the city's chief medical officer watches as dozens of "casualties" are unloaded from ‚Äãa train and get triaged before being transported in ambulances to nearby hospitals.
Thomas Hultstedt is here to observe and learn ‚Äåduring an exercise that is part of NATO's biennial drills in the European Arctic, Cold Response, which started on Monday and which this year places more focus on the role of civilians, businesses and public institutions in supporting the military in case of war.
He and other health personnel are rehearsing a scenario in which war has broken out ‚Äãin Finland and U.S. and Norwegian soldiers fighting on front lines, as well as civilians, have to be transported to Norway for ‚Äãmedical treatment.
Under the simulation, some 1,200 people are meant to be shipped over the next 10 days to Narvik, ‚ÅÝthough the actual exercise lasts just one day - Thursday - and involves about 100 volunteers, including students, playing the "casualties".
"I have never done this type of ‚Äãexercise before," Hultstedt said minutes before the train arrived at the quay.
"It is good in the sense that you prepare yourself for things that are ‚Äãout of the ordinary. This is very different from normal life. This is a war situation."
The train has come from Finland via Sweden to Narvik, where the volunteers will be "treated" in civilian hospitals in the region. In a real-life situation, they would then be sent on to southern Norway or abroad.
Hultstedt says this type of exercise was unthinkable five years ago.
"Then Sweden and Finland were not part of NATO... Now Norway has to step ‚Äãup. We must get equipment in and the injured out," he told Reuters.
Finland and Sweden joined the Atlantic military alliance in 2023 and 2024, respectively, following Russia's full-scale ‚Äåinvasion of ‚ÅÝUkraine on February 24, 2022. Norway joined NATO in 1949 as a founding member.
While NATO military planners never refer to Russia by name, the scenario they train for involves an attack from an enemy in the east. Russia lies roughly 600 km (372 miles) to the east, as the crow flies.
Moscow has repeatedly dismissed as baseless fear-mongering suggestions that it might attack a NATO member in the future.
Narvik, an industrial city nestled between a 1,006-metre (3,300 ft) ‚Äãsnow-capped mountain and the pristine Ofot ‚Äãfjord, is one of the ‚ÅÝkey places along Norway's coast where NATO allies plan to land if Finland, Sweden or Norway comes under attack.
The train line from Narvik to Kiruna in Sweden, and further east to Finland, is the main way to ‚Äãship equipment and troops between east and west in Arctic Scandinavia.
Narvik was the site in 1940 of an ‚Äãearly battle of ‚ÅÝWorld War Two, when Norwegian, British, French and Polish forces pushed back Nazi German troops, before withdrawing when Germany invaded France.
Norway has declared 2026 the year of "total defence", the latest move by a Nordic nation to boost civilian preparedness.
"It is very important to narrow the gap between military forces and civil society ‚ÅÝbecause in ‚Äãa really critical situation - war - there is a need for a lot of resources," ‚Äãsaid Elisabeth Aarsaether, outgoing director of the Norwegian Directorate for Civil Protection.
While Norwegian planners are confident most households are better prepared to cope with war, she said, they are concerned that ‚Äãlocal authorities are not ready and so are preparing new guidelines.
"We would really like to help them," she said.

item.title: First message from Iran's new supreme leader to be released soon
item.description: DUBAI, March 12 (Reuters) - First ‚Äåmessage from ‚ÄãIran's ‚Äãnew ‚ÅÝsupreme ‚Äãleader, Mojtaba ‚ÄãKhamenei, will ‚Äãbe ‚Äãreleased within ‚Äåminutes, ‚ÅÝIranian state ‚Äãmedia ‚Äãreported ‚ÅÝon ‚ÄãThursday.

item.title: Ghana to submit UN resolution on slavery reparations; eyes broad support
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - Ghana ‚Äãintends to propose a United Nations resolution recognising transatlantic slavery as ‚Äåthe "gravest crime in the history of humankind" and calling for reparations, and expects broad support despite resistance in Europe.
The West African nation, a prominent advocate of reparations , opens new tab on the continent, plans to ‚Äãtable the proposal at the U.N. General Assembly, possibly as early ‚Äãas this month, its Foreign Ministry said in a statement to ‚ÅÝReuters.
The plan marks a new step in Africa's efforts to seek accountability for ‚Äãhistorical injustices by former colonial powers, and the country hopes it will "enjoy the support ‚Äãof many member states".
"The proposed resolution seeks to recognise transatlantic slave trade as the gravest crime in the history of humankind, taking into account its scale, duration, legalisation and enduring consequences," ‚Äãit said.
While calls for reparations have gained momentum in recent years, there is also a growing backlash .
Several ‚ÄãEuropean leaders have opposed even discussing the subject, with critics arguing today's states and institutions ‚Äåshould not ‚ÅÝbe held responsible for historical wrongs.
Asked if it expected opposition, the ministry said: "A backlash against truth is one that we hope would not occur. Ghana is not seeking to reopen old wounds but to heal those wounds with truth."
African and Caribbean nations ‚Äãhave been seeking to ‚ÅÝestablish a special U.N. reparations tribunal, with lawyers noting previous tribunals had been created by resolution or by the Security ‚ÄãCouncil.
The African Union (AU) last year set out to create a "unified vision" ‚Äãamong its ‚ÅÝ55 member states on what reparations may look like, from financial compensation and formal apologies to policy reforms. AU leaders endorsed the proposed resolution at a summit last ‚ÅÝmonth.
Member ‚Äãstates of the Caribbean Community, which has outlined its ‚Äãown reparations plan , are also expected to support it.
"We remain optimistic that others would equally be able ‚Äãto do so," the ministry said.

item.title: Strait of Hormuz issue must be resolved through diplomacy, says German foreign minister
item.description:
BERLIN, March 12 (Reuters) - A solution to security issues in the strategically important Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz can only be achieved ‚Äådiplomatically, German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul said on Thursday.
"A reliable and sustainable solution can ‚Äãonly be achieved through diplomatic ‚Äãchannels, and that is why I ‚ÅÝbelieve ... that we should pool our ‚Äãcommon interests from the Gulf region, but ‚Äãalso here in the neighbourhood," he said during a visit to Turkey.
Iran's retaliation to U.S.-Israeli ‚Äãhas included strikes on ships ‚Äãin the Strait of Hormuz, bringing non-Iranian vessel ‚Äåtransits ‚ÅÝthrough the main gateway for much of Middle Eastern oil exports to a near standstill and forcing producers in ‚Äãthe region ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝcut output.
"Together, we must find a way out of this ‚Äãwar and, at the same ‚Äãtime, ‚ÅÝdevelop an initial idea of what a future security architecture for the region ‚ÅÝmight ‚Äãlook like," Wadephul said ‚Äãduring a news conference alongside his Turkish counterpart.

item.title: Taiwan rattled by 5.7 magnitude quake, no immediate reports of damage
item.description: TAIPEI, March 12 (Reuters) - A ‚Äã5.7 ‚Äåmagnitude earthquake struck ‚Äãjust ‚Äãoff the ‚ÅÝcoast ‚Äãof ‚ÄãTaiwan's eastern county of ‚ÄãHualien ‚Äãon Thursday, ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝisland's weather administration said, ‚Äãwith ‚Äãno ‚ÅÝimmediate ‚Äãreports ‚Äãof ‚ÅÝdamage.

item.title: Zefyros tanker hit by unknown projectile at Iraqi anchorage - ship manager
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - The Zefyros tanker was ‚Äãhit by an unknown ‚Äåprojectile during a ship-to-ship transfer with ‚Äãoil tanker Safesea ‚ÄãVishnu at Iraq's Umm ‚ÅÝQasar anchorage, the ‚Äãvessel's Greece based ‚Äãmanager Benetech Shipping SA said on Thursday.
The incident ‚Äãtook place at ‚Äã2341 local time on March ‚Äå11.
