"All ‚ÅÝ23 crew members are safe and accounted for. They ‚Äãhave ‚Äãbeen ‚ÅÝevacuated and were brought to ‚Äãshore," Benetech ‚ÄãShipping ‚ÅÝsaid in a statement, adding the vessel's ‚ÅÝcondition ‚Äãwas being ‚Äãassessed.

item.title: Oil unlikely to hit $200 a barrel, US energy chief says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright said ‚Äãon Thursday global oil prices are unlikely to hit $200 a ‚Äåbarrel even as crude tankers remained stalled in the Strait of Hormuz and the U.S. and Israeli war with Iran widened.
"I would say unlikely, but we ‚Äãare focused on the military operation and solving a problem," Wright ‚Äãtold CNN when asked if prices would reach $200 a ‚ÅÝbarrel - a level prices could hit if the war continues to escalate, an ‚ÄãIranian official said on Wednesday.
"Get ready for the oil barrel to be ‚Äãat $200 because the oil price depends on the regional security which you have destabilized," Ebrahim Zolfaqari, the spokesperson for Tehran's Khatam al-Anbiya military command headquarters, said on ‚ÄãWednesday.
Oil prices jumped 6% to nearly $100 on Thursday as two ‚Äãtankers blazed in an Iraqi port after a hit by suspected Iranian explosive-laden boats.
The ‚Äårise ‚ÅÝcame despite more than 30 countries in the International Energy Agency announcing a day earlier the biggest-ever coordinated drawdown of global oil reserves of 400 million barrels, about 40% of which will come from the U.S., the ‚Äãworld's largest oil ‚Äãproducer.
The war ‚ÅÝhas forced Middle East Gulf countries to cut total oil production by at least 10 million barrels ‚Äãper day, about 10% of world demand. The IEA ‚Äãsaid on ‚ÅÝThursday that is the biggest oil supply disruption in the history of the global market.
Wright also told CNBC on Thursday that the U.S. Navy cannot ‚ÅÝescort ships ‚Äãthrough the Strait of Hormuz now but ‚Äãit was "quite likely" that could happen by the end of the month.

item.title: EU sees no immediate oil supply concerns despite Iran disruption
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 12 (Reuters) - The European Union does not see any ‚Äãimmediate concerns regarding the ‚Äåsecurity of its oil supplies as a result of the ‚ÄãIran war, an ‚ÄãEU Commission spokesperson said on ‚ÅÝThursday.
The EU's top oil ‚Äãsuppliers are Norway and the ‚ÄãUnited States. The spokesperson added that EU countries would notify the ‚ÄãInternational Energy Agency (IEA) of ‚Äãtheir planned oil reserve releases by ‚Äå1700 ‚ÅÝGMT on Thursday.
The IEA had on Wednesday agreed to release a record 400 million ‚Äãbarrels of ‚Äãoil ‚ÅÝfrom strategic stockpiles to combat a spike ‚Äãin global crude ‚Äãprices ‚ÅÝsince the start of the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, ‚ÅÝwith ‚Äãthe U.S. contributing ‚Äãthe bulk of the supply.

item.title: Police say drone found at mine in western Poland
item.description:
WARSAW, March 12 (Reuters) - Police in ‚Äãwestern Poland were investigating on Thursday the origin of ‚Äåa drone discovered by a worker at a lignite mine and it did not appear to be a civilian model, a ‚Äãpolice spokesperson said.
NATO member Poland has been on ‚Äãhigh alert for airspace incursions since more than ‚ÅÝ20 Russian drones entered its airspace on the night ‚Äãof September 9-10, 2025, and some of them were found ‚Äãin the east of the country.
"It doesn't look like a civilian drone, but there's no information about the model," police spokesperson ‚ÄãMaciej Swiecichowski told Reuters by phone from the western city ‚Äãof Poznan.
State broadcaster TVP Info cited sources as saying it ‚Äåwas ‚ÅÝa Gerbera military drone . Gerbera drones are produced in Russia and are used as decoys.
Local police said on X earlier they had been alerted to an "Unmanned ‚ÄãAerial Vehicle ‚Äãof the ‚ÅÝdrone type" found by an employee in Galczyce, Konin County, on the premises ‚Äãof the lignite mine.
Lignite, also known as 'brown ‚Äãcoal', ‚ÅÝis a combustible sedimentary rock.
"At the scene, police officers from Konin and Poznan are securing the area," ‚ÅÝthey said, ‚Äãadding that nobody had been ‚Äãinjured in the incident.

item.title: ICC opens probe into alleged crimes against humanity by Belarus
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 12 (Reuters) - Prosecutors at the ‚ÄãInternational Criminal Court ‚Äåon Thursday said they had ‚Äãopened an ‚Äãinvestigation into acts ‚ÅÝof deportation ‚Äãallegedly committed by ‚ÄãBelarus.
Belarus is not a member of ‚Äãthe ICC, ‚Äãbut the case was ‚Äåbrought ‚ÅÝby Lithuania, which is.
Prosecutors determined that at ‚Äãleast ‚Äãpart ‚ÅÝof the alleged crimes ‚Äãby Belarusian ‚Äãauthorities ‚ÅÝwere committed on Lithuania's territory, ‚ÅÝgiving ‚Äãthem ‚Äãjurisdiction.

item.title: Nepal to ration cooking gas due to panic over shortage
item.description:
KATHMANDU, March 12 (Reuters) - Nepal will begin rationing cooking gas amid fears that the Middle ‚ÄãEast conflict could create a nationwide shortage, ‚Äåan official said on Thursday.
Chandika Prasad Bhatta, executive director of state-run Nepal Oil Corporation (NOC) said authorities would refill only ‚Äãhalf of consumers' empty cylinders starting from ‚ÄãFriday to make its liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) stocks ‚ÅÝlast longer.
The supply squeeze comes as the ‚ÄãU.S.-Israeli war on Iran and Tehran's retaliation across the ‚Äãregion effectively halts shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, raising energy and transport costs and reducing the output of Middle ‚ÄãEast oil and gas producers .
Bhatta said consumers were ‚Äãpanicking despite repeated assurances that Nepal had enough supplies ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝLPG.
"The rationing is expected to end the panic and rush for hoarding," Bhatta told Reuters.
Consumers carrying empty cylinders have formed long queues outside refilling ‚Äãplants throughout ‚Äãthe country, ‚ÅÝwhich is completely dependent on India for its supplies of fuel, ‚Äãincluding cooking gas.
Officials said Nepal requires about ‚Äã45,000 ‚ÅÝcylinders each containing 14.2 kg of cooking gas every month and there was no disruption to ‚ÅÝsupplies.
‚ÄúWe ‚Äãare getting regular supply of ‚Äãfuel including the cooking gas according to our demands," Bhatta ‚Äãsaid.

item.title: Israeli military expands orders for people in southern Lebanon to leave
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 12 (Reuters) - The Israeli military ‚Äãissued new evacuation ‚Äåorders for southern Lebanon on ‚ÄãThursday which ‚Äãnearly doubled the zone ‚ÅÝit said ‚Äãresidents should leave, ‚Äãsaying they should move north beyond the ‚ÄãZahrani river.
The ‚Äãnew zones, marked in ‚Äåred ‚ÅÝin a map published on X by ‚Äãa military ‚Äãspokesperson, ‚ÅÝmean that the Israeli ‚Äãmilitary has ‚Äãnow ‚ÅÝordered people out of 10% ‚ÅÝof ‚ÄãLebanese territory.

item.title: Iran's Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei says Strait of Hormuz should remain shut
item.description:
DUBAI, March 12 (Reuters) - Iranian Supreme Leader Mojtaba ‚ÄãKhamenei said on ‚ÄåThursday the Strait of Hormuz should ‚Äãremain closed as ‚Äãa tool of pressure.
In ‚ÅÝa message ‚Äãread out by ‚Äãa state TV broadcaster, the first the new ‚Äãsupreme leader ‚Äãhas issued since he was ‚Äåappointed ‚ÅÝto succeed his slain father, Mojtaba said all ‚ÄãU.S. bases ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝthe region should be ‚Äãclosed as they ‚Äãwould ‚ÅÝbe attacked.

item.title: Iran war is one 'TACO' too far
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 12 (Reuters) - The "Trump always chickens out" investment strategy - buying beaten-down stocks on the assumption that the U.S. president will ultimately back down from his more extreme policies - has, for the most part, been a profitable one. But the Iran war may change that.
The joint U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran on February ‚Äã28 has sparked war across the Middle East, triggering the biggest energy shock since the 1970s and record volatility in oil and gas.
So far, U.S. equities have been ‚Äåremarkably calm, likely, in part, on the assumption that Trump will pull back if necessary. Since February 28, European, Asian and emerging stocks have lost 4-7%. The S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab is down 1% and the Nasdaq is up 0.5%.
But this conflict may be a "TACO" too far.
Even if Trump were to declare an end to the war tomorrow, damage has been done that cannot be quickly reversed, least of all by him.
Shuttered oil production and refining capacity, bombed-out infrastructure, ‚Äãand the upending of global energy flows cannot be made right at the stroke of a pen ‚Äì or a Truth Social post. It will take months, possibly years, ‚Äãto get everything back to its pre-war state.
Similarly, the unprecedented surge in the cost of shipping out of the Middle East and insuring ‚ÅÝvessels in the region is unlikely to return to "normal" levels any time soon, even after hostilities officially end.
"While global financial markets have been able to shake off the volatility and uncertainty caused ‚Äãby Trump's mercurial policies, the economic and geopolitical ramifications of the Iran war are likely to prove far more consequential," says Eswar Prasad, professor of economics at Cornell University.
This is the first time the ‚ÄãStrait of Hormuz ‚Äì the vital waterway through which 20% of the world's daily energy use passes ‚Äì has effectively been closed. No previous conflict in the region, including the Iran-Iraq War of the 1980s, the first Gulf War in the early 1990s or the Iraq War of the 2000s, has brought oil tankers to a virtual standstill.
Even in the market's optimistic scenario, where the U.S. wraps up its attacks quickly, instability that is almost entirely ‚Äãbeyond Trump's control would likely remain. And who knows how long that could persist, or how bad it could get.
Trump's economic and geopolitical playbook during his second term has been ‚Äãto push the boundaries of accepted norms, rules and protocols to breaking point ‚Äì or sometimes beyond ‚Äì then wait and assess the market fallout. If investors barely blink, it's a green light to carry on, even ‚Äåaccelerate. If ‚ÅÝmarkets crater, Trump steps back from the brink.
Since Trump returned to the White House 14 months ago, he has unraveled America's 80-year alliance with Europe, imperiled the existence of NATO, threatened to annex Greenland and Canada, deposed Venezuela's president, and threatened to fire Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell.
To varying degrees, markets wobbled in response to each of these episodes, forcing Trump to tone down the rhetoric. That, in turn, gave rise to the so-called "TACO trade."
Despite the geopolitical tumult of the last year, U.S. equities have continued to appreciate, volatility has mostly remained subdued, and risk premia have stayed relatively ‚Äãwell-contained.
The biggest "TACO" of them all ‚Äì at least until now ‚Äì ‚Äãrevolved around the "Liberation Day" tariffs last April. After ‚ÅÝtrillions of dollars were wiped off U.S. stock markets in a couple of days, Trump backed down on the most punitive duties.
But today's conflict in the Middle East is different, and potentially riskier than anything seen in recent decades. If investors are looking for a historical precedent, ‚Äãthe oil shock of 1973-74 may be a useful guide in terms of its lasting economic impact.
Oil prices are unlikely to quadruple, as they ‚Äãdid then, especially now that ‚ÅÝthe U.S. is the world's biggest energy producer and exporter. But bear in mind, although the OPEC oil embargo lasted only six months, the inflation it spawned haunted the U.S. and other industrialized economies for the rest of the decade and into the 1980s.
A persistent surge in energy prices and disrupted global supply chains could easily light the stagflationary fuse. Investors may now start asking whether this ‚ÅÝcrisis is one ‚ÄãTrump can't relieve, even if he does "chicken out."
(The opinions expressed here are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for ‚ÄãReuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets ‚Äãand finance seven days a week.

item.title: Helium prices soar as Qatar LNG halt exposes fragile supply chain
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - Disruptions to Qatar's natural gas processing from the Iran war have driven helium prices sharply higher, exposing the fragility of a small but critical market that supports industries from semiconductors to medical imaging.
Helium spot prices have doubled since the Middle East crisis began, according to Phil Kornbluth, ‚Äãpresident of Kornbluth Helium Consulting, as buyers scramble to secure supply.
State energy giant QatarEnergy, the world's second-largest LNG exporter, announced a production halt at its 77 ‚Äåmillion tons per annum (mtpa) facility last week and declared force majeure on LNG shipments , amid the conflict.
Because helium is extracted as a byproduct of natural gas processing, any disruption to LNG output directly cuts helium supply.
Qatari Energy Minister Saad al-Kaabi told the Financial Times last week that it would take "weeks to months" for deliveries to return to normal even if the conflict ended immediately.
Qatar is a pivotal ‚Äãsupplier rather than a marginal one.
Data from the U.S. Geological Survey shows the country produced about 63 million cubic meters of helium in 2025, out of ‚Äãroughly 190 million cubic meters globally, accounting for close to one-third of world supply.
"If those conditions (supply disruption) persist, the market is ‚ÅÝeffectively missing about 5.2 million cubic meters of helium per month," said Aleksandr Romanenko, CEO of market research firm IndexBox.
The disruption is reverberating through a market with little ‚Äãspare production capacity and limited storage, leaving buyers with few short-term alternatives.
Japan's top helium supplier Iwatani (8088.T) , opens new tab said it had so far maintained stable supply to customers including semiconductor ‚Äãmanufacturers, partly because it also sources helium from the United States and maintains stockpiles in both Japan and the U.S.
Helium markets operate very differently from most commodities.
Most supply is sold through long-term contracts rather than a transparent spot market, meaning price signals often emerge slowly even as supply tightens.
That opacity makes price discovery difficult, but signs of tightening supply have ‚Äãalready begun to emerge.
"Early indications show about 50% spot price increases already," said Anish Kapadia, CEO of market research firm AKAP Energy.
"In a sustained disruption, prices could ‚Äãrise sharply and potentially retest past shortage peaks of more than $2,000 per thousand cubic feet."
Romanenko said a 30-day disruption could lift delivered helium prices by 10% to 20%, while a 60-to-90-day outage ‚Äåcould push ‚ÅÝprices higher by 25% to 50%, particularly for buyers without long-term supply contracts.
Helium's physical properties add another constraint. The gas is typically shipped in liquid form and gradually evaporates during transport.
"It's a commodity, but it also has a shelf life," said Chris Bakker, CEO of helium developer Avanti (AVN.V) , opens new tab .
"So when you liquefy it, and that's how they tend to ship it worldwide, you've got notionally 45 days to get it to the end-user."
If supply tightens further, suppliers typically prioritise key sectors when allocating volumes ‚Äãduring force majeure events.
Kornbluth said industries such ‚Äãas medical MRI systems and rocket ships would ‚ÅÝprobably get 100% of their needs, while semiconductor manufacturers might receive 95%.
Lower-priority uses, including welding, diving equipment and party balloons, would likely face deeper cuts.
Last week, South Korea's ruling party lawmaker Kim Young-bae warned the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran could disrupt supplies of ‚Äãkey semiconductor manufacturing materials, giving helium as one example.
The hierarchy reflects helium's role in technologies where substitutes are limited.
Industrial gas ‚Äãcompanies sourcing helium from Qatar, ‚ÅÝincluding Air Liquide, Linde (LIN.DE) , opens new tab and Air Products and Chemicals (APD.N) , opens new tab , are among those most exposed to the supply shock, said Kapadia.
Air Products said it was taking steps to ensure continuity of supply but did not give further details.
Linde declined to comment, while Air Liquide said it relied on multiple sources in different continents and on its storage cavern in Europe.
Iwatani Japan is ‚ÅÝalso exposed, ‚ÄãKornbluth and Bakker said.
Producers outside the region could benefit if disruptions persist.
Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab is the largest helium ‚Äãproducer outside Qatar, while Canada-based North American Helium and smaller developers such as Helix Exploration (HEXH.L) , opens new tab and Blue Star Helium (BNL.AX) , opens new tab could see stronger demand, Kapadia added.

item.title: Trump: US benefits from high oil prices, but priority is stopping Iran
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚ÄãPresident Donald ‚ÄåTrump said ‚Äãon ‚ÄãThursday that rising ‚ÅÝoil ‚Äãprices ‚Äãmean greater profits for ‚Äãthe ‚ÄãUnited States, but ‚Äåhis ‚ÅÝpriority is stopping ‚ÄãIran ‚Äãfrom ‚ÅÝhaving nuclear ‚Äãweapons.

item.title: EU looks to soften energy bill pressures for industry
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 7 (Reuters) - The European Union is examining energy ‚Äåtaxes, network charges and carbon costs as possible areas for short-term measures to ease pressure on industries hit by high energy prices, a document seen by Reuters showed.
Brussels is looking ‚Äãfor quick fixes after companies warned they cannot compete with rivals in ‚ÄãChina and the U.S. - even before this week's surge in oil ‚ÅÝand gas prices sparked by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
European Commission President ‚ÄãUrsula von der Leyen has pledged to present options for EU leaders to consider at ‚Äãa summit on 19 March.
A Commission paper prepared for a meeting of EU Commissioners on Friday showed the bloc is exploring short-term measures to help the hardest-hit regions and sectors, without ‚Äãundermining longer-term climate laws meant to shift Europe to a cheaper, low-carbon energy ‚Äãsystem.
"Any proposal for legislative change will not deliver immediately and a bridge solution may be ‚Äåneeded ‚ÅÝto reduce energy prices in the next 2-5 years until the clean transition eases pressure on power prices as already seen in some regions," said the document, seen by Reuters.
The paper said the Commission would look at network charges - which make ‚Äãup about 18% ‚Äãof industrial power ‚ÅÝbills - and national taxes and levies, as well as carbon costs, which account for around 11% of bills.
It noted that ‚Äãgovernments are underusing existing tools to cut companies' energy bills, ‚Äãincluding state ‚ÅÝaid to offset carbon costs and contracts for difference that guarantee industrial consumers a stable power price.
The document said that if energy supplies are disrupted further, Brussels must ‚ÅÝbe ‚Äãready to introduce measures to encourage consumers to ‚Äãuse less energy, as it did in 2022 when Russia slashed gas deliveries.
A Commission spokesperson did not ‚Äãimmediately respond to a request for comment.

item.title: EU to soften gas authorisation rules to help secure LNG supplies, diplomats say
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 12 (Reuters) - The European Commission will instruct governments to be flexible in enforcing EU rules on gas imports, diplomats told Reuters ‚Äãon Thursday, amid fears they could hold up liquefied natural gas ‚Äådeliveries needed to stabilise supplies during the Iran crisis .
The Commission plans to issue guidance before March 18 on how to apply rules within the European Union's law to ‚Äãphase out Russian gas, the diplomats said.
The guidance marks an attempt ‚Äãby Brussels to prevent its Russia phaseout rules from inadvertently ‚ÅÝchoking Europe's gas supply at a moment of vulnerability. It would not ‚Äãaffect the EU's phase-out of Russian gas.
With the Iran conflict rattling global LNG ‚Äãflows and raising the risk of cargo diversions, EU officials fear rigid enforcement could delay or strand shipments, undermining the bloc's ability to keep storage adequately stocked and ‚Äãsafeguard energy security heading into next winter.
The rules in question require ‚Äãthat shipments of LNG from certain non-Russian countries receive "prior authorisation" - meaning companies must provide European ‚Äåcustoms ‚ÅÝauthorities with proof of the gas's origin five days before it arrives in the EU.
The main country affected by a softening of the "prior authorisation" rules would be Azerbaijan, which last year supplied 4% of EU gas ‚Äãimports.
The EU has ‚Äãalready said its ‚ÅÝmain gas suppliers, including Norway and the U.S., will not face the prior authorisation rules since the EU deems ‚Äãthe risk of Russian gas entering their exports to ‚Äãbe low.
Europe's ‚ÅÝgas industry had already asked Brussels to temporarily suspend the rules on prior authorisation.
"We simply cannot afford that a single flexible LNG cargo is delayed ‚ÅÝin port, ‚Äãstranded at sea, or rerouted to Asia ‚Äãbecause prior authorisation is unclear or pending," industry group Eurogas said in a statement on ‚ÄãThursday.

item.title: Russia says eight medics killed in Ukrainian drone attack in Donetsk region
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - The Russian Defence Ministry said on ‚ÄãThursday that eight medics ‚Äåwere killed and 10 people were wounded in a ‚ÄãUkrainian drone attack ‚Äãthis week on a medical ‚ÅÝfacility in Ukraine's ‚ÄãDonetsk region.
There was no immediate ‚Äãcomment from Ukraine.
Reuters could not independently verify the incident. The Russian ‚Äãministry said it ‚Äãtook place on Tuesday in Donetsk ‚Äåregion, ‚ÅÝwhich is mostly controlled by Russian forces, but it did not state ‚Äãthe ‚Äãprecise ‚ÅÝlocation.
"This facility has never been used for ‚Äãmilitary purposes, and ‚Äãits ‚ÅÝdeliberate destruction by the Kyiv regime constitutes a ‚ÅÝgross ‚Äãviolation of international ‚Äãhumanitarian law and human morality," ‚Äãthe ministry said.

item.title: Morocco plans repatriation of IS-linked citizens from Iraq, official says
item.description:
RABAT, March 12 (Reuters) - Morocco is developing plans to repatriate its ‚Äånationals who fought for Islamic State in Syria and were transferred by the United States into Iraqi detention, a senior security official said on Thursday.
The U.S. began moving detained IS members out of Syria in ‚ÄãJanuary after the collapse of the Kurdish-led Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF), which had been guarding around ‚Äãa dozen facilities holding IS fighters and affiliated civilians, including foreigners.
Last month, ‚ÅÝthe U.S. Central Command said the U.S. military had completed its mission in Syria after transferring ‚Äã5,700 adult male Islamic State detainees to Iraq.
Iraq has since then urged Muslim and Western ‚Äãnations to repatriate their nationals.
Morocco "is currently developing an action plan on this matter ... taking into consideration the diversity of the population targeted by the procedure, namely the fighters, as well as the women who lived ‚Äãin the SDF camps in Syria, in addition to their children," a security official close ‚Äãto the matter told Reuters.
There were 1,667 Moroccan foreign fighters in Syria and Iraq, of whom 244 ‚Äåwere ‚ÅÝdetained in prisons controlled by the SDF in northeastern Syria, while 279 former fighters have returned to the Kingdom, the official said.
The official added that 269 Moroccan women remain in the conflict zone along with 627 minors, while 134 women and 354 children are being held ‚Äãin SDF-run camps in ‚Äãnortheastern Syria. So far, ‚ÅÝ125 women have returned to Morocco since the beginning of the Syrian crisis.
In March 2019, Morocco repatriated eight fighters detained by the ‚ÄãSDF, who stood trial in Morocco and now are serving sentences ranging ‚Äãfrom 13 ‚ÅÝto 18 years on terrorism charges.
Joining jihadist groups abroad is punishable by up to 10 years in jail under Moroccan law. In recent years, IS branches in Africa have recruited more than 130 ‚ÅÝMoroccan ‚Äãfighters, the head of Morocco's counter-terrorism agency, the Central ‚ÄãBureau of Judicial Investigations (BCIJ), Haboub Cherkaoui said last year.
Since its establishment in 2015, the BCIJ has dismantled dozens of militant ‚Äãcells and arrested more than 1,000 suspected jihadists.

item.title: UK exploring additional Gulf deployments after minehunter withdrawal
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - Britain is exploring additional options for deployments to the Gulf after Iran stepped up attacks on ‚Äãvessels, defence minister John Healey said on Thursday, ‚Äåadding that the UK already has autonomous mine hunting systems in the region.
The Royal Navy (RN) said last week its last minehunter ‚ÄãHMS Middleton, which was based in Bahrain, had ‚Äãarrived back in Britain on March 1. The vessel, ‚ÅÝwhich was more than 40 years old, was ‚Äãno longer certified to sail, it said.
"We do have some ‚Äãautonomous mine hunting systems already in the region," Healey said. "There are additional options we're starting to consider that we could deploy alongside ‚Äãallies."
Iran has deployed about a dozen mines , opens new tab in the Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz, two sources familiar with the matter told Reuters on ‚ÄåWednesday, ‚ÅÝin a move likely to complicate any reopening of the narrow waterway, an important route for shipping oil and LNG.
The U.S. military says it has targeted Iranian mine-laying ‚Äãvessels, eliminating 16 ‚Äãof them ‚ÅÝon Tuesday.
Two tankers were ablaze in an Iraqi port on Thursday after a hit by ‚Äãsuspected Iranian explosive-laden boats. Hours earlier, three ‚Äãother ships ‚ÅÝhad been struck in the Gulf. Healey described the situation as a "major escalation from Iran".
"It is a breach of ‚ÅÝinternational ‚Äãlaw," Healey said. "It's a very serious ‚Äãsituation that is having an impact on oil prices and the cost ‚Äãof living for everyone."

item.title: Caribbean islands seek $200 million for regional biodiversity push
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - A group of Caribbean countries plan to raise $200 ‚Äãmillion for a joint bid to boost biodiversity, in a push for more influence ‚Äåon internationally financed environment projects to maximise their impact, Grenada's climate ambassador has told Reuters.
The 30X30 initiative by the Organisation of Eastern Caribbean States (OECS) and its 12 members is part of a broader global drive towards targets, agreed in ‚Äã2022, that include protecting 30% of the world's land and sea by 2030.
The hope is ‚Äãthat such cross-border initiatives, with government oversight from the start, will be more ‚ÅÝeffective as they harness the power of multilateral, bilateral, philanthropic and private sector funding along with ‚Äãfinancial tools such as debt-for-nature swaps, Safiya Sawney said.
While around $650 ‚Äãmillion was raised to fund conservation in member states over the 20 years to 2024, governments often had to consider the priorities of multilateral donors before their own. For lack of coordination, some donors duplicated efforts.
"From the outside looking ‚Äãin, you say, 'Well, that's a lot of money. Why do you need more?'. The issue ... is ‚Äãthat we're not seeing that in the impact," she added, in an interview last week.
A report , opens new tab from the Back ‚Äåto Blue ‚ÅÝinitiative, which focuses on ocean sustainability policy, suggested regional approaches such as "OECS 30X30" could be transformative in closing a huge funding gap.
Small Island Developing States (SIDS) worldwide - which will bear the brunt of rising sea levels - need around $12 billion , opens new tab a year to adapt to the effects of climate change, and get just $2 ‚Äãbillion, according to the ‚ÄãGlobal Center on Adaptation.
"There's ‚ÅÝa huge disconnect between what the governments have invested time and effort into planning for themselves, based on their own indigenous circumstances, and what the ‚Äãdonor is funding," Sawney said.
"We're challenging them to say 'No, you have to ‚Äãdo a better ‚ÅÝjob if you really want to ensure that your money stretches. You have to trust us, trust that we know what would we need to do for ourselves'."
Sawney said that, as development aid budgets are ‚ÅÝcut in ‚Äãricher countries, it was important to scale projects regionally and ‚Äãattract more private sector investors.
"For us, the programme is really, really important because it's really on us to be able to ‚Äãshow investors, particularly non-traditional investors, that we're a good bet."

item.title: Ukraine opens battlefield data access to allies' AI models
item.description:
KYIV, March 12 (Reuters) - Ukraine is opening up access to its battlefield data for its allies to train drone AI software, the defence minister said ‚Äãon Thursday, as Kyiv seeks to harness the experience it has garnered ‚Äåfending off Russia's four-year, full-scale invasion.
The move comes as militaries across the globe start to use automated systems which can guide drones to their targets without a pilot, or ‚Äãquickly analyse vast pools of data.
Foreign allies and companies have sought ‚Äãaccess to Ukraine's datasets, as these are crucial for training models to ‚ÅÝrecognise patterns, shapes, and the behaviour of people and machines on the ‚Äãbattlefield.
Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov said a platform had been created to safely train ‚ÄãAI models without giving away sensitive data, but which nevertheless provides constantly updating datasets and large quantities of photos and video footage.
"Today, Ukraine has a unique array of battlefield data ‚Äãthat is unmatched anywhere else in the world," he wrote on Telegram.
"This ‚Äãincludes millions of annotated images collected during tens of thousands of combat flights."
Fedorov, a tech-savvy ‚Äåally ‚ÅÝof President Volodymyr Zelenskiy, said Ukraine would benefit from speeding up the development of AI models which it can then use in its war against Russia.
"We are ready to work with partners on joint analytics, model training, and the creation ‚Äãof new technological solutions," ‚Äãhe said, adding ‚ÅÝthat Ukraine wants to increase the role played by autonomous systems in the war.
When he was appointed in January, ‚ÄãFedorov laid out his plans to conduct a broader data-driven overhaul ‚Äãof ‚ÅÝUkraine's vast defence ministry.
Ukraine is keen to maximise its advantage from the experience gained from Europe's largest conflict since 1945, as it strives to retain its allies' ‚ÅÝinterest and ‚Äãfunding in the fifth year of full-scale ‚Äãwar.
It has sent anti-drone specialists to four Middle Eastern nations this week after they requested Kyiv's help in ‚Äãdowning Iran's vast barrages of Shahed UAVs.

item.title: European rights body says abortion refusal complaints from Poland have ceased
item.description:
WARSAW, March 12 (Reuters) - The number of legal abortions in Poland doubled in 2024, while complaints to the European Court of Human Rights over conscience clause refusals in ‚Äãthe country have ceased, the Council of Europe said on Thursday.
Poland, a ‚Äåpredominantly Catholic country, introduced a near-total abortion ban in 2021 under the previous nationalist government after pregnancy termination due to foetal abnormalities was ruled unconstitutional.
Prime Minister Donald Tusk's pro-European coalition won the ‚Äã2023 election partly on the promise it would ease the restrictions, but it ‚Äãhas so far failed to agree on a bill. Nationalist President ‚ÅÝKarol Nawrocki has signalled he will block any attempt at liberalisation.
However, Tusk's government ‚Äãhas taken steps to enforce the existing law. Ministers issued recommendations to hospitals and ‚Äãprosecutors clarifying that abortion on the grounds of a woman's mental health is legal, and that all hospitals are obliged to provide the procedure.
The Council of Europe's Committee of Ministers, which oversees ‚Äãthe implementation of European Court of Human Rights rulings, said on Thursday the ‚Äãgovernment's actions "appear to bring results in practice."
"The number of lawful abortions has doubled year to year ‚Äåand ‚ÅÝno complaints have recently been received about the refusal of abortion based on the conscience clause", the committee said in its decision on Poland's compliance with judgments concerning access to legal abortion.
It also noted "that since the introduction of the unconditional obligation of ‚Äãhospitals to provide abortion ‚Äãas a contracted ‚ÅÝmedical service, no complaint has been reported about a refusal of lawful abortion based on the conscience clause."
The committee, however, expressed ‚Äãregret over the lack of progress in adopting legislation on ‚Äãthe safe ‚ÅÝtermination of pregnancy.
Health ministry data shows that nearly 900 legal abortions were performed in Polish hospitals in 2024, compared with 425 a year earlier. There were 411 legal terminations ‚ÅÝin ‚Äãthe first half of 2025, with full-year data ‚Äãnot yet available.
Before the 2021 ruling that tightened the law, more than 1,000 abortions were performed annually in ‚ÄãPoland, nearly all attributed to foetal abnormalities.

item.title: Eight countries warn EU not to weaken carbon market, document shows
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 12 (Reuters) - Spain, the Netherlands and six other governments have urged the European Union not to dismantle or suspend the bloc's emissions ‚Äãtrading system, its main climate change policy, as Brussels hunts for ‚Äåways to curb energy prices.
With energy prices surging on disruption to Middle Eastern oil and gas supplies, Brussels is facing calls from governments including Italy to suspend its ETS, which ‚Äãrequires power plants to buy permits to cover their CO2 emissions.
"Making ‚Äãfundamental changes to the ETS, calling into question the ETS ‚ÅÝinstrument itself, or suspending it, would constitute a very worrying step backwards," ‚Äãa group of eight EU countries said in a joint paper, seen by ‚ÄãReuters.
Weakening the scheme would "dramatically penalise early movers who have already invested and innovated in decarbonisation", said the paper, which was also signed by Denmark, Finland, Luxembourg, Portugal, Slovenia and Sweden.
By putting ‚Äãa price on pollution, the ETS aims to provide a financial incentive ‚Äãto companies to invest in lower-carbon production.
The Commission has so far held off calls from ‚Äåother ‚ÅÝgovernments, including Slovakia and the Czech Republic , to weaken the ETS.
But officials are hunting for quick fixes to avoid the Iran conflict triggering a repeat of 2022, when Europe faced record-high energy prices after Russia invaded Ukraine and slashed ‚Äãgas deliveries to ‚ÄãEurope.
The European Commission ‚ÅÝhas said it will provide options for EU leaders to consider at a summit on 19 March. European Commission ‚ÄãPresident Ursula von der Leyen said on Wednesday Brussels was ‚Äãexploring ‚ÅÝa gas price cap , but did not mention changes to the ETS.
The ETS cost comprises around 11%, on average, of electricity bills in Europe, according to EU data. ‚ÅÝIt ‚Äãresults in higher costs for countries like Poland, ‚Äãwhere the ETS's contribution to power bills is 24% due to the dominant role of coal ‚Äãpower plants in the power mix.

item.title: Foreign investors threaten legal action against Vietnam over renewables, document says
item.description:
HANOI, March 12 (Reuters) - Foreign investors may take legal action if Vietnam does not fully pay electricity tariffs agreed with multiple wind and solar projects, according to ‚Äãa document signed by five chambers of commerce seen by Reuters.
The dispute ‚Äåbegan last year when Vietnam cut previously agreed subsidised prices for electricity from some solar and wind farms, citing irregularities. The move comes as Vietnam's energy sector struggles with higher prices and risks of shortages ‚Äãlinked to the conflict in Iran.
Lengthy negotiations with affected investors, who run ‚Äãprojects with a combined capacity of 12 gigawatts, failed to produce a ‚ÅÝcompromise, prompting the chambers of commerce in Vietnam from the European Union, Britain, Japan, ‚ÄãSouth Korea and Thailand to send a joint letter to the government on Thursday.
If ‚Äãpayment obligations are not met, electricity producers "may seek to enforce remedies, including pursuing dispute resolution in Vietnam or other jurisdictions," said the document, dated March 12.
The letter urges authorities to find an amicable ‚Äãsolution to a dispute that the chambers said could lead to defaults and significant ‚Äãlosses on multibillion-dollar investments in Vietnam's renewable energy sector.
Vietnam's industry ministry, which received the letter, did ‚Äånot ‚ÅÝimmediately respond to a request for comment.
In recent years, the Southeast Asian country saw a boom in renewable energy investments driven by generous subsidised, or feed-in, tariffs under which the state committed to buying electricity for 20 years at above-market prices.
However, the high ‚Äãtariffs increased losses for ‚Äãstate-owned power utility ‚ÅÝEVN, the sole buyer of the generated electricity, and pushed up power prices for households and factories.
The retroactive change to tariffs, which ‚Äãtook effect in January 2025, followed investigations into alleged abuses ‚Äãin accessing ‚ÅÝthe preferential rates.
EVN had no immediate comment.
The letter follows several coordinated actions aimed at persuading Vietnam to change course. These included a document in May signed by 16 affected firms, ‚ÅÝamong them ‚Äãprivate equity fund Dragon Capital, the Vietnamese subsidiary ‚Äãof Philippines' ACEN ACEN.PS energy group, and investors from Thailand, Portugal, the Netherlands, South Korea, Singapore and China.

item.title: US sues California over zero emission vehicle, greenhouse gas rules
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - The Trump administration on Thursday sued the state ‚Äãof California, claiming the state's zero-emission vehicle and tailpipe greenhouse gas emissions ‚Äårules are illegal and preempted by federal law.
The U.S. Transportation Department sued the California Air Resources Board in U.S. District Court in California over vehicle rules that remain in place after President ‚ÄãDonald Trump signed legislation last year to overturn California‚Äôs Advanced Clean Cars ‚ÄãII rules that aim to phase out new gasoline-powered cars by 2035.
The ‚ÅÝlawsuit wants a ruling declaring that all zero-emission vehicle mandates by California are ‚Äãunlawful and unenforceable.
"This litigation will help automakers design and produce cars and trucks to ‚Äãmeet one federal fuel economy regulation," said Jonathan Morrison, who heads the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration.
California won approval in 2022 from the Environmental Protection Agency for its current vehicle ‚Äãrules known as Advanced Clean Cars I, which the state says are still ‚Äãin effect. The California Air Resources Board did not immediately comment.
This is the latest in a ‚Äåseries ‚ÅÝof efforts by the Trump administration to bar California from setting vehicle rules. In August, it sued California to stop the state from enforcing stringent emissions standards for heavy-duty trucks.
California's rules require automakers to sell a rising number of electric ‚Äãvehicles and meet ‚Äãincreasingly stringent limits ‚ÅÝon tailpipe emissions.
Those rules are much stricter than those imposed by the Trump administration, which plans to roll back federal fuel economy rules.
California contends ‚Äãthe fuel savings for consumers from the rules far exceed ‚Äãthe higher ‚ÅÝupfront costs of EVs.
Congress rescinded authority for California to outlaw traditional gas-powered vehicles after 2035 after Toyota and the Detroit Three lobbied Congress and the White House for significant relief ‚ÅÝfrom ‚ÄãCalifornia's emissions regulations.
The White House also significantly weakened ‚Äãfederal tailpipe rules and Congress passed legislation to stop collecting penalties for not meeting vehicle tailpipe standards.
A group ‚Äãrepresenting major automakers did not immediately comment.

item.title: Hungary returns seized Ukrainian bank vehicles, withholds cash and gold
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 12 (Reuters) - Hungary returned two seized armoured bank vehicles to Ukraine on Thursday but withheld cash and gold worth approximately $82 million, ‚Äãciting an investigation into alleged money laundering, a move that ‚ÄåKyiv has denounced as theft.
Relations between Hungary and Ukraine, already strained by Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban's close ties to Russia, reached a new low ‚Äãlast week when Hungary detained seven Ukrainians transporting cash and gold .
Kyiv ‚Äãaccused Budapest of taking the bank employees, engaged in ‚ÅÝa legitimate transfer, hostage in order to pressure Ukraine into restarting ‚Äãsuspended oil shipments.
Those detained were expelled by Hungary and crossed into ‚ÄãUkraine on Friday.
Ukraine has demanded the return of the seized assets, with Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha on Sunday describing Hungary's actions as an "unprecedented act of state ‚Äãbanditism and racketeering".
The detainees' lawyer, Lorant Horvath, told reporters on Thursday ‚Äãthat the Ukrainian bank believed the money transport was "entirely lawful".
"Oschadbank will use all ‚Äålegal ‚ÅÝmeans at its disposal to recover its assets," he said.
Hungary's parliament passed a law proposed by Orban's Fidesz party on Tuesday, allowing the tax authority to retain the cash and gold for 60 days while ‚Äãit investigates the origin ‚Äãand destination ‚ÅÝof the assets and evaluates any implications for Hungary's national security.
The tensions come as Orban faces a serious ‚Äãelectoral challenge to his 16-year rule on April ‚Äã12. He ‚ÅÝhas made the war in Ukraine a central plank of his campaign , saying the centre-right opposition would drag Hungary into the conflict, which the ‚ÅÝopposition ‚Äãhas repeatedly denied.
Orban has also vetoed new ‚ÄãEU sanctions on Moscow as well as a huge loan for Ukraine over the ‚Äãoil dispute.

item.title: Gunman at Virginia university¬Ýshot dead after injuring two
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - A ‚Äãgunman opened fire on ‚ÄåThursday morning at Old Dominion University in Norfolk, Virginia, injuring two ‚Äãpeople before being shot dead, ‚Äãaccording to the university.
In an ‚ÅÝemergency alert, the university ‚Äãsaid the two injured individuals were ‚Äãtransported to hospital and classes and campus operations were cancelled for the ‚Äãrest of the day.
The ‚Äãuniversity's alert said that the gunman opened ‚Äåfire ‚ÅÝshortly before 10:49 a.m. in Constant Hall, the center of the university's college of business.
By ‚Äãmidday ‚Äãon Thursday, ‚ÅÝpolice vehicles with flashing lights had blocked ‚Äãoff streets around the ‚ÄãNorfolk ‚ÅÝcampus. The university serves some 24,000 students, according to the ‚ÅÝschool's ‚Äãwebsite.

item.title: FBI alert on possible Iran retaliation based on unverified tip, White House says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - An FBI alert to law enforcement agencies last month warning of the possibility that Tehran ‚Äãmight try to retaliate for any U.S. strikes ‚Äåon Iran by launching drone attacks in California was based on a single unverified tip, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said ‚Äãon Thursday, adding that there has never been such ‚Äãa threat to the U.S. from Iran.
The confidential alert , ‚ÅÝissued by the FBI through the multi-agency Los Angeles Joint ‚ÄãRegional Intelligence Center, surfaced publicly on Wednesday as the war ‚Äãthat began on February 28 with massive U.S. and Israeli bombardments of Iran stretched on.
The alert cited FBI information that, as of early ‚ÄãFebruary, Iran "allegedly aspired to conduct a surprise attack using ‚Äãunmanned aerial vehicles" launched from a sea vessel against targets in California "in ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝevent that the U.S. conducted strikes against Iran."
Leavitt in a post on X on Thursday said that the alert was based on one email sent to local law enforcement ‚Äãin California containing ‚Äãa single, ‚ÅÝunverified tip.
"TO BE CLEAR: No such threat from Iran to our homeland exists, and it ‚Äãnever did," Leavitt wrote.
ABC News first broke ‚Äãnews of ‚ÅÝthe FBI security bulletin.
U.S. President Donald Trump has shrugged off the notion of Iran-backed attacks on the U.S. homeland.
Asked on ‚ÅÝWednesday ‚Äãif he was worried that Iran ‚Äãmay ramp up its retaliation to include strikes on U.S. soil, Trump told ‚Äãreporters, "No, I'm not."

item.title: Israel drops charges against soldiers accused of abusing Gaza detainee
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 12 (Reuters) - Israel's military on Thursday dropped charges against five soldiers accused of torturing ‚Äãa Palestinian detained during the Gaza war , in a case that exposed divisions within Israel ‚Äåover whether soldiers can abuse enemy prisoners with impunity.
The military announced the decision at a time when much of the country's attention is focused on the war with Iran.
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu praised the decision. "The State of Israel must hunt ‚Äãdown its enemies, not its own heroic fighters," he said in a statement.
The case gained ‚Äãinternational attention after right-wing protesters, including members of Netanyahu's cabinet, stormed military compounds in ‚ÅÝprotest of the probe against the soldiers.
It drew further attention when the military's chief legal officer leaked ‚Äãvideo of the alleged abuse to local media.
The advocate general, Yifat Tomer-Yerushalmi, resigned in October and was later ‚Äãarrested over the leak, an action she said she took to fend off propaganda against the military's legal department entrusted with upholding the rule of law.
Her successor, Major General Itai Ofir, was withdrawing indictments against the soldiers in part due to "exceptional ‚Äãcircumstances that negatively affected the ability to prosecute the case while also preserving the right for a ‚Äãfair trial of the defendants in the case," the military said in a statement.
Those circumstances include the fact that the ‚ÄåPalestinian ‚ÅÝsubjected to alleged abuse "is currently present in the Gaza Strip and the implications this has on the evidentiary basis," the statement said.
The leaked security camera footage from the Sde Teiman military detention camp for Palestinians arrested during the Gaza war shows soldiers taking a prisoner aside and crowding around while holding a dog and blocking ‚Äãvisibility of their actions with ‚Äãtheir riot gear.
The soldiers ‚ÅÝfaced charges relating to causing severe abuse and injury. The indictment against them said that one of the soldiers stabbed the detainee with a sharp object, ‚Äãcausing a tear near his rectum.
Neither the Palestinian detainee, who was released back ‚Äãto Gaza as ‚ÅÝpart of the Israel-Hamas ceasefire deal in October, nor the Israeli soldiers involved have been identified by name.
There was no immediate comment from Palestinian leaders on the decision.
The Association for Civil Rights in Israel (ACRI) previously filed a ‚ÅÝpetition ‚Äãfor the closure of Sde Teiman over alleged abuse of Palestinian ‚Äãdetainees.
The Israeli military began phasing down use of the facility in June 2024, although accusations from rights groups and former Palestinian detainees of torture ‚Äãand abuse in Israeli detention facilities remain widespread.

item.title: US has arranged nearly 50 charter flights from Middle East amid Iran strikes, State Dept says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - The Trump administration has organized nearly 50 flights to return U.S. citizens from the Middle East since the U.S.-Israeli war ‚Äãwith Iran began, a State Department official said on Thursday, and ‚Äåofficials said demand for the flights has declined.
The State Department was criticized in the days after the conflict began on February 28 for its late preparations to relocate diplomats and ‚Äãoffer assistance to U.S. citizens as Iran's strikes led to ‚Äãairspace closures and flight cancellations across the region.
"By the end of ‚ÅÝtoday, we'll have completed approximately four dozen flights and safely evacuated ‚Äãthousands of Americans from the Middle East on those flights," said the official, ‚Äãwho briefed reporters on condition of anonymity.
The department, which waived the cost of government-assisted flights, has declined to give detailed numbers for how many people benefited from them. ‚ÄãThe official said that, as of Thursday, a total of 47,000 U.S. ‚Äãcitizens had returned from the region to the United States since February 28, mostly ‚Äåon ‚ÅÝcommercial flights, and that the State Department had provided guidance or assistance to thousands of Americans.
"Through the department's Task Force, we have directly provided security guidance and travel assistance to about 32,000 impacted Americans," the official said. "Most ‚ÄãAmericans who have ‚Äãrequested assistance have ‚ÅÝdeclined seats when offered, opting to remain in country or take commercial flight options, which offer greater flexibility in ‚Äãterms of destination and luggage," the official said.
The department ‚Äãsaid on ‚ÅÝWednesday it would scale down charter flights and ground transport options because commercial flight availability is improving.
U.S. facilities in the region have continued to be ‚ÅÝhit ‚Äãby Iran. A drone struck a major diplomatic facility ‚Äãin Iraq on Tuesday, amid U.S. warnings that Iran-backed militias in the country may target ‚ÄãAmericans and U.S. interests.

item.title: Eswatini receives four more third-country deportees from US, government says
item.description:
JOHANNESBURG, March 12 (Reuters) - Eswatini said it had received four more third-country deportees from the United States ‚Äãon Thursday, bringing the total to at least ‚Äå19 as the Trump administration continued its crackdown on immigration.
Two of the fresh deportees were from Somalia, one was from ‚ÄãSudan and one was from Tanzania. Previous deportees, ‚Äãarriving in July last year, included nationals of ‚ÅÝVietnam, Cuba, Laos and Yemen.
A lawyer for some ‚Äãof that earlier group, Alma David, told Reuters a ‚ÄãCambodian man, Pheap Rom, was due to be repatriated, the second to be released after another man was sent back to Jamaica ‚Äãlast year.
U.S. President Donald Trump's administration paid the ‚Äãsmall southern African country $5.1 million to receive them.
"In line with this ‚Äåagreement, ... ‚ÅÝthe nation has received another cohort of four third-country nationals from the United States," said the statement by the government of Eswatini, an absolute monarchy ruled by ‚ÄãKing Mswati ‚ÄãIII.
Eswatini is ‚ÅÝone of several African countries to have made such secretive deals. The high court ‚Äãlast month threw out a case filed by ‚Äãlocal ‚ÅÝhuman rights lawyers that challenged it, though they have appealed.
Despite having served their sentences for crimes on U.S. ‚ÅÝsoil, the ‚Äãremainder are still in prison ‚Äãin Eswatini.
"Intensive engagements with the respective countries of origin ... are ongoing," the ‚Äãstatement said.

item.title: Romania signs deal with Ukraine on joint defence production including drones
item.description:
BUCHAREST, March 12 (Reuters) - The presidents of ‚ÄãRomania and Ukraine signed a statement of intent on ‚ÄåThursday to produce Ukrainian defence systems including drones in Romania, they said after a meeting in Bucharest.
Romania, an EU and NATO member, shares a 650-km (400-mile) border with Ukraine, including ‚Äãalong the Danube river where Ukrainian ports on the ‚Äãopposite bank have come under Russian attack and Russian drones have breached Romanian ‚ÅÝairspace.
After four years of fending off Russia's assaults, Ukraine says ‚Äãit has developed drone and counter-drone systems that can help protect ‚ÄãNATO countries.
A statement signed by the two presidents said they had agreed to "enable the production of Ukrainian defence systems and capabilities in Romania with the purpose ‚Äãof strengthening the defence industries of Ukraine and Romania."
Last year, ‚ÄãRomanian officials said they aimed to partner with Ukraine in building drones under a new EU defence funding ‚Äåmechanism, ‚ÅÝSAFE.
"We talked about our military cooperation and one of the documents we signed also includes cooperating on drone production," said Romania's President Nicusor Dan, a centrist who last year defeated a right-wing candidate sceptical of ‚Äãclose ties to ‚ÄãUkraine.
Ukrainian President ‚ÅÝVolodymyr Zelenskiy said Ukraine's expertise lay particularly in integrating drone software into a country's wider defense system.
He said they also ‚Äãdiscussed energy, including the prospect of Ukraine importing U.S. ‚Äãliquefied ‚ÅÝnatural gas through pipelines across Romania, and cooperating on energy projects in the Black Sea.
The two presidents also signed a document promoting partnership ‚ÅÝin areas ‚Äãranging from defence to minority rights, and ‚Äãa separate document regarding energy cooperation.
Dan said Romania would support any legal means of unblocking EU aid to ‚ÄãUkraine that has been vetoed by Hungary .

item.title: Israeli airstrikes hit building in central Beirut, Reuters footage shows
item.description: BEIRUT, March 12 (Reuters) - Israeli airstrikes ‚Äãhit a ‚Äåbuilding twice in central ‚ÄãBeirut less ‚Äãthan a kilometre ‚ÅÝfrom ‚Äãthe government headquarters, ‚ÄãReuters video footage showed on ‚ÄãThursday, ‚Äãshortly after the Israeli ‚Äåmilitary ‚ÅÝsaid it had begun a ‚Äãnew ‚Äãwave ‚ÅÝof strikes on ‚Äãthe Lebanese ‚Äãcapital.

item.title: Active shooter at Michigan synagogue, authorities say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - The ‚ÄãMichigan State Police reported an active shooting incident on Thursday at ‚Äåthe Temple Israel Synagogue in West Bloomfield, Michigan, and law enforcement authorities rushed to the scene.
No official details were immediately available, but ‚Äãaerial news footage from over the synagogue showed ‚Äãplumes of smoke rising from the roof of the ‚ÅÝbuilding, with police and fire department vehicles nearby.
Oakland County ‚ÄãSheriff Michael Bouchard said on CNN: "Nobody at the moment ‚Äãhas been confirmed to be hurt except potentially the shooter."
Temple Israel runs an early childhood center that provides childcare and education to children ‚Äãas young as 6 weeks old, according to its ‚Äãwebsite.
FBI Director Kash Patel wrote on X: "FBI personnel are on the ‚Äåscene ‚ÅÝwith partners in Michigan and responding to the apparent vehicle ramming and active shooter situation out of Temple Israel Synagogue in West Bloomfield Township, Michigan."
The Jewish Federation of Detroit ‚Äãposted a message ‚Äãon its ‚ÅÝFacebook page saying its affiliated agencies were "currently in precautionary lockdown" in response to the ‚ÄãTemple Israel incident.
Jewish organizations throughout the United States ‚Äãhave ‚ÅÝbeen operating under heightened security since U.S. and Israeli warplanes initiated massive air strikes on Iran on February 28, sparking ‚ÅÝan ‚Äãintensifying war across the Middle East.

item.title: US Navy could escort vessels in Strait of Hormuz with international coalition, Bessent says
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - The U.S. Navy, ‚Äãperhaps with an ‚Äåinternational coalition, will escort vessels ‚Äãthrough the ‚ÄãStrait of Hormuz when ‚ÅÝit is ‚Äãmilitarily possible, U.S. ‚ÄãTreasury Secretary told Sky News in an ‚Äãinterview on ‚ÄãThursday.
"My belief, that as ‚Äåsoon ‚ÅÝas it is militarily possible, the US ‚ÄãNavy, ‚Äãperhaps ‚ÅÝwith an international coalition, ‚Äãwill be ‚Äãescorting ‚ÅÝvessels through," Bessent said.

item.title: US and allies clash with Russia and China over Iran nuclear program
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - The U.S. and Western allies clashed with Russia and China on Thursday ‚Äãover Iran's nuclear intentions, as Washington sought at the United Nations to further justify the war it launched ‚Äåon Iran two weeks ago.
At a meeting of the 15-member U.N. Security Council, which is chaired this month by the U.S., Russia and China moved unsuccessfully to block a discussion about a committee established to oversee and enforce U.N. sanctions on Iran. They were overruled 11-2 with two abstentions.
Addressing ‚Äãthe council, U.S. envoy to the United Nations Mike Waltz accused Moscow and Beijing of seeking to ‚Äãprotect Tehran by blocking the work of the so-called 1737 Committee.
"All member states of the ‚ÅÝUnited Nations should be implementing an arms embargo against Iran, banning the transfer and trade of missile technology, and ‚Äãfreezing relevant financial assets," Waltz said.
"The U.N. provisions to be re-imposed are not arbitrary, but instead, narrowly scoped to ‚Äãaddress the threat posed by Iran's nuclear, missile and conventional arms programs and Iran's ongoing support for terrorism," he said.
Waltz charged that both China and Russia did not want a functional sanctions committee "because they want to protect their partner, Iran, and continue to maintain defense ‚Äãcooperation that is now once again prohibited."
Waltz noted that last week the U.N.'s International Atomic Energy Agency had reiterated ‚Äãthat Iran was the only state in the world without nuclear weapons to have produced and accumulated uranium enriched up to ‚Äå60 percent, ‚ÅÝand had refused to provide the IAEA access to this stockpile.
Russia's U.N. ambassador Vasily Nebenzya charged that the U.S. and its allies had "whipped up hysteria surrounding supposed plans Iran had to get a nuclear weapon" that were never corroborated by IAEA reports.
"This was done in order to undertake yet another military venture against Tehran and to ensure great ‚Äãescalation of the situation in ‚Äãthe Middle East and beyond," ‚ÅÝhe said.
China's representative, Fu Cong, called Washington the "instigator" of the Iranian nuclear crisis and said it had "resorted to blatant use of force against Iran during the negotiation process, which ‚Äãrendered the diplomatic efforts futile."
U.S. President Donald Trump has used Iran's nuclear program to justify ‚Äãhis war ‚ÅÝon Iran. He said this month that Iran would have had a nuclear weapon within two weeks had the U.S. not struck three key nuclear sites in June, a claim sources have said was not supported by U.S. intelligence assessments.
Britain and France ‚ÅÝtold the ‚ÄãSecurity Council that re-imposing sanctions on Iran was justified by Tehran's ‚Äãfailure to address concerns about its nuclear program. France said the IAEA was no longer able to guarantee the peaceful nature of the program ‚Äãand Tehran's nuclear stockpile was sufficient for 10 nuclear devices.

item.title: Chile and US eye collaboration on critical minerals and rare earths
item.description:
SANTIAGO, March 12 (Reuters) - Chile and the United States ‚Äãhave signed a joint statement to begin discussions on critical ‚Äåminerals and rare earths, Chile's foreign ministry said on Thursday.
The first meeting ‚Äãwill take place within the ‚Äãnext two weeks, it added.
The Trump ‚ÅÝadministration has been pushing to ‚Äãreduce reliance on China for ‚Äãcritical minerals and rare earths, which are used in electric vehicles, semiconductors, defense systems ‚Äãand consumer electronics.
Chile is the ‚Äãworld's largest copper producer and second-largest lithium ‚Äåproducer.
"I ‚ÅÝbelieve there is much we can do with the United States and Chile to strengthen the ‚Äãsupply chains ‚Äãof these ‚ÅÝminerals," U.S. Deputy Secretary of State Christopher Landau ‚Äãtold journalists in Chile, where ‚Äãright-wing ‚ÅÝJose Antonio Kast was sworn in as president on Wednesday.
"We will discuss ‚ÅÝhow ‚Äãwe can work ‚Äãtogether," Landau added.

item.title: IAEA director discusses non-proliferation with head of Russia's Rosatom
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - The director general of ‚Äåthe U.N. nuclear watchdog discussed non-proliferation issues on Thursday with the head ‚Äãof Rosatom, Russia's state nuclear ‚Äãenergy corporation ahead of other ‚ÅÝmeetings with officials, the company ‚Äãsaid.
Emmanuel Grossi, head of the U.N.'s ‚ÄãInternational Atomic Energy Agency, is due to meet Russian Foreign Minister Sergei ‚ÄãLavrov on Friday, TASS news ‚Äãagency reported earlier.
"On the eve of the ‚Äåmeeting, ‚ÅÝthe head of the state corporation (Rosatom) Alexei Likhachev and the Director General of the IAEA Rafael ‚ÄãGrossi discussed ‚Äãstrategic ‚ÅÝissues concerning the enforcement of the non-proliferation regime ‚Äãand the development of nuclear ‚Äãenergy ‚ÅÝin the world," Rosatom said.
The statement gave no other details ‚ÅÝof ‚Äãthe discussions.

item.title: Iran to be high on G7 ministerial agenda at upcoming meeting, Canada says
item.description:
OTTAWA, March 12 (Reuters) - The foreign ministers from ‚Äãthe Group of Seven ‚Äåwill have detailed discussions on diplomatic efforts related to the ‚Äãwar in Iran ‚Äãwhen they meet near Paris ‚ÅÝat a scheduled ‚Äãmeeting at the end ‚Äãof March, Canada's foreign minister said on Thursday.
France, which is ‚Äãcurrently chairing the G7, ‚Äãwill host the first of ‚Äåtwo ‚ÅÝforeign ministers meeting on March 26-27 at the Vaux de Cernay abbey, ‚Äãsome 50km ‚Äãsouthwest ‚ÅÝof Paris.
"We will be having a ‚Äãfull-fledged conversation about ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝdiplomatic efforts relating to the war," Anita Anand ‚ÅÝtold ‚Äãreporters on Thursday.

item.title: Israeli military says it struck Basij checkpoints in Tehran
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 12 (Reuters) - The Israeli military struck checkpoints in Tehran operated by the Basij, ‚Äãit said on Thursday, as part of an ‚Äåeffort to weaken the rule of Iran's clerical leaders.
The Basij militia is a part-time paramilitary force under the control of the ‚ÄãIslamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) that is often used ‚Äãto quell protests inside Iran.
The Israeli military said ‚ÅÝin a written statement that it had recently ‚Äãidentified new Basij roadblocks in Tehran.
"After identifying the deployment, ‚Äãover the past day, the Israeli Air Force, acting on IDF (Israel Defense Forces) intelligence, has targeted the Basij roadblocks and operatives," the ‚Äãmilitary said.
"These forces led the regime‚Äôs primary efforts ‚Äãto suppress internal protests, particularly in recent months, employing severe violence, ‚Äåmass ‚ÅÝarrests, and the use of force against civilian demonstrators," the military said.
Israel and the United States launched a bombing campaign in Iran on February 28, with an ‚Äãopening strike that ‚Äãkilled Supreme ‚ÅÝLeader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
Both allies have called on Iranians to rise up and topple ‚Äãtheir clerical rulers. Many Iranians want change ‚Äãand ‚ÅÝsome openly celebrated the death of Khamenei, after his forces had killed thousands of anti-government protesters in January.
But there ‚ÅÝhas ‚Äãbeen no sign of organised dissent ‚Äãwhile the country is under attack, and no sign of Iran's ‚Äãrulers relinquishing power.

item.title: Suspected gunman involved in Michigan synagogue shooting is dead, media reports
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - The suspected ‚Äågunman involved ‚Äãin ‚Äãa shooting ‚ÅÝat ‚ÄãTemple ‚ÄãIsrael Synagogue in ‚ÄãWest ‚ÄãBloomfield, Michigan, ‚Äåis ‚ÅÝdead, according to ‚Äãmedia ‚Äãreports.

item.title: Senegal's PM says contract for BP-operated GTA gas project is unfair
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - Senegal's Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko said in a ‚Äãtelevised statement on Thursday that ‚Äåa gas contract for the Greater Tortue Ahmeyim (GTA) project operated by BP (BP.L) , opens new tab had ‚Äãbeen found to be one-sided and ‚Äãunfair after a review of strategic ‚ÅÝcontracts.
Sonko's address on Thursday aimed ‚Äãto cover the outcome of a ‚Äãreview of strategic contracts in a wide range of sectors launched by his government since ‚Äãcoming to power in 2024.
He ‚Äãsaid the oil and gas sector was still ‚Äåunder ‚ÅÝreview and discussions underway with various partners, and the priority through the talks was to ensure the supply ‚Äãof gas.
He ‚Äãdid ‚ÅÝnot provide specific details about any discussions with BP.
Sonko ‚Äãpromised to publish a document ‚Äãwith ‚ÅÝthe details of the contracts being studied. Senegal's review includes oil and gas ‚ÅÝcontracts, ‚Äãfishing contracts and ‚Äãprocurement contracts among others.

item.title: Netanyahu threatens Iran's new supreme leader, defends joint assault with US
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 12 (Reuters) - Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu ‚Äãon Thursday issued a veiled threat to kill ‚ÄåIran's new supreme leader as he sought to use his first press conference since the start of the war to defend ‚Äãhis joint military assault with the U.S. against ‚ÄãIran.
Netanyahu said that Iran was "no longer the same" after ‚ÅÝnearly two weeks of U.S.-Israeli air bombardment and ‚Äãthat Tehran had suffered blows to its elite Revolutionary Guards ‚ÄãCorps and Basij paramilitary force.
Standing between two Israeli flags and taking questions via video link, Netanyahu vowed to keep hitting Lebanon's Hezbollah ‚Äãafter the Iran-backed group opened fire on March 2 ‚Äãto avenge the killing of Iran's supreme leader at the start ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝthe war.
Asked about what actions Israel might take against Iran's new Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei and Hezbollah chief Naim Qassem, Netanyahu said:
"I wouldn‚Äôt issue life insurance policies on any ‚Äãof the leaders ‚Äãof the ‚ÅÝterrorist organization ... I don't intend to give an exact message here about what we ‚Äãare planning or what we are going ‚Äãto do."
The ‚ÅÝIsraeli leader said that Iran and Hezbollah no longer pose the same threats that they once did.
Netanyahu said he ‚ÅÝand ‚ÄãU.S. President Donald Trump speak daily ‚Äãand that their conversations are free and open in nature.

item.title: US Department of Energy to invest $1.9 billion for power grid upgrades
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - The U.S. Department of Energy will invest ‚Äãabout $1.9 billion to accelerate upgrades of ‚Äåpower grids in the country, it said on Thursday.
U.S. power consumption is set to rise ‚Äãsharply this year and the ‚Äãnext, driven by rapid growth in AI ‚ÅÝand cryptocurrency data centers and ‚Äãincreased electrification of heating and transport. However, ‚Äãthat demand has also led to higher electricity prices for a large swath of the ‚Äãcountry.
The DOE said the funding would ‚Äãbe used to prepare for the rise in ‚Äådemand ‚ÅÝand to lower electricity costs for American households and businesses.
The selected projects for the funding will need to showcase ‚Äãhow reconductoring ‚Äãor ‚ÅÝreplacing existing power lines with higher-capacity conductors, combined with transmission ‚Äãtechnologies, can bolster the nation's ‚Äãelectric ‚ÅÝgrid.
Utilities are adding power lines and other components to the grid as data ‚ÅÝcenters ‚Äãlift U.S. electricity consumption ‚Äãout of two decades of flat demand.

item.title: TotalEnergies output down 15% due to US-Iran war; confirms UAE outages
item.description:
PARIS, March 12 (Reuters) - TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab has lost 15% of ‚Äåits oil and gas output as the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran shuts fields across the Middle East, including in the ‚ÄãUAE, Qatar and Iraq, the French oil ‚Äãmajor said on its investor website.
That output ‚ÅÝaccounts for about 10% of Total's upstream cash ‚Äãflow, it added.
The statement became the first confirmation of ‚Äãwidespread output outages in the UAE due to the crisis. Qatar and Iraq have previously announced production cuts, but the ‚ÄãUAE has not released any official information.
Total said ‚Äãits offshore production in the UAE is shut. The UAE ‚Äåproduces ‚ÅÝaround half its oil output from offshore fields.
The French firm added that income from an $8 per barrel rise in oil prices that has occurred as ‚Äãa consequence of ‚Äãthe war ‚ÅÝwould more than offset the loss of output in the Middle East this ‚Äãyear as it brings online additional ‚Äãproduction ‚ÅÝelsewhere.
Operations at its SATORP refinery in Saudi Arabia are normal, it added.
The impact of shutdowns in Qatar of ‚ÅÝliquefied ‚Äãnatural gas production are limited ‚Äãto two million tonnes of LNG for Total.

item.title: Iran's Shi'ite allies step up strikes despite weakened hand
item.description:
BEIRUT/BAGHDAD/JERUSALEM, March 12 (Reuters) - Shi'ite Muslim armed groups in Lebanon and Iraq are stepping up their role in the war with the U.S. and Israel, showing the Iran-backed "Axis of Resistance" can still wage attacks despite damage inflicted on the alliance during the Gaza conflict.
Groups that have long been armed and financed by Iran and loyal ‚Äãto its Shi'ite Islamist rulers are now helping Tehran intensify the war around the region, strikes in recent days show.
Hezbollah and Iran's Revolutionary Guards on Wednesday launched their first-ever simultaneous rocket barrage on Israel, with the ‚ÄåLebanese group firing 200 missiles. Israel reported that only two of these hit its territory.
Iraqi Shi'ite militants have also picked up the pace of drone and missile attacks on U.S. interests in Iraq in the last 3-4 days, according to three Iraqi security sources and two sources close to the groups.
One group yet to enter the fray are Tehran's Houthi allies in Yemen, heavily armed and capable of disrupting maritime navigation around the Arabian peninsula, as shown during the Gaza war when they fired at Red Sea shipping and Israel. Houthi attacks could further disrupt oil markets because Saudi Arabia diverted exports ‚Äãto the Red Sea after Iran shut the Hormuz straits.
Last week, Houthi leader Abdul Malik al-Houthi said the group had its "fingers on the trigger" and was ready to act militarily when developments warrant it.
The alliance that Tehran calls the Axis of ‚ÄãResistance suffered major blows after Hamas - one of its key members - attacked Israel on October 7, 2023, igniting a war that decimated the Palestinian group and pummelled Hezbollah, with Israel killing the ‚ÅÝLebanese group's leader Hassan Nasrallah. The ripple effects helped topple Bashar al-Assad in Syria, knocking away a pillar of the Axis.
"Iran built the Axis for a moment like this," said Mohanad Hage Ali of the Carnegie Middle East Center think-tank, describing it as an "existential ‚Äãwar" for Iran and Hezbollah, which joined the fight even though its military power remains well below levels seen in 2023.
"If the Iranian regime is destroyed, there would be nothing left" of the Axis, he said.
Israeli military spokesman Lieutenant Colonel Nadav Shoshani said the ‚ÄãHezbollah attack on Wednesday comprised 200 rockets and 20 drones.
"There's no contradiction between the fact that we heavily, heavily diminished Hezbollah in the last three years, and the fact that they are still a relevant, dangerous force," he told reporters on Thursday.
Washington has long demanded that Iran end its support for the proxies. The U.S. State Department did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Iran's new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei thanked "the fighters of the Resistance Front", according to a statement issued on Thursday and read out by a state TV announcer.
"We consider the countries of the Resistance ‚ÄãFront as our best friends," he said in the statement, the first issued in his name since he was named as leader on Sunday.
Hezbollah, founded by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in 1982, entered the Iran war on March 2, saying it aimed to avenge the ‚Äãkilling of Mojtaba's father, former Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, killed on the first day of the war.
Israel has retaliated with a new offensive against the group in Lebanon, just 15 months after the last one, killing more than 600 people, forcing more than 800,000 from their homes.
Hezbollah's ‚Äårocket barrage on ‚ÅÝWednesday night - its heaviest during this war - was launched at the same time as Iran fired ballistic missiles at Israel, according to the Israeli military and two Lebanese sources familiar with Hezbollah operations.
The Lebanese sources said the coordinated strikes were part of Iran's plan in the event of a major war, aiming to confuse Israeli air defense systems.
Despite the volleys of rockets, Hezbollah's attacks have caused relatively little damage so far. Two Israeli soldiers have been killed in Lebanon.
No fatalities have been reported in Israel as a result of Hezbollah rocket attacks.
Hezbollah played a major part in Iran's regional strategy under the leadership of Nasrallah, the secretary general killed in 2024, backing Shi'ite factions in Iraq, Hamas, and the Houthis.
In Iraq, not all the Iran‚Äëbacked armed groups appear to support attacks ‚Äãon U.S. interests. Reuters reported last week that many of the fighters ‚Äãand militia groups the Iranians cultivated in Iraq had ‚ÅÝnot entered the fight.
But analysts and officials say a core group of Tehran‚Äëaligned factions remains active and capable of exerting pressure.
Operating under the umbrella of the Islamic Resistance in Iraq, these groups said on Thursday they'd carried out 31 attacks over the past 24 hours using dozens of drones and rockets against what they described as ‚Äúoccupation bases‚Äù in Iraq and the region
Security officials say the militias have also sought ‚Äãto extend pressure to energy projects and oilfields in southern Iraq, where several U.S. companies and U.S.‚Äëlinked service firms operate alongside international partners.
Among the claimed attacks, two security sources said two ‚Äãdrones hit the southern Majnoon oilfield ‚ÅÝon Wednesday, where U.S.-based KBR is the operator. No casualties were reported. The attack was corroborated by a field engineer who said there had been five such strikes in less than a week.
On Tuesday a U.S. diplomatic facility near Baghdad International Airport was struck by a drone, according to the U.S. State Department, which said there were no injuries and everyone was accounted for.
Four security sources told Reuters the same site has come under repeated attack and was also hit on Wednesday.
Separately, two drones targeted a U.S. military base near Erbil airport in Iraqi Kurdistan on Wednesday, ‚ÅÝthree Kurdish security ‚Äãsources said.
In northern Iraq, a drone attack struck an oilfield operated by U.S. firm HKN Energy in Iraq‚Äôs Kurdistan region on March 5, causing a fire ‚Äãand halting production, two security sources and an oilfield engineer said. The sources said the drones belonged to Iran-backed militias and came from an area they controlled.
Reuters could not independently verify who was behind the attacks.
Andreas Krieg, a lecturer at King's College London's security studies department, said that while the Axis of Resistance had ‚Äãbeen degraded since 2023, Hezbollah, Iraqi Shi'ite militias, and the Houthis were "very much operational".
"They still retain capabilities, they still show very strong intent, and they remain well resourced," he said.

item.title: Canada, Mexico say trilateral deal is key ahead of talks to review USMCA
item.description:
MEXICO CITY, March 12 (Reuters) - Canadian and Mexican officials on Thursday reaffirmed the importance of ‚Äãmaintaining the trilateral free trade agreement between their ‚Äåcountries and the U.S., amid signals from Washington it could be interested in bilateral deals.
Canada's ambassador to Mexico Cameron MacKay ‚Äãand Mexican deputy trade secretary Luis Rosendo Gutierrez ‚Äãboth told a conference in Mexico City that ‚ÅÝmaintaining the trilateral U.S.-Mexico-Canada agreement was a priority for ‚Äãtheir nations.
The meetings follow an announcement by U.S. President Donald ‚ÄãTrump's administration on Wednesday of two new trade investigations into alleged forced labor and industrial overcapacity among 16 major trade partners.
The move ‚Äãseeks to restore tariff pressure after a Supreme ‚ÄãCourt ruling last month struck down significant portions of the administration's ‚Äåtrade ‚ÅÝprogram.
Mexico is among the countries that could be targeted by the investigation into excess capacity under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, a legal provision that ‚Äãallows the ‚ÄãU.S. to investigate ‚ÅÝforeign trade practices deemed unfair.
However, Mexican Economy Minister Marcelo Ebrard downplayed the potential ‚Äãfallout, saying the bulk of Mexican trade falls ‚Äãunder ‚ÅÝthe USMCA pact and is not subject to Section 301.
The U.S. did not list Canada, the United States' second-largest ‚ÅÝU.S. ‚Äãtrade partner after Mexico, as ‚Äãamong the countries that could be subject to investigation.

item.title: UN mission says Venezuela's repressive apparatus persists after Maduro ouster
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - A United Nations fact-finding mission said on Thursday that Venezuela's "repressive state" remains fully operational following the removal of former President Nicolas Maduro by U.S. forces in early ‚ÄãJanuary.
The mission told the U.N. Human Rights Council in a report ‚Äåthat "institutional structures facilitating human rights violations have not been dismantled", adding that 87 politically motivated detentions had taken place since Maduro's ouster.
Venezuela's government has long denied accusations of human rights abuses and ‚Äãthe existence of political prisoners, saying those jailed committed common crimes.
The Venezuelan communications ‚Äãministry, which handles all press queries for the government, did not ‚ÅÝimmediately respond to a request for comment.
The report said senior government and military ‚Äãofficials previously identified as responsible for crimes against humanity continue to hold significant power.
Washington ‚Äãhas long criticized Venezuela's democratic record, accusing its socialist government of electoral fraud and political repression.
The U.S. announced it would "take control" of Venezuela but has not pushed for new elections, touting good relations ‚Äãwith Maduro's vice president Delcy Rodriguez - who has taken over as interim president ‚Äãand has backed major changes to oil and mining regulations, meant to stoke investment.
Rodriguez's government also passed ‚Äåan amnesty ‚ÅÝlaw as part of an agreement to free thousands of prisoners. Her administration said in late February that nearly 2,200 people have been released under this law, but local NGOs have criticized a lack of transparency and estimated that the number of freed ‚Äãprisoners is much ‚Äãlower.
Alfredo Romero, the director ‚ÅÝof legal rights NGO Foro Penal, said at a press conference on Thursday that "there is still a repressive system, a justice ‚Äãsystem that is used to persecute."
The U.N. mission called for ‚Äãthe immediate ‚ÅÝrelease of all remaining political detainees and unhindered access to the country to continue its investigations. It did not provide an estimate for the number of people still in ‚ÅÝprison.
"Venezuela cannot ‚Äãbe said to truly be on the road ‚Äãto human rights reform unless and until that repressive apparatus is dismantled," said lawyer Maria Eloisa Quintero, a ‚Äãrepresentative of the mission.

item.title: Exchanges oppose potential US Treasury intervention in oil futures market
item.description:
BOCA RATON, Florida, March 12 (Reuters) - The heads of a number of top exchanges, including CME Group (CME.O) , opens new tab and Toronto Stock Exchange parent TMX Group (X.TO) , opens new tab , oppose any potential ‚Äãintervention from the U.S. government involving the oil futures market, amid ‚Äårising energy prices in the aftermath of the Iran conflict.
The latest comments come as reports have emerged that the U.S. Treasury is weighing potential measures involving oil futures to combat rising prices.
