On ‚ÄãWednesday, the U.S. government announced it would release 172 million barrels of oil ‚Äãfrom its strategic petroleum reserve to reduce oil prices that have ‚ÅÝsurged due to supply disruptions from the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
"Markets do not like ‚Äãit when governments intervene on oil prices," said Terry Duffy, Chief Executive Officer of ‚ÄãCME, during a panel discussion earlier this week. The CME, which is the world's largest derivatives exchange, is among a group of U.S. exchanges that trade energy futures.
The White House and the ‚ÄãU.S. Treasury Department did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Another CEO of ‚Äãa leading exchange, who requested anonymity to discuss the matter candidly, echoed similar sentiments, saying that ‚Äåan intervention ‚ÅÝfrom the U.S. Treasury risked aggravating the problem, as it could raise the risk of hefty losses for the government if energy prices continue to rise.
Oil prices jumped nearly 5% on Wednesday as fresh attacks on ships in the Strait of Hormuz further ‚Äãaggravated supply shock fears. ‚ÄãSeveral analysts said ‚ÅÝthe International Energy Agency's proposal for a record release of oil reserves is inadequate to quell those concerns. The IEA the ‚Äãrelease of 400 million barrels of oil to try to ‚Äãcombat a ‚ÅÝsurge in energy prices, which are now up more than 25% since the war broke out.
"I usually I find those things (potential government intervention in markets) lead to unintended consequences. ‚ÅÝYou create ‚Äãa different problem by trying to solve the ‚Äãfirst problem. The market will sort this out itself," said John McKenzie, CEO of TMX Group.

item.title: Iran not going to close Strait of Hormuz, Iran UN envoy says
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - Iran's U.N. Ambassador said on Thursday Tehran was not going to close ‚Äãthe Strait of Hormuz, but added that it ‚Äåwas Iran's right to preserve the security of the key shipping route.
Amir Saeid Iravani made his comments to reporters at the United ‚ÄãNations when asked about remarks by new Iranian supreme ‚Äãleader, Mojtaba Khamenei , who said on Thursday that the "lever ‚ÅÝof blocking the Strait of Hormuz must continue to ‚Äãbe used."
"We are not going to close the Strait of ‚ÄãHormuz," Iravani said. "But it is our inherent right to preserve the peace and security in this waterway."
In a prepared statement he read to ‚Äãreporters before responding to questions, Iravani said that "Iran fully ‚Äãrespects and remains committed to the principle of freedom of navigation under ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝlaw of the sea.
"However, the current situation in the region, including in the Strait of Hormuz, is not the result of Iran's lawful exercise of its right of self-defense.
"Rather, ‚Äãit is ‚Äãthe direct consequence ‚ÅÝof the destabilizing actions of the United States in launching aggression against Iran and ‚Äãundermining regional security."
He said he had no response ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝa comment by U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, who told Sky News in an interview on Thursday that the U.S. ‚ÅÝNavy, ‚Äãperhaps with an international coalition, would escort ‚Äãvessels through the Strait of Hormuz when it is militarily possible.

item.title: Colombia's Petro, Trump spoke on phone, Trump said Petro welcome in U.S.
item.description:
BOGOTA, March 12 (Reuters) - Colombia's President Gustavo Petro and his U.S. counterpart President Donald Trump spoke on the phone on Thursday, Petro's office said on ‚ÄãX, ahead of Petro's meeting with Venezuela's acting President Delcy Rodriguez.
The two ‚Äåmen discussed the economy along the Colombia-Venezuela border, which is expected to be the top topic at the Petro-Rodriguez meeting on Friday, the statement said, and Trump said Petro, who he ‚Äãsanctioned last year, is welcome in the U.S. and apologized for not ‚Äãinviting the Colombian leader to a meeting in Miami last week with ‚ÅÝother Latin American heads of state.
Petro and Trump have had repeated squabbles, though ‚Äãthe two men were each positive after a face-to-face meeting in Washington last month.
Trump has ‚Äãrepeatedly demanded more cooperation from Colombia in the fight against drug trafficking and has accused Petro, without evidence, of being an "". Petro meanwhile, claims record drug seizures during his tenure ‚Äãand has said deadly strikes on alleged drug boats amount to war crimes.
"During ‚Äãthe call, the two leaders discussed energy issues, hydrocarbons, security, illicit crops, eradication efforts and the ‚Äåjoint ‚ÅÝfight against drug trafficking. They also talked about economic reactivation along the border and other matters of bilateral interest," the statement said.
"In the course of the conversation, the Colombian president extended an invitation to his U.S. counterpart to visit Cartagena, which ‚Äãwas warmly received. President ‚ÄãTrump, in turn, ‚ÅÝreiterated that President Petro will always be welcome in the United States and expressed apologies for any previous inconvenience related to ‚Äãan invitation to Miami," the statement said, adding Trump wished ‚ÄãPetro success ‚ÅÝin his meeting with Rodriguez.
The Rodriguez-Petro bilateral is Rodriguez's first presidential-level in-person meeting since she took power following her predecessor's ouster by the United States. Trump has supported a ‚ÅÝseries ‚Äãof moves by Rodriguez, formerly vice president, to ‚Äãattract investors in oil and mining and stabilize the country since the January raid that captured President Nicolas Maduro, with ‚ÄãTrump repeatedly praising Rodriguez for her cooperation with the U.S.

item.title: Pakistan's PM Sharif discussed regional developments with Saudi crown prince
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - Pakistan's Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif met Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed ‚Äãbin Salman in Jeddah on ‚ÄåTuesday, where the two leaders discussed regional developments, the prime minister's office ‚Äãsaid.
The meeting comes as ‚Äãthe Middle East grapples with escalating ‚ÅÝconflict, including tensions involving Israel, ‚Äãthe United States and Iran.
"The Prime ‚ÄãMinister expressed Pakistan's full solidarity and support for the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia ‚Äãin these challenging times," Mosharraf ‚ÄãZaidi, a spokesman for Sharif, shared a ‚Äåpress ‚ÅÝrelease from his office on X, adding that the two leaders held an in-depth exchange of ‚Äãviews on ‚Äãrecent ‚ÅÝregional developments and agreed to work together for peace ‚Äãand stability.
Sharif also assured ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝcrown prince that Pakistan would always stand firmly with Saudi Arabia, ‚ÅÝthe ‚Äãpost read.

item.title: Drone attack wounds at least six French soldiers at base in Iraq‚Äôs Makhmour, Erbil governor says
item.description: March 12 (Reuters) - At least ‚Äãsix French soldiers ‚Äåwere wounded in ‚Äãa ‚Äãdrone attack targeting ‚ÅÝa ‚Äãjoint Peshmerga-French ‚Äãbase in the Makhmour area ‚Äãor ‚ÄãIraq, Erbil Governor ‚ÄåOmed ‚ÅÝKoshnaw said a statement and ‚Äãa ‚Äãsecurity ‚ÅÝsource informed ‚Äãof the ‚Äãincident ‚ÅÝsaid on Thursday.

item.title: Brazil's top diplomat raises concerns over Trump aide's planned visit to Bolsonaro
item.description:
BRASILIA, March 12 (Reuters) - Brazil's Foreign Minister Mauro Vieira told ‚Äãa Supreme Court Justice that ‚Äåa planned visit from a Trump administration official to ‚Äãimprisoned former President ‚ÄãJair Bolsonaro could be an "interference" ‚ÅÝin the country's internal ‚Äãaffairs, a document seen by ‚ÄãReuters showed on Thursday.
Lawyers for Bolsonaro asked the Brazilian Supreme Court ‚Äãon Tuesday to allow ‚ÄãDarren Beattie, who was recently tapped ‚Äåby ‚ÅÝU.S. President Donald Trump for a senior advisory role overseeing Brazil, to ‚Äãvisit him ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝprison next week.
Bolsonaro is serving a 27-year prison ‚Äãsentence since November ‚Äãfor ‚ÅÝplotting a coup against his successor, President Luiz ‚ÅÝInacio ‚ÄãLula da Silva.

item.title: Ships must coordinate with Iran's navy to pass through Strait of Hormuz, foreign ministry says
item.description:
DUBAI, March 12 (Reuters) - Ships must coordinate with Iran's navy to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, the country's ‚Äãforeign ministry spokesperson said in comments carried by ‚ÄåMehr news agency on Thursday.
Iran will fight on and keep the Strait of Hormuz shut as leverage against the United States and ‚ÄãIsrael, new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei said on ‚ÄãThursday in defiant first comments attributed to him since he ‚ÅÝsucceeded his slain father.
"The security of the Strait of ‚ÄãHormuz is of vital importance to Iran, because the country‚Äôs ‚Äãsecurity is tied to the security of the region. With the longest coastlines on the Persian Gulf and the Sea of Oman, ‚ÄãIran has always borne costs to protect this strategic ‚Äãwaterway," said Esmaeil Baghaei, the spokesperson.
Khamenei also called on Iran's neighbours to ‚Äåshut ‚ÅÝU.S. bases on their territory and warned that Iran would continue to target them .
"The insecurity created in the region by the United States and the Zionist regime can affect the movement of ‚Äãships. However, Iran ‚Äãdoes not ‚ÅÝwant this strait to become insecure, and ships must coordinate with the Iranian navy ‚Äãwhen passing through so that maritime security is ‚Äãmaintained," ‚ÅÝBaghaei added.
The prospect that one of the most severe disruptions ever to hit global energy supplies could drag on sent oil prices ‚ÅÝsurging ‚Äãback above $100 a barrel, after falling ‚Äãearlier in the week on hopes of a swift end to the conflict.

item.title: Polish president vetoes EU defence loan bill
item.description:
WARSAW, March 12 (Reuters) - Poland's president said on Thursday that he would not sign legislation creating a mechanism to spend 43.7 billion euros ($50.30 billion) in European Union loans to boost the ‚Äãmilitary, triggering condemnation from the government.
The clash between nationalist President Karol Nawrocki and ‚Äåthe pro-EU government of Prime Minister Donald Tusk over the 150 billion-euro Security Action for Europe initiative is the latest example of the deep divisions complicating policymaking at Poland's highest levels of state.
"The SAFE mechanism ‚Äãis a massive foreign loan taken out for 45 years in a foreign ‚Äãcurrency, with interest costs that could reach up to 180 billion zlotys," ‚ÅÝNawrocki said in a televised address to the nation.
"Poles will therefore have to repay twice ‚Äãthe value of the loan, while Western banks and financial institutions will profit from it."
He ‚Äãadded that Brussels could halt the payout of funds arbitrarily through deploying conditionality rules, thus limiting Poland's sovereignty, and questioned its constitutionality.
"Poland's security cannot depend on foreign decisions," he said.
Warsaw was the biggest beneficiary of ‚ÄãSAFE, but the nationalist opposition party Law and Justice labelled it a German plot to ‚Äãmeddle in Polish affairs that would saddle the country with debt and limit its flexibility regarding arms ‚Äåpurchases.
Tusk's ‚ÅÝgovernment says the cheap financing provided by SAFE is essential to Poland's security in the face of what it views as a rising threat from Russia.
"The President has lost the chance to act like a patriot. Shame!," Tusk wrote on X, adding that the government would ‚Äãpresent its response at ‚Äãan extraordinary council ‚ÅÝof ministers on Friday morning.
Nawrocki and Central Bank Governor Adam Glapinski have proposed an alternative that would involve using unrealised profits from the ‚Äãincreasing value of the National Bank of Poland's gold reserves to ‚Äãfinance military ‚ÅÝspending.
In his address, Nawrocki called on the government to cooperate on the alternative, national proposal.
However, Glapinski says the government has expressed " zero interest " in the proposal. The bill proposed by Nawrocki is unlikely ‚ÅÝto ‚Äãbe approved by parliament, where Tusk's coalition has a ‚Äãmajority.
The government has previously said it is preparing a "plan B" that would still allow Poland to access SAFE funds, ‚Äãwithout giving further details.
($1 = 0.8687 euros)

item.title: TRADING DAY The destructive power of $100 oil
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 12 (Reuters) - Global stocks fell on Thursday, slammed by a 10% spike in oil prices, spiking bond yields and a stronger dollar , all of which point to a deteriorating outlook for consumers, businesses and economic growth.
In my column today I argue that, although the "Trump always chickens out" strategy of buying beaten-down ‚Äãstocks on the assumption that the U.S. president backs down from his more extreme policies, the Middle East war may be a "TACO" too ‚Äåfar .
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* 2026 Fed rate cut bets evaporate
And just like that, it was gone. Not so long ago - i.e., only a few weeks ago, before the U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran - many analysts were predicting three interest rate ‚Äãcuts from the Fed this year. As of Thursday, not even one U.S. rate cut in 2026 is fully priced in at all.
Traders have made it ‚Äãclear where they think the stagflation risks lie with oil at $100 a barrel, and the 'transitory' lessons of 2021-22 are weighing heavily too. No rate hikes are priced in yet though, ‚Äåand next ‚ÅÝweek would be far too soon. Right? Let's see what tomorrow's PCE inflation figures hold.
* Global bond rout
Zoom right out the curve, and geographically, and inflation fears really are intensifying, as the accelerating global bond market selloff shows. Investors are fleeing fixed income everywhere.
On Thursday, the two-year U.S. yield hit its highest since August, and the 2s/10s curve flattened the most since April; Germany's 10-year yield is near 3% and the highest since October 2023, and UK yields are up 60 ‚Äãbps in two weeks.
* Central bank ‚Äãbonanza
Central bankers are in an unenviable ‚ÅÝposition, and could be forgiven for just wanting to close their eyes, sit on their hands and wish the unfolding crisis away. But many of them are under the spotlight next week in what will be one of the busiest ‚Äãweeks of central bank meetings in a long time.
Here's a rundown of who's meeting next week: the central banks ‚Äãof Australia , Canada, Brazil, Japan , Sweden, ‚ÅÝSwitzerland, the euro zone , the UK and, of course, the Fed. The most likely to hike rates is the RBA, then possibly the BOJ, with the rest on hold. But if oil's at, say, $120 or higher, you never know.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want ‚Äãto receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust ‚ÄãPrinciples , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: US carrying out rescue effort after losing aircraft in Iraq, US military says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - A U.S. military refueling aircraft crashed in western Iraq on ‚ÄãThursday, in an incident U.S. Central ‚ÄåCommand said involved another aircraft but was not the result of hostile or ‚Äãfriendly fire.
The United States has ‚Äãsurged a large number of aircraft ‚ÅÝinto the Middle East to take ‚Äãpart in operations against Iran.
In a ‚Äãstatement, U.S. Central Command said it was carrying out rescue efforts after the U.S. KC-135 ‚Äãrefueling aircraft went down. The second ‚Äãaircraft landed safely.
"The incident occurred in friendly airspace ‚Äåduring ‚ÅÝOperation Epic Fury, and rescue efforts are ongoing," the statement said, using the name of the U.S. operation ‚Äãagainst Iran.
Since ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝU.S. and Israel started carrying out strikes against Iran on ‚ÄãFebruary 28, seven U.S. troops ‚Äãhave ‚ÅÝbeen killed.
Reuters reported on Tuesday that as many as 150 U.S. troops have ‚ÅÝbeen ‚Äãwounded in the U.S.-Israeli ‚Äãwar on Iran.

item.title: North Korea says Japan's missile expansion is raising regional security risks
item.description:
SEOUL, March 13 (Reuters) - North Korea accused Japan of ‚Äãheightening regional security risks by accelerating ‚Äåthe deployment and development of long-range missiles, saying Tokyo's military buildup amounted to preparations for a ‚Äãfuture attack, state media KCNA said ‚Äãon Friday.
KCNA said in a commentary that ‚ÅÝJapan had begun deploying an upgraded ‚Äãversion of its Type 12 surface-to-ship missile in ‚ÄãKumamoto and planned to position other long-range systems across multiple prefectures, putting neighbouring countries within range.
The commentary ‚Äãsaid Japan was pouring resources into ‚Äãextending missile ranges and adapting them for launches from ‚Äåland, ‚ÅÝsea and air, calling the systems "clearly long-range offensive weapons."
KCNA dismissed Japan's claim that the buildup was for self-defence, saying it reflected ‚Äãambitions to secure ‚Äãattack ‚ÅÝcapabilities and was part of "war preparations for re-invasion."
It added Japan's expanded ‚Äãpurchases of long-range strike weapons and ‚Äãparticipation ‚ÅÝin joint military drills showed the country was moving closer to using those capabilities ‚ÅÝand warned ‚ÄãTokyo risked serious consequences ‚Äãif it continued on its current path.

item.title: Canada to boost Arctic defenses, says it can no longer rely on others
item.description:
YELLOWKNIFE, Northwest Territories, March 12 (Reuters) - Prime Minister Mark Carney unveiled a C$35 billion ($25.7 ‚Äåbillion) plan on Thursday to boost Canada's defenses in the giant Arctic region as it tries to decrease its reliance on the United States.
Canada has traditionally relied on U.S. help to monitor the Canadian Arctic, which covers 4.4 million square km (1.7 million square miles) of land and ‚Äãsea - larger than India - and is almost completely uninhabited. President Donald Trump's tariffs and musings about annexing Canada ‚Äãhave put ties under strain.
"We will no longer depend on any one nation, and instead build ‚ÅÝa stronger, more independent country. With this new plan, Canada is taking full responsibility for defending our Arctic sovereignty," Carney ‚Äãsaid.
Even before Trump re-entered the White House last year, Canada had long been under pressure from the United States to ‚Äãincrease defense spending and vowed last June to boost funding for the armed forces. It is promising to hit NATO's 2% military spending target five years earlier than planned.
In January, Carney said the United States and other major nations were eroding the traditional rules-based order that had long ‚Äãbenefited Canada.
"The assumptions that shaped decades of Canadian defense and security are being upended," he said on Thursday.
"Climate change is ‚Äãcausing our Arctic region to warm nearly three times faster than the global average, a shift that great powers are actively looking to ‚Äåexploit," Carney ‚ÅÝsaid in a speech in Yellowknife, capital of the Northwest Territories and home to Canada's Arctic military command.
The plan laid out specifics of how previously announced funding for the Arctic would be spent. In 2022, Ottawa announced a C$38.6 billion plan to modernize its defenses and the joint North American Aerospace Defense Command that Canada operates with the United States.
Canada has four ‚Äãrudimentary Arctic airfields that can ‚Äãaccommodate six fighters each, ‚ÅÝand around 2,000 soldiers dotted around the region.
Carney's plan calls for investing C$32 billion to expand the military airfields in the region and to build four operational support hubs.
The plan would ‚Äãalso upgrade two commercial airports and fast-track two proposed roads from the Arctic to Canada's ‚Äãsouthern regions.
Trump has ‚ÅÝexpressed keen interest in the Arctic and its mineral potential. In addition to commenting about annexing Canada, he has insisted that the U.S. needs Greenland to fend off threats from Russian and Chinese interests in the region.
Canada's Arctic region comprises approximately 25% of ‚ÅÝthe global ‚ÄãArctic. Although the region is rich in rare minerals, it has very ‚Äãlittle infrastructure which, combined with the extreme cold, makes mining operations extremely complex and costly.
Later on Thursday Carney is due to fly to northern Norway ‚Äãto observe biennial NATO drills .
($1 = 1.3620 Canadian dollars)

item.title: Swedish coast guard boards second stateless ship in a week
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 12 (Reuters) - The Swedish coast guard said on Thursday it boarded ‚Äãand took control of a suspected ‚Äåstateless vessel in Swedish waters of the Baltic Sea, in the second such incident in ‚Äãless than a week.
The 228-meter-long (250 yards) ‚Äãtanker Sea Owl I was flying ‚ÅÝthe Comorian flag, and the coast guard ‚Äãsaid it suspected the ship was sailing ‚Äãunder a false flag.
The ship is also on the sanctions list of several countries, including the ‚ÄãEU.
"The threats to maritime safety and ‚Äãthe environment are too high," said Daniel Stenling, ‚Äådeputy ‚ÅÝchief of operations at the coast guard. "Therefore, there is reason to intervene against the vessel."
Sweden's minister for civil defence, Carl-Oskar ‚ÄãBohlin, said ‚Äãin a ‚ÅÝpost on X the ship was suspected of belonging to ‚Äãthe Russian shadow fleet.
The boarding took ‚Äãplace ‚ÅÝoff the coast of the southern Swedish town of Trelleborg.
On Friday, the coast guard boarded ‚ÅÝthe ‚Äãship Caffa, which was ‚Äãsailing under a Guinean flag, near Trelleborg.

item.title: Six French soldiers wounded in Iraq after drone attack, army says
item.description:
BAGHDAD/PARIS, March 12 (Reuters) - Six French soldiers providing counter-terrorism training in northern Iraq were wounded after a drone attack in the Erbil region of Iraq, ‚ÄãFrance's army said on Thursday, just hours after an Italian base was ‚Äåalso targeted in the area.
France has hundreds of troops based in the Erbil region as part of a broader international coalition to fight Islamic State ‚Äãmilitants in the area.
It was not immediately clear where the ‚Äãdrone had come from.
Iraqi Shi'ite militants have picked up ‚ÅÝthe pace of drone and missile attacks on U.S. interests in ‚ÄãIraq in the last three to four days, according to three Iraqi ‚Äãsecurity sources and two sources close to the groups.
"Today, six French soldiers providing counter-terrorism training to Iraqi partners were wounded after a drone attack in the Erbil ‚Äãregion of Iraq," the French army said in a statement, adding that ‚Äãthey had been taken to the nearest medical facility.
The statement did not provide ‚Äåfurther ‚ÅÝdetails on the nature of their injuries or the origins of the drone.
An officer from the regional Kurdish Peshmerga forces said the wounded soldiers were stationed at the base where coalition forces are based.
‚ÄãErbil Governor Omed Koshnaw ‚Äãsaid in ‚ÅÝa statement that the drone attack was in the Makhmour area.
An overnight airstrike that hit an Italian ‚Äãmilitary base in Iraqi Kurdistan was deliberate, the Italian ‚Äãdefence ministry ‚ÅÝsaid earlier on Thursday, targeting a facility hosting NATO personnel.
France is deploying about a dozen naval vessels, including its aircraft carrier strike group, ‚ÅÝto the ‚ÄãMediterranean, Red Sea and potentially the Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz as part of defensive support to allies threatened by the conflict in the ‚ÄãMiddle East.

item.title: Trump nominates State Dept official to lead diminished US global media agency
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump nominated a senior State Department official to take on the ‚Äåadditional role of leading the U.S. Agency for Global Media, the department said on Thursday, after a federal judge invalidated the previous Trump-appointed CEO's staff cuts at the agency.
Under Secretary of State for Public Diplomacy Sarah Rogers, who ‚Äãhas led the administration's campaign against European Internet regulations that Washington says constitute censorship , was named as ‚ÄãTrump's pick to take over the agency that oversees U.S.-funded media outlets like ‚ÅÝVoice of America that are aimed at populations in oppressive countries and reach hundreds of ‚Äãmillions of people.
"USAGM's mission has long been closely aligned with the Department of State, and the Under ‚ÄãSecretary for Public Diplomacy has always held consultative authorities with the agency," a State Department spokesperson said.
"If confirmed as the CEO of USAGM, Under Secretary Rogers would be uniquely well positioned to significantly strengthen coordination between U.S. ‚Äãinternational broadcasting and American public diplomacy in the national interest."
Rogers must be confirmed by the U.S. ‚ÄãSenate before she can assume the role.
The Department of Justice said in a court filing on Thursday that ‚ÄåDeputy Secretary ‚ÅÝof State Michael Rigas would perform the duties of the agency's CEO in the interim.
Kari Lake, who was appointed by Trump as a senior adviser to the agency, ushered in sweeping cuts to USAGM's staff and operations at outlets that also include Radio Free Asia and Radio Free Europe/Radio ‚ÄãLiberty that broadcast in 64 ‚Äãlanguages.
U.S. District Judge ‚ÅÝRoyce Lamberth on Saturday granted a summary judgment in favor of plaintiffs, including VOA journalists and a union representing federal employees. They had argued that Lake's ‚Äãappointment as acting CEO and actions she took in that role ran ‚Äãafoul of ‚ÅÝthe Federal Vacancies Reform Act and the Constitution's Appointments Clause, dealing another blow to the Trump administration's attempts to diminish various government agencies.
Lake, in a post on X, said what she called her efforts at "right-sizing" ‚ÅÝthe ‚Äãagency were "so effective over the last year that the Deep ‚ÄãState has done everything they can to stop us, including launching malicious lawsuits at me and the agency."

item.title: Venezuela signs deal with Repsol for gas production
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - Venezuela and Spanish energy group Repsol ‚Äãhave signed "strategic agreements," the government of the ‚ÄåSouth American country said in a statement on Thursday.
Speaking on state-run television, acting President Delcy Rodriguez said ‚Äãthe agreement will allow for gas ‚Äãproduction at Cardon IV, which is a ‚ÅÝ50-50 joint venture between Repsol and Italy's ‚ÄãEni.
She added that the deal would allow ‚Äãfor the expansion for exports.
"I am very pleased that this is being done hand in hand with two ‚ÄãEuropean companies that stayed in Venezuela, that ‚Äãbelieved in Venezuela and did not turn their backs ‚Äåon ‚ÅÝour people," Rodriguez said.
A government statement added that Venezuela is committed to seeking agreements with global investors in oil and gas.
After ‚Äãthe United ‚ÄãStates captured ‚ÅÝPresident Nicolas Maduro in January, it eased sanctions on Venezuela's energy ‚Äãsector, issuing general licences that allow ‚Äãglobal ‚ÅÝenergy companies to operate oil and gas projects in the OPEC country.
Venezuela holds one ‚ÅÝof ‚Äãthe largest oil reserves in ‚Äãthe world but has dilapidated energy infrastructure.

item.title: Both sides dig in, as Iran war approaches two-week mark
item.description:
DUBAI/BEIRUT, March 13 (Reuters) - The leaders of Iran, Israel and the United ‚ÄãStates all voiced defiance and vowed to fight on as the Middle East war approached the two-week mark on Friday, killing thousands of people, disrupting the lives of millions of others and ‚Äåshaking financial markets.
New Iranian Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei issued his first comments, read out by a television presenter on Thursday, vowing to keep the Strait of Hormuz shut and calling on neighboring countries to close U.S. bases on their territory or risk Iran targeting them.
"I assure everyone that we will not neglect avenging the blood of your martyrs," said the hardline cleric, who is close to Iran's top military force. It was not clear why he did not appear in person.
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu held his first news conference since the ‚ÄãU.S. and Israeli airstrikes on Iran started on February 28, taking questions via video-link and issuing a veiled threat to kill Khamenei and defending the military assault.
"I will not detail the actions we are taking. We ‚Äãare creating the optimal conditions for toppling the regime but I won't deny that I can't tell you with all certainty that the people of Iran will topple ‚ÅÝthe regime - a regime is toppled from the inside," Netanyahu said.
"But we can definitely help and we are helping."
The prospect that one of the most severe disruptions ever to global energy supplies could endure sent oil prices up about 9% to $100 ‚Äãa barrel on Thursday, helping drive down U.S. stocks.
The S&P 500 notched up its biggest three-day percentage drop in a month, and shares in Asia were also under pressure on Friday.
In an effort to stabilize global energy markets, the U.S. on ‚ÄãThursday issued a 30-day license for countries to buy Russian oil and petroleum products currently stranded at sea.
"The temporary increase in oil prices is a short-term and temporary disruption that will result in a massive benefit to our nation and economy in the long-term," Bessent said in the statement, echoing earlier comments from President Donald Trump.
Trump, who has already declared that the U.S. and Israel won the war, said the United States stood to make significant money from oil prices driven higher because of supply issues tied to ‚Äãthe closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of global oil normally passes.
"The United States is the largest Oil Producer in the World, by far, so when oil prices go up, we make a lot of ‚Äãmoney." Stopping Iran from having nuclear weapons was far more important, he said on social media.
Trump's comments angered opposition Democrats, who accused the Republican president of caring too little about the war's impact on average Americans and demanded more information about civilian casualties, particularly ‚Äåa strike that ‚ÅÝkilled dozens of children at an Iranian girls' school .
Trump's administration has not provided a public assessment of the expected cost or duration of the war, which is unpopular with the American public, or a strategy for Iran after the fighting stops. The president and top aides have also given conflicting reasons for starting to fight.
The death toll has risen to more than 2,000 people, most in Iran. Almost 700 have died in Lebanon, where Israel has targeted central Beirut and ordered residents out of a swathe of the south in an offensive against the Iran-backed Hezbollah group.
Drones have been reported flying into Kuwait, Iraq, the United Arab Emirates, Bahrain and Oman, undermining U.S. and Israeli claims to have knocked out much of Iran's stock ‚Äãof long-range weapons.
In Iraq, U.S. Central Command said it ‚Äãwas carrying out rescue efforts after one of its refueling ‚ÅÝaircraft went down in an incident that involved another aircraft but was not the result of hostile or friendly fire. The Islamic Resistance in Iraq, an umbrella group of Iran-backed armed factions, claimed responsibility for downing the aircraft.
France's President Emmanuel Macron said one soldier had died and several were wounded during an attack in northern Iraq, hours after an Italian ‚Äãbase was also targeted in the area.
Two tankers were set ablaze in the Iraqi port of Basra earlier this week after being hit by suspected Iranian explosive-laden boats and ‚Äãother ships have been struck in ‚ÅÝthe Gulf near the Strait of Hormuz.
Inside Iran, residents said security forces were increasing their presence to demonstrate continued control.
"Security forces are everywhere, more than before. People are afraid to come out, but supermarkets are open," teacher Majan, 35, said by phone from Tehran.
Israel and the United States have called on Iranians to rise up and topple their clerical rulers.
Many Iranians want change and some openly celebrated the elder supreme leader's death, after his forces had killed thousands of anti‚Äëgovernment protesters in ‚ÅÝJanuary. But there ‚Äãhas been no sign of organised dissent while the country is under attack.
Iran's message is that its strategy now is to impose prolonged ‚Äãeconomic shock to force Trump to back off. A spokesperson for Iran's military command said on Wednesday the world should prepare for oil prices of $200 a barrel.
U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright said on Thursday he did not expect that to happen, but did not totally rule it out. "I ‚Äãwould say unlikely, but we are focused on the military operation and solving a problem," Wright told CNN.

item.title: One-fifth of Australian teens still use TikTok, SnapChat after social media ban
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 13 (Reuters) - One-fifth of Australian teenagers under ‚Äã16 were still using social media two months after the country banned platforms from allowing ‚Äåminors, industry data showed, raising questions about the effectiveness of age-gating methods they are using.
The number of 13-to-15-year-olds using TikTok and SnapChat, among the most popular social media apps with Australian teenagers, fell from before the ban took effect in December to ‚ÄãFebruary, but still more than 20% used the apps, according to a report by parental control ‚Äãsoftware maker Qustodio provided to Reuters.
The data is among the first to show the ‚ÅÝeffects on youth online behaviour since Australia rolled out the ban which is being copied by governments around ‚Äãthe world. The Australian government and at least two university studies are tracking the ban's impact but none ‚Äãhas published data yet.
"Among children whose parents haven't blocked access, a meaningful number continue to use restricted platforms in the months following the ban," Qustodio said in the report, which was based on data collected from Australian families from late 2024 to ‚ÄãFebruary.
Under the ban, platforms including Meta's (META.O) , opens new tab Instagram, Facebook and Threads, Google's (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab YouTube, TikTok and Snapchat (SNAP.N) , opens new tab must block people ‚Äãaged under 16 or face a fine of up to A$49.5 million ($35 million).
The internet regulator, the eSafety Commissioner, has said ‚Äåit will ‚ÅÝgive platforms time to adapt and will only take enforcement action for systemic non-compliance.
The eSafety Commissioner and Communications Minister were not immediately available for comment.
A representative for Snapchat was not immediately available for comment. A TikTok spokesperson declined to comment.
The Qustodio data showed the number of Australians aged 13-15 using Snapchat tumbled 13.8 percentage points ‚Äãto 20.3% from November ‚Äãto February, while the number ‚ÅÝin that age group using TikTok fell 5.7 percentage points to 21.2%.
The number in that age group using YouTube dipped by one percentage point to 36.9%, although ‚Äãthe data did not specify whether the users were logged into accounts. The ‚ÄãAustralian ban allows ‚ÅÝpeople of all ages to use YouTube without logging in.
Australian teenage social media use typically dips in December and January due to the country's long summer school break, but the data showed a steeper decline than the previous ‚ÅÝyear, suggesting ‚Äãthe ban had an impact, Qustodio said.
But "some dips seen in December-January ‚Äãare slowly beginning to recover", the report added.
Fears that teenagers might migrate to unregulated platforms have not materialised, the data showed, although ‚ÄãWhatsApp recorded a small uptick in use among 13-15-year-olds.
($1 = 1.4122 Australian dollars)

item.title: South Korea to host French President Macron for April 2-3 state visit, Blue House says
item.description:
SEOUL, March 13 (Reuters) - South Korean President ‚ÄåLee Jae Myung will host French President Emmanuel Macron ‚Äãfor a state ‚Äãvisit to South Korea ‚ÅÝbetween April 2 to ‚Äã3, Lee's office said.
The ‚Äãtwo leaders will meet on April 3 for an ‚Äãofficial welcome ceremony, summit ‚Äãtalks, agreement signings and a ‚Äåstate ‚ÅÝluncheon, the presidential Blue House said in a statement.
Lee and Macron are ‚Äãexpected ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝdiscuss trade, investment and cooperation in ‚Äãareas such as ‚ÄãAI, ‚ÅÝquantum technology, space and nuclear energy, as well ‚ÅÝas ‚Äãregional and ‚Äãglobal issues, it said.

item.title: Cuba to release 51 prisoners in Vatican-brokered deal
item.description:
HAVANA, March 12 (Reuters) - Cuba said on ‚ÄãThursday it will release 51 prisoners in the coming days under an agreement with the Vatican, at ‚Äåa time the Communist government has come under increasing pressure from the United States to reform its one-party rule.
The prisoner release follows two weeks after Foreign Minister Bruno Rodriguez met with Pope Leo in the Vatican and at a time when Cuba faces a severe economic crisis, ‚Äãone aggravated by U.S. President Donald Trump's imposition of a virtual oil blockade on the Caribbean island.
"In the ‚Äãspirit of goodwill and the close and fluid relations between the Cuban state and the ‚ÅÝVatican, with which communication has historically been maintained regarding the review and release of prisoners, the Cuban government has decided ‚Äãto release 51 people sentenced to imprisonment in the coming days," the Foreign Ministry said in a statement.
"All have served a ‚Äãsignificant portion of their sentences and have maintained good conduct in prison," it said.
Cuba said it has granted pardons to 9,905 inmates since 2010 while granting early release to another 10,000 in the past three years.
In March 2025, it granted early release to 553 prisoners in ‚Äãanother Vatican-brokered deal.
But human rights groups say the Communist government is holding hundreds of political prisoners, with estimates varying. It ‚Äãwas unclear how many of the 51 prisoners subject to the latest release have been held on common crimes or charges related to ‚Äåpublic displays ‚ÅÝof dissent.
"This sovereign decision is a common practice in our criminal justice system and has characterized the humanitarian trajectory of the revolution, which this time coincides with the approach of the religious celebrations of Holy Week," Cuba said.
The Cuban government has always rejected any suggestion it makes decisions under U.S. pressure. The timing of Thursday's announcement happens to coincide with the ‚Äãmost intense pressure campaign applied ‚Äãby Washington in decades. On ‚ÅÝJan. 3, the U.S. captured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro, removing from power Cuba's most important foreign benefactor.
Trump in recent weeks had made a series of threatening statements, saying either that ‚ÄãCuba was on the verge of collapse or eager to make a deal with the ‚ÄãUnited States. On ‚ÅÝMonday he said Cuba may be subject to a "friendly takeover," then added, "it may not be a friendly takeover."
"Wouldn't really matter because they're really down to ... as they say, fumes. They have no energy, they have no money," Trump told reporters in Doral, Florida.
The ‚ÅÝCuban government ‚Äãhas denied that any official talks are underway with the United States, but ‚Äãhas yet to explicitly deny press reports that U.S. officials were in talks with Raul Guillermo Rodriguez Castro, the grandson of former Cuban President Raul ‚ÄãCastro, who is 94 and still wields great influence.

item.title: South Korea police raid transport ministry in widening Jeju Air crash probe
item.description:
SEOUL, March 13 (Reuters) - South Korean police investigating the 2024 Jeju Air crash said ‚Äãon Friday they had raided ‚Äåthe transport ministry as part of a widening probe into whether regulatory lapses ‚Äãcontributed to the disaster at Muan ‚ÄãInternational Airport.
The raid follows an ongoing ‚ÅÝreinvestigation of wreckage led by ‚Äãthe ministry and the Aviation and ‚ÄãRailway Accident Investigation Board, which has uncovered additional human remains and personal belongings more than a ‚Äãyear after the crash, prompting ‚Äãrenewed questions over the initial response.
Authorities have already ‚Äåsearched ‚ÅÝregional aviation offices and contractors involved in the airport's construction as part of a broader effort to determine ‚Äãresponsibility for ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝcrash, which killed 179 people.
A police official declined to ‚Äãcomment further on Friday's raid, ‚Äãbut ‚ÅÝYonhap News Agency cited an official as saying it was aimed at securing ‚ÅÝadditional ‚Äãmaterial needed for ‚Äãthe ongoing probe into the cause of the ‚Äãdisaster.

item.title: Cuban foreign minister speaks to Chinese, Russian counterparts
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - Cuban Foreign Minister Bruno Rodriguez spoke by ‚Äãtelephone to his counterparts from both China and ‚ÄåRussia on Thursday, three days after U.S. President Donald Trump said the Communist island was in " deep trouble ".
Trump, speaking to reporters on Monday, said ‚ÄãU.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio was dealing ‚Äãwith the issue which may or may not be ‚ÅÝa "friendly takeover".
Cuba has faced blackouts and fuel shortages amid ‚Äãan oil embargo imposed by the United States.
Russia's Foreign Ministry, ‚Äãin a statement on its website, said Minister Sergei Lavrov "confirmed Russia's principled position as regards the unacceptability of the U.S. exerting economic and ‚Äãpolitical pressure on Cuba."
The Russian statement said that in ‚Äãthe conversation, initiated by Cuba, "support was expressed for the fraternal Cuban people ‚Äåin ‚ÅÝdefending their state sovereignty and their right to choose their own path of development."
Earlier in the day, Russian Foreign Ministry spokeswoman Maria Zakharova condemned what she described as blackmail ‚Äãand threats ‚Äãby Trump against ‚ÅÝCuba, a traditional ally of Moscow.
Rodriguez met Russian President Vladimir Putin and Lavrov during a ‚Äãvisit to Moscow last month. Putin described the ‚Äãrestrictions ‚ÅÝplaced on the island as "unacceptable".
China's state-run Xinhua news agency said Rodriguez had requested the call with Foreign Minister Wang Yi.
Xinhua ‚ÅÝsaid ‚Äãthe two sides "agreed to continue to ‚Äãadvance the development of bilateral relations."

item.title: Iran-backed Iraqi group claims responsibility for downing US military aircraft
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - The Islamic Resistance ‚Äãin Iraq, ‚Äåan umbrella group of ‚ÄãIran-backed ‚Äãarmed factions, claimed ‚ÅÝresponsibility for ‚Äãdowning a U.S. ‚Äãmilitary refueling aircraft in western ‚ÄãIraq ‚Äãon Thursday.
The group said ‚Äåin ‚ÅÝa statement it had shot ‚Äãdown ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝKC-135 aircraft "in ‚Äãdefense of ‚Äãour ‚ÅÝcountry's sovereignty and ‚ÅÝairspace".

item.title: Michigan synagogue, Virginia university targeted in unnerving day of US violence
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - A convicted Islamic State supporter opened fire in a Virginia university classroom, killing one person ‚Äãand injuring two others before he was killed, while in Michigan a Lebanon-born U.S. citizen crashed his truck into a synagogue and its preschool and was shot dead.
The back-to-back outbursts of ‚Äåviolence added to rising concerns about the possibility of attacks on U.S. soil amid the tension since U.S. and Israeli forces launched airstrikes on Iran , and Iran responded with its own strikes on Israel and Gulf states with U.S. bases, sparking an intensifying war across the region.
Jewish communities, already facing a surge in antisemitic incidents, have been especially on edge, as have Muslim communities.
In the suburban Detroit township of West Bloomfield, authorities credited quick action by well-trained synagogue security personnel for preventing any loss of ‚Äãlife at Temple Israel and its daycare center other than that of the attacker.
One security officer was struck by the man's vehicle and knocked briefly unconscious, Oakland County Sheriff Michael Bouchard told ‚Äãreporters. All 140 children were safely evacuated from the preschool and accounted for, the synagogue said.
But 30 law enforcement officers were taken to hospital ‚ÅÝsuffering from inhalation of smoke that filled the synagogue from a fire of undetermined origin that erupted when the suspect's truck plowed into the building, Bouchard said.
"What happens around the world sometimes affects us, so we ‚Äãhave to prepare for it," Bouchard said.
On Monday night, the U.S. Department of Homeland Security identified the truck's driver as Ayman Ghazali, a 41-year-old man who was born in Lebanon and became a U.S. citizen in ‚Äã2016.
Ghazali had been traumatized when an Israeli airstrike on his family's village in Lebanon about 10 days ago killed two of Ghazali's brothers and two of their children, CBS News reported as law enforcement investigated Ghazali's motive.
The FBI said it was leading the investigation of what special agent Jennifer Runyan called a "targeted act of violence against the Jewish community."
The man crashed his truck through the doors of the synagogue and drove the vehicle down a hallway before a security officer fired ‚Äãat him, according to Bouchard.
The attacker was killed during the confrontation. Authorities said it was not immediately clear whether he took his own life or was shot dead by security personnel.
Separately, the FBI opened a ‚Äãterrorism investigation into an earlier Thursday shooting at Old Dominion University in Norfolk, Virginia, that killed one person and injured two others, both U.S. Army personnel, officials said.
All three victims were affiliated with the university, which according to its ‚Äåwebsite has close ‚ÅÝties with the military as well as a student body of which nearly a third are service members.
The shooter in that incident also was killed. He died at the hands of a group of Reserve Officer Training Corps students who subdued him, FBI special agent Dominique Evans told reporters hours later.
She said the suspect shouted ‚ÄúAllahu Akbar‚Äù ‚Äì an Arabic phrase meaning ‚ÄúGod is great‚Äù ‚Äì before he opened fire.
FBI officials identified the gunman as Mohamed Jalloh, who pleaded guilty in 2016 to federal charges of providing material support to Islamic State, the Syria- and Iraq-based Islamist militant group and long-time foe of Iran, and was released from prison in 2024.
Antisemitic incidents have ‚Äãspiked in recent years in the U.S., with anti-Jewish ‚Äãincidents accounting for nearly two-thirds of 5,300-plus religiously ‚ÅÝmotivated hate crimes since February 2024, according to FBI data.
‚ÄúAntisemitism has no place in Michigan and cannot be tolerated," Michigan's attorney general Dana Nessel said in a statement. "In moments like these, it‚Äôs more important than ever that we come together, stand with our neighbors, confront hate whenever it appears, and build stronger communities."
U.S. President ‚ÄãDonald Trump on Thursday said he had been briefed on the Michigan attack, calling it terrible.
"I want to send our love to the Michigan Jewish ‚Äãcommunity and all of the people ‚ÅÝin the Detroit area following the attack on the Jewish synagogue earlier today," he said.
Jewish and Islamic organizations throughout the United States have been operating under heightened security measures since the U.S. and Israel began their attacks on Iran on February 28.
"We've been talking for two weeks about the potential, sadly, of this happening. So there was no lack of preparation," Bouchard said on CNN. "All Jewish facilities in the area are going to have a lot ‚ÅÝof extra presence ‚Äãaround it until we figure this out."
The U.S. as a whole has been on edge since the start of the war, ‚Äãwith security scares at airports in Kansas City and the Washington suburbs, and the arrest of two men accused of igniting homemade bombs at a chaotic anti-Islam protest outside Gracie Mansion in New York City. Police said the two men in the Gracie Mansion incident ‚Äãtold them they were inspired by Islamic State.

item.title: Recent acts of Jewish-targeted violence in the United States
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - Rights advocates note a rise in anti-Muslim , anti-Palestinian and antisemitic hate in the United States since the start of U.S. ally Israel's war in Gaza following an October 7, 2023, attack by Palestinian ‚ÄãHamas militants.
The United States and Israel also launched a war against Iran on February 28.
Jewish ‚Äåadvocacy group the Anti-Defamation League tallied 9,354 antisemitic incidents in the U.S. in 2024 a 5% increase over 2023 and a record high since it began keeping track in 1979. The group said the ‚Äãfigure represented a 344% increase over the past five years and a 893% ‚Äãincrease over the last decade.
Following are some U.S. attacks that occurred before a ‚ÅÝsuspect crashed his truck into the hallway of a Detroit-area synagogue where children were attending ‚Äãpreschool on Thursday and was shot dead by security personnel, the only fatality in what ‚Äãthe FBI called "a targeted act of violence against the Jewish community."
A car crashed into the entrance of the headquarters of a Jewish religious order in New York City. No injuries were reported.
Police are investigating ‚Äãthe incident as a hate crime.
Two Israeli diplomats were shot and killed outside an ‚Äãevent hosted by the American Jewish Committee in Washington, D.C.
The gunman, who was charged with terrorism and ‚Äåhate ‚ÅÝcrimes, is believed to have been motivated by the Israel-Gaza conflict.
He told police on the scene, "I did it for Palestine, I did it for Gaza," according to the charging documents. Witnesses recounted hearing him chant, "Free Palestine" after he was taken into custody.
Authorities in Florida ‚Äãlaunched a hate crime investigation ‚Äãafter a man ‚ÅÝopened fire on two men he thought were Palestinians but turned out to be Israeli visitors.
The victims survived. One was shot in the ‚Äãshoulder and the other in the forearm.
Masked men attacked two ‚ÄãJewish students ‚ÅÝwho were demonstrating in support of Israel at Chicago's DePaul University, resulting in minor injuries.
The suspect, who was facing hate crime charges, later pleaded guilty to battery and causing bodily harm.
A ‚Äã39-year-old Jewish man wearing a religious skullcap was shot ‚Äãand wounded as he walked to synagogue in Chicago, Illinois.
The suspect, who was apprehended 30 minutes after the attack, ‚Äãwas charged , opens new tab with attempted murder, among other crimes.

item.title: US issues 30-day sanctions waiver for purchase of Russian oil at sea
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - The United States issued a 30-day waiver for ‚Äãcountries to buy sanctioned Russian oil and petroleum products currently stranded at sea, in what Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said was a step to stabilize ‚Äåglobal energy markets roiled by the Iran war.
Oil prices eased on Friday morning in Asia after the U.S. waiver announcement.
The move was the latest attempt by President Donald Trump's administration to tame energy prices after the U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran and the subsequent response by Tehran widened regional tensions and paralyzed shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, disrupting vital Middle East oil and gas flows and sending energy prices higher.
On Wednesday, Washington ‚Äãannounced it would be releasing 172 million barrels of oil from the strategic petroleum reserve in an effort to curb sky-rocketing oil prices in the wake of the ‚Äãwar in Iran. That release was part of a broader commitment by the 32-nation International Energy Agency to release 400 million barrels of ‚ÅÝoil.
The IEA said earlier on Thursday that he war in the Middle East was creating the biggest oil supply disruption in history.
The license issued by Washington on Thursday authorizes the ‚Äãdelivery and sale of Russian crude oil and petroleum products loaded on vessels as of March 12 and valid through midnight Washington time on April 11, according to the text of the ‚Äãlicense posted on the Treasury Department's website.
The move reflects White House worries that the surge in oil prices after nearly two weeks of U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran will hurt U.S. businesses and consumers ahead of the November midterm elections, when Trump's fellow Republicans hope to retain control of Congress.
Bessent, in a statement on X released hours after benchmark oil prices shot above $100 a barrel , said the measure was "narrowly tailored" ‚Äãand "short-term" and would not provide significant financial benefit to the Russian government.
"The temporary increase in oil prices is a short-term and temporary disruption that will result in a massive benefit ‚Äãto our nation and economy in the long-term," Bessent said in the statement, echoing Trump.
There were about 124 million barrels of Russian-origin oil on water across 30 different locations globally as of Thursday, ‚ÄåFox News reported, ‚ÅÝadding that the U.S. license would provide around five to six days of supply when taking into account the daily loss of oil from the Strait.
Even as the sanctions reprieve was expected to boost world supplies of oil, it could also complicate the West's efforts to deprive Russia of revenue for its war in Ukraine and put Washington at odds with its allies.
European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen, after participating in a call with G7 leaders on Wednesday to discuss the impact of the Iran war on oil and gas markets, ‚Äãsaid now was not the time to relax ‚Äãsanctions against Russia.
Also on Thursday, Russian presidential ‚ÅÝenvoy Kirill Dmitriev said he had discussed the current energy crisis with a U.S. delegation that included Trump's special envoy Steve Witkoff and Trump's son-in-law Jared Kushner at a meeting in Florida.
The U.S. Treasury previously issued a 30‚Äëday waiver on March 5 specifically for India, allowing ‚ÄãNew Delhi to buy Russian oil stuck at sea.
Trump has also ordered the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation to provide political risk insurance ‚Äãand financial guarantees for ‚ÅÝmaritime trade in the Gulf and said the U.S. Navy could escort ships in the region.
In another attempt to control prices, the Trump administration is considering temporarily waiving a shipping rule known as the Jones Act to ensure energy and agricultural products can move freely between U.S. ports, the White House said. Waiving the rule would allow foreign ships to carry fuel between U.S. ports, potentially ‚ÅÝlowering costs ‚Äãand speeding deliveries.
"The president is taking every action he can to lower prices ... and you're going to see ‚Äãmore and more in the days to come," White House Deputy Chief of Staff Stephen Miller told Fox News' "Primetime" program on Thursday.
Trump said earlier on Thursday the United States stood to make significant money from oil prices driven higher ‚Äãby the war, prompting criticism from some lawmakers who accused him of caring only about rich people.

item.title: Magnitude 5.5 earthquake hits central Turkey, EMSC says
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - An earthquake ‚Äãmeasuring ‚Äå5.5 magnitude ‚Äãstruck central ‚ÄãTurkey on Friday, the ‚ÅÝEuropean ‚ÄãMediterranean ‚ÄãSeismological Centre said.
The ‚Äãquake ‚Äãhit at a ‚Äådepth ‚ÅÝof 35 km (21.75 ‚Äãmiles), ‚Äãaccording ‚ÅÝto ‚ÄãEMSC.

item.title: Trump says he thinks Iran's new supreme leader is alive but 'damaged'
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said that he thinks new Iranian Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, whose father, the former supreme leader, was killed ‚Äãon the first day of the U.S. and Israel's war ‚Äãon Iran , is alive but "damaged."
Khamenei has not been seen ‚ÅÝby Iranians since his selection on Sunday by a clerical ‚Äãassembly, and his first comments were read out by a television ‚Äãpresenter on Thursday.
An Iranian official told Reuters on Wednesday that the newly appointed supreme leader was lightly injured but was continuing to operate, after ‚Äãstate television described him as war-wounded .
"I think he probably is (alive). I ‚Äãthink he is damaged, but I think he's probably alive in some form, ‚Äåyou ‚ÅÝknow," Trump said in an interview on Fox News' "The Brian Kilmeade Show." His remarks were published by Fox News late on Thursday.
In Khamenei's first comments, he vowed to keep the Strait of ‚ÄãHormuz shut and ‚Äãcalled on ‚ÅÝneighboring countries to close U.S. bases on their territory or risk Iran targeting them.
The U.S. and ‚ÄãIsrael began attacks on Iran on February 28. ‚ÄãIran ‚ÅÝhas responded with its own strikes on Israel and Gulf countries with U.S. bases.
As the war approached the two-week mark, having ‚ÅÝkilled thousands ‚Äãand shaken financial markets, the leaders ‚Äãof Iran, Israel and the United States all voiced defiance and have vowed to ‚Äãfight on.

item.title: Exclusive: New US weapons for Taiwan could be approved after Trump's China trip, sources say
item.description:
WASHINGTON/TAIPEI, March 13 (Reuters) - A major U.S. arms package for Taiwan that includes advanced interceptor missiles is ready for President Donald Trump 's approval and ‚Äãcould be signed after his trip to China this month, sources briefed on the discussions said.
With a price tag of about $14 billion, the arms deal ‚Äåwould be the largest ever for the democratically governed island, which faces steadily rising military pressure from China.
Sources familiar with the administration's thinking told Reuters the deal is being held under wraps ahead of Trump's planned March 31 to April 2 trip to Beijing to meet President Xi Jinping, but could be announced after he returns.
Xi told Trump in February that arms sales to Taiwan, which Beijing claims as its ‚Äãown territory, must be handled with "prudence."
Trump's moves to secure a favorable trade deal with China have raised concerns among some China watchers that he could curb ‚ÄãU.S. military support for Taiwan. But the queued arms approval suggests that the administration plans to maintain or even increase support.
That in ‚ÅÝturn would ease anxiety in Taipei.
The package largely consists of PAC-3 and NASAMS air defense missiles, one of the sources said.
"As soon as the president gives the thumbs up, those ‚Äãare ready to be officially announced. Everything is done," said that person, who like the others spoke on condition of anonymity given the sensitivity of the matter.
Another $6 billion in "asymmetric" defense ‚Äãcapabilities was also awaiting approval and could be announced in linked or subsequent packages, the source added. The source declined to specify what those capabilities are.
Trump's National Security Strategy issued late last year states that deterring conflict over Taiwan is a priority for Washington, "ideally by preserving military overmatch."
But Trump's comments, including a February remark to journalists that he was "talking" to Xi about Taiwan arms sales, have stirred speculation ‚Äãthat he could scale back the long-standing U.S. practice of providing Taiwan with the means to defend itself, which is written in to U.S. law.
Nonetheless, Trump's arms sales approvals ‚Äãto the island in his second term have already surpassed the amount approved by his Democratic predecessor Joe Biden over four years, including an $11 billion package in December that greenlighted various missiles, drones, artillery ‚Äåand aircraft ‚ÅÝparts.
Analysts say U.S. administrations going back decades have timed weapons approvals for Taiwan around sensitive political engagement with Beijing.
China's foreign ministry said in a statement to Reuters that the government's "opposition to U.S. arms sales to China's Taiwan region is consistent and unequivocal."
China, which considers Taiwan its own territory , regularly demands that the U.S. stop selling it weapons.
In response to questions to the White House, a senior U.S. official confirmed more weapons approvals were in the works.
"Arms sales are working their way through the process. There is no change to our policy with ‚Äãrespect to Taiwan," the official said.
Two Taiwan sources familiar with the ‚ÅÝmatter said that Washington has repeatedly assured them of its support.
But the upcoming summit has been a source of uncertainty.
Beijing was seeking to delay U.S. arms sales to Taiwan and has been trying to create a better atmosphere ahead of the summit, including by toning down ‚Äãmilitary pressure on the island, another Taiwan source familiar with the matter said.
"We have confidence in Trump, though. Beijing has underestimated ‚Äãthe strategic vision of ‚ÅÝthe White House," the source said.
Taiwan's defense ministry told Reuters that its arms procurement projects have completed "preliminary coordination" with the U.S. side, and the U.S. Defense Security Cooperation Agency has provided information on items to be purchased and delivery schedules.
Taiwan's defense ministry said in January that an arms deal with the U.S. covering four items was in the offing, but said it could not give ‚ÅÝdetails before ‚Äãthey were provided to the U.S. Congress, the usual process for approval of such sales.
The U.S. State Department ‚Äãhas said it supports increased defense spending by the island. Raymond Greene, Washington's top diplomat in Taiwan, said in January that the U.S. was "fully committed to delivering critical systems as quickly as possible."

item.title: Nepal ex-rapper's party wins election in landslide after Gen Z protests
item.description:
KATHMANDU, March 13 (Reuters) - A three-year-old party won Nepal's general elections by a landslide, authorities said, positioning its candidate Balendra Shah to become the next prime minister, with a ‚Äãmandate for the rapper-turned-politician to restore political stability.
The March 5 election was the ‚ÄåHimalayan nation's first vote since demonstrations against corruption last September led by Gen Z protesters that killed 77 people and toppled the government.
"If everything goes well, we can expect that it can give a stable ‚Äãgovernment for five years," said constitutional expert Purna Man Shakya, referring to splits over ‚Äãdividing up the spoils of office that doomed prior majority governments.
Shah‚Äôs Rastriya ‚ÅÝSwatantra Party (RSP) won 182 seats in the 275-member parliament, the Election Commission said on Thursday, ‚Äãthe largest majority of any party in more than six decades.
That holds out hope for ‚Äãstability in a nation that has seen 32 changes of government in the last 35 years, battering investors' confidence while crippling economic and jobs growth.
"We are encouraged by the victory," said newly-elected lawmaker Sisir Khanal, a ‚Äãsenior leader of the winning RSP. "The mandate has made us very responsible."
The election relegated the ‚Äãoldest party, the Nepali Congress, to distant second place with just 38 seats, while the Communist Party ‚Äåof Nepal (Unified ‚ÅÝMarxist-Leninist) of former Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli won only 25.
Former Chief Justice Sushila Karki succeeded Oli as the interim prime minister tasked with holding the election.
The election has been dominated by Shah , the former mayor of Kathmandu, the capital, whose rap music critical of the establishment ‚Äãgained him near-rockstar-like fame ‚Äãon social media.
He ‚ÅÝis the first politician expected to become prime minister who hails from the southern plains, known as Madhesh, where smaller regional groups failed ‚Äãto win a single seat.
His RSP canvassed on a programme to fight graft, ‚Äãcreate jobs ‚ÅÝand more than double the $42 billion-economy in five years.
But its firebrand leader Ravi Lamichhane, a former television host, faces charges of misusing the funds of small saving companies. He denies the accusations ‚ÅÝand has ‚Äãbeen freed on bail.
Last year's youth-led uprising in the ‚Äãnation of 30 million nestled between China and India followed a social media ban that drew thousands into the ‚Äãstreets, triggering clashes and deaths that forced Oli's resignation.

item.title: Taiwan parliament authorises government to sign stalled US arms deals
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 13 (Reuters) - Taiwan's parliament on Friday authorised the government to sign U.S. agreements for four arms sales packages, after officials warned that Taipei would ‚Äãgo to the back of the line if it missed ‚Äåthe deadline.
The back and forth on Taiwan's defence spending has provoked concern in the United States, as it is the most important international backer and arms supplier of the ‚ÄãChinese-claimed island, despite a lack of formal diplomatic ties.
President Lai Ching-te's ‚Äãgovernment has tried to get parliament to pass $40 billion in ‚ÅÝextra defence spending but the opposition, which controls the most seats, says ‚Äãthe proposals are unclear, and it cannot be expected to pass "blank cheques" despite ‚Äãsupporting defence.
Both opposition parties have come up with their own, less expensive proposals, but the defence ministry has said the letters of offer and acceptance for the weapons ‚Äãwith the United States have to be signed or Taiwan would lose ‚Äãits place in the production and delivery queue.
After lawmakers from both sides agreed on Thursday ‚Äåthat ‚ÅÝthe government could still sign the agreements in advance, even if the reviews of the spending proposals are not approved in time, parliament formally gave its legal authorisation.
The authorisation was passed unanimously and announced by parliament speaker ‚ÄãHan Kuo-yu.
The weapons ‚Äãto be signed ‚ÅÝfor include TOW anti-tank missiles, M109A7 self-propelled howitzers, Lockheed Martin-made (LMT.N) , opens new tab Javelin missiles and the HIMARS multiple launch rocket system.
On ‚ÄãTuesday, Defence Minister Wellington Koo told reporters the letters of ‚Äãoffer and ‚ÅÝacceptance for 82 HIMARS systems the U.S. announced as part of an $11-billion arms sale package for Taiwan would expire on March 26.
Sunday is the deadline to ‚ÅÝsign ‚Äãfor the other weapons systems, the ministry ‚Äãsays.
The Trump administration has pressed allies to increase defence spending, a plank Lai and his government ‚Äãhave enthusiastically embraced.

item.title: Pakistan bombs airline fuel depot near Kandahar airport, Afghan Taliban says
item.description:
KABUL, March 13 (Reuters) - Pakistan has bombed the fuel depot of private airline Kam Air ‚Äãnear Afghanistan's Kandahar airport, the Taliban said on Friday, as ‚Äåthe worst conflict in years between the South Asian neighbours flared again despite efforts to calm tensions.
The fighting erupted last month with Pakistani air strikes inside Afghanistan that ‚ÄãIslamabad said targeted militant strongholds. Afghanistan called the strikes a ‚Äãviolation of sovereignty as it launched retaliatory attacks.
"The company (Kam ‚ÅÝAir) supplies fuel to civilian airlines as well as to United ‚ÄãNations aircraft," Taliban spokesperson Zabihullah Mujahid said.
Pakistan also carried out bombings ‚Äãin other areas, including the capital Kabul, with women and children among those killed as civilian homes were targeted in some locations, he said, adding that ‚Äãthe aggression would "not go unanswered".
Before the latest attack, neither side ‚Äãhad reported any Pakistani air strikes on Afghanistan in recent days and ground fighting ‚Äåalong ‚ÅÝthe 2,600 km (1,600 mile) border had also tapered off.
Pakistan's military did not respond to a request for comment.
Militancy has been a bone of contention between allies-turned-foes Pakistan and Afghanistan, with Islamabad saying Kabul ‚Äãprovides safe haven ‚Äãto militants ‚ÅÝexecuting attacks on Pakistan.
The Taliban, however, denies the allegation and says militancy is Pakistan's internal problem.
Reuters had ‚Äãreported on Thursday that mediation efforts by China , which had ‚Äãbeen ‚ÅÝurging an end to the violence, had helped ease the fighting between the countries.
Pakistan's foreign ministry spokesperson Tahir Andrabi had also said that ‚ÅÝIslamabad and ‚ÄãBeijing were engaged in a "dialogue process" ‚Äãon Afghanistan.

item.title: South Korea says considering energy vouchers, boosting coal and nuclear power
item.description:
SEOUL, March 13 (Reuters) - South Korea is considering providing additional energy vouchers to subsidise vulnerable households if rising global fuel prices in the wake ‚Äãof the Middle East crisis push up electricity costs, the ‚Äågovernment said on Friday.
Asia's fourth-largest economy is also preparing to boost nuclear and coal-fired power generation in the event that oil prices remain high and liquefied natural gas (LNG) supplies ‚Äãare disrupted.
"The government will take all necessary measures to minimise the ‚Äãburden on the people, such as stabilising energy supply ‚ÅÝand demand, price management, and supporting people vulnerable (to rising energy costs)," ‚ÄãIndustry Minister Kim Jung-kwan said on Friday.
South Korea relies almost totally on ‚Äãimports for its energy, buying about 70% of its oil and 20% of its LNG from the Middle East, according to Korea International Trade Association data.
To reduce ‚Äãdependence on LNG, the Climate and Energy Ministry said this week ‚Äãit would accelerate the restart of nuclear reactors under maintenance, with two units expected ‚Äåto ‚ÅÝcome back online as soon as March and four more by mid-May.
It also said coal-fired power output could be flexibly increased during periods when the impact on air quality from fine dust was low, if LNG supply ‚Äãshortfalls materialised.
The government ‚Äãis considering expanding ‚ÅÝthe current budget for energy vouchers for low-income and other vulnerable groups in a supplementary budget currently being ‚Äãdrafted, a government official told Reuters.
South Korea already has ‚Äãin place ‚ÅÝlast year's energy voucher budget of about 500 billion won ($335.93 million), valid through May, and another roughly 500 billion won in this year's energy ‚ÅÝvoucher ‚Äãbudget.
The government will monitor the funds and consider ‚Äãadditional support, the official said.
South Korea began capping domestic fuel prices on Friday to combat the ‚Äãrise in energy costs.
($1 = 1,488.4000 won)

item.title: Saudi Aramco offers 2 mln bbls Arab Light crude for March loading at Yanbu port, traders say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 13 (Reuters) - Saudi Aramco offered ‚Äã2 million ‚Äåbarrels of its flagship Arab ‚ÄãLight crude ‚Äãfor late March loading ‚ÅÝat the ‚ÄãRed Sea ‚Äãport of Yanbu, traders said, its ‚Äãfourth oil ‚Äãtender since the U.S.-Iran ‚Äåconflict ‚ÅÝdisrupted exports from the Middle East.
The tender ‚Äãwill ‚Äãclose ‚ÅÝat 5 pm Tokyo ‚Äãtime (1000 ‚ÄãGMT) ‚ÅÝon Friday.

item.title: Exclusive: With Iran war exit elusive, Trump aides vie to affect outcome
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - A complex tug-of-war inside the White House is driving U.S. President Donald Trump's shifting public statements on the course of the Iran war, as aides debate when and how to declare victory even as the conflict spreads across the Middle East.
Some officials and advisers are warning Trump that surging gasoline prices could exact a political cost from the U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran, while some hawks are pressing the president to maintain the offensive against the Islamic Republic, according to interviews with a Trump ‚Äãadviser and others close to the deliberations.
Their observations to Reuters offer a previously unreported glimpse inside White House decision-making as it adjusts its approach to the biggest U.S. military operation since the 2003 Iraq war.
The behind-the-scenes maneuvering underscores the high stakes Trump, who ‚Äåreturned to office last year promising to avoid ‚Äústupid‚Äù military interventions, faces nearly two weeks after plunging the nation into a war that has rattled global financial markets and disrupted the international oil trade.
The jockeying for Trump's ear is a feature of his presidency, but this time the consequences are a matter of war and peace in one of the world's most volatile and economically critical regions.
Shifting from the sweeping goals he framed in launching the war on February 28, Trump in recent days has emphasized that he views the conflict as a limited campaign whose objectives have mostly been met.
But the message remains unclear to many, including the energy markets, which have lurched in both directions in response to Trump's statements.
He told a campaign-style rally in Kentucky on Wednesday that ‚Äúwe won" the war, then abruptly pivoted: "We ‚Äãdon't want to leave early, do we? We've got to finish the job.‚Äù
Economic advisers and officials, including from the Treasury Department and the National Economic Council, have warned Trump that an oil shock and rising gasoline prices could quickly erode domestic support for the war, said the adviser and two others ‚Äãclose to the deliberations, speaking on the condition of anonymity to disclose internal discussions.
Political advisers, including Chief of Staff Susie Wiles and deputy chief James Blair, are making similar arguments, focusing on the political fallout from higher gas prices and urging Trump ‚ÅÝto define victory narrowly and signal the operation is limited and nearly finished, the sources said.
Pushing in the other direction are hawkish voices urging Trump to sustain military pressure on Iran, including Republican lawmakers such as U.S. Senators Lindsey Graham and Tom Cotton, and media commentators such as Mark Levin, according to people familiar with the ‚Äãmatter.
They argue the U.S. must prevent Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon and respond forcefully to attacks on American troops and shipping.
A third force comes from Trump‚Äôs populist base and figures such as strategist Steve Bannon and right-wing television personality Tucker Carlson, who have been pressing him and his top aides to avoid getting dragged into another prolonged ‚ÄãMiddle East conflict.
‚ÄúHe is allowing the hawks to believe the campaign continues, wants markets to believe the war might end soon and his base to believe escalation will be limited,‚Äù the Trump adviser said.
Asked for comment, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said in a statement: "This story is based on gossip and speculation from anonymous sources who aren‚Äôt even in the room for any discussions with President Trump.
"The President is known for being a good listener and seeking the opinions of many people, but ultimately everyone knows he‚Äôs the final decision maker and his own best messenger," she said. "The President‚Äôs entire team is focused on ensuring the objectives of Operation Epic Fury are fully achieved."
Other people named for their roles in the deliberations did not immediately respond to Reuters' questions.
In taking America to war, Trump offered little explanation, and the administration's stated war aims have ranged from thwarting an imminent attack by Iran to crippling its nuclear program to replacing its government.
As he seeks an exit from an unpopular conflict, Trump is trying to juggle competing narratives that some critics say have complicated an already difficult situation, with ‚ÄãIran defiant despite the devastating U.S.-Israeli air assault.
Top political aides and economic advisers, whose warnings before the war of the potential economic shock were largely ignored, appear to have played a major role in pushing Trump‚Äôs efforts this week to reassure skittish markets and contain rising oil and gas prices.
His public shift to downplaying the war's impact, describing it as a "short-term excursion," ‚Äåand his insistence that ‚ÅÝgas price hikes would be short-lived appeared aimed at calming fears of an open-ended conflict.
Some top aides have advised him to work toward a conclusion to the conflict that he can call a triumph, at least militarily, the sources said, even if much of the Iranian leadership survives, along with remnants of a nuclear program that the campaign was meant to target.
Wave after wave of U.S. and Israeli air strikes have killed a number of top Iranian leaders among some 2,000 people overall - some as far away as Lebanon - devastated its ballistic missile arsenal, sunk much of its navy and degraded its ability to support armed proxies around the Middle East.
But the military achievements have been seriously undercut by Iran‚Äôs stepped-up attacks on oil tankers and transport facilities in the Gulf, driving up oil prices.
Trump has said he will decide when to end the campaign. He and his aides say they are far ahead of the four- to six-week timeframe Trump initially announced.
The shifting reasons for launching the conflict, which has spilled over into more than half ‚Äãa dozen other countries, have only made it more difficult to predict what ‚Äãcomes next.
For their part, Iran's rulers will claim victory, analysts say, for simply ‚ÅÝsurviving the U.S.-Israeli onslaught, especially after demonstrating their ability to fight back and inflict damage on Israel, the U.S. and its allies.
Critical to the war's final trajectory will be the Strait of Hormuz. A fifth of the world's oil shipments, which normally traverses the narrow waterway, has come to a near-standstill. Iran in recent days has struck tankers in Iraqi waters and other ships near the strait, and the new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei has vowed to keep it shut.
If Iran's stranglehold on the waterway ‚Äãpushes U.S. gas prices high enough, that could increase political pressure on Trump to end the military campaign to help his Republican Party, which is defending narrow majorities in Congress in November's midterm elections.
Trump has recently refrained from pushing the ‚Äãidea that the war seeks to topple the ‚ÅÝgovernment in Tehran. U.S. intelligence indicates that Iran's leadership is not at risk of collapse anytime soon, Reuters reported on Wednesday.
At least some of the confusion over the war's trajectory appears rooted in the quick U.S. military success in Venezuela.
Since the start of the war, some aides have struggled to convince Trump that the Iran campaign was unlikely to unfold in the same way as the January 3 Venezuela raid that captured President Nicolas Maduro, according to another source familiar with the administration‚Äôs thinking.
That operation opened the way for Trump to coerce former Maduro loyalists into giving him considerable sway over the country's vast oil reserves ‚Äì without requiring extended U.S. military action.
Iran, by contrast, has proved a much tougher, better-armed foe with an entrenched ‚ÅÝclerical and security establishment.
Experts have ‚Äãrejected claims by Trump aides that Iran had been within weeks of being able to produce a nuclear weapon, despite the president‚Äôs insistence in June that U.S.-Israeli bombing had ‚Äúobliterated‚Äù its nuclear program.
Most of ‚ÄãIran‚Äôs stockpile of highly enriched uranium is believed to have been buried by the June strikes, meaning the material potentially could be retrieved and purified to bomb grade. Iran has always denied seeking nuclear weapons.
If the war drags on, American casualties mount and the economic costs multiply, some analysts say it could erode backing from Trump's political base. But despite criticism from some supporters opposed to military interventions, members of his "Make America ‚ÄãGreat Again" movement have so far largely stayed with him on Iran.
‚ÄúThe MAGA base is going to give the president wiggle room,‚Äù said Republican strategist Ford O'Connell.

item.title: Iran war shows up folly of slow energy transition
item.description:
MELBOURNE, March 13 (Reuters Breakingviews) - If only there was a way to reduce the fat tail risks generated by the Strait of Hormuz. The effective closure of the roughly 100-mile-long passage due to the U.S.-Israel attacks on ‚ÄãIran has sent fuel prices soaring and prompted countries across Asia to restrict supplies to consumers or halt their exports - or ‚Äåboth. There is an alternative: electrification. But the slow pace of the energy transition has kept countries in thrall to gasoline. Australia's lack of progress is particularly embarrassing.
Governments have known about the economic and societal impacts of climate change for decades, and 195 of them signed the Paris Agreement in 2015 pledging to speed up adoption of low-carbon ‚Äãresources. Almost all are woefully short of their targets.
