On paper, the island Down Under ought to be leading the charge into renewables. It ‚Äãhas plenty of sun and wind to harness. And rising temperatures aren't the only reason to act. Australia's vehicle ‚ÅÝfleet is incredibly energy insecure: imports account for 90% of all petrol and diesel burned, much of it coming from the Middle East. That ‚Äãin turn impacts food security: while the country grows what it needs, produce is transported by diesel-chugging trucks.
The lines of defence are pretty weak: Australia has ‚Äãjust over 30 days' average imported supply of oil, Energy Minister Chris Bowen said last week. While the highest in more than a decade, it's far short of the 90-day requirement set by the International Energy Agency, whose end-2025 data put the country's tally at 49 days, the lowest of the OECD, a club of mostly rich countries. ‚ÄãThis week Bowen pledged to release around 20% of reserves , opens new tab and relaxed limits on sulphur , opens new tab content to get more fuel to market.
Unsurprisingly, even though cargo ships ‚Äãare still delivering fuel, the war has sparked a run on the pumps as drivers fear running out or having to pay more. That has pushed up prices and ‚Äåcaused ‚ÅÝshortages, especially in regional agricultural areas. Encouraging faster adoption of electric vehicles like those sold by BYD (002594.SZ) , opens new tab and Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab would have reduced this pain. Yet fewer than 10% of new cars sold Down Under are purely battery powered. China's oil demand growth has slowed in the past couple of years in large part because almost half of new cars and, as of last year, 20% of trucks are electric.
The transition is an inherently slow process at the ‚Äãbest of times. Electric vehicles have ‚Äãmade up more than 90% of ‚ÅÝnew car sales in Norway for a couple of years now, yet account for just a third of vehicles on the road. The Iran war shows up the folly of reducing the change to a crawl.
Follow Antony Currie ‚Äãon Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Mexico has a chance to reap nearshoring boons
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 13 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Rarely has Mexico been so popular. The country pulled in , opens new tab a record $41 billion of foreign investment last year, while trade with the United States ‚Äì despite the havoc of tariffs ‚Äì reached , opens new tab a fresh high of $873 billion. Foxconn, the world's largest electronics maker, has touted plans , opens new tab to expand beyond its already-sizable footprint , opens new tab in Chihuahua. The peso ‚Äãis up 18% since 2024, outmuscling every major global currency. Uncle Sam‚Äôs splintering of the world trade order has, in key ways, benefited its close neighbor.
Yet, despite it ‚Äåall, economic growth for 2025 came in at 0.8%, the weakest pace since the Covid-19 pandemic. Attractiveness abroad contrasts with weakness at home. Mexico‚Äôs finance ministry projects that debt service costs will reach , opens new tab 4.1% of gross domestic product this year. This comes even as belt-tightening led to a 28% year-over-year cut , opens new tab in investment in physical infrastructure.
The tumult of Washington‚Äôs protectionist turn might offer cover for a policy shift. Mexican trade officials will meet with U.S. and Canadian counterparts next week to review the ‚ÄãUSMCA, the free-trade agreement tenuously binding the economies of North America. Trump administration officials have made clear , opens new tab that they will seek new concessions, while the threat of scrapping the treaty altogether ‚Äãlooms.
Disentangling the continent would be no mean feat. Roughly 30 cents of every dollar spent , opens new tab in the U.S. on Mexican goods starts its life on the American ‚ÅÝside of the border, according to the Atlantic Council. Components, materials, and intellectual property cross south before returning north in finished form. Benefits flow between partners, and shutting off this trade would reduce ‚Äãthem. There is a reason that, as the White House has ramped up hostilities with China, Mexico has become the top U.S. trading partner.
Still, despite that momentum, policymakers could probably use an opportunity for serious ‚Äãchanges. After all, a significant share of the income generated by Mexico‚Äôs export boom accrues to overseas shareholders, because so many of the factories lining its border are owned and financed from abroad. Improvements to the labor force, whether through up-skilling or otherwise, are blunted by the fact that over half , opens new tab of workers are in what statistics agency INEGI dubs informal occupations. The extent of this unregulated ‚Äúshadow economy‚Äù means that gains accrue to a narrow slice of formal manufacturing workers ‚Äãin a handful of industrial cities.
Little wonder then that, over the decade to 2024, real output per person grew at about 0.6% annually, below the rate of Latin American peers like Chile or Brazil, ‚Äãaccording to World Bank data , opens new tab . Productivity growth has totally stalled , opens new tab .
The state, similarly, struggles to capture the fruits of increased activity. Mexico collected , opens new tab tax revenues equivalent to about 18% of GDP in 2024, lower than El Salvador , opens new tab and roughly half the rate ‚Äåof wealthy economies.
The ‚ÅÝcomparison with developing-economy peers is unflattering. Vietnam, which competes directly for manufacturing investment, grew , opens new tab 8% last year, having sustained a high pace for a decade by investing aggressively in its energy grid, ports, and technical education. India is growing at 6.5%. Even Poland, despite higher wages, grew , opens new tab by nearly 4%.
Negative consequences flow from the combination of state incapacity, weak productivity and low investment. Unreliable power is causing some , opens new tab companies to pull back on planned expansion, according to the Baker Institute for Public Policy. Government choices can compound these issues: a 2021 reform subordinated private and renewable generators to the state utility regardless of cost or efficiency.
Despite all of these ‚Äãsetbacks, Mexican industry has navigated its northern neighbor‚Äôs ‚Äãtrade tantrums with relative success. The share of exports , opens new tab ‚ÅÝentering the United States under the USMCA's preferential terms doubled , opens new tab to nearly 88% by year-end, as firms sought to avoid punitive tariffs. The treaty‚Äôs impending review will determine whether terms tighten, and what Mexico must do to maintain them. Potential agenda items, including tighter rules of origin and restrictions on Chinese content, ‚Äãoverlap neatly with the idea that the country should produce more value domestically.
It would be easier to do so if the government fixes its various ‚Äãchronic issues. Mexico's value-added tax ‚ÅÝrate of 16% is riddled with exemptions, including reduced rates in border zones. Closing some of these could help bolster the $43 billion infrastructure budget. Formalizing even a fraction of the shadow economy would expand the tax base further. Confronting state-owned oil-and-gas giant Pemex, by letting it shrink or finding private partners willing to bear its multi-billion-dollar annual losses, would free up further fiscal space. It would also help to unshackle fossil-focused national energy ‚ÅÝpolicy, potentially ‚Äãalleviating a chronic industrial concern.
In a fragmenting global economy, geographic proximity is worth more than it once was. Deep integration with ‚Äãthe United States, the world‚Äôs largest consumer market, is a particularly rare advantage. The USMCA review is an uncomfortable moment, but also a clarifying one. Whether Mexico treats Washington‚Äôs impatience as a threat to be managed or an opportunity too valuable to ‚Äãwaste will define its next decade.
Follow Sebastian Pellejero on LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: US wants to 'divide Europe', EU's Kallas tells FT
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - European Union foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas said the U.S. wants to "divide ‚ÄãEurope" and doesn't "like the European Union" in an interview ‚Äåpublished by the Financial Times on Friday, after more than a year of turmoil in transatlantic relations.
"What I think is actually important for everybody ‚Äãto understand is that the U.S. has been very clear ‚Äãthat they want to divide Europe. They don't like ‚ÅÝthe European Union," Kallas told the FT.
U.S. President Donald Trump has ‚Äãrepeatedly targeted the European Union in his second term, imposing tariffs on ‚Äãmember countries and others and talking about annexing Greenland - a move that could effectively end the NATO alliance.
This week, Trump's administration launched trade investigations into the ‚ÄãEU and other countries, including China, India, Japan, South Korea ‚Äãand Mexico, over what it said were unfair trade practices. Under the investigation, ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝcountries could face new tariffs by this summer, after the U.S. Supreme Court tore down much of Trump's tariff program last month.
Kallas said the United States' approach to the EU echoed tactics ‚Äãused by the ‚Äãbloc's adversaries, according ‚ÅÝto the FT.
EU countries should not be looking to deal with Trump bilaterally, she said, and ‚Äãinstead should deal with him together, "because we are ‚Äãequal powers ‚ÅÝwhen we are together," the FT reported.
On defence, however, Kallas said the bloc needed "to buy from America because we don't have the ‚ÅÝassets ‚Äãor the possibilities or the capabilities that ‚Äãwe need," adding that Europe needed to invest in its own defence industry.

item.title: Goldman hikes average Brent oil forecast to over $100 a barrel for March
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Goldman Sachs ‚Äãexpects Brent oil to average over $100 a barrel ‚Äåin March, and $85 in April, it said on Friday, as energy prices remain volatile due to the Iran war and damage ‚Äãto Middle East energy infrastructure and disruptions in ‚Äãthe Strait of Hormuz.
Brent futures for May were ‚ÅÝtrading at $100.13 a barrel at 0530 GMT, heading ‚Äãfor about an 8% weekly increase. On Monday, prices ‚Äãhit $119.50 a barrel, its highest since mid-2022.
Goldman sees Brent crude gradually easing back to the low $70s later in the year, however, ‚Äãif the disruption to oil flows proves longer-lived, ‚Äãoil prices could reach higher peaks and end the year at ‚Äåhigher ‚ÅÝlevels.
Goldman said a two-month disruption of the Strait of Hormuz, which has been effectively shut since the start of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran on February ‚Äã28 and ‚Äãthrough which ‚ÅÝone-fifth of the world's oil and natural gas supply passes, would push its fourth ‚Äãquarter average Brent price estimate from $71 a ‚Äãbarrel ‚ÅÝto $93 a barrel.
Goldman on Thursday raised its Brent and WTI crude oil price forecasts for the fourth quarter of ‚ÅÝ2026 ‚Äãto $71 a barrel for Brent, ‚Äãfrom $66, and $67 per barrel for WTI from $62.

item.title: Debt-burdened Europe has fewer options to buffer energy shock
item.description:
LONDON, March 13 (Reuters) - The surge in energy prices triggered by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran is putting European governments under pressure to help households and businesses, but strained finances in some major economies mean their firepower is limited.
That makes it unlikely ‚Äãthey will match the broad support provided after Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine three years ago, when subsidies and other assistance ran into the hundreds of billions of ‚Äåeuros.
Mindful of the 2022 energy crisis that exacerbated cost-of-living worries and angered voters, governments are beginning to respond, including with a record release of oil reserves .
France, Greece and Poland have introduced oil price caps, restrictions on profit margins and discounts - measures that come at little cost to the public purse - while Germany also wants to regulate pump prices.
But they may yet need to do more.
"If you get an interruption for more weeks of gas deliveries from Qatar and gas prices go higher, ‚Äãit's likely you will see governments stepping in and reintroducing some subsidies," said Frank Gill, S&P Global Ratings' lead EMEA analyst.
Governments can't yet know where whipsawing energy prices will land. But ‚Äãit's clear they are cautious about fiscal measures.
Britain has said it is too early to freeze fuel duty, while the French government has pushed back ‚ÅÝagainst opposition calls to cut value-added tax (VAT) on petrol. Italy is looking at using VAT revenue generated by higher prices to fund a cut in fuel excise duty.
The difference from 2022 is that the COVID-19 ‚Äãpandemic and the energy crisis that followed have left budget deficits across European economies nearly 3 percentage points higher than in 2019, Gill said.
Economic growth is weaker than four years ago and interest costs ‚Äãare higher, while European governments are already raising defence spending. Germany is ramping up borrowing for a massive stimulus plan.
Although oil prices flirted with $120 this week, nearing their 2022 peak, Europe's energy situation is not quite the same as 2022. Gas prices are up over 50% since the war started, but only one-sixth of the levels above 300 euros per megawatt hour they reached then. And Europe isn't rushing to replace a ‚Äãsingle supplier, as it did with Russia.
But if high prices persist and governments have to provide support, that could add to fiscal pressures in France and Britain given their high budget deficits, Fitch's head of ‚ÄãWestern European ratings Federico Barriga-Salazar told Reuters last week.
In Central Europe, S&P said Hungary 's investment-grade rating faces risks given already generous support measures in place ahead of an April election.
Spain, Portugal and Greece have stronger finances but higher ‚Äåspending could compromise ‚ÅÝtheir recoveries, Barriga-Salazar said.
As for Italy, which has gone a long way to repairing its fiscal reputation, Scope Ratings warned that slowing growth could complicate its exit from the European Union's budgetary discipline measures.
Given the limited room to act, support measures, which governments kept broad in 2022, will need to be limited and more targeted this time around, Barclays economists said. Britain and Germany have already echoed that message.
Morgan Stanley said euro zone governments' energy support measures added up to 3.6% of output across 2022-23, when EU rules limiting deficits were suspended during the pandemic. Now, it estimates, they could only provide support of ‚Äãaround 0.3% of output per year while sticking to ‚Äãthe EU's rules.
If the Strait of Hormuz ‚ÅÝstays shut for over a month and there are signs growth is weakening, the EU could allow some countries to deviate temporarily from the rules, Morgan Stanley said, expecting they would spend up to 0.6% of output per year to fund targeted measures.
It would take a severe downturn for the EU to ‚Äãsuspend its rules again, Morgan Stanley added.
More importantly, higher debt costs are themselves a constraint.
"I don't see any country being big on fiscal for ‚Äãthe moment because they fear ‚ÅÝthey will be punished," said Gregoire Pesques, chief investment officer, fixed income at Europe's biggest asset manager Amundi.
Bond investors have become more sensitive to fiscal slippage in recent years, and Britain and France have been in the firing line in Europe.
Germany, with low debt, and Spain, with high growth, have more room to respond, Pesques said.
Key to the affordability of any support measures will be how much of the cost governments can offset.
One ‚ÅÝstrategy is windfall ‚Äãtaxes on energy companies, which many European countries implemented last time and Italy has already signalled. But S&P's Gill noted ‚Äãrevenues last time had been far from matching the cost of subsidies.
Critics say subsidies and price caps would raise energy demand and put upward pressure on already high prices.
"In the short term, the best option is to enable and incentivize reductions in ‚Äãdemand," said Georg Zachmann, senior fellow at think-tank Bruegel.

item.title: Ukraine's Naftogaz says Swiss court rejected Gazprom's motion over $1.4 billion payment
item.description: March 13 (Reuters) - Ukraine's state ‚Äãenergy firm ‚ÄåNaftogaz on ‚ÄãFriday ‚Äãsaid the Swiss ‚ÅÝSupreme ‚ÄãCourt has ‚Äãrejected a motion ‚Äãfrom ‚ÄãRussia's Gazprom (GAZP.MM) , opens new tab to ‚Äåoverturn ‚ÅÝa June 2025 ruling ‚Äãon ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝpayment ‚Äãof $1.4 billion ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝNaftogaz.

item.title: Israeli military strikes bridge over Litani River in Lebanon
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 13 (Reuters) - The Israeli military said it carried out an air strike on a ‚Äãbridge in southern Lebanon on Friday, in what ‚Äåappeared to be the first time in the current campaign against Hezbollah that Israel acknowledged it had targeted civilian infrastructure.
The Israeli military ‚Äãsaid that the Zrariyeh bridge, which spans the ‚ÄãLitani River, was targeted because it was a ‚ÅÝkey crossing used by Hezbollah militants moving between ‚Äãnorthern and southern Lebanon, but provided no evidence to ‚Äãsupport the claim.
It also said Hezbollah militants had positioned launchers near the bridge and carried out attacks on Israel from the area.
Striking ‚Äãthe bridge was necessary to remove a threat ‚Äãto Israeli civilians, the military said in a statement.
Earlier in the ‚Äåday, ‚ÅÝLebanon's state media reported that a drone struck a residential apartment in Beirut's Burj Hammoud district on the northern outskirts of the Lebanese capital on Friday.
It is ‚Äãthe first time ‚Äãthe area ‚ÅÝhas been targeted.
Israel's military has carried out daily strikes this week on Lebanon in ‚Äãan offensive against Tehran-backed Hezbollah after it ‚Äãlaunched ‚ÅÝattacks on Israel on March 2 to avenge the killing of Iran's supreme leader at the start of the ‚ÅÝU.S.-Israeli war ‚Äãon Iran .
Hundreds of thousands of Lebanese ‚Äãhave been displaced.

item.title: Kazakhs to vote on new constitution in referendum on Sunday
item.description:
ALMATY, March 13 (Reuters) - Kazakhstan holds a referendum on Sunday on a new constitution that some ‚Äåcritics say could allow President Kassym-Jomart Tokayev to remain in charge of Central Asia's largest economy beyond his current term limit of 2029.
Tokayev, a former diplomat who has maintained good relations with Russia, the U.S. and China, has called the referendum "a ‚Äãtruly historic moment" that moves Kazakhstan, an energy and minerals giant, away from a "super-presidential" system towards ‚Äãa greater separation of powers.
But some analysts say the draft retains an excessively powerful ‚ÅÝpresidency.
"[It] significantly increases the powers of the head of state and does not create a system of ‚Äãchecks and balances," said political analyst Dosym Satpayev.
The proposal merges parliament's two chambers into one and restores the ‚Äãpost of vice-president, abolished in 1996, who would be picked by the president.
Kazakh presidents would remain limited to a single term of seven years, a change Tokayev himself introduced in 2022.
Tokayev, 72, has said he will step down in 2029, but ‚Äãsome observers say a new constitution could reset his term limit, a tactic used by the leaders ‚Äãof several other ex-Soviet republics, including Russian President Vladimir Putin.
Referring to a rumour widespread in Kazakhstan that ‚ÄåTokayev may ‚ÅÝrun for the post of United Nations secretary-general this year, Satpayev said the vice-presidency would allow Tokayev to anoint a successor before leaving office early.
A Kazakh diplomatic source told Reuters that Tokayev, who headed the UN's Geneva office in 2011-2013, is considering both being a candidate for secretary-general and seeking a possible second presidential ‚Äãterm.
Tokayev has said he ‚Äãdoes not want to ‚ÅÝreturn to the UN.
State-approved pollsters say a large majority backs the new constitution, while public opposition to it has been subdued.
The vote comes at a fraught time ‚Äãfor Kazakhstan, whose economy is closely intertwined with Russia's, and which has suffered ‚Äãfrom the Ukraine war ‚ÅÝfallout.
Economic growth has accelerated but so has inflation, hitting 11.7% in February, while interest rates are at 18%, a record high. A tranche of tax rises has deepened discontent.
Asya Tuligenova, who sells horsemeat, a Kazakh delicacy, at ‚ÅÝa bazaar ‚Äãin the commercial capital Almaty, said traders were shouldering rising ‚Äãcosts to avoid passing them on to consumers.
"We're kind of afraid. If we raise prices, it will be difficult for our regular customers."
She ‚Äãdid not say whether she would vote on Sunday.

item.title: Exclusive: Pakistan's domestic power sources cushion LNG supply risk, minister says
item.description:
KARACHI, March 13 (Reuters) - Pakistan's growing reliance on domestic power, ‚Äãincluding solar and wind energy, nuclear reactors, coal and hydropower, has reduced its vulnerability to global LNG supply disruptions , Power Minister Awais ‚ÄåLeghari told Reuters.
The war in the Middle East threatens shipments from Qatar , the world's No. 2 producer after the U.S. which supplies most of Pakistan's imported LNG , used to fuel power plants during peak electricity demand.
"Pakistan has been steadily increasing reliance on indigenous energy resources, and about 74% of our electricity generation now comes from local sources," Leghari said, adding the ‚Äãgovernment aims to raise that above 96% by 2034.
The figures have not been previously reported.
"The people-led solar revolution , and earlier decisions to invest ‚Äãin nuclear, hydropower and local coal have all played a role in increasing Pakistan's self-reliance," he added.
Pakistan has long struggled ‚ÅÝwith electricity shortages and historically faced hours of daily load shedding during peak summer demand.
The country now has surplus generation capacity after adding coal, ‚ÄãLNG and nuclear plants, while demand growth has slowed and the use of rooftop solar has surged , at times exceeding grid demand in some hubs.
Outages still occur in ‚Äãparts of the country due to theft, line losses and financial constraints, rather than a lack of power.
Qatar halted LNG production earlier this month, and Asian nations, who buy 80% of its output, are scrambling to meet the shortage.
LNG now accounts for about 10% of Pakistan's electricity generation, mainly used to meet evening demand peaks and stabilise ‚Äãthe grid, Leghari said.
During the global energy crisis triggered by Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022, the country was forced to cut power for extended ‚Äãperiods after failing to secure LNG cargoes on the spot market.
"Even if LNG was disrupted or became too expensive, the impact on production capacity, industry or agriculture would ‚Äåbe minimal," ‚ÅÝLeghari said.
But he said prolonged disruptions could still lead to additional shortages during summer, when demand surges from the use of air-conditioners.
"In a worst-case scenario, if LNG cargoes stopped for several months, Pakistan might see one to two hours of load shedding during peak summer evenings," Leghari said,
Such outages would likely affect some urban and rural areas, not industry or agriculture, he said, adding Pakistan is developing battery storage to shift excess daytime solar to evening ‚Äãpeaks.
Pakistan cancelled 21 LNG cargoes due in 2026-27 ‚Äãunder a long-term deal with ‚ÅÝItaly's Eni (ENI.MI) , opens new tab as domestic power and solar growth cut gas demand.
Pakistan is not expected to invest in any source of power that could put it at risk in terms of energy security," Leghari said, saying the government's ‚Äãplans for the next six to eight years is to focus on indigenous clean power.
About 55% of electricity ‚Äãgeneration now comes from ‚ÅÝclean sources, which the government aims to raise above 90% by 2034, Leghari said.
Hydropower produces about 40 terawatt hours of electricity annually, while nuclear generates roughly 22 TWh and domestic coal about 12 TWh, according to the minister, forming a significant share of Pakistan's electricity supply without relying on imported fuel.
Rooftop solar installations ‚ÅÝhave surged ‚Äãto more than 20 GW across Pakistan, with behind-the-meter capacity estimated at 12‚Äì14 GW and ‚Äãpossibly up to 18 GW, sharply reducing daytime grid demand, he said.
Hydropower output also rises in summer as river flows increase, adding up to 7,000 megawatts of capacity and helping meet ‚Äãhigher electricity demand from air-conditioning.

item.title: Operator probes incident at Kazakhstan's Tengiz oilfield, sources say output intact
item.description:
ALMATY, March 13 (Reuters) - Kazakhstan's Tengizchevroil consortium said on Friday it was checking the cause of a March 11 "incident" at the country's largest ‚Äãoilfield of Tengiz that caused no injuries, while sources said ‚Äãthe field had not stopped production.
The oilfield at the ‚ÅÝnortheastern tip of the Caspian Sea had been pumping oil at ‚Äãlower levels after fires at power stations on January 18, boosting ‚Äãuncertainty on global markets battling constricted supplies because of the US-Israel war on Iran.
"The company is assessing the root cause in line with established processes," said TCO, ‚Äãoperated by the U.S. oil major Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab , as it confirmed ‚Äãthe incident at one of its facilities at Tengiz, and added that there ‚Äåwere ‚ÅÝno injuries.
"Beyond this, TCO does not comment on specific details of its operations," it said in a statement.
Oil output at Tengiz rose to 810,000 barrels on March 11, but had averaged 495,000 barrels per ‚Äãday (bpd) over the first ‚Äã10 days ‚ÅÝof this month, lagging the capacity target, two industry sources said on Thursday, speaking on condition of ‚Äãanonymity.
Output recovery at Tengiz is capped by loading ‚Äãconstraints such ‚ÅÝas adverse weather and the threat of drone attacks on the main export route, the Caspian Pipeline Consortium (CPC) system.
Oil production at the ‚ÅÝTengiz field ‚Äãhad recovered to 120,000 tons per ‚Äãday, or the equivalent of 955,000 bpd, Kazakhstan's Energy Minister Erlan Akkenzhenov told reporters on ‚ÄãWednesday.

item.title: Death toll nearly doubles to 42
item.description:
NAIROBI, March 9 (Reuters) - The death toll from heavy rains and flooding in Kenya's capital Nairobi and elsewhere has nearly ‚Äãdoubled to 42, the government said in a ‚Äåstatement issued late on Sunday.
Intense rains on Friday unleashed heavy and widespread flooding, causing some people to drown, washing away vehicles ‚Äãand disrupting traffic at the country's largest airport.
The ‚Äãprevious death toll was 23.
Emergency workers from various ‚ÅÝagencies including the military were still conducting search ‚Äãand rescue operations across the country, Geoffrey Kiringa Ruku, ‚Äãminister for public service and human capital development said in the statement.
"Search mission is still in progress by the multi agency emergency ‚Äãresponse teams with the aim of ensuring that ‚Äãbodies of all the flood victims are found and retrieved," he ‚Äåsaid.
The ‚ÅÝfloods had also done extensive damage to infrastructure and livelihoods. But 172 vehicles that had been swept away by flood waters have been recovered.
On Saturday, President William ‚ÄãRuto said he ‚Äãhad ordered relief ‚ÅÝfood from the country's national strategic reserves be immediately released for distribution to families ‚Äãaffected by the floods.
Scientists say global warming ‚Äãis ‚ÅÝworsening floods and droughts across East Africa by concentrating rainfall into shorter, more intense bursts. A 2024 World Weather ‚ÅÝAttribution ‚Äãstudy found climate change had made ‚Äãdevastating rains in the region twice as likely as before.

item.title: A backlash against data centres is spilling into French municipal election races
item.description:
LE BOURGET, France, March 13 (Reuters) - In the town of Le Bourget outside Paris, Sofiane Milous, a candidate in France's municipal elections, is vowing to reverse ‚Äãplans for a data centre he says will intensify heat islands, worsen noise pollution and create few local jobs.
The former judo champion, running on a green ticket, said ‚Äåthe race to build artificial intelligence data centres was no answer to the industrial decline of working‚Äëclass towns like his, where manufacturer Alstom shut a plant three decades ago.
"We lost an industry that gave us a livelihood, even if it polluted, and now we face this new 'industry 4.0' that doesn‚Äôt create jobs for residents," Milous told Reuters.
President Emmanuel Macron has championed data centres as key to regaining control over critical tech infrastructure and last year declared France was "back in the AI ‚Äãrace" with plans for 109 billion euros ($126 billion) in private-sector investment.
But local resistance is growing, fuelled by concerns over strains on the power grid, pollution and the dominance of U.S. Big ‚ÄãTech.
France holds municipal elections over two rounds on March 15 and 22, with the race for the control of more than 35,000 town and city halls seen ‚ÅÝas a test of the far right's ability to deepen its support base ahead of a presidential vote in 2027.
A Reuters tally found that candidates in at least 10 towns - including Marseille and Bordeaux - ‚Äãare campaigning against new data centres or calling for moratoriums or more transparency.
While not decisive in this election, these campaigns highlight rising voter concern over the issue, which has become central in smaller communities such as ‚ÄãLe Bourget.
This mirrors trends across Europe and the United States, where a surge in data centre construction has fuelled a backlash and turned power use and Big Tech's footprint into electoral issues on a local and national level.
In Ireland, where data centres consume 22% of national electricity, opposition parties have criticised the government's move to lift an effective four-year moratorium on grid connection. Near London, a hyperscale data centre faces a legal challenge from campaigners who argue the ‚Äãproject failed to account for climate-change impacts.
Amid a surge in grid requests, France is also cracking down on speculative investors acquiring land for "zombie" projects, following similar steps taken in Britain late last year.
Opponents range from ‚Äãenvironmentalists and academics to property owners and labour groups, said Chris Adams, director of technology and policy at the Green Web Foundation.
"It's an unregulated industry that is now upsetting people across the political spectrum," he said.
France is ‚ÅÝpromoting its nuclear power as a relatively cheap, abundant source of carbon‚Äëfree energy as it competes with neighbours such as Britain and Germany to attract new data‚Äëcentre investment.
The Seine-Saint-Denis prefecture approved the Le Bourget project in January, requiring developer Segro Bourget to conduct additional studies on noise and air pollution and hold a public meeting. Segro Bourget did not comment.
Outgoing right-wing mayor Jean‚ÄëBaptiste Borsali has said he trusts the state's assessment and that the centre could benefit half the town's residents through heat recovery. He did not respond to interview requests.
At the Le Bourget market one Saturday ahead of the election, several shoppers said the project would ‚Äãdetermine their vote.
"I signed the petition ‚Äî it's right ‚Äãnext to schools. I want a park for ‚ÅÝmy children; we have no green space," said Veronique Pernolet, a 28-year-old teacher who lives near the former H&M warehouse where the facility is planned.
In Marseille, where undersea cables have made the port city a data centre hotspot, left‚Äëwing candidate Sebastien Barles is calling for a moratorium.
"We have significant electricity needs - powering ‚Äãships at dock, ship‚Äërepair facilities - and these data centres consume a large share of available power," said Barles, who is running with the France Unbowed ‚Äãlist.
In Wissous, south of Paris, ‚ÅÝmunicipal candidate Philippe de Fruyt is bringing a legal challenge due to be heard in court next week to block the extension of an existing data centre used by Amazon. Amazon did not comment.
Connecting a new data centre in Europe's major markets can take on average seven to 10 years, though access to the French grid is relatively unconstrained compared to some of its neighbours, according to climate think tank Ember.
A draft law ‚ÅÝproposes classifying data ‚Äãcentres as ‚Äúprojects of national interest‚Äù, reducing legal and environmental hurdles and allowing the state to bypass local authorities. The bill is ‚Äãcurrently stuck in France's parliament.
Some academics and politicians say the measures would weaken public input.
"We've seen what happened in the U.S., where so many data centres were built that serious opposition emerged," said Anthony Devillet, of The Cloud Is Under Our Feet advocacy ‚Äãgroup. "With the rise of AI, I think the debate will become omnipresent here."
($1 = 0.8651 euros)

item.title: Global EV sales fall again in February
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Global EV registrations fell 11% in February, dragged by ‚ÄåChina's largest sales drop since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic in early 2020, data by consultancy Benchmark Mineral Intelligence (BMI) showed on Friday.
As governments worldwide hit the brake on policies designed ‚Äãto encourage purchases of electric cars, China has pulled funding for auto trade-ins, while ‚Äãa tax exemption on EV purchases in the country expired ‚ÅÝat the end of last year.
China, the world's largest EV market, recorded in ‚ÄãFebruary a 32% year-on-year drop in new battery-electric and plug-in hybrid car registrations, a ‚Äãproxy for sales, to less than 500,000 vehicles, BMI said.
That is in line with a 34% drop in overall car sales in the month recorded by the China Association of Automobile ‚ÄãManufacturers .
"Consumers are very price sensitive", BMI data manager Charles Lester said.
Worldwide, registrations fell ‚Äãfor a second month in February to just over one million cars sold, their lowest since ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝsame month of 2024.
The North American market contracted by 35% to under 90,000 EVs sold, falling for a fifth consecutive month after the end of an EV tax credit scheme in the United States last September and proposals by President Donald ‚ÄãTrump's administration to further ‚Äãcut Co2 emission ‚ÅÝstandards .
Trump's policies and cooling global demand for electric cars have forced some of the carmakers with the largest exposure to ‚Äãthe U.S. market to book over $70 billion in writedowns .
Europe has also ‚Äãbacktracked on ‚ÅÝemission targets. The continent's EV sales however rose by 21% in February, maintaining growth despite a slowing pace compared to most of last year.
EV registrations in the rest ‚ÅÝof ‚Äãthe world grew by 78% to over 180,000 ‚Äãcars, as Chinese carmakers continued to expand their presence in Asian markets and Australia, as well as ‚ÄãEurope, while fending off fierce competition domestically.

item.title: 'Dream holidays' in disarray, trips diverted as travellers count cost of Iran war
item.description:
SYDNEY/LONDON, March 13 (Reuters) - In the remote Western Australian town of Dardanup, accountant Natasha Earle and her family are feeling the financial pain of the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.
Their "once-in-a-lifetime" five-week trip to Europe - booked last May on Emirates and ‚Äãtaking them to London, Paris, Berlin, Vienna and Rome - has been upended by the conflict, and is costing roughly A$10,000 ($7,000) more as they reroute to avoid disruptions from drone and missile fire in the Middle East.
"We've ‚Äåspent tens of thousands of dollars on this holiday," said Earle, who is due to fly at the end of this month amid the biggest disruption to global travel since the pandemic. "We should get at least half of that back from Emirates eventually."
With the Gulf serving as a global crossroads for commercial aviation, the Iran conflict underscores how quickly a problem in a single region can paralyse travel worldwide, driving up prices, squeezing capacity and throwing holiday plans into disarray.
Drone and missile fire have regularly left aircraft circling near Dubai as the war enters its third week, heavily impacting Middle East tourism worth some $367 billion annually to the region.
Combined, ‚ÄãEmirates, Qatar Airways and Etihad Airways normally fly more than half of all passengers between Europe and Australia, New Zealand and the nearby Pacific Islands, according to Cirium data.
The war has already led to the closure of much of the ‚ÄãMiddle East‚Äôs airspace due to the risk of missiles and drones, leading to tens of thousands of flight cancellations, reroutings and schedule changes worldwide, disrupting the lives of millions of people.
New Zealander ‚ÅÝJacob Brown, 34, who lives in the Qatari capital of Doha, drove through the desert of neighbouring Saudi Arabia to Riyadh where he caught a plane to London after his Qatar Airways flight from Doha to New Zealand - where he was due to be ‚Äãbest man at a wedding - was cancelled.
A journey that should have taken him less than 24 hours ended up taking several days due to closed airspace, additional flight chaos, delays and lost baggage.
"It was pretty nerve-wracking flying out of Riyadh just considering that that morning, there ‚Äãhad been a few missile interceptions south of the capital," he said.
Aditya Kushwaha, an Australian disability support worker living in Orange near Sydney, has booked a family holiday to London and Paris from April 13 to 29, with Emirates through Dubai. He is unsure if it will go ahead.
"We are very much in a dilemma of what to do," Kushwaha said, estimating he might lose more than $10,000 if he cancels the trip and only be able to afford it again in a few years.
For Australian Kellee Smith, her "dream holiday" to Europe at the end of March - planned 12 months ‚Äãago - with her husband and two children has also turned into a "nightmare" and left her roughly A$5,500 out of pocket.
"I've had many sleepless nights as I was stressed thinking I‚Äôm going to lose my dream holiday ... and all the money we paid," said Smith.
She is ‚Äãwaiting for a refund from Emirates of more than A$4,000 after securing back-up flights with Cathay Pacific (0293.HK) , opens new tab and Qantas (QAN.AX) , opens new tab to fly through Asia rather than the Middle East.
The war has narrowed an already-slim flight corridor for long-haul flights between Europe and Asia, complicating operations for global air carriers ‚Äåand sending ticket prices sky-high .
As ‚ÅÝthe conflict rattles businesses worldwide and drives oil prices higher, concerns over jet fuel costs and supplies are also weighing on airlines, with many raising fuel surcharges and some, such as Air New Zealand (AIR.NZ) , opens new tab , cutting flights.
The International Energy Agency has said the war is creating the biggest oil supply disruption in history, while some analysts have warned it could be only a matter of weeks before airlines run out of jet fuel. Vietnam said this week it could face aviation fuel shortages as soon as April due to the conflict.
In the British city of Bath, John Moore, 81, and his wife Pauline - who describe themselves as "travel virgins" - forked out "a couple of hundred pounds" to switch their flights to the Australian city of Brisbane from Qatar Airways to Qantas.
They will transit through Singapore instead of the Middle East.
"We decided ‚Äãwe'd rather pay the extra to book via Singapore, which is ‚Äãno guarantee, but clearly it's likely to be safer than ‚ÅÝthe current route," Moore told Reuters.
For others, the prospect of travel disruptions due to the war is not worth the risk.
Sumit Sharma, who lives in Sydney and works at Westpac Banking Corp., had planned to travel on Etihad Airways with his family to Dubai, but changed his plans after Etihad confirmed he was eligible for a refund.
"We changed the plan from the Middle East to Hong Kong," ‚ÄãSharma said, adding that he was now flying with Cathay Pacific and looking forward to taking his son to Disneyland.
Shobana Gopal, a senior consultant with Alliance Insurance in Sydney, has switched her ‚Äãfamily's travel plans to China instead ‚ÅÝof flying through Dubai to get to Austria.
"We're going to three cities in China," Gopal said.
Qantas said more passengers were choosing to travel to Europe via the U.S., other Asian cities and Johannesburg in South Africa, using its partner network.
Hong Kong's Cathay Pacific, said this week the airline was seeing "drastic changes in terms of demand patterns due to the Middle East situation," while budget carrier Ryanair (RYA.I) , opens new tab highlighted a surge in bookings to European destinations as travellers avoided the Middle East.
Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab said demand for routes through Europe had surged, with the German carrier flagging that 12-month forward bookings for ‚ÅÝdirect flights to ‚ÄãAsia were up 75% year-on-year.
"The large Middle Eastern airlines like Emirates and Etihad can't fly the routes they should, and therefore the European and Asian airlines have ‚Äãto pick up that traffic," aviation specialist Hans Joergen Elnaes said.

item.title: Police investigate overnight fire attack on Rotterdam synagogue
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 13 (Reuters) - Dutch police open an investigation ‚Äåinto an arson attack on a synagogue in Rotterdam on Friday, in the latest incident of suspected antisemitic ‚Äãviolence since the start of the U.S-Israeli ‚Äãwar on Iran .
No one was injured as ‚ÅÝa result of the fire, which was ‚Äãnow over, and no arrests have been made, ‚Äãthe city's police said.
"Around 3:40 a.m., a fire was started at the synagogue," Rotterdam police said in a ‚Äãpost on X. "The fire burned for a ‚Äãshort time before extinguishing," the police added, calling for any ‚Äåwitnesses ‚ÅÝto come forward with evidence.
Another synagogue, 200 km (124 miles) away in neighbouring Belgium, was hit by an explosion on Monday in what authorities said ‚Äãwas an ‚Äãantisemitic attack. ‚ÅÝThat attack caused damage but no injuries.
In Michigan in the U.S., ‚Äãa Lebanon-born U.S. citizen crashed his truck ‚Äãinto ‚ÅÝa synagogue and its preschool and was shot dead.
Concerns about possible attacks against Jewish communities around the ‚ÅÝworld ‚Äãhave risen following U.S. and ‚ÄãIsraeli airstrikes on Iran and a subsequent response by Iran.

item.title: D8 summit in Indonesia delayed due to Middle East war, Indonesian foreign ministry says
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 13 (Reuters) - A summit of eight major Muslim ‚Äãdeveloping countries, including Iran, that ‚Äåwas to be held in Jakarta next month has been delayed due ‚Äãto the war in the Middle ‚ÄãEast , an official with Indonesia's foreign ‚ÅÝministry said on Friday.
The D-8 ‚ÄãOrganization for Economic Cooperation, which ‚Äãgroups Bangladesh, Egypt, Indonesia, Iran, Malaysia, Nigeria, Pakistan and Turkey, was established in 1997 ‚Äãto improve cooperation between the ‚Äãcountries stretching from Southeast Asia to Africa.
A ‚Äårevised ‚ÅÝdate for the conference has not been discussed, senior foreign ministry official Tri Tharyat told journalists.
It ‚Äãwas originally ‚Äãset to ‚ÅÝbe held on April 13 to 15.
The U.S. ‚Äãand Israel began attacks on Iran ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝFebruary 28. Iran has responded with strikes on Israel and Gulf ‚ÅÝcountries ‚Äãwith U.S. bases.
Egypt ‚Äãhosted the previous D-8 summit in 2024.

item.title: NATO-partner Serbia admits buying Chinese missiles after photos leaked
item.description:
BELGRADE, March 13 (Reuters) - Serbia recently purchased Chinese CM-400AKG air-to-surface ballistic missiles for its air force, becoming the weapon's first European operator, Serbian President Aleksandar Vucic ‚Äãsaid late on Thursday.
Serbia is striving to balance its partnership ‚Äåwith NATO and aspirations to join the European Union with its centuries-old religious, ethnic and political alliance with Russia and strategic ties with China, a major investor.
"We have a ‚Äãsignificant number of those missiles, and we will have even more," ‚ÄãVucic said in a live broadcast by Serbia's state RTS ‚ÅÝTV, days after the first images of the missiles mounted on a ‚ÄãSerbian plane leaked online.
Vucic said the Serbian air force had adapted its ‚ÄãSoviet-made MiG-29 fighter jets to carry the CM-400AKG.
Croatia - an EU and NATO member, and Serbia's foe during the wars of the 1990s - has criticised the missile purchase as a ‚Äãthreat to regional stability, an attempt to alter the military balance, and ‚Äãa sign of a growing arms race in the Balkans.
The CM-400AKG, manufactured by China Aerospace ‚ÄåScience ‚ÅÝand Industry Corporation (CASIC), is a supersonic air-to-surface ballistic missile. It can carry either a 150 kg (330 lb) blast warhead or a 200 kg (440 lb) penetrator warhead and has a range of up to 400 km (248 miles).
It saw ‚Äãits first combat use ‚Äãduring the 2025 ‚ÅÝIndia‚ÄìPakistan conflict, when Pakistan's air force targeted an Indian S-400 air defence system.
Vucic declined to disclose the price ‚ÄãSerbia paid for the missiles, saying only it received a "slight ‚Äãdiscount".
Serbia has ‚ÅÝallocated around 2.6% of its GDP for military expenditures this year.
In recent times, Serbia has purchased the FK-3 surface-to-air defence system - similar to Russia's S-300 or ‚ÅÝthe U.S. ‚ÄãPatriot system - and CH-92A combat drones from ‚ÄãChina, while at the same time buying 12 new Rafale fighter jets from France's Dassault along with ‚Äãhelicopters and cargo planes from Airbus.

item.title: Oil heads for weekly gains despite US sanctions waiver on Russian oil
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 13 (Reuters) - Oil prices headed for weekly gains as ‚Äãof Friday, despite the U.S. trying to ease supply concerns by issuing a 30-day license for countries ‚Äåto buy Russian oil and petroleum products stranded at sea.
Brent futures for May rose $1.02, or 1%, to $101.48 a barrel at 0730 GMT, heading for a weekly increase of nearly 10%. U.S. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude for April was up by 94 cents, or 1.0%, at $96.67 a barrel, ‚Äãpoised for more than a 6% uptick for the week.
The license was issued in what Treasury Secretary Scott ‚ÄãBessent said was a step to stabilise global energy markets roiled by the U.S.-Israeli war on ‚ÅÝIran , but analysts said this has failed to resolve wider supply constraints.
"ICE Brent futures have already breached $100 per barrel and ‚Äãare still supported today, despite moves to calm the markets with the Russian oil waiver and the unprecedented release of ‚Äãemergency stockpiles," said Emril Jamil, senior analyst at LSEG.
"The market sees this as a short-term solution that does not address the crux of the supply disruption. The crude intermonth spreads for future months indicate an unresolved and continued tightness in supply," Jamil said.
Brent is more supported than ‚ÄãWTI as Europe is more susceptible to energy security issues, while the U.S. is able to stave off its exposure ‚Äãdue to its domestic output, Jamil said.
Yang An, analyst at Haitong Futures, said: "Issuing the license has eased market concerns, but it won't ‚Äåresolve the ‚ÅÝmost fundamental issue. The most important thing is the restoration of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz."
The announcement on Russian oil came a day after the U.S. Energy Department said the U.S. would release 172 million barrels of oil from its Strategic Petroleum Reserve to help curb skyrocketing oil prices.
That plan was coordinated with the International Energy Agency, which has agreed to ‚Äãrelease a record 400 million barrels ‚Äãof oil from strategic stockpiles, ‚ÅÝincluding the U.S. contribution.
Fleeting relief sparked by the IEA release, however, was shattered by a re-escalation of Middle East risks, IG analyst Tony Sycamore said in a note.
Both benchmark prices ‚Äãsurged more than 9% on Thursday and hit their highest levels since August 2022.
Iran's new ‚Äãsupreme leader Mojtaba ‚ÅÝKhamenei said Iran would fight on and keep the Strait of Hormuz shut as leverage against the United States and Israel.
Two fuel tankers in Iraqi waters were struck by explosive-laden Iranian boats, Iraqi security officials said on Thursday. An Iraqi official told state media ‚ÅÝthat the ‚Äãcountry's oil ports have completely stopped operations.
Meanwhile, U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent ‚Äãtold Sky News in an interview that the U.S. Navy, perhaps with an international coalition, would escort vessels through the Strait of Hormuz when it is ‚Äãmilitarily possible.

item.title: Analysts reassess oil price estimates as Iran conflict disrupts markets
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Major brokerages, including Goldman Sachs and Bank of America, have revised their average oil price forecasts for 2026 ‚Äãas the war in Iran approached the two‚Äëweek mark ‚Äåon Friday.
Analysts expect oil prices to remain elevated in the near term as they assess the impact of supply disruptions through the Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz, a vital conduit for more than 20% ‚Äãof global oil flows. However, they broadly anticipate prices ‚ÅÝstabilizing later in the year.
Brent futures and U.S. West ‚ÄãTexas Intermediate (WTI) futures rose to their highest since June 2022 this ‚Äãweek, and were headed for more than 10% and 7% weekly rises respectively.
Iran‚Äôs new Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, vowed on Thursday to keep the ‚ÄãStrait of Hormuz shut as leverage against the United States ‚Äãand Israel, amid a Middle East conflict that continues to disrupt millions of ‚Äålives ‚ÅÝand rattle energy and financial markets worldwide.

item.title: Exclusive: Philippines looks to regulate power market as LNG prices surge
item.description:
SINGAPORE/MANILA, March 13 (Reuters) - The Philippines plans to rein in power bills as Middle ‚ÄåEast shipping disruptions drive up LNG prices, its energy secretary told Reuters on Friday, by boosting coal-fired power output and by regulating electricity prices, maybe as soon as next week.
Shipping in the Gulf and through the narrow Strait of Hormuz has slowed sharply as the U.S.-Israel war with Iran ‚Äãescalates, driving LNG prices to their highest since 2022, after an output halt by Qatar, which provides a fifth ‚Äãof global supply.
The Southeast Asian nation could face a rise of 16% in power prices by ‚ÅÝnext month unless the government intervenes, Energy Secretary Sharon Garin told Reuters, adding that Manila was in talks with Indonesia ‚Äãto ensure steady supplies of coal.
"The basic idea is to ramp down liquefied natural gas and ramp up coal and renewables," ‚ÄãGarin said, flagging that LNG-fired power plants now supplying the grid can readily be replaced with electricity generated from coal in the short term.
"We're asking for emergency powers in order to regulate the market," she said, adding that the government's effort to limit power prices at current levels ‚Äãcould begin as soon as next week.
"Because the cost of living will increase, we are trying to do some temporary ‚Äãrelief."
In addition to measures such as speeding renewable linkages and rescheduling maintenance, the Philippines's return to coal will follow its first annual decline in ‚Äåcoal-fired ‚ÅÝoutput in nearly two decades last year.
That now stands to be reversed, highlighting Asia's struggle with LNG price swings and supply disruptions.
After Qatar's production halt, spot liquefied natural gas prices more than doubled to their highest in over three years.
The Philippine government's plan to intervene in one of the few Asian markets where power prices are not regulated could begin as early ‚Äãas next week, Garin said.
Power tariffs ‚Äãin the archipelago of ‚ÅÝmore than 100 million are already the second highest in the region, behind Singapore.
"With the exaggerated increase in fuel transportation costs, there's a multiplier effect," Garin said.
Some market rules might have ‚Äãto be suspended to afford temporary relief, Garin said, adding that distribution utilities had offered to ‚Äãboost coal-fired power ‚ÅÝin place of LNG.
"We'll intervene in the market, or whatever is allowed by law, especially for Meralco (MER.PS) , opens new tab , which is the biggest distribution utility we have," she said.
The government was in talks with First Gas Power (FGEN.PS) , opens new tab to supply any unused domestically extracted gas to ‚ÅÝpower plants ‚Äãrunning purely on LNG, she added.
First Gas did not immediately respond to a ‚Äãrequest for comment.
In an email, Meralco told Reuters it supported the energy department's initiatives to rein in prices, had sufficient contracted coal, and was coordinating with ‚Äãpower suppliers to hold down generation charges.

item.title: Bodies of 84 Iranian sailors to be repatriated from Sri Lanka, Iran embassy source and local media say
item.description: COLOMBO, March 13 (Reuters) - The bodies ‚Äãof 84 ‚ÄåIranian sailors killed ‚Äãin a ‚ÄãU.S. submarine attack ‚ÅÝon ‚Äãa warship ‚Äãoff Sri Lanka's coast last ‚Äãweek ‚Äãwill be repatriated, a ‚ÄåIran ‚ÅÝembassy source and local ‚Äãmedia ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝSri Lanka reported ‚Äãon ‚ÄãFriday.

item.title: Trump claimed in G7 call that Iran is "about to surrender," Axios reports
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Trump ‚Äãtold G7 leaders in a ‚Äåvirtual meeting on Wednesday that Iran is "about to surrender," Axios reported on Friday citing three officials ‚Äãfrom G7 countries briefed on ‚Äãthe contents of the call.
According to ‚ÅÝthe report, Trump told allies that ‚Äãhe "got rid of a cancer that ‚Äãwas threatening us all," while boasting about the results of Operation "Epic Fury" on the G7 ‚Äãcall Wednesday morning.
Trump said that "nobody ‚Äãknows who is the leader, so there is ‚Äåno ‚ÅÝone that can announce surrender," Axios reported.
The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment ‚Äãoutside regular ‚Äãbusiness hours.
Trump ‚ÅÝon Friday derided Iran's leaders as "deranged scumbags" and said it ‚Äãwas his great honor to ‚Äãkill ‚ÅÝthem as the war in the Middle East approached the two-week mark ‚ÅÝwith ‚Äãheavy exchanges of drone ‚Äãand missile strikes across the region.

item.title: Thai family waits for news of missing relative on ship attacked in Straits of Hormuz
item.description:
BAN SAI, Thailand, March 13 (Reuters) - Thousands of miles from the Middle East in Thailand's rural northeast, Sommai Butdee waits for news of her nephew, one ‚Äãof three crew members missing from the Thai-flagged vessel after it was struck with ‚Äåtwo projectiles earlier this week as it travelled through the Strait of Hormuz.
On Friday, Sommai, 58, gathered with other relatives under their wooden stilt home in Surin province's Ban Sai village. Holding out her phone, she ‚Äãshowed a photo of her nephew, 27-year-old Panupong Muentan, on his graduation from ‚Äãa merchant marine training centre.
"He didn‚Äôt say much. I wished him a ‚ÅÝsafe journey. He had gone to work at sea once or twice before. He told ‚Äãme, ‚ÄòDon‚Äôt be afraid. There‚Äôs nothing to worry about'," Sommai said, recalling the last time they had ‚Äãseen each other in February.
Sommai raised her nephew and described him as a good and devoted man, who hoped to help pay off the family's debt and his sibling's tuition fees through his work as ‚Äãan engine room mechanic.
"They only went there to make a living, to work. They didn‚Äôt ‚Äãknow anything about the bigger things happening in the world. We never thought it would turn out like ‚Äåthis. ‚ÅÝHis parents and relatives are all sad," said his uncle, Dechawat Ratanapakul, 70.
Bangkok has demanded an apology and clarification from Tehran over the incident.
"Thailand is not a party to the conflict, the ship was not carrying anything controversial and there have been ships that have had safe passage ‚Äãand our vessel should ‚Äãhave been treated ‚ÅÝthat way also," Thai Foreign Minister Sihasak Phuangketkeow told reporters in Bangkok on Friday.
The U.S.-Israeli war on Iran has threatened Gulf ports and disrupted global ‚Äãtrade through the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards said in a statement ‚Äãcarried by ‚ÅÝthe Tasnim news agency that the ship was "fired upon by Iranian fighters".
Twenty other crew members abandoned ship in a lifeboat before being rescued by the Omani navy.
The three missing crewmen are believed ‚ÅÝto be ‚Äãin the vessel's engine room, the ship's owner, Precious ‚ÄãShipping (PSL.BK) , opens new tab said previously.
‚ÄúI just want to hug him if he survives. I hope he is safe. I would be ‚Äãso happy," Sommai said.

item.title: Cuban president, under pressure from economic crisis and from Trump, to address media
item.description:
HAVANA, March 13 (Reuters) - Cuban President Miguel Diaz-Canel is scheduled to address Cuban media on Friday in a rare appearance that comes amid a ‚Äãsevere economic crisis and as the Communist government has come ‚Äåunder increasing pressure from U.S. President Donald Trump.
The appearance "to address issues of national and international importance" is scheduled for 7:30 a.m. EDT (1130 GMT), Cuba's government announced on Thursday ‚Äãnight. Though it is rare for the Cuban president to face ‚Äãthe media, foreign press was not invited, and if questions ‚ÅÝare allowed, they will come from carefully screened national reporters.
The address was ‚Äãbilled as a continuation of a February 5 event when Diaz-Canel warned that ‚ÄãCuba was approaching a situation that would require "extreme measures" given the economic crisis, frequent power blackouts and fuel shortages exacerbated by Trump's imposition of an oil blockade on ‚Äãthe Caribbean island.
But Diaz-Canel also reaffirmed Cuba's willingness to engage in talks ‚Äãwith the United States, as long as Cuba was treated with respect and not subject ‚Äåto ‚ÅÝthreats or preconditions.
Diaz-Canel could address repeated statements by Trump that the United States is already in high-level talks with Cuban representatives. The Cuban government has denied that any official encounters are underway but has not explicitly denied media ‚Äãreports of back-channel ‚Äãdiscussions.
Since the U.S. ‚ÅÝcaptured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro and removed from power Cuba's most important foreign benefactor in January, Trump has cut ‚Äãoff Venezuelan oil shipments to Cuba and threatened to ‚Äãslap tariffs ‚ÅÝon any country that sells oil to Cuba.
Trump in recent weeks had made a series of statements, saying Cuba was on the verge of collapse or ‚ÅÝeager ‚Äãto make a deal with the United ‚ÄãStates. On Monday he said Cuba may be subject to a "friendly takeover," then added, "it may not ‚Äãbe a friendly takeover."

item.title: Explosion heard overnight near Turkish military base hosting US troops
item.description:
ANKARA, March 13 (Reuters) - An explosion was heard and windows were shaken overnight near the Incirlik military base in ‚Äãsouthern Turkey where U.S. troops and others are ‚Äåstationed, according to a Reuters witness and social media footage.
NATO air defences stationed in the eastern Mediterranean Sea have in ‚Äãthe last week intercepted two ballistic missiles fired from ‚ÄãIran towards Turkey amid the U.S. and Israeli ‚ÅÝwar on Iran.
NATO has since beefed up its ballistic ‚Äãmissile defences in the region, deploying a U.S. Patriot air ‚Äãdefence system to the southeastern Turkish province of Malatya to increase defence at a radar base there used by the alliance.
Social ‚Äãmedia footage appeared to show a projectile flying ‚Äãin the night sky near the Incirlik Air Base in Adana ‚Äåprovince.
Turkey's ‚ÅÝstate-owned Anadolu news agency said sirens were heard at the base early on Friday, without providing further details.
Ankara has not officially commented on the incident and it ‚Äãwas not immediately ‚Äãclear what ‚ÅÝhad caused the explosion.
Turkey has also not said where the previous missiles fired ‚Äãfrom Iran were aimed at, but U.S. ‚Äãair forces - ‚ÅÝalong with personnel from other countries - are stationed at the Incirlik base.
Ankara says that Washington has not used ‚ÅÝIncirlik ‚Äãin its air assault with Israel ‚Äãagainst Iran, which triggered Tehran's missile and drone attacks.

item.title: Japan to join Trump's 'Golden Dome' project, expects missile requests
item.description:
TOKYO, March 13 (Reuters) - Japan will inform the U.S. next week that it intends to join the "Golden Dome" missile defense initiative and expects that ‚ÄãWashington may seek its help with missile production due to the Middle ‚ÄåEast war and other conflicts, two sources said.
Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi will announce the latest plan when she meets U.S. President Donald Trump in Washington D.C. at a leaders' summit on March 19, the Japanese government ‚Äãsources said, requesting anonymity due to the sensitivity of the matter.
Trump's Golden Dome ‚Äãproject, announced last year with an ambitious 2028 timeline, envisions expanding existing ‚ÅÝground‚Äëbased defenses such as interceptor missiles with more experimental space‚Äëbased elements, meant to detect, ‚Äãtrack and potentially counter incoming threats from orbit.
But the project has made little visible progress so ‚Äãfar. Details of how Japan will participate also remain unclear.
The Yomiuri newspaper, which first reported Japan's plans on Friday, said Tokyo hoped the initiative could be used to defend the country against new ‚Äãhypersonic glide weapons being developed by China and Russia.
Tokyo anticipates that Trump may request ‚ÄãJapan to produce or co-develop missiles that could help replace stocks of U.S. munitions depleted by the ‚ÄåU.S.-Israeli ‚ÅÝwar on Iran, as well as its support for Ukraine, the sources said. It is still considering how to respond to any such request, they added.
Japan exported a batch of surface-to-air Patriot missiles built under license to the United States late last year, marking a ‚Äãhistoric break from its ‚Äãlong-standing ban on ‚ÅÝlethal weapons exports.
The Trump administration is pushing defense contractors to step up production of missiles and other munitions that have been drawn down ‚Äãin recent years. Tokyo is seeking to bolster its own munitions reserves ‚Äãto deter ‚ÅÝan increasingly assertive China and nuclear-armed North Korea.
Patriot interceptors have been critical in intercepting hundreds of Iranian ballistic missiles and drones fired at Gulf countries since the U.S. and Israel ‚ÅÝlaunched their ‚Äãair war against Iran earlier this month.
Ukraine has also ‚Äãrelied on Patriots to defend its energy and military infrastructure since Russia invaded it in 2022.

item.title: Russian seaborne oil product exports in February fall 3.3% from month earlier, data shows
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 13 (Reuters) - Russia's seaborne oil product exports in February 2026 fell 3.3% from ‚ÄãJanuary to 7.97 million metric tons ‚Äåamid unplanned refinery outages and harsh weather conditions at key ports, data from industry sources ‚Äãand Reuters calculations showed.
The oil product ‚Äãexports from Russia's Baltic ports ‚Äî Primorsk, Vysotsk, ‚ÅÝSt. Petersburg and Ust-Luga - decreased 11.4% ‚Äãmonth-on-month to 4.7 million tons, according to ‚Äãthe data.
Unlike the two previous mild winters, non-ice-class tankers have been barred from entering Russian Baltic ‚Äãports since mid-February, while Ice1 and ‚ÄãIce2 vessels have been allowed with icebreaker escorts.
In contrast ‚Äåto ‚ÅÝthe Baltic, oil product exports from Black Sea and Azov Sea ports rose in February by 13.7% from the previous ‚Äãmonth to ‚Äã2.565 million ‚ÅÝtons, the data showed.
Oil product exports from the Arctic ports ‚Äãof Murmansk and Arkhangelsk fell last ‚Äãmonth ‚ÅÝto 30,400 tons, down from 61,200 tons in January.
Fuel export loadings at Russia's ‚ÅÝFar ‚ÄãEast ports increased in February ‚Äãby 8% month-on-month to 665,000 tons, data from industry ‚Äãsources showed.

item.title: Turkey says NATO defences intercepted third missile from Iran, asks Tehran to clarify
item.description:
ANKARA, March 13 (Reuters) - NATO air defences in the eastern Mediterranean have shot down a third ballistic ‚Äãmissile that was fired from Iran towards Turkey, the ‚ÄåTurkish Defence Ministry said on Friday, adding that it was asking Tehran for clarification.
NATO air defences shot down the first Iranian ballistic missile fired ‚Äãat Turkey on March 4, followed by the shooting ‚Äãdown of a second Iranian missile on March 9 . The ‚ÅÝministry has said the first incoming missile was downed en ‚Äãroute to Turkish airspace, while the second entered it.
The missiles ‚Äãincreasingly pose a test for NATO member Ankara and the alliance. Turkey, NATO's second-largest army and Iran's neighbour, has against any more attacks. ‚ÄãIt also protested to Tehran after every incident, but has ‚Äãnot suggested it wants to formally call on alliance members for protection.
"All necessary ‚Äåmeasures ‚ÅÝare being taken decisively and without hesitation against any threat directed at our country's territory and airspace. Consultations are being conducted with the relevant country to clarify all aspects of the ‚Äãincident," the ministry ‚Äãsaid in ‚ÅÝa statement.
There was no immediate comment from Iran, but it has so far repeatedly rejected deliberately ‚Äãtargeting Turkey amid its war with the United ‚ÄãStates ‚ÅÝand Israel.
Following the incidents, NATO has beefed up its ballistic missile defences in the region and deployed a U.S. Patriot air defence ‚ÅÝsystem to ‚Äãthe southeastern Turkish province of Malatya ‚Äãto increase defence at the Kurecik radar base there used by the alliance.

item.title: Four US service members killed in plane crash over Iraq
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - Four of the six crew members aboard a U.S. military aircraft that ‚Äãcrashed in western Iraq are confirmed to have been killed, the U.S. ‚Äåmilitary said on Friday, as rescue efforts continued for the remaining two.
A U.S. military refueling aircraft crashed in western Iraq on Thursday, in an incident the military said involved another aircraft but ‚Äãwas not the result of hostile or friendly fire.
The deaths add to the ‚Äãseven U.S. service members who have already been killed as part ‚ÅÝof U.S. operations against Iran which began on February 28.
"The circumstances of the incident are ‚Äãunder investigation. However, the loss of the aircraft was not due to hostile fire ‚Äãor friendly fire," a statement from U.S. Central Command said.
A U.S. official told Reuters that the second aircraft involved in the crash, which landed safely, was also a military refueling aircraft known as the KC-135.
The ‚ÄãUnited States has deployed a large number of aircraft into the Middle East to ‚Äãtake part in operations against Iran and the incident highlights the risk of not just operations, ‚Äåbut ‚ÅÝof refueling aircraft in the air.
The KC-135, built by Boeing (BA.N) , opens new tab in the 1950s and early 1960s, has served as the backbone of the U.S. military's air refueling fleet and is critical to allowing aircraft to carry out missions without having to land.
The Islamic ‚ÄãResistance in Iraq, an ‚Äãumbrella group of ‚ÅÝIran-backed armed factions, claimed responsibility for downing the U.S. military refueling aircraft.
Reuters reported on Tuesday that as many as 150 U.S. ‚Äãtroops have been wounded in the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran. News ‚Äãof the ‚ÅÝcrash comes the same day two U.S. sailors were injured after the USS Gerald Ford suffered a non-combat-related fire on board.
The first seven U.S. troops were killed when a drone ‚ÅÝslammed into ‚Äãa U.S. military facility in Port Shuaiba, Kuwait.
President ‚ÄãDonald Trump and other senior officials have warned the Iran conflict will result in more U.S. military deaths ‚Äãas Tehran retaliates against U.S. and Israeli strikes.

item.title: How an obscure banker slipped into Brazil‚Äôs political inner circle, triggering a scandal
item.description:
BRASILIA, March 13 (Reuters) - For years Daniel Vorcaro was best known in Brazil‚Äôs financial world as the head of Banco Master, a bank whose breakneck expansion left some analysts puzzled.
Less noticed during Master's ascent was Vorcaro's ability to build a contact book that included Supreme Court justices, the heads ‚Äãof Brazil's Senate and House, and top officials in the country‚Äôs central bank.
But after Vorcaro was arrested last week for the second time for his role in an alleged multibillion-dollar fraud scheme, the contents of his cellphone, ‚Äåleaked by lawmakers who obtained files from a Federal Police investigation, revealed a spider web of influence which could embarrass some of the nation's top power brokers in an election year.
The case is a ticking time bomb, said Senator Alessandro Vieira, who is pushing for a congressional inquiry into Vorcaro's relationship with Supreme Court justices.
‚ÄúThere are very powerful figures in the Republic with clear involvement,‚Äù said Vieira, whose party is part of the ruling coalition.
Some are already facing scrutiny. Two central bank officials were pushed out of their roles when regulators found they had been advising Vorcaro. Supreme Court Justice Dias Toffoli stepped down from overseeing a Federal Police investigation into ‚Äãthe fraud case after the Brazilian press revealed his family's company had financial links to the banker. Toffoli said in a statement at the time that he never received any payments from Master or Vorcaro.
In July, as Vorcaro worked to save his bank from ‚Äãliquidation, he vented to his girlfriend in a text message that ‚Äúthis bank business ... is just like the mafia," one of several occasions when he suggested that the country's dominant lenders were out to get ‚ÅÝMaster.
Federal Police investigators found text messages that suggest Vorcaro plotted to intimidate people he viewed as foes, including a journalist, with the help of an associate he called "Sicario," as hit men employed by Mexican drug cartels are known, according to the leaked files.
Vorcaro's lawyers denied, in ‚Äãa statement, that Vorcaro had committed any irregularities or fraud, intimidated journalists, coopted public agents, or took any action to interfere with law‚Äëenforcement work.
Vorcaro, 42, got his start in his family's real estate business. His rapid rise in finance began when he bought a troubled ‚Äãbank, rebranded as Banco Master in 2021, initially used to finance property developments.
His lack of a track record in banking and preference for open‚Äëcollar shirts without a tie set him apart from the buttoned-up world of Brazil's financial sector.
But what Master was selling drew scrutiny.
The bank's debt securities offered above-average returns as the bank invested in extremely risky assets, such as payouts in lawsuits against the government, which can take years to materialize.
Master's biggest calling card was that its deposits were covered by the Credit Guarantee Fund, which is financed with mandatory contributions from banks.
But when regulators tightened rules in 2023 on the capital required ‚Äãto support securities backed by legal payouts, the bank faced a liquidity crisis.
Vorcaro promised regulators he would raise $3 billion in the following year to address the problem.
In a 2025 deposition, Vorcaro blamed an ensuing "reputational campaign against the bank" as well as anticompetitive behavior by Brazil's top ‚Äãbanks for his failure to stem the crisis.
Even as Master was teetering, documents obtained in the police investigation, seen by Reuters, show he also spent millions of dollars on events that seemed designed to cement his access to the country's political elite.
In April 2024, for example, he funded a $6 million "ideas forum" in ‚ÄåLondon that attracted ‚ÅÝluminaries including Supreme Court justices and the head of the Federal Police. The event closed with a $640,000 tasting of Macallan whisky, the documents show.
Vorcaro separately hired the wife of Brazil's most powerful Supreme Court justice, Alexandre de Moraes, as the bank's lawyer. In a statement, the office of Viviane Barci de Moraes detailed several services it performed for the bank, but stressed that it never represented it in the Supreme Court.
Another adviser, former finance minister Guido Mantega, helped the banker arrange a December meeting with President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva and the head of Brazil's central bank to discuss what he described as his struggle against big banks. Mantega did not reply to a request for comment.
In an interview with news website UOL last month, Lula said he told Vorcaro that there would be no political interference in his bank's case, but "a ‚Äãtechnical investigation conducted by the central bank."
Reuters confirmed the president's account ‚Äãwith an official who was present at the meeting.
Vorcaro's underdog ‚ÅÝnarrative has at times clashed with descriptions of an extravagant lifestyle.
A party he threw for his girlfriend in Saint-Tropez, France in 2025, flying more than 30 guests in private jets, drew particular attention.
Documents reviewed by Reuters show Vorcaro spent at least $120 million on luxurious trips and parties between 2024 and April 2025. The provenance of those funds remains unclear.
For a medium-sized bank with 80 ‚Äãbillion reais ($15 billion) in assets, regulators would expect Master to have liquidity of 3‚ÄØ billion or 4 ‚ÄØbillion reais in unencumbered securities, Ailton de Aquino Santos, a central bank official, told the police ‚Äãin a deposition, or about $530 million to $700 ‚ÅÝmillion.
But regulators examining its books in 2024 found that Master only had 4 ‚ÄØmillion reais in cash, he said.
Vorcaro and his allies tried many avenues to keep the bank alive after regulators flagged the liquidity problems, including raising hundreds of millions of dollars from public servants' pension funds, lobbying for congressional relief and finally trying to sell Master to a state-owned bank .
None of that seemed to work.
In November, investigation documents suggest Vorcaro was texting an unidentified number in what seemed like a final effort to stave off disaster: "Did you manage to block it?" one of the messages said.
Vorcaro was arrested that ‚ÅÝnight at an ‚Äãairport in Sao Paulo. Police have said they believe he wanted to flee the country. The central bank liquidated Master the next day. A judge later freed him, ‚Äãbut police arrested him again last week, arguing he was interfering with the investigation.
Weeks later, newspaper O Globo reported the texts Vorcaro exchanged on the day of his arrest had been sent to Justice de Moraes, which the judge denies.
Still, Vorcaro's political reach has many in Brasilia worried about whether others might be implicated in the investigations, Vieira ‚Äãsaid.
"Facts influence politics," he said. And the facts, he added, "are very alarming, it's impossible to hide them."
($1 = 5.1792 reais)

item.title: ASEAN ministers urge halt to Middle East war as crisis rattles energy and trade
item.description:
MANILA, March 13 (Reuters) - ASEAN foreign and economic ministers on Friday called for an immediate halt to the war in ‚Äãthe Middle East, and said the effects of surging oil prices and ‚Äådisrupted trade are already hitting Southeast Asia's economies.
Several members of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations have begun rolling out measures to counter the economic impact, with governments moving quickly to ‚Äãconserve energy, stabilise domestic markets and protect vulnerable sectors such as tourism.
"We expressed ‚Äãserious concern over the situation in the Middle East and its ‚ÅÝimpacts in the region, and emphasised the importance of the immediate cessation of ‚Äãhostilities," Philippine Foreign Affairs Secretary Ma. Theresa Lazaro told a press conference after a ‚Äãspecial meeting on the crisis, adding that ASEAN called on all parties to exercise the utmost self-restraint.
The Philippines, which is chairing ASEAN this year, convened the special meeting as concerns over the ‚ÄãIran conflict deepened. The ministers called for global energy supply chains to be kept ‚Äãopen and to activate regional mechanisms to mitigate the economic fallout.
Crude oil is trading near $100 a ‚Äåbarrel ‚ÅÝon worries about supplies, which have been heightened by the vow from Iran‚Äôs new supreme leader to keep the Strait of Hormuz, the route for a fifth of global oil supply, closed.
"The escalating conflict has generated broader economic repercussions beyond the region, particularly ‚Äãthrough heightened volatility in ‚Äãglobal energy markets ‚ÅÝand disruption of key maritime and supply chain routes," the economic ministers of the 11-member bloc said in a statement after ‚Äãa separate meeting.
Lazaro said the Philippines, which is heavily dependent ‚Äãon imported ‚ÅÝfuel from the Middle East, was considering buying oil from Russia, but she did not elaborate.
The economic ministers warned that the region‚Äôs exposure to global oil and LNG supply ‚ÅÝroutes ‚Äãmade it especially vulnerable to further shocks, adding ‚Äãthat reinforcing supply chain resilience, accelerating renewable energy transitions and deepening regional cooperation would be essential to preserving ‚Äãeconomic stability.

item.title: Exclusive: Sinopec plans to cut crude runs by over 10% on Mideast supply squeeze, sources say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 13 (Reuters) - China's Sinopec, the world's ‚Äãbiggest refiner by capacity, aims to cut throughput this month by more than 10% from its original plan ‚Äåin response to a crude supply gap caused by the war in the Middle East, two sources familiar with its operations said.
The cuts by state-owned Sinopec, which accounts for a third of China's refinery output, are part of Beijing's widening measures to curb oil supply disruptions due to Iran's ‚Äãblockage of the Strait of Hormuz, a conduit for 20% of the world's oil.
Throughput is likely to fall by ‚Äã600,000 to 700,000 barrels per day (bpd) on average in March, the two sources estimated, adding that ‚ÅÝthe cuts exclude losses from plant maintenance planned before the Israel-U.S. war on Iran began on February 28.
A Sinopec representative said the ‚Äãcompany does not comment on operational matters.
Sinopec imports roughly 4 million bpd of crude oil, of which 2.4 million bpd come from ‚Äãthe Middle East, including regular shipments under yearly contracts from Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Iraq and Qatar.
China, the world's biggest oil importer, brought in 11.55 million bpd last year, roughly half from the Middle East.
This week, Beijing ordered an immediate ban on exports of diesel, gasoline ‚Äãand aviation fuel to prioritise domestic supply. It also rejected Sinopec's request to tap a government-controlled oil reserve, Reuters reported.
The planned cuts ‚Äãrepresent a decline of 11% to 13% from an initial plan to process 5.2 million bpd in March, one of the sources said.
"Sinopec has ‚Äålittle option ‚ÅÝother than cutting runs, and immediately," said the second person.
In Asia, which buys 60% of its oil from the Middle East, refiners had already shut at least 1 million barrels of capacity since the start of the war, Reuters has reported.
Nearly 1.9 million bpd of Gulf refining capacity has been shut due to the war, consultancy IIR said on Tuesday.
In a further step to ‚Äãavert a shortage of domestic ‚Äãfuel, Sinopec will focus on ‚ÅÝmaximising fuel output at the expense of petrochemicals production, which garners weaker margins, four people familiar with the matter said.
Sinopec's run cuts include last week's shutdown of an 80,000-bpd crude unit at its ‚ÄãFujian Refining & Petrochemical Corp unit.
That facility also cut operations by 20% to 30% at its 1.1 ‚Äãmillion ton-per-year steam ‚ÅÝcracker, two people said this week.
Separately, Sinopec's Zhenhai Refining and Chemical Corp also slashed operation rates at both of its steam crackers to around 70-80% of their combined capacity of 2.2 million tpy, one of the sources said.
