On Monday, Sinopec-invested Fujian Gulei Petrochemical shut down ‚ÅÝits full ‚Äãcomplex, comprising a 1.1-million-tpy steam cracker, for maintenance through April, the company ‚Äãsaid in a notice.
Asia's naphtha market is grappling with a lack of supplies , as the region takes about 60% of the petrochemical feedstock from the Middle East, with ‚Äãsteam crackers cutting runs and declaring force majeure since last week.

item.title: Kremlin says Russia and US share interest in stabilising energy markets
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 13 (Reuters) - Russia sees a U.S. sanctions waiver on its oil as an attempt by Washington to stabilise global energy markets, ‚Äãand the two countries have a shared interest ‚Äãin this, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said ‚ÅÝon Friday.
The United States issued a 30-day waiver for ‚Äãcountries to buy sanctioned Russian oil and petroleum products ‚Äãcurrently at sea, in what Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said was a step to stabilise global markets roiled by the Iran ‚Äãwar .
Without significant volumes of Russian oil entering the ‚Äãmarket, such stabilisation is impossible, Peskov said.
"We see actions by the ‚ÄåUnited ‚ÅÝStates aimed at trying to stabilise energy markets. In this respect, our interests coincide," he said.
The move, the second significant rollback of Ukraine war-related U.S. sanctions in just ‚Äãover one week, was ‚Äãthe latest attempt by President ‚ÅÝDonald Trump's administration to tame energy prices after the U.S. and Israeli strikes ‚Äãon Iran paralysed shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
Peskov ‚Äãsaid ‚ÅÝthere was a risk the global energy crisis could escalate.
"Of course such actions will, to some extent, help ‚ÅÝstabilise ‚Äãthis market, because without significant ‚Äãvolumes of Russian oil, market stabilization is impossible," he said.

item.title: Zelenskiy heads to France as Iran war distracts from Ukraine
item.description:
PARIS, March 13 (Reuters) - Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy will seek reassurances of support from one of Kyiv‚Äôs key Western allies on Friday, arriving ‚Äãin Paris at a time when the war in the ‚ÄåMiddle East is diverting attention from Ukraine‚Äôs fight against Russia.
The widening conflict has raised questions about the supply of weapons ‚Äì notably air defences ‚Äì from Western partners to ‚ÄãUkraine, as Gulf Arab states draw down their own air defence ‚Äãstocks to repel daily attacks by Iran.
Zelenskiy has warned Ukraine's ‚ÅÝacute shortage of air defence missiles will be exacerbated. This week ‚Äãhe said that Gulf states had used more PAC‚Äë3 air defence missiles ‚Äãagainst Iranian strikes in a few days than Kyiv had received from Washington over four years.
Zelenskiy did not specify the source of his figures.
The war has also sent ‚Äãthe price of oil, an important revenue source for Russia, surging while ‚ÄãKyiv struggles on financially.
The European Union has yet to agree on a proposed 90 ‚Äåbillion ‚ÅÝeuro loan that would, in part, provide Kyiv with funding to purchase weapons. Ukraine hopes the loan will be in place by mid‚ÄëApril.
"The key message of this visit, and its primary purpose, is to show that ‚Äãnothing ‚Äì no crisis, ‚Äãno development ‚Äì will ‚ÅÝdivert our attention from Ukraine, which remains for us a major security issue," a French presidency official told ‚Äãreporters ahead of the visit.
"Our support for Ukraine will ‚Äãnot waver."
Zelenksiy ‚ÅÝhas sought to show Gulf Arab states ‚Äì many of which maintain close ties with Moscow ‚Äì that Ukraine can be a useful and reliable partner.
The Ukrainian ‚ÅÝleader ‚Äãhas offered Ukrainian drone interception technology in ‚Äãexchange for air defence missiles.
Ukraine sent experts and officials to four Middle Eastern countries this week ‚Äãas part of those efforts.

item.title: Mexico's heartthrob security minister now available as miniature doll, shirtless or dressed as Batman
item.description:
MEXICO CITY, March 13 (Reuters) - Mexico's Security Minister Omar Garcia Harfuch ‚Äãin real life wears a suit, but he can also now be found in Mexican markets ‚Äåand stores as a miniature doll, dressed as Batman, or sometimes even shirtless.
Cuddly blankets, soft toys and cardboard cutouts featuring the minister have gone viral in the Latin American country following the February 22 raid that killed infamous cartel leader Nemesio Oseguera, better known as El Mencho, head ‚Äãof the brutal Jalisco New Generation Cartel.
Images of 44-year-old Garcia Harfuch, who has become a heartthrob in Mexico thanks ‚Äãto his good looks, now adorn a long list of products: party decorations, cushions, towels and ‚ÅÝdolls known as "Harfuchitos" or little Harfuches.
Political and security analysts say Garcia Harfuch is a likely presidential contender in 2030, when ‚ÄãPresident Claudia Sheinbaum's term ends.
Early polling , opens new tab shows Garcia Harfuch ahead of other prominent political figures, including Mexico's Economy Minister Marcelo Ebrard, who sought ‚Äãthe candidacy in 2023 and is considered likely to try to run again.
Being immortalized as a doll is an honor most often reserved for presidents in Mexico, as well as the beloved mascot of the country's omnipresent chain of pharmacies , Dr. Simi.
Garcia Harfuch's popularity has exploded following the raid ‚Äãthat killed El Mencho.
The operation, which Garcia Harfuch helped lead, was personal for the security chief, who blamed El Mencho for a ‚Äã2020 assassination attempt that left him with three bullet wounds and killed two of his bodyguards.
Garcia Harfuch did not respond to Reuters' request ‚Äåfor ‚ÅÝcomment regarding the popularity of merchandise featuring his image.
"He is Mexico's crush," said Carolina Garcia, who sells Garcia Harfuch merchandise on demand from her home printing business in Tlaxcala state. "Of course, we get more orders from women, as gifts, but we've also gotten some orders from men."
Meanwhile, images that appear AI-generated showing the politician promoting products such as hair oil have proliferated ‚Äãonline, as have apps and ‚Äãtutorials that allow users ‚ÅÝto generate photos of themselves hugging Garcia Harfuch or lying together on the grass.
On MercadoLibre (MELI.O) , opens new tab , Latin America's top online marketplace, a Harfuch blanket was listed among the platform's bestsellers. Amazon (AMZN.O) , opens new tab , meanwhile, offered a ‚Äãbed-sized flannel throw emblazoned with the minister's face.
Ingrid Rebeca Sanchez, 22, who designs Garcia Harfuch merchandise ‚Äãat a factory ‚ÅÝin Mexico City, told Reuters the products were initially slow to take off, but since El Mencho's killing, business has boomed, with as many as 150 blankets selling per day and orders even coming from the United States.
Now, the factory is ‚ÅÝalmost exclusively ‚Äãproducing Garcia Harfuch products and Sanchez is working on a new line of ‚Äãbobbleheads and life-size pillows.
"All the ladies love him, they want to sleep with him, they want to dry themselves off with him," she said. "He's Mexico's ‚Äãnew daddy."

item.title: Explainer: Why haven‚Äôt the Houthis, Iran‚Äôs allies in Yemen, stepped into the war?
item.description:
RIYADH, March 13 (Reuters) - Iran's Shi'ite allies in Lebanon and Iraq have joined the war in the region unleashed by U.S. and Israeli strikes on Tehran. But Yemen's Houthi rebels, heavily armed and capable of striking Gulf neighbours and causing major disruption to maritime navigation around ‚Äãthe Arabian Peninsula, have not yet entered the fray.
Here is a look at why that might be:
The Houthis are a military, political and religious movement led by the Houthi family and based in northern Yemen. They adhere to the Zaydi sect of Shi'ite Islam.
The Houthis have a history of fighting guerrilla wars with the Yemeni army but expanded their power and built closer ties with Iran after the 2011 "Arab ‚ÄãSpring" protests.
Seizing on instability in the country, the group captured the Yemeni capital Sanaa in 2014.
The following year, Saudi Arabia led ‚Äãa coalition of Arab states in a military intervention to attempt to dislodge the group.
The Houthis demonstrated significant ‚ÅÝmissile and drone capabilities, attacking oil installations and vital infrastructure in Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates.
After years of fighting that led ‚Äãto one of the world's worst humanitarian crises, the U.N. brokered a 2022 truce between the warring sides in Yemen that has since held.
After the October 7, 2023, attack on Israel led by Palestinian militant group Hamas, which triggered a devastating Israeli military campaign in Gaza, the Houthis began firing on international shipping in the Red Sea, saying it was doing so in support of Palestinians.
They also fired drones and missiles at Israel, which responded with air ‚Äãstrikes on Houthi targets. The U.S. also launched strikes against the Houthis.
The Houthis ceased their attacks following a U.S.-brokered ceasefire between Israel and Hamas ‚Äãin October 2025.
On March 5, Houthi leader Abdul Malik Al-Houthi said his group was ready to strike at any ‚Äåmoment.
‚ÄúRegarding military ‚ÅÝescalation and action, our fingers are on the trigger at any moment should developments warrant it," he said in a televised speech.
But unlike Lebanon's Hezbollah and Iraqi armed groups , they have not made any formal announcement of joining the war.
Houthi religious doctrine does not adhere to Iran's supreme leader in the same way Hezbollah and the Iraqi groups do.
While Iran champions the Houthis as part of its regional "Axis of Resistance", Yemen experts ‚Äãsay the movement is motivated primarily ‚Äãby a domestic agenda though ‚ÅÝthey share a political affinity with Iran and Hezbollah.
The U.S. says Iran has armed, funded and trained the Houthis with help from Hezbollah.
The Houthis deny being an Iranian proxy and say they develop their own ‚Äãweapons.
Observers have been split on what course of action the Houthis, a notoriously ‚Äãmercurial group, may ‚ÅÝtake.
Some diplomats and analysts believe they may have already undertaken individual attacks on targets in neighbouring states. Reuters could not substantiate those claims.
Others say the Houthis are keeping their powder dry for an opportune moment to enter the conflict, in coordination with Iran, in order to exert maximum pressure.
The effective closure ‚ÅÝof the ‚ÄãStrait of Hormuz to Gulf Arab hydrocarbon exports and a shift to heavy reliance ‚Äãon the Red Sea might provide such an opportunity.
Lastly, given growing economic pressure at home and the likelihood of intense U.S., Israeli and even Saudi attacks should they join ‚Äãthe war, some analysts say the Houthis may decide to sit out the conflict altogether.

item.title: Brazil's jailed ex-president Bolsonaro taken to hospital after feeling ill
item.description: SAO PAULO, March 13 (Reuters) - Brazil's imprisoned ‚Äåformer President Jair ‚ÄãBolsonaro ‚Äãwas taken to ‚ÅÝhospital early ‚Äãon ‚ÄãFriday after waking up with ‚Äãchills ‚Äãand vomiting, his ‚Äåson, ‚ÅÝSenator Flavio Bolsonaro, said ‚Äãin ‚Äãa ‚ÅÝpost on ‚ÄãX.

item.title: Rosatom boss warns of 'increased military risks' around Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 13 (Reuters) - The head of Russia's state ‚Äãnuclear corporation Rosatom warned on Friday ‚Äåof "increased military risks" around Ukraine's Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant due to intensifying combat in the ‚Äãarea.
"The number of artillery ‚Äãstrikes, drone attacks and mortar fire ‚ÅÝhas been increasing in recent ‚Äãmonths," Alexei Likhachev said following ‚Äãa meeting with International Atomic Energy Agency Director General Rafael Grossi in Moscow.
"The ‚Äãindustrial zone is under constant ‚Äãattack from both artillery and drones, which ‚Äåis ‚ÅÝcausing power cuts from time to time," he added.
Likhachev said Russia welcomed an initiative by Grossi ‚Äãto establish ‚Äãa ceasefire ‚ÅÝaround the nuclear plant, which has seen heavy ‚Äãfighting between Russian and ‚ÄãUkrainian ‚ÅÝforces, in order to restore the power supply.
Such a ceasefire was ‚ÅÝdeclared ‚Äãfor the fifth time ‚Äãearlier this month.

item.title: Morning Bid: Markets over a barrel
item.description:
March 13 - Everything Mike Dolan and the ROI team are excited to read, watch and listen to over the weekend.
From the Editor
Hello Morning Bid readers!
Wall Street just recorded its worst day since the Iran war began, as markets contemplated a shuttered Strait of Hormuz, an escalating tanker war and triple-digit oil.
Given that backdrop, however ‚Äì and indications that the conflict may be far from over despite President Donald Trump‚Äôs statements to the contrary ‚Äì the roughly 1.5% drops posted by the ‚ÄãS&P 500, Dow and Nasdaq on Thursday seem like barely a flesh wound.
Indeed, U.S. stocks, unlike their Asian and European counterparts, have been remarkably calm during the past two weeks as energy markets have been roiled.
Investors may be betting ‚Äåthat Trump will eventually pull back to avoid more market pain, the so-called ‚ÄòTACO‚Äô trade (‚ÄúTrump always chickens out‚Äù). But the problem is that even if the president does wrap up U.S. involvement in Iran quickly, it may not matter. Given the damage that has already occurred in the Middle East, this may be one TACO too many .
The week in oil has been truly historic. It kicked off on Monday with a record-breaking $35 intraday move in Brent crude, as prices neared $120 per barrel only to fall below $90 at one point. Since then, prices have remained volatile, spiking 9% on Thursday to back above $100/bbl amid Tehran‚Äôs bombastic threats that crude could ‚Äãreach $200.
This occurred even after the International Energy Agency said on Wednesday that its 32 member countries were moving forward with a 400-million-barrel reserve release ‚Äì the biggest collective drawdown ever. That market response is understandable, though, as this emergency move appears to be ‚Äãlittle more than a very large Band-Aid .
One of the more disturbing elements of this crisis is that no one seems to have any clue how to price it , with the physical market often ‚ÅÝexhibiting far more pain than paper trading has reflected. It‚Äôs telling that an errant social media post from U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright was enough to send prices lower on Tuesday.
(For an in-depth discussion on the energy market confusion, watch ROI Asia Commodities Columnist Clyde Russell ‚Äãon the Gulf Intelligence podcast , opens new tab .)
The region most at risk remains Asia, which imports the vast majority of its energy from the Middle East. The biggest squeezes are in refined fuel products, like gasoline, diesel and jet fuel. In response, the U.S. is now temporarily lifting some restrictions on ‚Äãbuying Russian oil and petroleum products.
While we don‚Äôt know when or how this conflict will end, a few things are clear. One, you can rip up all of those oil supply glut forecasts , and two, you might want to question previous arguments about fossil fuels being inherently more reliable than renewables .
U.S. consumers are already feeling the pinch from the energy crisis, with average gasoline prices soaring 20% since the war began to $3.58 per gallon , as of Wednesday.
On the topic of price rises, U.S. inflation figures were released on Wednesday, with the consumer price index rising 2.4% in the 12 months through February, unchanged from the prior ‚Äãmonth, despite a modest uptick in the month-over-month gain. Given that this data predates the outbreak of the war in Iran, markets paid the announcement little heed.
Today‚Äôs release of personal consumption expenditures inflation data may be watched a bit more closely, as it‚Äôs the ‚ÄãFederal Reserve‚Äôs preferred inflation gauge and is likely to remain well above the 2% target.
Speaking of central banks, almost all of the big ones have meetings next week, including the Fed, Bank of England, European Central Bank, and the Reserve Bank of Australia, among others. Only the RBA ‚Äåis expected to move ‚Äì a ‚ÅÝhike is expected ‚Äì but the real news will be the communications. Markets will be keen to hear how policymakers are approaching what is shaping up to be the biggest crisis since the pandemic.
Finally, away from the Middle East, investors are increasingly worrying about risks hiding in the opaque private credit markets. JPMorgan this week said it was marking down the value of some loans to private credit funds . Worryingly, parallels are beginning to emerge between today‚Äôs private credit tremors and those in U.S. subprime housing that led to the 2007-09 global financial crisis .
That certainly doesn‚Äôt mean another financial meltdown is around the corner, but it‚Äôs a reminder that when exogenous shocks occur, pockets of risk in financial markets are often revealed .
For more data-driven insights on markets and commodities, check out Reuters Open Interest . You can learn:
I‚Äôd love to hear from you, so please reach out to me at anna.szymanski@thomsonreuters.com , opens new tab .
This weekend, we're reading...
JAMIE MCGEEVER, ROI Markets Columnist: Amid Reuters' strong coverage of the war in the Middle East, this recent piece by fellow ROI columnist Ron ‚ÄãBousso stands out. It cuts to the heart of why this crisis's economic and market impact will endure.
GAVIN MAGUIRE, ROI Global Energy Transition Columnist: This recent report from JPMorgan , opens new tab , appropriately titled "Fighting Words‚Äù, covers ‚Äãall things energy.
ANNA SZYMANSKI, ROI Editor-in-Charge: This ‚ÅÝsharp analysis by Reuters Breakingviews' Hugo Dixon outlines what the Iran conflict means for Europe. Short answer - nothing good. But he argues Europe can still emerge with a stronger hand if it treats this as a wake-up call to strengthen and unite.
We're listening to...
MIKE DOLAN, ROI Finance & Markets Columnist: On this Bruegel think tank podcast , opens new tab , former Dutch central bank chief Klaas Knot discusses the ECB's best response to today's oil shock, explains how it compares with 2022, and explores Europe's emerging "Industrial Accelerator" policies.
RON BOUSSO, ROI Energy Columnist: This episode of the Foreign Affairs podcast , opens new tab with American foreign ‚ÅÝpolicy experts Nate Swanson ‚Äãand Richard Haass clearly explains how U.S. relations with Iran have evolved over the years and how the current war fits into the countries' decades-long tensions.
And ‚Äãwe're watching...
JAMIE MCGEEVER, ROI Markets Columnist: In this wide‚Äëranging conversation , opens new tab with economist Joe Stiglitz on Jack Farley's Monetary Matters podcast, the Nobel laureate shares his views on the economic impact of the Middle East conflict, artificial intelligence, tariffs, and the Trump agenda.
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the ‚Äãnewsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the authors. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: France and Italy open talks with Iran on securing safe Hormuz passage, FT reports
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - France and Italy ‚Äãhave opened ‚Äåtalks with Iran seeking ‚Äãto ‚Äãnegotiate a deal to ‚ÅÝguarantee ‚Äãsafe passage ‚Äãfor their ships through the ‚ÄãStrait of ‚ÄãHormuz, the Financial ‚ÄåTimes ‚ÅÝreported on Friday, citing people ‚Äãbriefed ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝthe efforts.
Reuters could ‚Äãnot immediately ‚Äãverify ‚ÅÝthe report.

item.title: Paris mayoral race tests support for green transformation
item.description:
PARIS, March 13 (Reuters) - Marion Soulet rides to Paris' City Hall along a once car-choked road that is now a cycleway and a symbol of the French capital's urban green transformation that faces a test in a mayoral election ‚Äãon Sunday.
Soulet welcomes the construction of about 1,000 km (620 miles) of cycle lanes by leftist Mayor Anne Hidalgo over the last decade, which she ‚Äåsays means nearly half of Parisians now ride a bicycle at least once a week.
"The more the city is redesigned to accommodate it, the more cycling increases," Soulet, head of the Paris en Selle cyclists' group, told Reuters after rolling to a halt following her ride along the Rue de Rivoli. "People like it because it's easy, inexpensive, and fast."
The push to transform Paris from a polluted metropolis into a "15-minute-city" ‚Äãof bike lanes with more trees is the result of efforts by Hidalgo and her leftist predecessors, who have run City Hall for a generation.
That ecological legacy ‚Äãfaces a reckoning in Sunday's election, with Hidalgo not running and right-wing rivals hoping to profit from voter fatigue over the increasingly car-free ‚ÅÝcity, roadwork disruptions and mounting debt.
Opinion polls suggest the winner will either be Socialist Emmanuel Gr√©goire, who wants to double down on the green agenda, or conservative former minister ‚ÄãRachida Dati, who says the classical allure of Paris is being destroyed.
Sarah Knafo , a far-right nationalist aged 32, is rising in the polls and could complicate matters for Dati if she ‚Äãreaches the second round on March 22. Knafo polls above the 10% threshold needed to reach a run-off.
Gr√©goire, 48, is polling at around 33%, with Dati, 60, on about 30%.
"We're not fighting an ideological battle on mobility issues," Dati told Reuters while greeting shoppers in northern Paris. "We just want things to be organised."
Under Hidalgo, city authorities have sought to adapt Paris ‚Äãto climate change and make it more liveable for its 2 million residents, within a wider metropolitan population of 10 million.
City Hall has planted 130,000 trees and removed tens of ‚Äãthousands of on-street parking spaces. Highways along the River Seine have been pedestrianised.
Car traffic has fallen by over 60% since 2002 and bike use has more than tripled, according to city hall data. ‚ÄåAir pollution ‚ÅÝhas improved.
"There aren't many major cities in the world that have known such a spectacular transformation," said Patrick Le Gales, an urbanist at Paris' Sciences-Po University.
"But there's been strong criticism over cleanliness and the debt," he said, referring to a municipal debt of around 10 billion euros ($11 billion), up 42% since 2020.
Pierre Chasseray, head of motorists' lobby group 40 Million Motorists, said Hidalgo had built a "Berlin Wall" between wealthy inhabitants in the centre of Paris and car-reliant residents from poorer suburbs with no say over city decisions.
"We've ended up with a ‚Äãcaricatured image of the capital: motorists on ‚Äãone side, cyclists on the other ‚Äî the ‚ÅÝgood guys versus the bad guys," he said.
Hidalgo has also faced viral social media posts using the #saccageParis hashtag that highlight social blight - everything from chronic roadworks to trash-strewn sidewalks.
Gr√©goire put this down to Hidalgo being too ambitious.
"We did too many things at the same ‚Äãtime," he said. "I would have chosen a different timetable, above all for reasons of implementation quality."
Dati, a lawyer ‚Äãof North African descent, ‚ÅÝhas toned down criticism of popular cycle lanes to focus on condemning dirty streets, and released a video of herself in a fluorescent jacket, joining garbage crews on their rounds.
"The city is increasingly dirty ‚Äî it hasn't escaped anybody," she said.
Dati's increasingly moderate stance on transportation issues - and the fact she faces a September trial on corruption charges she denies - has provided an opening for ‚ÅÝKnafo.
Knafo has ‚Äãunveiled a plan generated by Artificial Intelligence to return cars to the banks of the Seine and staged ‚Äãinterviews from the passenger seat while cruising through Paris.
Soulet believes Knafo's appeal is limited to "a very small group of Parisians who...want to turn the clock back."

item.title: Incumbent Sassou favoured in Congo Republic ballot amid rights concerns
item.description:
DAKAR/BRAZZAVILLE, March 13 (Reuters) - The Republic of Congo heads to the polls on Sunday in a presidential election ‚Äãexpected to keep longtime ruler Denis Sassou Nguesso in power, with his challengers hampered by voter apathy ‚Äåand what rights groups describe as an atmosphere of repression.
Sassou , 82, has ruled the oil-producing Central African nation for all but five years since 1979. He faces six little-known challengers after the main opposition parties opted not to field candidates.
Two of the best-known opposition figures, General Jean-Marie ‚ÄãMichel Mokoko and Andr√© Okombi Salissa, have been in jail for nearly a decade after being convicted of ‚Äãundermining state security.
Much of the pre-election unease stems from what rights groups describe ‚ÅÝas a clampdown on dissent.
Activists have been arrested, several opposition parties suspended, and public gatherings closely monitored, said Joe ‚ÄãWashington Ebina, a prominent human rights activist.
He added that a recent violent crackdown by the state on youth gangs, known locally ‚Äãas "b√©b√©s noirs", had further heightened tensions and "created a climate where people no longer feel safe leaving their homes and expressing themselves".
Security forces cast their ballots on Thursday.
"Intimidation and repression by state security forces will once again play a key role in sustaining Sassou Nguesso's rule. The ‚Äãtactic of having security forces vote before election day - freeing them to patrol polling stations - proved effective in 2021," ‚Äãsaid Maja Bovcon, an independent consultant focused on West and Central Africa.
In cities across Congo this week, Sassou's posters ‚Äålined ‚ÅÝmajor roundabouts, while small groups of opposition campaigners handed out leaflets in markets with little visibility.
‚ÄúEverywhere I look, I see only the president's face, as if the others weren‚Äôt even in the race,‚Äù said Rosalie Tsianko, a communications professional in Brazzaville.
Prime Minister Anatole Collinet Makosso rejected accusations of repression and political bias, telling Reuters that ‚Äúno election is won in advance‚Äù. He ‚Äãsaid Congo was equipped with ‚Äãoversight bodies ‚Äúto ensure transparency, ‚ÅÝfairness and the vitality of our democracy‚Äù.
Some voters backed Sassou's emphasis on stability. ‚ÄúI support him because he is a man of peace,‚Äù said businessman Roger Ennel Ahoue.
Meanwhile, opposition candidate Uphrem ‚ÄãDave Mafoula urged a small crowd of supporters at a rally in Brazzaville this week ‚Äãto consider an ‚ÅÝalternative. "Forty years of suffering is enough,‚Äù he said, calling Sassou's economic and social record negative.
Over 3.2 million Congolese are eligible to vote, but many say they won't, expecting little suspense.
‚ÄúI prefer to stay at home rather than go and vote,‚Äù ‚ÅÝsaid Boudibangoyi ‚ÄãBrunel, a student in Brazzaville.
Official turnout was nearly 68% in the 2021 ‚Äãpresidential election, but civil society groups predict it will now be lower.
"The actual abstention rate will be very high. Congolese people are tired," Ebina said.

item.title: No evidence Iran has mined Strait of Hormuz, Pentagon's Hegseth says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - There is no ‚Äãclear evidence that Iran has placed ‚Äåmines in the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told a ‚Äãnews briefing on Friday.
The assessment ‚Äãby Hegseth contrasts with news reports ‚ÅÝearlier this week suggesting Iran ‚Äãhad deployed about a dozen mines ‚Äãin the Strait of Hormuz, the conduit for a fifth of the world's oil.
Asked ‚Äãabout the possibility of mines in ‚Äãthe Strait, Hegseth said: "We've heard them talk ‚Äåabout ‚ÅÝit just like you've reported recklessly and wildly about it. But ... we have no clear evidence of that." Hegseth's ‚Äãcomments come ‚Äãas ‚ÅÝthe United States and other countries face spiking oil ‚Äãand gas prices with the ‚ÄãU.S-Israel ‚ÅÝwar entering its 14th day, with oil prices hovering near $100 a barrel ‚ÅÝon ‚ÄãFriday.

item.title: France's position in Middle East is defensive, Macron says, after attack on its soldiers
item.description:
PARIS, March 13 (Reuters) - President Emmanuel Macron said on ‚ÄãFriday a drone ‚Äåattack in Northern Iraq against French soldiers was "unacceptable" ‚Äãand that ‚ÄãFrance's position in the region ‚ÅÝwas "defensive".
Macron told a ‚Äãnews conference with ‚ÄãUkrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy in Paris that he had ‚Äãasked for a ‚Äãfull military analysis to be ‚Äåmade ‚ÅÝof the attack.
One French soldier was killed and six others were ‚Äãwounded ‚Äãafter ‚ÅÝa drone attack in northern ‚ÄãIraq that President ‚ÄãEmmanuel ‚ÅÝMacron earlier had said was said was ‚ÅÝunacceptable ‚Äãand unjustified.

item.title: Hegseth says US military has designated officer to complete probe on Iran school strike
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - The United ‚ÄåStates has designated an officer outside of U.S. Central Command to complete an investigation on a ‚Äãstrike on an Iranian school, ‚ÄãU.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth ‚ÅÝsaid on Friday.
"I can report ‚Äãthat CENTCOM has designated an ‚Äãinvestigating officer to complete a command investigation," Hegseth told a press briefing.
Reuters ‚Äãfirst reported last week that ‚Äãan ongoing, internal U.S. military investigation showed ‚ÄåU.S. ‚ÅÝforces were likely responsible for the strike on the girls' school in southern Iran.
"The ‚Äãcommand investigation ‚Äãwill ‚ÅÝtake as long as necessary to address all ‚Äãthe matters surrounding this ‚Äãincident. ‚ÅÝAnd the investigating officer is from outside CENTCOM and is ‚ÅÝa ‚Äãgeneral officer," Hegseth ‚Äãsaid.

item.title: Swiss Federal Council adopts draft law to deepen ties with EU
item.description:
ZURICH, March 13 (Reuters) - The Swiss government on Friday adopted a draft law containing ‚Äãa raft of measures aimed at ‚Äådeepening the Alpine country's economic ties with the European Union, which now passes ‚Äãto parliament.
If lawmakers pass the ‚Äãbill, it is poised to face ‚ÅÝa national referendum, where it is ‚Äãlikely to face stiff resistance from ‚Äãnationalists who argue that further integration with the EU will compromise Swiss sovereignty and hold ‚Äãthe country back.
The governing Federal Council ‚Äãsaid the package is in Switzerland's interest and ‚Äåwould ‚ÅÝensure continuity of relations with the EU, the landlocked country's most important trading partner.
"In view of the tense ‚Äãgeopolitical situation, ‚Äãstable ‚ÅÝand reliable relations with neighbouring European countries are of strategic ‚Äãimportance," the Federal Council said.
The ‚Äãcouncil ‚ÅÝsaid the deal that was initially agreed between Switzerland and the EU ‚ÅÝin ‚ÄãDecember 2024 would create ‚Äãcertainty for its export-led economy and secure prosperity.

item.title: Britain says allies should keep pressure on Russia after US eases oil sanctions
item.description:
LONDON, March 13 (Reuters) - Britain and its partners should maintain collective pressure on Russia ‚Äãthrough sanctions, a Downing Street spokesperson said ‚Äåon Friday, joining a chorus of European criticism of the United States after Washington eased sanctions on Russian oil.
"It's obviously a ‚Äãdecision for the U.S., but our position ‚Äãis clear. All partners should maintain pressure ‚ÅÝon Russia and its war chest," the spokesperson ‚Äãfor Prime Minister Keir Starmer told reporters.
The United States has issued ‚Äãa 30-day waiver for countries to buy sanctioned Russian oil and petroleum products stranded at sea, in order to ‚Äãstabilise global energy markets roiled by the Iran ‚Äãwar.
But European allies criticised the move, which risks complicating Western ‚Äåefforts ‚ÅÝto deprive Russia of revenue for the war in Ukraine.
The spokesperson said that Western sanctions had deprived the Russian state of at least $450 billion since ‚Äãthe start ‚Äãof the ‚ÅÝwar in Ukraine, adding "our sanctions are working".
He said that collective pressure was the ‚Äãbest way to achieve a fair ‚Äãpeace ‚ÅÝin Ukraine and stop Russian support for hostile actors around the world including Iran.
"We remain committed to ‚ÅÝexerting ‚Äãmaximum economic pressure on Russia ‚Äãin pursuit of a just and lasting peace in Ukraine," the ‚Äãspokesperson said.

item.title: In modern Netherlands, one local keeps traditional Dutch dress alive
item.description:
VOLENDAM, Netherlands, March 13 (Reuters) - The Volendam women's costume, with its high pointed bonnet (hul), is one of ‚Äãthe most recognisable forms of traditional Dutch dress, appearing often on ‚Äåpostcards from the Netherlands.
But outside the tourist industry, no one publicly wears it in daily life anymore - except Annie In de Betouw-Kwakman, 85.
In the Netherlands, clothing used to ‚Äãbe linked to a person's place of origin.
"Almost every Dutch village ‚Äãused to have their own look," said fashion historian Birthe ‚ÅÝWeijkamp. "You could recognise where someone was coming from, what village they belonged ‚Äãto. So it was very much about identity, about belonging somewhere."
In Volendam, a ‚Äãvillage 22 kilometres north of Amsterdam, nearly everyone dressed like Annie until the mid-twentieth century, said Simone Kwakman-Brinkkemper, a specialist in Volendam's traditional dress.
The daily outfit includes a black ‚Äãjacket (jak), a short scarf (dasje), an apron (bontje), a long skirt, a tight red ‚Äãcoral beaded necklace and black slip‚Äëon shoes (muilen).
But as people began working outside the village, ‚Äåthe clothes, ‚ÅÝwhich are handmade and require great skill to create, became impractical. Annie quickly watched her friends and sisters switch to modern dress.
In Volendam, the only other person who wears the daily attire is another older woman who ‚Äãno longer goes ‚Äãoutdoors.
When Annie had ‚ÅÝschool-aged children, she briefly tried to modernise for them, as they said classmates were making fun of her attire. ‚ÄãShe bought a dress, but quickly abandoned it.
"The neighbour said, 'What ‚Äãare ‚ÅÝyou wearing? You look like a candy cane,'" she said. "I'm colour‚Äëblind ... I looked like a fool."
Annie said she is proud of her traditional clothes and it ‚ÅÝpains her ‚Äãthat the tradition will disappear when she ‚Äãdoes.
"Everyone is different, and everyone finds something else beautiful or comfortable. For me, this is freedom," ‚Äãshe said.
(This story has been refiled to update the byline)

item.title: Explosion heard in Homs caused by old missile, state media and sources say
item.description:
DAMASCUS, March 13 (Reuters) - An explosion was ‚Äãheard and plumes ‚Äåof smoke were seen rising ‚Äãon Friday ‚Äãfrom eastern neighborhoods of ‚ÅÝSyria's central ‚Äãprovince of Homs, ‚Äãstate media reported.
The explosion was caused ‚Äãby an ‚Äãold missile in an ‚Äåabandoned ‚ÅÝmilitary barracks that was mishandled, a security source ‚Äãand ‚Äãa ‚ÅÝwitness in the area ‚Äãtold Reuters. ‚ÄãNo ‚ÅÝcasualties were reported.

item.title: Italy weighs options as damaged Russian LNG tanker drifts in the Mediterranean
item.description:
ROME, March 13 (Reuters) - Italian authorities are weighing ‚Äãhow to deal with a Russian liquefied natural gas tanker left adrift in the Mediterranean ‚Äåafter what Moscow described as a Ukrainian drone attack , sources said on Friday.
Russia's transport ministry said the Arctic Metagaz, carrying LNG from the Arctic port of Murmansk, was attacked last week by Ukrainian naval drones launched from the Libyan coast. Kyiv ‚Äãhas not claimed responsibility.
Libya's maritime rescue agency initially said the tanker had sunk, but the vessel ‚Äãhas remained afloat and is now drifting between Italy and Malta, around 30 ‚ÅÝnautical miles off the small Italian island of Linosa, the Italian navy said.
"The situation is under control. ‚ÄãThe ship is in international waters and the navy, a tugboat and an environmental response vessel are escorting ‚Äãit," said Filippo Mannino, mayor of Lampedusa, which includes Linosa.
Two Italian sources said it was still unclear how much LNG and fuel remained aboard the tanker. The 30 crew members were evacuated after the attack, which the ‚Äãsources said occurred south of Malta.
One source said all options were being considered, but the preferred solution ‚Äãwould be for the vessel's manager, Russia-based LLC SMP Techmanagement, to hire a specialised company to tow it to safety.
Italy ‚Äådoes ‚ÅÝnot want the tanker to dock at one of its ports, the source said, describing it as a "ticking time bomb filled with gas".
It added that the drone appeared to have struck the ship above the waterline. The hull was still holding, although photos showed a gaping hole on the port side, with the ‚Äãstern sitting lower in the ‚Äãwater than the rest ‚ÅÝof the ship.
A spokesperson for Italy's Civil Protection agency said the vessel was being monitored and was not heading towards Italian waters.
The boat got as close ‚Äãas 22 nautical miles to Malta's coast earlier this week, but now appeared ‚Äãto be ‚ÅÝdrifting away, and was reported about 61 nautical miles offshore by Thursday.
Malta's transport authority warned other ships to keep at least five nautical miles away from the Arctic Metagaz at all times. Prime Minister Robert Abela said ‚ÅÝMalta's government ‚Äãwas ready to act if necessary, without giving details.
Since Russia's full-scale ‚Äãinvasion of Ukraine in February 2022, Kyiv has repeatedly targeted Russian oil refineries and other energy infrastructure in an effort to undermine ‚Äãfunding for Moscow's war machine.

item.title: Iran's new supreme leader wounded, likely disfigured, Hegseth says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - Iran's new Supreme ‚ÄãLeader Mojtaba Khamenei is wounded and likely disfigured, U.S. ‚ÄåDefense Secretary Pete Hegseth said on Friday, questioning Khamenei's ability to lead after nearly two weeks of U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran.
Khamenei ‚Äãhas not been seen by Iranians since his ‚Äãselection on Sunday by a clerical assembly, and ‚ÅÝhis first comments came in a statement read out by ‚Äãa television presenter on Thursday. In them, he vowed ‚Äãto keep the Strait of Hormuz shut and called on neighboring countries to close U.S. bases on their territory or risk ‚ÄãIran targeting them.
An Iranian official told Reuters on ‚ÄãWednesday that the newly appointed supreme leader was lightly injured but ‚Äåwas ‚ÅÝcontinuing to operate, after state television described him as war-wounded.
"We know the new so-called not so supreme leader is wounded and likely disfigured. He put out a ‚Äãstatement yesterday. A ‚Äãweak one, ‚ÅÝactually, but there was no voice and there was no video. It was ‚Äãa written statement," Hegseth told a briefing.
"Iran ‚Äãhas plenty ‚ÅÝof cameras and plenty of voice recorders. Why a written statement? I think you know why. His father - ‚ÅÝdead. ‚ÄãHe's scared, he's injured, he's on ‚Äãthe run and he lacks legitimacy."

item.title: Uruguayan alleged cartel leader Marset arrested in Bolivia
item.description:
ASUNCION/MONTEVIDEO, March 13 (Reuters) - Uruguayan alleged drug trafficker Sebastian Marset ‚Äãwas arrested in Bolivia, the ‚Äåhead of Paraguay‚Äôs National Anti-Drug Secretariat said in a local radio interview on ‚ÄãFriday.
Marset, who is accused of ‚Äãleading the First Uruguayan Cartel, is ‚ÅÝa wanted fugitive sought by ‚ÄãInterpol. He is charged with organized crime ‚Äãviolations in Paraguay and Bolivia, and has been indicted in the United States on ‚Äãmoney laundering charges, according to ‚Äãthe U.S. State Department.
Paraguayan official Jalil Rachid said ‚ÄåBolivian ‚ÅÝauthorities carried out the operation, in an interview with Paraguayan local radio.
Marset's brother, Diego Nicolas Marset, was ‚Äãarrested in ‚ÄãBrazil ‚ÅÝin 2023 as one of South America‚Äôs most wanted ‚Äãfugitives, according to Interpol, in ‚Äãa ‚ÅÝpolice operation that involved agencies from Brazil, Bolivia, Paraguay and Uruguay.

item.title: Algerian army kills four militants in counter-terrorism operation
item.description:
TUNIS, March 13 (Reuters) - Algerian army ‚Äãunits killed ‚Äåfour armed militants ‚Äãduring ‚Äãa counterterrorism operation in ‚ÅÝthe ‚Äãnortheastern region ‚Äãof Tebessa, the defence ministry ‚Äãsaid ‚Äãon Friday.
The troops ‚Äårecovered ‚ÅÝfour Kalashnikov guns, a significant ‚Äãquantity ‚Äãof ‚ÅÝammunition, and ‚Äãother military ‚Äãgear, ‚ÅÝthe ministry added.

item.title: Israeli evacuation orders affect 14% of Lebanon, NGO says
item.description:
GENEVA, March 13 (Reuters) - Over an eighth of Lebanon's ‚Äãterritory is under Israeli orders for people to leave their homes, an aid ‚Äågroup said on Friday, while the United Nations peacekeeping mission said Israeli ground troops were making incursions and erecting roadblocks.
Israel has been carrying out daily strikes on Lebanon since March 2 when the Iran-backed group Hezbollah ‚Äãlaunched rockets at Israel to avenge the killing of Iran's supreme leader in Tehran ‚Äãon the first day of the U.S.-Israeli .
Almost 700 people in ‚ÅÝLebanon have died in Israeli attacks and over 800,000 have been displaced. Israel's military says ‚Äãit has targeted Hezbollah militants and Iranian forces.
The Norwegian Refugee Council said Israel's evacuation orders for ‚Äãsouthern Lebanon and parts of Beirut now covered about 1,470 square kilometres or about 14% of the country.
"Israel‚Äôs mass evacuation orders have expanded to broad geographic directives, often demanding immediate movement, creating panic and fear across ‚Äãcommunities that strikes are imminent ‚Äì even when they are not," said Maureen Philippon, NRC Country ‚ÄãDirector in Lebanon.
U.N. human rights chief Volker Turk has said the blanket Israeli evacuation orders raise serious international ‚Äålaw concerns .
NRC's ‚ÅÝoffice in Tyre, south Lebanon, was badly damaged, it said, with no injuries. The Israeli military has carried out several strikes on Tyre since March 2, including a Tuesday strike on what it described as a Hezbollah command centre in the area.
The International Organization for Migration's Mathieu ‚ÄãLuciano told a Geneva ‚Äãpress briefing that ‚ÅÝaround 600 shelters had been set up across the country, with many of them almost full. Hospitals are increasingly overstretched due to surging trauma ‚Äãcases, a World Health Organization official added.
The U.N. Interim Force in ‚ÄãLebanon told ‚ÅÝthe same briefing its operations had been limited by the ongoing hostilities which injured two soldiers a week ago. Still, its troops had observed Israeli troop incursions, saying they had travelled up to ‚ÅÝ7 ‚Äãkilometres inside Lebanon and erected roadblocks restricting access.
‚ÄúWe are ‚Äãdeeply concerned that the situation will deteriorate further," UNIFIL spokesperson Kandice Ardiel said by video link from Lebanon.

item.title: Trump says US would escort ships in Strait of Hormuz 'if needed' - Fox News interview
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - President ‚ÄãDonald Trump said the United States ‚Äåwould escort vessels through the Strait of Hormuz if needed, adding in a Fox ‚ÄãNews interview that aired on ‚ÄãFriday that the U.S. would strike ‚ÅÝIran "very hard over the next week."
Asked ‚Äãabout helping oil tankers pass through the ‚Äãkey shipping strait, Trump said: "We would do it if we needed to. But, you know, ‚Äãhopefully things are going to ‚Äãgo very well. We're going to see what ‚Äåhappens." ‚ÅÝHe gave no other details.
"We're going to be hitting them very hard over the next week," he added in ‚Äãthe interview ‚Äãwith ‚ÅÝFox's Brian Kilmeade that aired in part on its "Fox & Friends" ‚Äãprogram.
His comments come as the ‚ÄãUnited ‚ÅÝStates and other countries face spiking oil and gas prices with the U.S-Israel war ‚ÅÝentering ‚Äãits 14th day, with ‚Äãoil prices hovering near $100 a barrel on Friday .

item.title: Gulf states can make a financial point to Trump
item.description:
LONDON, March 13 (Reuters Breakingviews) - ‚ÄòThe defense of Saudi Arabia is vital to the defense of the United States‚Äô. President Franklin Roosevelt‚Äôs 1943 assertion , opens new tab , which other Gulf states like Qatar and the United Arab Emirates came to see as equally relevant to them, now looks different given all three states have endured nearly two weeks ‚Äãof Iranian drone and missile attacks in retaliation for U.S. and Israeli strikes. Traditionally warm relations between Washington and Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, UAE President Sheikh Mohamed ‚Äåbin Zayed Al Nahyan and Qatari Emir Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad al-Thani make it unlikely these Gulf leaders will publicly register any displeasure they might feel. But there are still various financial ways to send a message.
The Gulf disquiet is real. Dubai billionaire businessman Khalaf al-Habtoor questioned whether the costs of dragging the Gulf into the conflict were ignored. Sources close to Gulf Arab government circles told Reuters that many believe U.S. President Donald Trump brought the Gulf into a war shaped heavily ‚Äãby Israel, and agree the political and economic fallout for allies was not fully weighted.
Such displeasure is unsurprising. Saudi, Qatar and the UAE have invested a lot ‚Äì in hard cash ‚Äì in ‚Äãpublic displays of support for Trump that have gone way beyond the norm. Ahead of the president‚Äôs visit to the region last May, Qatar gave Trump ‚ÅÝa luxury Boeing 747 airliner, while entities linked to a senior Abu Dhabi royal took a stake in World Liberty Financial, the Trump family‚Äôs stablecoin business. Meanwhile the three Gulf states collectively contributed $24 billion to the ultimately ‚Äãsuccessful bid for Warner Bros Discovery (WBD.O) , opens new tab by Paramount Skydance, backed by the Trump-aligned Ellison family.
As such, when some of the Gulf states counselled the U.S. president against striking Iran, they had grounds to at least ‚Äãhope he‚Äôd listen. Instead, they now have to contend with a war of uncertain duration, and a blocked Strait of Hormuz that means they can‚Äôt ship their main fossil fuel exports. They also, according to Adnan Mazarei, senior fellow at the Peterson International Institute for Economics, face rising defence bills, reconstruction costs and shrinking FDI inflows ‚Äì all while the cost of borrowing climbs.
That‚Äôs probably why regional governments are examining their options. High-level representatives of three of the four biggest Gulf states ‚Äãare reviewing how they globally deploy trillions of dollars invested by their sovereign wealth funds, Reuters reported on Wednesday, citing a Gulf official. The main reason for the move is to offset any losses triggered ‚Äãby the war. But the rethink could also be used as a way to register irritation at Washington.
On the face of it, a high-profile way to do that would be to pull out of either the Paramount acquisition, or ‚Äåeven for ‚ÅÝSaudi‚Äôs PIF to reverse course on the $30 billion of equity it‚Äôs sinking into the $55 billion Electronic Arts buyout (EA.O) , opens new tab . Neither promises a good return, and it would represent a pointed snub. Unfortunately, as Elon Musk found buying Twitter, it‚Äôs extremely difficult legally to wriggle out of a nearly done deal.
That still leaves scope for a major rethink on investment pledges made during Trump‚Äôs 2025 Gulf visit, which eventually led to Qatar pledging $1.2 trillion in the U.S., Saudi $1 trillion and the UAE $1.4 trillion. These are long-term commitments over a decade or so, and only a minority came with specific contracts at the time. Scope for a potential AI bubble to pop ‚Äãmight suggest that Abu Dhabi‚Äôs MGX investment vehicle ‚Äãshould dump its stakes in OpenAI and the ‚ÅÝTrump-endorsed Stargate venture to build out AI infrastructure. Yet with the emirate laser-focused on pushing its status as an AI hub ‚Äì and still reliant on Nvidia chips ‚Äì that might hurt the UAE more than the U.S.
There are other less high-profile ways to send a message to Washington, though. Among last May‚Äôs pledges, bets ‚Äãthat look less strategically important include Saudi entity SURJ Sports Investment‚Äôs $4 bln U.S. tie-up, and backing for U.S. LNG. Saudi Arabia‚Äôs drive to localise ‚Äã50% of defence spending by 2030 ‚ÅÝis gathering pace, including through non-U.S. partnerships with Chinese , opens new tab and Turkish drone manufacturers. Riyadh could emphasise the point by doing more defence deals in Europe.
Gulf leaders may feel it‚Äôs better to avoid ditching even these commitments. But with Iranian regime change looking unlikely, they may find themselves locked into a riskier situation in which they can‚Äôt count on Washington. It would be odd if that didn‚Äôt come with financial as well as political consequences.

item.title: Swiss sticking to mandate for trade talks with US, minister says
item.description:
ZURICH, March 13 (Reuters) - Switzerland is sticking to its existing mandate for trade negotiations with the ‚ÄãUnited States that aim to finalise ‚Äåa deal over tariffs struck last year, Economy Minister Guy Parmelin said on Friday.
Parmelin was speaking ‚Äãafter the United States said on ‚ÄãWednesday it was launching trade investigations into excess ‚ÅÝindustrial capacity in 16 major trading ‚Äãpartners, including Switzerland, and into allegations of ‚Äãforced labour.
"And we will most certainly also consult with these countries to see how to respond to ‚Äãthe United States," Parmelin told reporters at ‚Äãa press conference.
Switzerland was subject to the highest U.S. ‚Äåtariffs ‚ÅÝin Europe when U.S. President Donald Trump imposed in August a 39% duty on products imported from the country.
In November, Bern ‚Äãsealed an ‚Äãinitial deal ‚ÅÝthat cut the tariffs to 15%, mirroring the rate for ‚Äãthe European Union.
Switzerland has since begun ‚Äãtalks ‚ÅÝto formalise that deal, which Washington has been pressing to conclude by the end of ‚ÅÝMarch.
"The ‚Äãnegotiations and discussions are ‚Äãcontinuing," said Parmelin, who is also Switzerland's president for ‚Äã2026.

item.title: Pope Leo says Christians who start wars should go to confession
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 13 (Reuters) - Pope Leo suggested on Friday that Christian political leaders who start wars should go to ‚Äãconfession and assess whether they are following the teachings ‚Äåof Jesus, without naming any specific leaders or conflicts.
"Do those Christians who bear grave responsibility in armed conflicts have the humility and courage ‚Äãto make a serious examination of conscience and to ‚Äãgo to confession?" the pope asked in a speech ‚ÅÝto priests.
While Leo did not name anyone on Friday, in ‚Äãrecent days he has been ramping up calls for an end ‚Äãto the ongoing Iran war , which began with joint U.S.-Israeli airstrikes on February 28.
U.S. President Donald Trump was raised in the Presbyterian Christian faith. Several ‚Äãof his top deputies, including Vice President JD Vance and ‚ÄãSecretary of State Marco Rubio, are Catholic.
Jesus taught his followers to be non-violent. ‚ÄåThe ‚ÅÝCatholic Church generally opposes war.
For centuries, the Church has evaluated conflicts according to the just war tradition, which uses a series of criteria to evaluate whether a conflict can be considered ‚Äãmorally justifiable, for ‚Äãexample repelling ‚ÅÝan unjust invasion.
Washington, D.C. Cardinal Robert McElroy said earlier this week that the U.S.-Israeli strikes against ‚ÄãIran were "not morally legitimate" because they did not ‚Äãmeet ‚ÅÝthe Church's just war criteria.
Leo was speaking on Friday to a Vatican conference on the practice of confession, in which Catholics ‚ÅÝadmit their ‚Äãsins to a priest and ask ‚ÄãGod's forgiveness.
The pope said that the ritual helps individual Catholics and promotes peace ‚Äãand unity in society.

item.title: South Africa's president authorises deployment of 2,200 troops to help fight crime
item.description:
JOHANNESBURG, March 13 (Reuters) - South African President Cyril Ramaphosa ‚Äãon Friday authorised the ‚Äådeployment of 2,200 members of the national defence force ‚Äãto help combat ‚Äãcrime.
($1 = 16.7304 rand)

item.title: Swedish Liberals and Sweden Democrats agree pact to boost right's September election chances
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 13 (Reuters) - Sweden's Liberal Party, the smallest of the ruling coalition, said on Friday it would back bringing the ‚Äãanti-immigration Sweden Democrats into government after parliamentary elections ‚Äåin September, boosting the right's chance of victory.
The opposition left bloc currently leads in polls and the right needs to club together ‚Äãto have a chance of forming a government ‚Äãafter the vote on September 13.

item.title: UAE says Iran must halt attacks on neighbours to allow diplomacy
item.description:
DUBAI, March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump will bring about a diplomatic conclusion to the war with Iran "in his time", and Tehran must halt attacks on its neighbours before they can mediate, ‚Äãone of the UAE's top diplomats said in an interview on Friday.
Lana Nusseibeh, a former UAE ‚Äåambassador to the United Nations who now holds the rank of minister of state in the Gulf country's foreign ministry, expressed confidence that the war would end in a negotiated settlement. But she said neighbours were shocked that Iran had unleashed ‚Äãattacks on them.
"Ultimately, it will be a diplomatic solution, but there needs to be that tipping ‚Äãpoint moment, and I think that president Trump will lead us all to that ‚ÅÝmoment in his time," Nusseibeh said in an interview.
Asked about efforts to mediate, she said Iran must ‚Äãhalt its attacks first: "It is difficult to talk about mediation when under attack... Mediation can only happen when the ‚Äãguns go silent."
She said Iranian officials had given her no indication the UAE was a target when she visited Tehran for talks aimed at finding a diplomatic solution to the crisis two weeks before it erupted into conflict. That lack of ‚Äãwarning made Iran's attacks on the UAE "so shocking and so egregious".
Iran has said its strikes are aimed at ‚Äãthe U.S. presence in the region. The UAE, along with other Gulf countries, Iraq, Jordan and Turkey, hosts U.S. military ‚Äåfacilities.
Drones or ‚ÅÝmissiles have been fired at UAE civilian infrastructure including Dubai airport, landmark hotels and the financial hub.
Nusseibeh said the UAE was in constant touch with the Trump administration and praised it as a strong strategic partner.
She offered no criticism of the U.S. or Israel for launching the ‚Äãattack on Iran, and said ‚Äãtheir military campaign should ‚ÅÝbe viewed separately to what she called Iran's "egregious illegal and unlawful attacks on the Gulf countries and Jordan".
It would be hard to restore relations with Iran to ‚Äãthe pre-war status quo "as you look around at the destruction and the chaos ‚Äãthat Iran has ‚ÅÝcaused in the region," she said.
She said Iran was trying to attack the UAE's economic model, which had attracted 700,000 Iranians to live there, but that the Iranian strikes had shown that "our economy is strong and robust ‚ÅÝand resilient".
"People ‚Äãare back at work, our airports are open, flights are taking ‚Äãoff. It's the Iranian economy that was already strangled by sanctions and economic pressure that is going into freefall. It's their currency ‚Äãthat's going into freefall," she said.

item.title: Putin questions crucial infrastructure protection amid intensified Ukrainian attacks
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 13 (Reuters) - President Vladimir Putin discussed measures to protect Russia's critical infrastructure with his Security Council, the Kremlin said on Friday, after intensified Ukrainian attacks that ‚Äãhit a major military plant among other targets.
Putin asked Deputy Prime ‚ÄåMinister Alexander Novak, Transport Minister Vitaly Savelyev and Construction Minister Irek Faizullin to report on the proposed measures in his opening remarks, with the rest of the meeting not made public.
Ukraine said ‚Äãon March 10 that it had used British Storm Shadow missiles to ‚Äãhit a factory that produced semiconductor devices and integrated micro chips ‚ÅÝfor missiles in the city of Bryansk, just over 100 km (60 miles) from ‚ÄãUkraine's border.
After the attack, which killed six people, some Russian war bloggers expressed bewilderment ‚Äãthat such a crucial site for meeting Russia's battlefield needs had not been evacuated during four years of war and was operating within reach of Ukrainian missiles.
On February 25, Ukrainian drones hit ‚Äãa chemical plant owned by fertiliser producer Akron in the town of Dorogobuzh, ‚Äãknocking off about 5% of Russia's fertiliser output just before the supply crunch caused by U.S.-Israeli ‚Äåattacks ‚ÅÝon Iran.
Russia's defence ministry said on Thursday that Ukraine had attempted to attack a pumping station operated by gas giant Gazprom that exports natural gas via the TurkStream subsea pipeline to European customers, but that the attack was foiled.
On March 2, ‚ÄãSheskharis, a major oil ‚Äãterminal on Russia's ‚ÅÝBlack Sea coast, suspended loadings following a Ukrainian drone attack that injured five, damaged 20 buildings and set a fuel ‚Äãterminal on fire.
Amid the stepped-up attacks, Russia has slowed down ‚Äãor turned ‚ÅÝoff mobile internet in Moscow and some other major cities as part of what the Kremlin described as security measures.
Although such outages have become common across Russia during ‚ÅÝthe war ‚Äãin Ukraine, Moscow has not previously experienced them ‚Äãon such a scale since the war started, with millions of people losing access to popular services ‚Äãsuch as maps or taxi hailing applications.

item.title: Africa-bound India-flagged fuel tanker sets sail from east of Hormuz, Indian official says
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 13 (Reuters) - The Indian-flagged oil tanker, Jag Prakash, which is carrying gasoline from Oman ‚Äãto Africa, has set sail from east ‚Äåof the Strait of Hormuz, an Indian government official said on Friday.
Gulf shipping has been disrupted over the past ‚Äãtwo weeks as Iran targets the region in ‚Äãretaliation for the U.S.-Israeli attacks on it.
Rajesh Kumar ‚ÅÝSinha, India's special secretary at the ministry of ‚Äãshipping, told reporters that there were four Indian-flagged ‚Äãvessels stuck east of the Strait of Hormuz, in the Gulf of Oman, on Thursday, of which one, the Jag Prakash, ‚Äãhad now set sail.
"Three vessels remain stuck at ‚Äãthe east side, they have 76 Indian sailors onboard," he ‚Äåsaid.
Sinha ‚ÅÝadded that 24 Indian-flagged vessels were stuck west of the Strait, in the Persian Gulf, on Friday, the same number as on Thursday, with a total ‚Äãof 677 Indian ‚Äãsailors onboard.
Iran's ‚ÅÝnew supreme leader said on Thursday that the country will fight on and ‚Äãkeep Hormuz shut as leverage against the ‚ÄãUnited States ‚ÅÝand Israel, in defiant first comments attributed to him since he succeeded his slain father.
Ship-tracking data from ‚ÅÝKpler ‚Äãshowed the Jag Prakash will discharge ‚Äãfuel loaded at Oman's Sohar Port at Tanzania's Tanga Port on ‚ÄãMarch 21.

item.title: Iran unleashes oil shock to blunt US firepower
item.description:
DUBAI, March 13 (Reuters) - Long before the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran , the Islamic Republic had devised its own weapon: holding the world‚Äôs main oil lifeline hostage to offset its foes' military superiority, three regional sources familiar with Iranian ‚Äãplanning said.
For decades Iran has signalled that if pushed into a confrontation , it would restrict tanker traffic in the Strait of Hormuz , the chokepoint where its adversaries are most exposed because ‚Äådisruptions there reverberate instantly through global energy markets.
With the Gulf‚Äôs main export artery in the crosshairs , Tehran has turned the region‚Äôs greatest economic asset into its most powerful deterrent , the sources said.
About a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas normally passes through the vital Strait, and Iran, which lies on its northern coast, has now effectively closed it. Traffic via the strait has dropped by 97% since the war against Iran began on February 28, according to United Nations data.
Iran has used similar tactics before. In ‚Äãthe ‚ÄúTanker War‚Äù of the 1980‚Äì88 Iran‚ÄëIraq conflict, attacks on vessels turned the Gulf into one of the world‚Äôs most dangerous waterways, forcing Washington to escort tankers through the Strait.
But Iran now wields ‚Äãfar more potent tools, including large arsenals of cheap missiles and drones capable of threatening shipping across a far wider area. Its attacks this month have shown how quickly ‚ÅÝTehran can disrupt traffic through the strait without heavily mining it.
"Iran is outgunned -- there is no way it can defeat them in a direct confrontation,‚Äù said ‚ÄãAli Vaez, the International Crisis Group‚Äôs Iran Project Director. Anticipating further U.S.-Israeli strikes after a 12-day war in June last year, Tehran examined how to extend any conflict ‚Äúin time and space‚Äù.
‚ÄúIf Iran takes the global economy hostage, ‚ÄãTrump would blink first,‚Äù added Vaez.
The regional sources, who declined to be identified as they were not authorised to speak publicly, said Iran‚Äôs powerful Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps (IRGC) had long prepared for a showdown with Israel and Washington.
The Guards' plan, which seeks to protect Iran's 47-year-old system of rule by fiercely anti-Western Islamic clerics, was activated on February 28, after Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed on the first day of the conflict.
The core of the strategy is a recognition of Iran‚Äôs military ‚Äãlimits against superior forces, the sources said. Tehran‚Äôs planners instead seek to pressure oil flows while inflicting asymmetric attacks on U.S. assets stationed across the region.
The strategy seeks to conjure ‚Äãeconomic pressures -- both at home and overseas -- on President Donald Trump to halt the war.
"This is asymmetric warfare par excellence, in which Iran achieves outsized, even global effects through a small number of attacks that impose painful costs,‚Äù said ‚ÄåMichael Eisenstadt of ‚ÅÝthe Washington Institute.
‚ÄúThe goal is to create economic pain, further undermining support for the war in the United States and increasing pressure on Washington to end it.‚Äù
Rather than concentrate forces on a single battlefield, Tehran is dispersing its campaign with waves of low-cost missile and drone strikes across the Gulf, of the kind once outsourced to Iran-allied forces in Iraq, Yemen, Syria and Lebanon.
The approach reflects a doctrine shaped over decades by the IRGC, built on the assumption that a stronger foe would try to decapitate Iran‚Äôs leadership and command structure at the outset of any war, the sources said.
The Guards are applying lessons from years of shadow conflict with the U.S., ‚Äãthey said. But this time, instead of relying mostly ‚Äãon regional proxies that once formed its forward line ‚ÅÝof defence, Tehran is now executing the playbook itself.
Ali Vaez said the U.S. had entered the war unprepared, driven by ‚Äúa lot of wishful thinking and not a lot of well-thought-through strategies.‚Äù
Washington, he said, failed to anticipate drone attacks on Gulf states, disruptions to shipping lanes or the need to ‚Äãevacuate citizens, shortcomings he said reflected a failure to absorb lessons from the risk of drones in modern warfare.
By contrast, Iran‚Äôs decentralised ‚ÄúMosaic‚Äù doctrine --dispersing command and ‚Äãcontrol to withstand decapitation -- remains ‚ÅÝin place, under one coordinating hub. Even after Khamenei's death, two sources said Parliament Speaker Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf, a former Guards commander, and Ali Larijani , head of Iran's national security council, continued to direct the war effort from Tehran.
Vaez argued that while the U.S. can significantly weaken Iran, total defeat would need a land invasion involving up to a million troops operating in unforgiving terrain, a commitment Washington has shown ‚Äúit doesn‚Äôt have the stomach for.‚Äù
Trump, who once promised to ‚ÅÝkeep the U.S. ‚Äãout of "stupid‚Äù military interventions, is now pursuing what many experts see as an open-ended war of choice that could be the ‚Äãbiggest military campaign since those in Iraq and Afghanistan.
Iran‚Äôs immediate objective is survival, Vaez said. Beyond that, its broader aim is to force Washington to accept that coercion, whether through military force, economic pressure or diplomatic isolation, does not work.
Whether such a lesson is learned remains ‚Äãuncertain. But by weaponising the world‚Äôs most critical energy corridor and stretching the battlefield far beyond Iran‚Äôs borders, Tehran is betting it can endure longer than a far stronger enemy.

item.title: Saudi Arabia cuts oil output 20% to 8 million bpd amid Iran war, sources say
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Top oil exporter Saudi Arabia has cut oil production by some 2 million barrels per day to around 8 million bpd after ‚Äãreducing output from two major offshore fields amid the Iran ‚Äåwar, two sources told Reuters.
Middle East Gulf oil producers have had to shut vast volumes of production due to the blocking of the Strait of Hormuz, a ‚Äãnarrow waterway between Iran and Oman, since the U.S. and Israel ‚Äãbegan airstrikes on Iran on February 28. Iran has said ‚ÅÝthe world should prepare for oil prices at $200 a barrel.
While Saudi ‚ÄãArabia is routing more oil to Yanbu on the Red Sea coast ‚Äãto avoid the Strait, production is down to around 8 million bpd after the Safaniya and Zuluf offshore fields were shut, one source said, declining to be identified ‚Äãby name. Another source said Saudi production was down to below ‚Äã8 million bpd.
The two offshore fields produce over 2 million bpd of mainly heavy ‚Äåand ‚ÅÝmedium heavy crude, while the pipeline to Yanbu mainly takes light crude.
State oil giant Saudi Aramco (2222.SE) , opens new tab declined to comment.
A cut in Saudi production to 8 million bpd is a sizeable drop from February, when Saudi Arabia ‚Äãsupplied 10.111 million bpd ‚Äãto the ‚ÅÝmarket and produced 10.882 million bpd. The February production boost was a contingency plan in case any U.S. ‚Äãstrike on Iran disrupted Middle East supplies, sources said at ‚Äãthe time.
The ‚ÅÝInternational Energy Agency in a report on Thursday said Middle East Gulf countries including Iraq, Qatar, Kuwait, the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia have cut ‚ÅÝtotal ‚Äãoil production by at least 10 million ‚Äãbpd, adding that without a rapid restart of shipping flows these losses were set to increase.

item.title: Ivory Coast welcomes 'talking drum', first artifact sent back from France
item.description:
ABIDJAN, March 13 (Reuters) - Traditional chiefs wearing crowns and gold chains gathered at Ivory Coast's main airport on Friday to welcome ‚Äãthe return of a "talking drum" looted more than a ‚Äåcentury ago, the first artifact returned to the West African country by former colonial power France.
The drum - known as "Djidji Ayokw√®", meaning Panther-Lion in the Atchan ‚Äãlanguage - is more than three metres long and weighs ‚Äãnearly 400 kg, according to Ivory Coast's culture ministry.
It ‚ÅÝwas used by the Atchan people, native to southern Ivory Coast, ‚Äãto alert locals to forced labour operations being carried out by ‚Äãcolonizers and to mobilize fighters.
The Ivorian culture ministry is seeking the restitution of 148 artifacts from France, and the talking drum, previously displayed at the ‚ÄãQuai Branly Museum in Paris, is the first to make ‚Äãthe return journey.
Confiscated in 1916, the talking drum was initially kept at the ‚Äågovernor's ‚ÅÝpalace in Abidjan before being sent to France in 1930, according to the culture ministry.
"This is a historic day, and I am deeply moved," Fran√ßoise Remarck, Ivory Coast's minister of culture and Francophonie, ‚Äãsaid during Friday's ‚Äãceremony, punctuated ‚ÅÝby traditional songs and war dances.
Pressure has grown in recent years for former colonial powers such as ‚ÄãFrance and Britain to return artefacts taken from ‚ÄãAfrica ‚ÅÝand Asia.
Donning a traditional loincloth, Gervais Djoman, chief of an Atchan village, told Reuters on Friday that the return of the talking drum ‚ÅÝwas ‚Äãa source of joy and pride for ‚Äãthe Atchan people. "We are reclaiming our identity. Psychologically, something had been taken from us," ‚Äãhe said.

item.title: All six US service members killed in plane crash over Iraq
item.description: March 13 (Reuters) - All six ‚Äãcrew ‚Äåmembers aboard ‚Äãa ‚ÄãU.S. military aircraft ‚ÅÝthat ‚Äãcrashed ‚Äãin western Iraq ‚Äãare ‚Äãconfirmed to have ‚Äåbeen ‚ÅÝkilled, the U.S. ‚Äãmilitary ‚Äãsaid ‚ÅÝon ‚ÄãFriday.

item.title: Poland presses on with EU defence plan despite president's veto
item.description:
WARSAW, March 13 (Reuters) - Poland's government on Friday pushed ahead with a European Union defence-funding programme despite the president vetoing the scheme, amid a row over debt and the role Brussels should play in the security of the deeply divided country.
Pro-European Prime Minister ‚ÄãDonald Tusk's government says the EU's Security Action for Europe (SAFE) scheme provides cheap financing and ‚Äãis essential to Poland's security in the face of what it views as ‚ÅÝa rising threat from Russia.
However, opposition-backed President Karol Nawrocki on Thursday vetoed a bill intended to implement ‚ÄãSAFE in Poland, saying the scheme would leave future generations with large debts and allows the EU ‚Äãto infringe on Poland's sovereignty.
The bill would have created a mechanism to spend the 43.7 billion euros ($50 billion) in EU loans Poland was entitled to under the programme.
"Given the unprecedented and rapid deterioration of security in Europe, Poland must urgently ‚Äãand significantly increase its military investments," said a government resolution backing the scheme on Friday, which authorised ministers ‚Äãto sign the loan agreements despite the veto.
The head of the president's chancellery Zbigniew Bogucki accused the government of ‚Äåcircumventing the ‚ÅÝlaw.
Government ministers, meanwhile, lashed out at Nawrocki's veto, saying it would delay vital defence investment.
Warsaw is the biggest beneficiary of SAFE but nationalist opposition party Law and Justice (PiS) has labelled it a German plot to meddle in Polish affairs, which would saddle the country with debt and limit its flexibility on arms ‚Äãpurchases.
European Commission spokesperson Thomas ‚ÄãRegnier said Brussels would ‚ÅÝnot interfere in domestic debates, but that it remained committed to Poland's plan.
"We need Poland on board for European security," he said.
Due to the veto, the ‚Äãgovernment will have to use an existing armed forces fund whose rules ‚Äãmean it will ‚ÅÝbe unable to disburse some 7 billion zlotys ($1.87 billion) that had been earmarked for the border guard and the police.
Nawrocki has proposed an alternative bill that would involve using unrealised profits from the rising value of central ‚ÅÝbank gold ‚Äãreserves to fund defence.
The government rejected this proposal, saying the ‚Äãcentral bank has not been making a profit in recent years.
($1 = 0.8740 euros)
($1 = 3.7361 zlotys)

item.title: Greenland's Siumut party withdraws from governing coalition
item.description:
COPENHAGEN, March 13 (Reuters) - Greenland's Siumut party has withdrawn from the coalition government, the prime minister said on Friday, weakening ‚Äãefforts to present a united front against U.S. President ‚ÄåDonald Trump's campaign to take control of the Arctic island.
The departure follows Siumut chair Aleqa Hammond's warning that the party would leave after ‚Äãtwo Greenlandic ministers announced candidacies for Denmark's March 24 ‚Äãparliamentary election without prior leave.
Greenland's Prime Minister Jens-Frederik ‚ÅÝNielsen expressed disappointment but said his government would carry ‚Äãon, emphasising the importance of governance during heightened global scrutiny.
"I ‚Äãthink it's terribly bad timing and I'm very frustrated and disappointed that it's happening at a time when we should be standing ‚Äãtogether," he told reporters.
"Anything that might look like division ‚Äãin our country is grist to the mill for foreigners and we ‚Äåshould ‚ÅÝavoid that at all cost," he said.
The broad coalition had been a cornerstone of Nielsen's strategy to respond to what he has termed Greenland's most serious time in ‚Äãrecent history.
Siumut's departure ‚Äãmeans that ‚ÅÝGreenlandic Foreign Minister Vivian Motzfeldt , who has played a key role in diplomatic talks with the ‚ÄãUnited States, is leaving her post, according to ‚Äãbroadcaster ‚ÅÝKNR.
The loss of Siumut's support, which holds four seats in Greenland's 31-seat Inatsisartut assembly, does not threaten the government's majority, ‚ÅÝas ‚ÄãNielsen's coalition retains control with its ‚Äãremaining 19 seats.
Greenland will elect two members to Denmark's parliament later this month.

item.title: US veteran flees ahead of US trial over failed 2020 Maduro ouster plot
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 13 (Reuters) - A ‚ÄãU.S. military ‚Äãveteran awaiting trial ‚ÅÝon U.S. ‚Äãcharges concerning a ‚Äãfailed May 2020 armed incursion to ‚Äãoust now-jailed ‚ÄãVenezuelan President Nicolas Maduro ‚Äåhas ‚ÅÝabsconded and is a fugitive, federal prosecutors ‚Äãsaid ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝFriday.
Marissel Descalzo, a lawyer for ‚Äãthe defendant ‚ÄãJordan ‚ÅÝGoudreau, said, "We do not know ‚ÅÝwhere ‚Äãhe ‚Äãis."

item.title: Lebanon's offer for direct talks with Israel falls on deaf ears, sources say
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 13 (Reuters) - Israel has rebuffed a historic offer of direct talks from Lebanon, deeming it too little too late from a government that shares its goal of disarming Hezbollah but cannot act against the heavily armed Lebanese group ‚Äãwithout risking a civil war.
Lebanese President Joseph Aoun expressed the state's willingness to begin direct negotiations with Israel this week, seeking to secure an end to the ‚Äåconflict that erupted on March 2 when Hezbollah entered the regional war in support of its patron Iran.
Two sources familiar with Aoun's position said he has begun appointing a negotiating delegation and in some private meetings, he went as far as to say he was ready to move toward normalizing ties.
"Everything is on the table," a third source familiar with his position told Reuters, when asked about normalization.
The Lebanese state's stance reflects unprecedented levels of domestic opposition ‚Äãto Hezbollah's status as an armed group: the government last week banned the group from military activities.
But with Hezbollah still wielding a powerful arsenal and backed by a significant portion ‚Äãof Lebanon's Shi'ite Muslim community, carrying out the order is easier said than done for a fragile Lebanese state now facing one of its ‚ÅÝmost precarious moments since the 1975-90 civil war.
On Friday, Aoun told United Nations Secretary General Antonio Guterres that he had not received a response to his offer, according to a statement from the ‚Äãpresidency.
Just a few years ago, such an offer from a Lebanese president would have been a major diplomatic overture - and a chance for the United States to claim success ‚Äãin ending nearly 80 years of hostilities between the two countries.
But Aoun's proposal generated little interest from either Israeli or American officials, according to the two sources, a Lebanese official and two foreign officials.
The sources all said Lebanon's inability to rein in Hezbollah over the last year and prevent the group's March 2 attack left Beirut with little credibility and nothing tangible to offer at a negotiating table.
Israel's Foreign Minister Gideon Saar told the Times of ‚ÄãIsrael this week that his country was ready for dialogue with the Lebanese government to normalize ties.
"But the current problem is that dialogue with the Lebanese government cannot stop the fire from Lebanese ‚Äãterritory," he said.
Israel's ambassador to the United Nations Danny Danon told the U.N. Security Council this week that Israel could not negotiate with Lebanon "while rockets are flying into our northern border."
"The time has come to decide: ‚Äåwill Lebanon stick ‚ÅÝto declarations or actually act?" he said.
Lebanon's presidency, the U.S. State Department, Israel's foreign ministry and the office of Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Over the last year, Lebanese authorities have been treading carefully to confiscate the group's weapons in the country's south.
The moves would have been unimaginable just a few years ago, when Hezbollah was at the zenith of its power and exercised immense sway over Lebanon's multisectarian political system.
But the measures have had mixed results.
Hezbollah was still able to spend months re-arming , even stationing new rockets in ‚Äãsouthern Lebanon as the Lebanese army said it ‚Äãhad secured full operational control of the ‚ÅÝarea.
After the new war started, Lebanese authorities detained around 50 people for carrying arms without a license in southern Lebanon and near Beirut, Lebanese security sources told Reuters, saying the detained men were suspected of being Hezbollah members.
But several were swiftly released after paying a small fine, the sources said.
When ‚ÄãLebanon tried to reach out to U.S. officials this week to make the offer on negotiations, they were rebuffed, a Lebanese official said.
"They ‚Äãsaid that 2025 was our ‚ÅÝwindow to confront Hezbollah and we didn't, so there's nothing they can do now," the official said.
Three people familiar with U.S. policymaking on the Middle East told Reuters that Washington also had little bandwidth to deal with Lebanon given its current war on Iran, and was allowing Israel to deal with Lebanon as it saw fit.
Israel still wants to see Lebanese troops dismantle Hezbollah's ‚ÅÝrocket and drone ‚Äãlaunch sites and seize the group's weapons, Dannon told the Security Council.
The army has avoided directly confronting Hezbollah , worried about ‚Äãinflaming tensions with the Shi'ite Muslim community and fracturing the army, which split during Lebanon's 15-year civil war.
"That is the problem: Lebanon cannot deliver. And I understand that. This is a multi-sectarian society and Lebanon cannot afford to declare war on ‚Äãa community," the Carnegie Middle East Center's Michael Young told Reuters.

item.title: Cuba says FBI to help investigate speedboat incursion by Cuban exiles
item.description:
HAVANA, March 13 (Reuters) - The FBI ‚Äãwill send a team to Cuba to investigate an incursion by 10 Cuban exiles aboard ‚Äåa speedboat accused of provoking a gun battle with Cuban forces that killed half the infiltrators, Cuba said on Friday.
Cuba announced bilateral cooperation over the incident the same day as it acknowledged it was talking with the United States to try to defuse tensions ‚Äãbetween the two neighbors at a time when Washington's oil blockade on the Caribbean island has pushed the ‚Äãnation deeper into economic crisis.
On Feb. 25, Cuban nationals tried to infiltrate the country ‚ÅÝby speedboat, armed with nearly 13,000 rounds of ammunition, 13 rifles and 11 pistols and other material, the ‚Äãgovernment in Havana has said. Five were killed; the others, who were wounded, are in Cuban custody and receiving ‚Äãmedical attention, Cuba said.
Cuba had previously said the U.S. had expressed willingness to cooperate in the investigation. U.S. officials have also expressed interest in gaining consular access as at least two of those aboard the speedboat were dual U.S.-Cuban citizens, and others were legal U.S. ‚Äãresidents.
