"There is cooperation with our American counterparts, and we are awaiting a group of FBI experts to continue ‚Äãadvancing this investigation," Cuban President Miguel Diaz-Canel told a press conference on Friday, saying the exchange between the FBI and Cuba's ‚ÄåInterior Ministry ‚ÅÝwas arranged through diplomatic and consular channels.
The FBI did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The incident took place at a moment of heightened tension in already frosty U.S.-Cuban relations, with U.S. President Donald Trump pressuring the Communist government by imposing a virtual oil blockade after capturing and ousting Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro, a crucial ‚ÄãCuban benefactor, on January 3.
Cuba ‚Äãsaid the assailants were ‚ÅÝCuban exiles, some of whom had been previously identified as accused terrorists, who came from the United States with the intent to sow chaos and attack military units.
A ‚ÄãCuban patrol of five border guard members on a nine-meter boat spotted the incoming ‚Äãvessel early that ‚ÅÝmorning about one nautical mile off a channel on the Caribbean island's northern coast, some 100 miles (160 km) from Marathon, Florida.
The infiltrators fired on the patrol from 185 meters away, striking the captain in the abdomen, Cuba said. Bleeding heavily, ‚ÅÝthe ‚Äãwounded captain remained at the helm and steered toward the enemy vessel, ‚Äãleading to a firefight at a distance of about 20 meters.
Secretary of State Marco Rubio said it was not a U.S. operation and ‚Äãthat no U.S. government personnel were involved.

item.title: UN seeks $325 million for Lebanon as war displaces 800,000 people
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 13 (Reuters) - The ‚ÄãUnited Nations launched a $325 million flash appeal on Friday to help Lebanon cope with ‚Äåthe fallout of a war that has forced more than a seventh of its population from their homes.
"Solidarity in words must be matched by solidarity in action," U.N. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres said as he announced the campaign from Beirut.
Israel ‚Äãlaunched an offensive against Hezbollah last week after the Iran-backed Lebanese militant group opened fire ‚Äãon it on March 2, saying it was avenging the killing of Iran's ‚ÅÝsupreme leader.
Hezbollah has kept up daily rocket and drone attacks, while Israel has expanded its ground ‚Äãoperations and air strikes, bombing the capital on Thursday along with other parts of the country on Friday.
Nearly ‚Äã700 people have been killed in the attacks and more than 800,000 displaced by Israel's orders for people to leave ever larger swathes of Lebanon.
But aid organisations say funding constraints have already forced them to ration supplies and that substantial ‚Äãnew contributions are essential.
"We're only targeting those who are really on the verge of starvation or ‚Äãin starvation," said Carl Skau, the World Food Programme's deputy executive director.
"There isn't any more margin, so with needs ‚Äågoing up, ‚ÅÝresources will have to come up - and they can certainly not drop," Skau told Reuters.
Humanitarian organizations say global crises have restricted their response in Lebanon, a country already hit hard by a 2019 economic collapse, the 2020 Beirut port explosion and the 2024 war between Hezbollah and Israel.
Skau said ‚Äãthe WFP fears donor ‚Äãgovernments will face new ‚ÅÝbudget constraints following the spike in global energy prices triggered by the Iran war.
The U.N.'s refugee agency UNHCR last September said it had only received ‚Äã25% of the resources required for Lebanon in 2025, forcing it to ‚Äãslash cash assistance ‚ÅÝprogrammes.
"The current spike or the current escalation of hostilities compounds an already tight funding landscape," said Kirollos Fares, Lebanon country director at humanitarian organisation Medair.
Aid group Solidarit√©s International had already seen a drop in both ‚ÅÝthe number ‚Äãand size of grants, said Lebanon country director Daniele Regazzi.
‚Äú...Unless ‚Äãfresh money comes out, what we are deploying now as emergency response...will be gone in the next roughly couple of weeks," ‚Äãhe said.

item.title: Brazil top court panel keeps Banco Master owner Vorcaro in jail
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 13 (Reuters) - The majority ‚Äãof a ‚Äåpanel of Brazil's Supreme Court ‚Äãvoted on ‚ÄãFriday to keep ‚ÅÝthe owner ‚Äãof liquidated lender ‚ÄãBanco Master, Daniel Vorcaro, in jail.
The ‚Äãbusinessman ‚Äãwas detained earlier this month ‚Äåafter ‚ÅÝa judge cited "strong indication" that Vorcaro attempted ‚Äãto ‚Äãbribe ‚ÅÝa former central ‚Äãbank director ‚Äãwith ‚ÅÝgifts in return for ‚ÅÝpreferential ‚Äãtreatment.

item.title: Brazil to revoke Trump adviser Beattie's visa, source says
item.description:
BRASILIA, March 13 (Reuters) - Brazil's government will revoke the visa of Darren Beattie, who ‚Äãwas recently tapped by U.S. President Donald Trump for a ‚Äåsenior advisory role monitoring the South American country, a source familiar with the matter told Reuters on Friday.
Earlier in the day, President Luiz Inacio Lula ‚Äãda Silva suggested that Beattie would be barred from entering ‚ÄãBrazil until the U.S. reinstated a visa for his ‚ÅÝHealth Minister Alexandre Padilha, which was revoked last year .
Beattie, a critic of ‚ÄãBrazil's government, was appointed to the position shaping U.S. policy toward the ‚Äãcountry last month, suggesting relations between the two nations remain delicate despite recent rapprochement.
Lawyers for imprisoned former President Jair Bolsonaro asked the Brazilian Supreme Court to ‚Äãallow Beattie to visit him in jail next week, when he had ‚Äãa trip scheduled to Brazil, but Justice Alexandre de Moraes denied it on Thursday.
Citing ‚Äåa ‚ÅÝdocument sent by Foreign Minister Mauro Vieira, Moraes noted the U.S. official only committed to attend a critical minerals forum and meetings with the government while in Brazil.
Bolsonaro, who served as president between ‚Äã2019 and 2022, was ‚Äãa close ‚ÅÝTrump ally during the period when both were in office. He is currently serving a 27-year prison ‚Äãsentence for plotting a coup against Lula, his ‚Äãsuccessor.
"That American ‚ÅÝguy who said he was coming here to visit Jair Bolsonaro was prohibited from visiting, and I forbade him from coming to Brazil ‚ÅÝuntil ‚Äãthey release the visa for my health ‚Äãminister," Lula told an event on Friday.

item.title: Trump says Putin may be helping Iran a bit
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald ‚ÄãTrump said he ‚Äåthinks Russian President Vladimir Putin ‚Äãmay be ‚Äãhelping Iran a "little bit" ‚ÅÝin an ‚Äãinterview with Fox ‚ÄãNews Radio that aired on Friday.
"I think ‚Äãhe might ‚Äãbe helping him (Iran) a ‚Äålittle ‚ÅÝbit, yeah, I guess. And he probably ‚Äãthinks ‚Äãwe're ‚ÅÝhelping Ukraine, right?," Trump ‚Äãtold "The Brian ‚ÄãKilmeade ‚ÅÝShow".

item.title: JP Morgan sees crude supply cuts nearing 12 million bpd as tanker halt tightens markets
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Crude oil supply cuts are on track to reach nearly 12 million barrels per day by the end of next week, intensifying deficits in physical ‚Äãmarkets as tanker movements through a key Middle Eastern waterway face a ‚Äåtwo-week disruption, JPMorgan said in a note on Friday.
"Commercial tanker traffic remains extremely limited, with most vessels now Iranian and likely headed to China," the bank said, adding that while cargoes ‚Äãleaving the Gulf before the shutdown are still arriving, new shipments have ‚Äãlargely stopped, while supplies to Asia could run out this week, ‚ÅÝwhile Europe-bound flows are likely to halt next week.
Production cutbacks by major Gulf ‚Äãproducers began after the conflict nearly two weeks ago, disrupting the Strait of Hormuz, ‚Äãthrough which one-fifth of global oil supply flows.
Meanwhile, an India-flagged oil tanker carrying gasoline to Africa exited the Strait of Hormuz, an Indian government official said on Friday.
Additionally, the U.S. issued a 30-day ‚Äãlicense permitting countries to purchase stranded Russian oil and petroleum products.
"Production shut-ins have ‚Äãalready reached about 6.5 million bpd, roughly 1 million bpd above our earlier estimates," JPMorgan said.
With ‚Äåglobal ‚ÅÝsupply approximately 7 million bpd below demand, the bank noted that markets are contending with a severe shortage of diesel, jet fuel, LPG, and naphtha.
JPMorgan highlighted that roughly 5 million bpd of refined products typically transit the disrupted waterway, a major ‚Äãartery for middle distillates ‚Äãand petrochemical feedstocks. ‚ÅÝIt added that Europe remains particularly exposed, as the region heavily depends on Middle Eastern diesel and jet fuel following its ‚Äãban on Russian imports.
The bank pointed out that approximately 2 ‚Äãmillion bpd ‚ÅÝof Middle Eastern refining capacity is effectively offline due to export constraints and infrastructure attacks, tightening global supply balances almost immediately.
While refiners in the U.S., Europe, India, and ‚ÅÝNortheast ‚ÄãAsia might increase operations to capture strong margins, JP ‚ÄãMorgan cautioned that limited spare capacity and reduced crude supply mean most adjustments will likely result in ‚Äãhigher product prices and firmer margins.

item.title: Colombia ministers travel to Caracas after postponement of presidential meeting-sources
item.description:
BOGOTA, March 13 (Reuters) - Colombia's foreign and defense ministers were traveling to Caracas ‚Äãon Friday, after the postponement of a meeting between Colombian President Gustavo Petro and his counterpart, acting President ‚ÄåDelcy Rodriguez, two sources with knowledge of the matter told Reuters.
The meeting was to be Rodriguez's first presidential-level bilateral since she took power following her predecessor's ouster by the United States.
The officials are likely to discuss bilateral trade, which Petro, who leaves office in August, reopened at the start of his term, ‚Äãand cooperation on energy, including an agreement this week to repair a damaged section of a binational pipeline that will allow ‚ÄãBogota to import natural gas from its neighbor.
The administration of U.S. President Donald Trump has supported a ‚ÅÝseries of moves by Rodriguez, formerly vice president, to attract investors in oil and mining and stabilize the country since the January raid ‚Äãthat captured President Nicolas Maduro, with Trump repeatedly praising Rodriguez for her cooperation with the U.S.
Rodriguez has repeatedly asserted that dialogue is the ‚Äãway to resolve long-standing disagreements with the U.S., has welcomed the U.S. secretaries of energy and interior and potential investors in Caracas, and the two countries have formally re-established . Despite this outward cooperation, Reuters reporting has shown the Trump administration is also quietly building a legal case against her to ‚Äãstrengthen its leverage with Caracas.
Petro, who had a cordial relationship with Maduro, has had repeated squabbles with Trump, though the two men ‚Äãwere each positive after a face-to-face meeting in Washington last month and had a friendly call on Thursday, according to Petro's office, where they discussed the ‚Äåeconomy along ‚ÅÝthe Venezuela-Colombian border.
Trump has repeatedly demanded more cooperation from Colombia in the fight against drug trafficking and has accused Petro, without evidence, of being an "". Petro meanwhile, claims record drug seizures during his tenure and has said deadly strikes on alleged drug boats amount to war crimes.
Colombia, home to nearly 3 million Venezuelan migrants who fled economic collapse in their home country, and Venezuela have deep ‚Äãhistorical and cultural ties, especially ‚Äãin border areas where many ‚ÅÝfamilies are bi-national.
Colombia recorded a trade surplus with Venezuela of $973.4 million in 2025, after exporting some $1.07 billion in goods like food, tobacco, chemicals, plastics and machinery, according to Colombian statistics agency DANE. Imports from ‚ÄãVenezuela totaled $98.3 million and included iron and steel, fertilizer and paper.
The two leaders had been set ‚Äãto meet near ‚ÅÝa border bridge between the Colombian town of Villa del Rosario and the Venezuelan village of Tienditas, but postponed the meeting late on Thursday for what the countries said in a joint statement were reasons of "force majeure," a term meaning extraordinary or unforeseeable circumstances. They gave no details, ‚ÅÝsaying they ‚Äãintended to reschedule soon.
Venezuelan state oil company PDVSA will carry out the repairs ‚Äãon the Antonio Ricaurte gas pipeline, which has been inactive for years, Colombia's energy ministry said in a Thursday statement.
The pipeline stretches 225 kilometers (140 mi) and has a ‚Äãtransport capacity of 500 million cubic feet of gas.

item.title: Lured by profits, some shipowners brave mines and missiles to sneak oil past Iran
item.description:
ATHENS, March 13 (Reuters) - A handful of Greek shipowners have sent crude oil and dry bulk tankers through ‚Äãthe Strait of Hormuz during the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, risking destruction from mines, missiles and drones for a chance at millions of dollars ‚Äåin quick profits.
The voyages reflect the financial allure of soaring crude oil prices and surging tanker rates since the start of the war, which has effectively sealed off a fifth of the world‚Äôs oil and liquefied natural gas supply from global markets.
U.S. President Donald Trump has urged ships to "show some guts" and run the strait, although the U.S. military has declined requests from the shipping industry ‚Äãfor escorts through the waterway because of the risk of attack.
"The risks are huge. But the sea has always been a risky business," said a ‚ÄãGreek shipowner involved in the voyages who declined to be identified because of the sensitivity of the trade.
At least 10 ‚ÅÝships operated by Greek companies, and at least two Chinese-operated vessels, have sailed through the strait between Iran and Oman since U.S. and Israeli strikes began on February ‚Äã28, according to data from maritime specialists Lloyd's List Intelligence and MarineTraffic.
Companies involved include shipping magnate George Prokopiou's Dynacom, and the Embiricos family‚Äôs Aeolos Management, according to six ‚Äãindustry sources familiar with the matter.
Dynacom and Aeolos did not respond to requests for comment.
Iran‚Äôs military has struck several ships moving through the narrow channel, vowing to keep it closed and warning that oil will reach $200 per barrel . At least 16 ships have been attacked, including Greek-operated vessels hit by drones.
U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said on Friday there was no clear evidence ‚Äãthat Iran has placed mines in the Strait of Hormuz, after news reports suggesting Tehran had deployed about a dozen mines there.
A second Greek shipping source involved ‚Äãin the trade, who also asked not to be named, described the tense and fraught sailings in the narrow waterway as "like entering an enemy's bathtub".
The profits are good, though. Average daily ‚Äåearnings have ‚ÅÝsurged to the highest levels in six years, with tanker owners able to earn $500,000 a day for a charter, according to ship broking data.
Even with huge war insurance costs and higher salaries for crew members, companies can still make millions of dollars on each voyage, industry sources familiar with the matter said.
Trump, who is seeking to combat soaring prices caused by the war, has encouraged oil tankers to traverse the strait.
"These ships should go through the Strait of Hormuz and ‚Äãshow some guts, there‚Äôs nothing to be ‚Äãafraid of," Trump said, according to ‚ÅÝFox News‚Äô Brian Kilmeade, who recounted the president‚Äôs remarks during a recent interview.
The White House did not immediately comment.
Trump has said the U.S. Navy will provide escorts through the strait when needed, but the Navy has told the shipping industry in ‚Äãregular briefings that the risks are too high for escorts.
Stephen Cotton, General Secretary of leading seafarers' union the International Transport ‚ÄãWorkers' Federation (ITF), told Reuters ‚ÅÝthat navigating the strait now was a bad idea.
"Sending seafarers through the Strait of Hormuz right now is sending them into a live war zone," he said.
Tactics deployed by shipowners so far include switching off AIS ship-tracking transponders in the hope that vessels become less visible to Iran‚Äôs military, and travelling at night.
"Reports that some operators are switching ‚ÅÝoff AIS ‚Äãto try and slip ships through and dodge attacks are extraordinarily alarming - it‚Äôs gambling with seafarers' ‚Äãlives," the ITF's Cotton said.
The voyages are among the most daring by shipowners since Norwegian-born billionaire John Fredriksen made his fortune in the "tanker wars" of the 1980s during the Iran-Iraq conflict, when his vessels ‚Äãrisked missile fire to load and then transport crude oil cargoes from the conflict area.

item.title: Pentagon chief says he's eager for Trump ally to buy¬ÝCNN as he blasts war coverage
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth on Friday said he was ‚Äãeager to see Trump ally and Paramount Skydance (PSKY.O) , opens new tab CEO David Ellison take over CNN, as he criticized ‚Äåthe U.S. news media's coverage of the Iran war.
"The sooner David Ellison takes over that network, the better," said Hegseth, a former Fox News show host and combat veteran, referring to Paramount's $110 billion deal to acquire CNN-parent Warner Bros (WBD.O) , opens new tab .
On the war's 14th day, Hegseth criticized CNN's reporting ‚Äãon the impact of Iran's disruption of traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical shipping passage, which has ‚Äãdriven oil prices sharply higher and shaken the stock market. Reuters/Ipsos polling has shown little public ‚ÅÝsupport for the war, which Americans fear will drive gasoline prices higher .
The Pentagon has restricted press access under Hegseth, imposing policies ‚Äãthat have led about 30 major news organizations, including Fox, the Washington Post and Reuters, to give up their credentials, ‚Äãwith defense officials inviting in new outlets.
On Thursday, CNN reported that the Pentagon and the White House's National Security Council significantly underestimated Iran's willingness to close the strait, citing multiple sources familiar with the matter.
"We stand by our reporting," a CNN spokesperson ‚Äãsaid. Representatives for Paramount had no comment.
David Ellison, son of billionaire Larry Ellison, has deep ties to President Donald Trump's ‚Äãadministration and is leading Paramount's Warner Bros takeover. Ellison took over Paramount after acquiring broadcaster CBS News in 2025 as part of ‚Äåits merger ‚ÅÝwith Skydance Media.
The Trump administration must approve the Paramount-Warner Bros deal, and the U.S. Federal Communications Commission chair this month signaled the agency would not block it.
Both Democratic and Republican lawmakers have raised concerns that the media merger could reduce choices and raise costs for consumers. Critics have also raised concerns about the impact on editorial independence ‚Äãand the erosion of a ‚Äãfree press despite protections under ‚ÅÝthe U.S. Constitution's First Amendment.
Hegseth has invited mainstream media outlets back for briefings in the past two weeks on Iran operations but has mostly taken questions from reporters with non-traditional ‚Äãoutlets.
He spent much of his opening statement on Friday criticizing war coverage. He also ‚Äãfaulted ABC News ‚ÅÝfor reporting on an FBI bulletin that warned that Tehran could try to retaliate for U.S. strikes by launching surprise drone attacks in California. ABC News did not immediately reply to a request for comment.
"We're used to bad reporting. We're used to ill-informed reporting, ‚ÅÝand so ‚Äãit doesn't change how we operate, but we engage with it to ‚Äãprove that it's not true," he said. He also called CNN's latest report on the strait "fake news," "patently ridiculous" and "a fundamentally unserious report."

item.title: White House official: Trump has said a US-Cuba deal could be done easily
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - Donald Trump has said the U.S. is talking to Cuba, its leaders should ‚Äãagree to a deal and that could easily be done, ‚Äåa White House official said on Friday, reiterating the U.S. president's previous remarks after Havana confirmed that negotiations were under way.
Cuban President Miguel Diaz-Canel ‚Äãsaid earlier that his government had opened talks with ‚ÄãWashington, as an oil blockade imposed by Trump pushes ‚ÅÝthe Communist-run nation deeper into economic crisis.
"As the President stated, ‚Äãwe are talking to Cuba, whose leaders should make a deal, ‚Äãwhich he believes 'would be very easily made'," the White House official said, on condition of anonymity.
Diaz-Canel said in a video aired on state television ‚Äãthat negotiations "have been aimed at finding solutions through dialogue to ‚Äãthe bilateral differences we have between the two nations."
Since the U.S. captured ‚ÄåVenezuelan ‚ÅÝPresident Nicolas Maduro in January, removing Cuba's most important foreign benefactor, Trump has cut off Venezuelan oil shipments to Cuba and threatened to slap tariffs on any country that sells oil to ‚ÄãCuba.
On Monday, ‚ÄãTrump said Cuba ‚ÅÝmay be subject to a "friendly takeover," then added, "it may not be a friendly takeover."
The Caribbean ‚Äãnation's citizens, exhausted by years of economic crisis ‚Äãand ‚ÅÝshortages, now live the majority of their days without electricity, strictly rationed fuel and shortages of medicine.
"Cuba is a failing nation whose ‚ÅÝrulers have ‚Äãhad a major setback with the ‚Äãloss of support from Venezuela and with Mexico ceasing to send them oil," the ‚ÄãWhite House official said.

item.title: Israel targets Iranian checkpoints using tip-offs from informants, source says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 13 (Reuters) - Israel has launched a new phase of its assault on Iran, targeting checkpoints manned by ‚Äãthe Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) based on tip-offs from ‚Äåinformants on the ground, a source briefed on Israel's military strategy told Reuters.
The targeting of checkpoints secured by IRGC personnel suggests an intensification ‚Äãof Israel's efforts to weaken Iran's elite forces as ‚Äãit carries out joint aerial bombardment with the ‚ÅÝUnited States.
Israel says its war objectives include destroying Iran's military ‚Äãand nuclear capabilities but also "creating the conditions" for Iranians to ‚Äãoverthrow their government, though Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has said this is an uncertain prospect.
There has been no sign of organised dissent while the ‚Äãcountry is under attack, and no sign of Iran's ‚Äãrulers relinquishing power.
On Thursday, Israel's military said it had struck checkpoints in ‚ÄåTehran ‚ÅÝoperated by the Basij, a part-time paramilitary force under IRGC control that is often used to quell protests inside Iran.
The source, who spoke on condition of anonymity because they were not ‚Äãauthorised to ‚Äãspeak publicly, said ‚ÅÝinformants on the ground in Tehran had passed intelligence to Israel on the locations of ‚Äãthree checkpoints that were struck in the past ‚Äãthree ‚ÅÝdays. The source was not able to confirm whether these included the Basij positions mentioned by the military.
The use of informants ‚ÅÝto ‚Äãidentify checkpoint locations was first reported ‚Äãby the Wall Street Journal.
Israel's military did not immediately respond to a request ‚Äãfor comment.

item.title: Deputy head of Russian space centre detained on suspicion of bribery, TASS reports
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 13 (Reuters) - The deputy head of Russia's Star City, a settlement near ‚ÄãMoscow that hosts the country's main ‚Äåspace training facility, has been detained along with six associates on suspicion of ‚Äãbribery, state news agency TASS reported ‚Äãon Friday.
According to RIA news agency, ‚ÅÝin January the mayor of ‚ÄãStar City Yevgeny Barishevsky was detained on ‚Äãsuspicion of accepting a bribe of 365,000 roubles ($4,557) and put under house arrest.
Investigators said on ‚ÄãFriday that the inquiry had ‚Äãestablished that between 2023 and 2025 the mayor ‚Äåand ‚ÅÝhis deputy received several bribes from businessmen, both directly and through intermediaries, in exchange for favourable outcomes in the ‚Äãawarding of ‚Äãmunicipal ‚ÅÝcontracts.
The total amount involved exceeded 9.9 million roubles ($123,602).
Star City lies ‚Äãabout an hour's drive northeast ‚Äãof ‚ÅÝthe Kremlin. For decades, the town did not appear on maps, with ‚ÅÝits ‚Äãlocation revealed only after ‚Äãthe collapse of the Soviet Union.
($1 = 80.0955 roubles)

item.title: Drone strike in Sudan kills 11, injures over 20, MSF says
item.description: GENEVA, March 13 (Reuters) - French medical charity M√©decins Sans Fronti√®res said on Friday it had treated ‚Äãover 20 people for injuries after a ‚Äådrone strike set fuel reserves alight in western Sudan, killing 11 people.

item.title: Russia names great-granddaughter of Soviet leader Khrushchev as 'foreign agent'
item.description:
LONDON, March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. academic Nina Khrushcheva, the great-granddaughter of a ‚Äãformer Soviet leader, was designated by Russia on Friday as a "foreign agent" - a term ‚Äåwith connotations of spying that Moscow applies to people it views as engaged in anti-Russian activity.
Khrushcheva, 62, is a professor at The New School university in New York and has continued to make research trips to Russia since ‚Äãits full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
Her ancestor Nikita Khrushchev led the Soviet Union from ‚Äã1953 to 1964, when he was ousted by fellow members of the ruling ‚ÅÝPolitburo.
Contacted by Reuters, Khrushcheva said she was not surprised at being added to Russia's "foreign agent" list, ‚Äãwhich as of Friday contains 1,164 names including politicians, journalists, artists, NGOs and media organisations.
"It would have ‚Äãbeen sloppy on their part not to do this sooner or later," she said, adding that it was too early to say what the practical impact would be.
"There is certainly historical irony but not anything shocking. When ‚ÄãStalin is up, Khrushchev is down," she said.
She was alluding to a resurgence in Russia of ‚Äãthe reputation of Soviet dictator Josef Stalin, whose reign of terror was denounced by Khrushchev in a famous ‚Äå1956 address ‚ÅÝto a Communist Party congress.
Russia marked the 70th anniversary of the speech last month, prompting renewed debate about the legacies of both men.
Khrushchev was the Soviet leader who transferred Crimea to Ukraine from Russia in 1954, an act reversed in 2014 when Russian forces invaded the peninsula and President Vladimir ‚ÄãPutin declared its annexation .
Khrushchev is also ‚Äãremembered for facing ‚ÅÝoff against U.S. President John F. Kennedy in the 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis, when the world teetered on the brink of nuclear war.
Russian news agency ‚ÄãTASS cited the Justice Ministry as saying Nina Khrushcheva had disseminated false ‚Äãinformation about Russian ‚ÅÝpolicies and opposed what Moscow calls its "special military operation" in Ukraine.
People listed as foreign agents are subjected to onerous bureaucratic requirements and restrictions on their income in Russia. They are obliged to place the foreign ‚ÅÝagent label ‚Äãon social media posts or anything else they publish.
Some ‚ÄãKremlin critics have worn the label as a badge of honour, while others say it is a burden that hampers them ‚Äãin their work because it causes other Russians to shun them.

item.title: Iran coach Jafari says television comments created difficult atmosphere for players during Asian Cup
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Comments from an Iranian state television presenter about the country's women's team during their Asian Cup campaign affected the players psychologically and drove some to ‚Äãseek asylum in Australia, coach Marziyeh Jafari said on Friday.
The tournament, ‚Äåhosted by Australia, started just as the U.S. and Israel launched air strikes on Iran, killing the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei.
After the team opted to stand in silence during Iran's ‚Äãanthem before their first match against South Korea, they were labelled as "wartime traitors" ‚Äãby Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting presenter Mohammad Reza Shahbazi.
In a ‚ÅÝstatement posted by Iran's football federation (FFIRI) on its Telegram page on Friday, which ‚Äãhas since been deleted, Jafari said: "Our girls were affected in the first match ‚Äãby the heavy atmosphere that had been created...
"But the greater mistake was made by those who, at home, failed to understand that atmosphere and sounded the call to arms against the daughters ‚Äãof this land," she added, referring to the presenter's comments.
"What I have asked ‚Äãof the federation is to pursue the matter, because it affected our players psychologically, and ‚Äåwe suffered ‚ÅÝthe consequences. I am certain that if that atmosphere had not been created, not a single one of our players would have stayed in Australia."
Australia this week granted humanitarian visas to five Iranian players who sought asylum during the tournament.
Two more ‚Äãsquad members were granted ‚Äãasylum on Wednesday, ‚ÅÝand one decided to return to Iran .
"Australian police called the players in several stages and sat down with them one-on-one to ‚Äãpersuade them to stay, influenced by the political atmosphere that ‚Äãhad arisen," ‚ÅÝJafari said.
"Fortunately, the majority of the team members responded negatively. Even Mohaddeseh Zolfi, who had responded positively, soon changed her mind and, God willing, will come to Iran ‚ÅÝwith the ‚Äãteam.
"The rumours about Golnoosh Khosravi and Afsaneh Chatrenoor ‚Äãare also not true at all, and they are now with us in Malaysia and we will ‚Äãbe leaving for Iran soon."

item.title: Pentagon elevates investigation into Iran school strike
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - The U.S. military said on Friday it has elevated the investigation into a devastating February 28 strike on an Iranian girls' school after media reports revealed ‚Äãthe probe shows U.S. forces were likely responsible.
Iran has said the strike on the Shajareh Tayyebeh School killed 168 children. If U.S. fault is confirmed, ‚Äåit would rank among the worst incidents of civilian deaths in decades of U.S. military strikes in the Middle East.
Reuters first reported the investigation's initial findings on March 5.
Citing the seriousness of the matter, U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth declined to comment on the preliminary finding of the investigation, saying: "We're not going to let reporting lead us or force our hand into indicating what happened."
He said a higher-level investigation would be led by ‚Äãan unnamed U.S. general officer from outside of U.S. Central Command, which is overseeing operations against Iran. Such a move is typically taken by the U.S. military to ‚Äãensure greater independence for investigators.
"The command investigation will take as long as necessary to address all the matters surrounding this incident," Hegseth told ‚ÅÝa press conference at the Pentagon.
Iran's new supreme leader blamed the country's enemies for the school strike in his first message to the country on Thursday, without explicitly saying the ‚ÄãU.S. was responsible.
Three U.S. officials told Reuters the command investigation Hegseth referred to is an administrative probe known as a 15-6, which can become the ‚Äãbasis for disciplinary action if warranted. It usually includes sworn statements or interviews with those involved. The officials spoke on condition of anonymity to describe internal military processes.
The head of U.S. Central Command, Admiral Brad Cooper, ordered the 15-6 probe last week after the initial review was completed. The outside officer was appointed on Thursday, one official said.
The development underscores the serious concern about how to respond to possible U.S. responsibility ‚Äãfor the tragedy and the need to comprehensively examine the incident.
"It signals a recognition that something went wrong and to try to understand what happened and why," said Annie Shiel, ‚ÄãU.S. advocacy director with the Center for Civilians in Conflict.
It remains unclear how U.S. forces could have struck the school. Preliminary findings suggest U.S. forces may have relied upon outdated targeting data ‚Äåthat apparently did ‚ÅÝnot distinguish between the school and the adjoining Iranian military base in Minab, a town in southern Iran.
Video and other evidence suggest the school was hit by a Tomahawk cruise missile , a powerful, precision-guided munition that few nations other than the U.S. possess.
The investigation comes despite public comments by President Donald Trump initially doubting the possibility of U.S. involvement that stoked concern among some defense officials about whether he would be willing to accept blame.
The president initially said, without offering evidence, that Iran may have attacked the school and suggested Tehran may have Tomahawk missiles, ‚Äãwhich military experts call extremely unlikely.
Still, Trump ‚Äãon Monday said publicly he would "certainly" accept ‚ÅÝthe results of the U.S. investigation: "Whatever the report shows, I'm willing to live with that report."
A second U.S. official pointed to those remarks as evidence that Trump was moving toward accepting the preliminary investigation results. Further, the official said public discussion of the investigation had made it ‚Äãpolitically difficult for Trump to reject the findings once they are finalized.
The official noted that a 15-6 investigation reflected the seriousness of ‚Äãthe Trump administration's approach ‚ÅÝto the investigation because it would comprehensively examine what went wrong and how to correct those issues.
A Reuters visual investigation published on Thursday showed the Iranian school had a years-long, public online presence, including dozens of photos of the children and their activities.
The Shajareh Tayyebeh School was one of 59 schools within the Persian Gulf Martyrs' Cultural Educational Institute, a network affiliated with the ‚ÅÝIslamic Revolutionary ‚ÄãGuard Corps, the military force that reports to Iran's supreme leader, according to archived copies of the network‚Äôs ‚Äãwebsite.
The school‚Äôs website includes photos of students gathered in the yard, which matched verified videos outside the building after the strike.
Satellite imagery from mid-2015 shows the building was walled off from the Iranian military base and appears to have ‚Äãoperated as a school since at least 2018, when the painted murals are first visible on its outer walls.

item.title: Israeli strike kills three in Gaza as regional offensives escalate
item.description:
CAIRO/JERUSALEM, March 13 (Reuters) - An ‚ÄãIsraeli airstrike in Gaza killed three Palestinians, including two 17-year-olds, Palestinian medics ‚Äåsaid on Friday, with violence continuing in the West Bank and Gaza even as Israel expands its offensive across the region.
Israel has used lethal force in Iran , Lebanon , Gaza and the occupied ‚ÄãWest Bank over the past 24 hours.
Israeli forces killed two in ‚Äãthe West Bank city of Nablus on Thursday, according to Palestinian officials, ‚ÅÝwhile the death toll in Lebanon reached 773, its Health Ministry said on ‚ÄãFriday.
After nearly two weeks of war, 2,000 people have been killed, most of ‚Äãthem in Iran, with several million people displaced from their homes.
The Israeli military told Reuters that they were not aware of the strike in Gaza.
The military said separately in a statement ‚Äãon Thursday that they had killed two individuals after they attempted to carry ‚Äãout a car-ramming attack on soldiers. It did not immediately respond to a request for ‚Äåevidence ‚ÅÝof the attempted ramming.
While Israeli attacks on Gaza declined at the beginning of the war with Iran, they have since begun to rise. Israel has killed 23 in Gaza since the U.S. and Israel jointly struck Iran on February 28.
While ‚Äãa ceasefire went into ‚Äãeffect in Gaza ‚ÅÝin October, there have been regular outbreaks of violence since then.
Reuters has reported that U.S. President Donald Trump's peace plan ‚Äãfor Gaza has been on hold since the start of the ‚ÄãIran war.
Violence ‚ÅÝhas also persisted in the West Bank. Israeli settlers and military forces have killed at least eight Palestinians across the West Bank since the U.S.-Israeli attacks on ‚ÅÝIran began.
Reuters ‚Äãhas reported that there has been a spike ‚Äãin settler violence since then, as Israel has put much of the West Bank under lockdown.

item.title: Mother and three sons in custody over US embassy bomb in Norway
item.description:
OSLO, March 13 (Reuters) - Three brothers and their mother have been ‚Äãremanded in police custody for ‚Äåup to four weeks on suspicion of taking part in ‚Äãthe bombing of the ‚ÄãU.S. embassy in Oslo last ‚ÅÝweek, a Norwegian court ‚Äãsaid in a ruling on ‚ÄãFriday.
The U.S. embassy was hit by an explosion on Sunday and police later ‚Äãsaid they had apprehended the ‚Äãsuspects, accusing them of a "terror bombing" ‚Äåintended ‚ÅÝto kill or cause significant damage.
The powerful early-morning blast from an improvised explosive device (IED) ‚Äãdamaged the ‚Äãentrance ‚ÅÝto the embassy's consular section but caused ‚Äãno injuries, Norwegian authorities ‚Äãhave ‚ÅÝsaid.
One of the men has admitted to placing a ‚ÅÝdevice, ‚Äãwhile the three ‚Äãother suspects have denied involvement, their lawyers ‚Äãhave said.

item.title: Exclusive: Iran lets two gas tankers sail to India through Hormuz, sources say
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 13 (Reuters) - Iran has allowed two Indian-flagged liquefied petroleum gas carriers to sail through the Strait of Hormuz, four sources with direct knowledge of the matter said, a ‚Äãrare exception to the Iranian blockade that has disrupted global energy supplies.
The Indian-flagged LPG tanker Shivalik ‚Äåcrossed the Strait under escort from the Indian Navy, said two of the sources, and the second vessel, Nanda Devi, was expected to clear in the next few hours.
Since the United States and Israel launched a bombing campaign on Iran, Tehran has largely halted ‚Äãtraffic through the strait, which runs past its coast and normally supplies around 20% of global oil ‚Äãand seaborne liquefied natural gas.
Iran has said it will not permit any supplies for ‚ÅÝthe United States or its allies to leave the strait, but India has sought exemptions. Prime Minister Narendra Modi said ‚Äãon Thursday he had spoken to Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian and discussed the transit of goods and energy from ‚Äãthe Gulf.
India has also given safe harbour to 183 Iranian sailors from a vessel that docked after the war broke out. The vessel was one of three that had participated in exercises in India; one of the others was sunk by a U.S. torpedo off Sri ‚ÄãLanka.
India is reeling under its worst gas crisis in decades with the government cutting supplies for industries to shield ‚Äãhouseholds from any shortage of cooking gas.
Shivalik and Nanda Devi are owned by state-run Shipping Corp of India. (SCI.NS) , opens new tab
Shivalik, which had arrived in ‚ÄåQatari ‚ÅÝwaters on February 25, last reported its position on March 12 as within the exclusive economic zone waters up to 24 miles off the United Arab Emirates, according to tracking data on MarineTraffic.
Nanda Devi was last seen on Friday in Iranian waters close to the entrance of the Strait of Hormuz, according to MarineTraffic data. It had called ‚Äãat Qatar‚Äôs Ras Laffan anchorage ‚Äãon February 27 before ‚ÅÝsailing to UAE waters, the data showed.
Separately, a crude tanker is expected to arrive in India on Saturday carrying Saudi Arabian oil after sailing through the Strait around March ‚Äã1, according to two of the sources and data from Lloyd's List Intelligence.
The Liberia ‚Äãflagged Smyrni crude ‚ÅÝoil suezmax tanker, which can carry a maximum capacity of 1 million barrels, is expected to arrive at an Indian port for state refiner Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab .
India's foreign ministry, navy, Shipping Corp and HPCL did not respond to Reuters emails ‚ÅÝsent out ‚Äãafter business hours.
India consumed 33.15 million metric tons of cooking gas ‚Äãlast year, with imports accounting for about 60% of demand. About 90% of those imports came from the Middle East.
On Thursday India said 24 Indian-flagged ‚Äãvessels were stuck in the Gulf area past the narrow strait.

item.title: US proposes easing limits on cancer-causing gas used to clean medical devices
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - The Trump administration is proposing to lift some limits on the use of ethylene oxide, a carcinogenic gas used for sterilization, saying ‚Äãprevious constraints imposed under former President Joe Biden may hamper medical device makers' ‚Äåability to clean their facilities.
In a statement on Friday, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency said its proposed rule would follow current law but make it easier for companies to ‚Äãfollow commercial sterilization regulations while also saving costs and protecting the ‚Äãnation's supply chain for devices such as heart stents and wound ‚ÅÝdressings.
The medical community, industry officials and the Food and Drug Administration all ‚Äãexpressed concerns about the more stringent rule issued in 2024 under the Democratic ‚ÄãBiden administration, which included a second required risk review, as well as standards that required new monitoring systems, vents and enclosures.
The new proposal would allow medical device companies a choice ‚Äãbetween installing new monitoring systems or making other adjustments to new aeration ‚Äãroom vents in areas where ethylene oxide is greater than 10 tons per year.
"These changes ‚Äåbetter ‚ÅÝreflect the complexity of facilities and give them the flexibility to work to use safe and effective equipment to sterilize medical tools and devices without compromising clean air for all Americans," the EPA said in a statement.
They would ‚Äãalso save an estimated $43 ‚Äãmillion annually, ‚ÅÝit added.
Ethylene oxide, or EtO, is a colorless gas used to sterilize equipment, but its toxic emissions pollute the air. ‚ÄãLong-term exposure has been linked to cancer, especially for ‚Äãpeople living ‚ÅÝclose to manufacturing plants that make or use it.
The gas is used to sterilize about half of all U.S. medical devices made each year, according to the ‚ÅÝEPA.
The ‚Äãproposed rule will face a public hearing in ‚Äãabout 15 days and is open to public comment for 45 days before the administration makes ‚Äãa final decision.

item.title: Britain's energy price-cap forecast to rise about 11%, Cornwall Insight says
item.description:
LONDON, March 13 (Reuters) - Britain's domestic energy price cap is forecast to rise by about 11% in July, analysts ‚Äãat Cornwall Insight said on Friday, increasing ‚Äåtheir forecast due to the ongoing impact of the Iran war on wholesale energy prices.
British finance minister Rachel Reeves said ‚Äãon Wednesday she would consider action to shield households ‚Äãfrom surging energy costs due to the ‚ÅÝconflict in the Middle East, but that it was ‚Äãpremature at this stage.
Wholesale gas prices have risen more ‚Äãthan 60% over the past two weeks. Those prices are the largest single factor contributing to British energy regulator Ofgem's ‚Äãdomestic price cap, which is set quarterly using ‚Äãa formula that also reflects suppliers' network costs and environmental and ‚Äåsocial ‚ÅÝlevies.
Disruptions to shipping in the Middle East and a halt to liquefied natural gas exports from Qatar, one of the world's largest gas exporters, has led ‚Äãto a surge ‚Äãin international ‚ÅÝgas prices which is showing no signs of abating.
Cornwall Insight forecasts Ofgem's price ‚Äãcap will rise in July to 1,827 ‚Äãpounds ($2,422) ‚ÅÝa year for typical use, from 1,641 pounds in April.
Last week they had forecast a rise of 10% ‚ÅÝin ‚Äãthe cap.
Ofgem is expected to ‚Äãpublish the next price cap level by May 27.
($1 = 0.7545 pounds)

item.title: Brazil's Petrobras expects little impact on consumers from diesel price hike
item.description:
SAO PAULO/RIO DE JANEIRO, March 13 (Reuters) - Brazilian state-run ‚Äåoil company Petrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab will hike the price of diesel sold to distributors by 0.38 reais ($0.0725) per liter, but expects next to no impact on consumers, CEO Magda Chambriard said on Friday.
The Petrobras hike starting on Saturday should lead to an added cost of ‚Äãup to 0.06 reais per liter for consumers, Chambriard told journalists, calling it "residual" as federal tax cuts ‚Äãease the impact.
"Petrobras is following its commercial strategy, avoiding passing on the volatility of ‚ÅÝinternational prices, and the government is doing its part by eliminating federal taxes," said Chambriard.
The move comes after ‚Äãthe recent spike in oil prices due to the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran , which widened the gap between Petrobras' local diesel ‚Äãprices and international benchmarks to a record level in recent days.
Diesel prices have become a major source of concern for Brazil's President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, who is running for reelection this year.
On Thursday, Lula's government scrapped taxes on diesel in a bid ‚Äãto soften the impact of higher global prices on local consumers, while imposing a 12% levy on oil ‚Äãexports to compensate for the lost revenue.
The Petrobras CEO said the export tax will be more than offset by higher oil ‚Äåprices. Exports ‚ÅÝhave become a major driver of the company's revenue growth, as it sold record volumes of oil abroad in the last quarter.
"I was exporting for $60," said Chambriard. "Now it's at $100. Can I complain about a temporary 12% tax?"
The CEO added that the higher oil prices are "good" for dividends to shareholders and said that shareholders - both the government ‚Äãand private ones - are happy ‚Äãwith the firm.
While most of the diesel Brazil consumes is produced locally by Petrobras, about 25% of the fuel is imported, leaving the country vulnerable to price hikes ‚Äãabroad.
The imbalance between local and international prices was making distributors reluctant to sell at ‚ÄãPetrobras' prices, ‚ÅÝas they were worried they would have to resupply at higher cost.
To increase diesel production, Petrobras is operating its refineries at around 97% of capacity and postponed maintenance stoppages at two of them, Chambriard said.
Last year, the company was operating ‚ÅÝrefineries ‚Äãat around 91% of capacity, and had previously announced it would raise ‚Äãit to 95% over the first quarter.
"We are making an effort to deliver more product to the Brazilian market," she said.
($1 = 5.2441 reais)

item.title: Storms cut power to 396,000 customers in US Midwest, Mid-Atlantic; Ohio hardest hit
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 13 (Reuters) - Over 396,000 homes ‚Äãand businesses were without ‚Äåpower on Friday as severe storms battered the U.S. ‚ÄãMidwest and Mid-Atlantic ‚Äãregion, according to data from ‚ÅÝPowerOutage.us.
The hardest-hit state ‚Äãwas Ohio, with around ‚Äã123,000 outages, representing about 2.3% of the state's roughly 5.4 ‚Äãmillion customers, according to ‚Äãthe data.
The hardest-hit utility in ‚ÄåOhio ‚ÅÝwas a unit of American Electric Power (AEP.O) , opens new tab , with around 40,000 homes and ‚Äãbusinesses in ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝstate without service. AEP provides service ‚Äãto around 1.5 ‚Äãmillion ‚ÅÝelectric customers in Ohio, the data showed.
Here are ‚ÅÝthe ‚Äãmajor outages by ‚Äãstate:

item.title: US offers reward for information on Iran's supreme leader, senior officials
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - The United States is offering ‚Äãa reward of up to $10 million for information about senior Iranian military and intelligence ‚Äåofficials, including its new Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei.
The reward targets 10 officials associated with Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), according to the State Department website. The military force, created after Iran‚Äôs 1979 Islamic Revolution, is ‚Äãloyal to the supreme leader and tasked with protecting the Shi‚Äôite clerical establishment.
Mojtaba Khamenei ‚Äãrecently succeeded his father, Ali Khamenei, as Iran's supreme leader after the elder Khamenei ‚ÅÝwas killed along with several other top Iranian officials in joint U.S. and Israeli strikes ‚Äãthat began on February 28. The younger Khamenei, believed to have been injured in the strikes , hasn't ‚Äãbeen seen publicly since, although he released his first statement on Thursday.
In addition to the supreme leader, the U.S. is seeking information about Iran's security chief Ali Larijani, Intelligence Minister Esmail Khatib, Interior Minister Eskandar Momeni and ‚Äãtwo officials in Khamenei's office.
Larijani appeared Friday in videos verified by Reuters alongside President Masoud Pezeshkian ‚Äãand Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi attending a rally in Tehran, despite an assertion by U.S. Defense Secretary Pete ‚ÄåHegseth ‚ÅÝthat Iran's leadership was "cowering" underground.
The reward website also lists four other officials, including the IRGC commander and secretary of the defense council, but doesn't include their names or photos.
"These individuals command and direct various elements of the IRGC, which plans, organizes, and executes terrorism around the world," the ‚ÄãState Department said.
The Revolutionary Guards ‚Äãcould not be ‚ÅÝimmediately reached for comment on Friday ‚Äî the weekly day of rest in Iran. Iran's mission to the United Nations in New York didn't immediately ‚Äãrespond to a request for comment.
The U.S. has designated the IRGC as ‚Äãa foreign ‚ÅÝterrorist organization, accusing it of being responsible for attacks that have killed U.S. citizens. Washington has also accused Iran of orchestrating assassination plots against President Donald Trump and other U.S. officials in retaliation for ‚ÅÝthe killing ‚Äãof Iranian military commander Qassem Soleimani in 2020.
Iran denies being ‚Äãa sponsor of terrorism. Iranian officials routinely dismiss U.S. terrorism allegations as baseless political attacks, arguing Washington raises such claims ‚Äãto justify pressure campaigns or sanctions.

item.title: Zelenskiy tells Ukrainian media US sought postponement of latest talks on war settlement
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy was quoted as saying ‚Äãon Friday the U.S. ‚Äåhad sought a postponement of the latest round of ‚Äãthree-sided talks on ‚Äãa settlement to Ukraine's four-year-old ‚ÅÝconflict with Russia.
Zelenskiy, ‚Äãwhose comments were quoted by ‚Äãvarious Ukrainian media outlets at the end of a visit ‚Äãto France, said ‚Äãthe U.S. side said its negotiators ‚Äåwere ‚ÅÝnot permitted to leave the United States in view of circumstances in ‚Äãthe ‚ÄãMiddle ‚ÅÝEast.
He said Russia had not wanted to ‚Äãhold the talks ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝthe United States and had proposed alternative sites ‚ÅÝin ‚ÄãSwitzerland or Turkey.

item.title: US expands Venezuela sanctions waivers amid rising prices
item.description:
LOS ANGELES, March 13 (Reuters) - The United States on Friday ‚Äãupdated waivers to Venezuela sanctions ‚Äåaimed at easing the way for investment and activities related to the ‚Äãcountry's oil and the export ‚Äãof fertilizer, as prices rise ‚ÅÝamid U.S. strikes on Iran.
The ‚ÄãU.S. Treasury Department issued three updated ‚Äãgeneral licenses on Friday as part of the move. In a post on ‚ÄãX, the department said it "updated ‚Äãseveral Venezuela-related licenses to further support the revitalization ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝthe country‚Äôs energy sector and help ensure a well-supplied global commodity market."
"These authorizations expand permitted investment ‚Äãand activities ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝVenezuela‚Äôs energy industry and allow for the export ‚Äãof fertilizer directly to the ‚ÄãU.S. ‚ÅÝto support our great American farmers," the Treasury Department said in ‚ÅÝa ‚Äãpost on X.

item.title: Earthquake of magnitude 6.5 strikes near coast of central Chile, GFZ says
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - A magnitude ‚Äã6.5 ‚Äåearthquake struck near ‚Äãthe ‚Äãcoast of ‚ÅÝcentral ‚ÄãChile ‚Äãon Friday, the German ‚ÄãResearch ‚ÄãCentre for ‚ÄåGeosciences (GFZ) ‚ÅÝsaid.
The quake was at ‚Äãa ‚Äãdepth ‚ÅÝof ‚Äã10 ‚Äãkm (6.21 ‚ÅÝmiles), GFZ ‚ÅÝsaid.

item.title: US-owned tanker attacked near Iraq was hit by unmanned boats, early findings show
item.description:
LOS ANGELES, March 13 (Reuters) - Two explosive-laden, unmanned ‚Äåboats rammed the Safesea Vishnu tanker in an Iraqi seaport on Wednesday, sparking a fiery blast that engulfed the vessel's port side in flames and left crew only seconds to react, according to an early assessment conducted by the vessel's ‚ÄãU.S. owner and operator.
"After speaking to the surviving crew members, the attack appears to have ‚Äãbeen deliberate and calculated," New Jersey-based Safesea Group said in a statement.
At least ‚ÅÝ16 tankers and other vessels have come under attack in the Gulf during the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran. ‚ÄãHundreds more have dropped anchor due to Tehran's threat to attack vessels in or near the Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz, which is used to transport roughly one-fifth of the world's oil.
The Marshall Islands-flagged Safesea Vishnu was anchored at the Iraqi port of Khor Al Zubair and engaged in ship-to-ship loading of 53,000 metric tons of naphtha at ‚Äãthe time of the attack.
The vessel's 28 crew members, without time to deploy lifeboats, jumped into ‚Äãthe water to escape the burning vessel. One died; the remaining 27 crew are safe and receiving assistance from ‚Äåthe Embassy ‚ÅÝof India in Iraq, Safesea said. The tanker is reported to be listing in the water, and a salvage team has been sent to stabilize the ship and make sure the surrounding marine environment is safe.
The attack must serve as a wake-up call for governments, maritime authorities, and the international community, ‚ÄãSafesea said.
"Commercial shipping lanes ‚Äãcannot become battle zones," ‚ÅÝit said.
The Malta-flagged Zefyros was the other ship involved in the transfer. A projectile struck that vessel during the Wednesday night assault, its Greece-based manager ‚Äãsaid on Thursday. All 23 crew on the Zefyros were safely evacuated.
Some 20,000 ‚Äãseafarers aboard ‚ÅÝvessels operating in the region face "a dangerous and highly uncertain security situation," according to the World Shipping Council.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said the United States is prepared to escort tankers through the Strait of Hormuz when ‚ÅÝnecessary, ‚Äãbut so far the U.S. Navy has refused near-daily requests ‚Äãfrom the shipping industry for military escorts since the start of the war on Iran, saying the risk of attacks is too ‚Äãhigh for now, according to sources familiar with the matter.

item.title: UN official calls for humanitarian cargo to be allowed through Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - A top United Nations humanitarian official on Friday called for humanitarian aid to be ‚Äãallowed to pass safely through the Strait of ‚ÄåHormuz amid the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
The comments made by Tom Fletcher, the U.N. under-secretary-general for humanitarian affairs and emergency relief coordinator, comes as ‚ÄãIran threatens to close the strait , which is considered a key ‚Äãtrade route .
"When routes close and costs surge, the help ‚ÅÝwe can deliver shrinks ‚Äì and the people who need ‚Äãit most are the ones who lose it first," Fletcher ‚Äãsaid in a statement.
"So my message to the parties to the conflict and all those with influence over them is simple: humanitarian cargo ‚Äãmust be allowed to pass safely through the Strait of ‚ÄãHormuz."
The war started two weeks ago when the U.S. and Israel carried out ‚Äåstrikes ‚ÅÝon Iran, killing top officials, including its supreme leader. Hundreds others have also died since the Feb. 28 start of the war, which has spread across the Gulf region.
Iran retaliated ‚Äãto the initial ‚ÄãU.S.-Israeli attack ‚ÅÝby launching its own strikes and deploying about a dozen mines in the strait.
The ongoing conflict ‚Äãhas already caused a near halt in traffic ‚Äãthrough ‚ÅÝthe Strait of Hormuz, Fletcher said. This, he added, will make it harder and more expensive to deliver critical supplies, including ‚ÅÝfood ‚Äãand medicine.
"I am speaking directly with ‚Äãkey parties, pressing for humanitarian supplies to be allowed to keep moving unobstructed ‚Äãthrough the Strait," he said.

item.title: Refiner MOL complains to EU over Croatian pipeline fees
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 13 (Reuters) - Hungarian refiner MOL (MOLB.BU) , opens new tab said on Friday it and subsidiary Slovnaft had ‚Äãfiled a complaint with the European Commission ‚Äåover Croatian pipeline operator JANAF's pricing policy.
It follows a complaint MOL filed last week with the EU's competition ‚Äãwatchdog over JANAF's refusal to deliver ‚ÄãRussian crude to MOL and its Slovak ‚ÅÝsubsidiary.
An outage since January on the Druzhba pipeline delivering ‚ÄãRussian oil via Ukraine has left landlocked Hungary ‚Äãand Slovakia reliant on oil supply via JANAF.
"JANAF has consistently applied abusive pricing practices," MOL said in its ‚Äãlatest complaint, adding its orders by volume ‚Äãhad increased by one and a half times while ‚ÄåJANAF's ‚ÅÝfees had nearly doubled.
"The fee increases imposed by JANAF are excessive compared to its costs and cannot be objectively justified."
Janaf did not immediately ‚Äãto a ‚Äãrequest for ‚ÅÝcomment.
Hungary and Slovakia hold exemptions to EU restrictions on Russian oil imports.
MOL ‚Äãis allowed to source Russian seaborne ‚Äãcrude ‚ÅÝif the Druzhba pipeline is inoperable, the company has said.
Croatia has expressed its willingness to help ‚ÅÝsupply ‚Äãcrude, but has baulked at ‚Äãsending Russian crude via the JANAF's pipeline.

item.title: Iran war puts many in US on high alert, but synagogue attack shows limits
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. law enforcement officials are on heightened alert ‚Äãas the Iran war enters its third week, but the limits of their vigilance were on display as seeming lone-wolf attacks unfolded more than 500 miles apart in Virginia and Michigan on ‚ÄåThursday alone.
In Michigan, where a Lebanon-born man rammed an explosives-laden truck into the Temple Israel synagogue, it appeared to be one of the first known instances of a specific bombing in the Iran war leading to retaliatory violence on American soil since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on February 28.
Although authorities have not announced a motive, the man, identified by U.S. officials as Ayman Ghazali, carried out his attack a week after Israel bombed his family's town in Lebanon on March 5, killing two of his brothers ‚Äãand a niece and nephew, according to news reports.
Around the same time as the synagogue attack, a man who was previously convicted of supporting a designated terrorist group fatally shot one person and injured ‚Äãtwo others, both U.S. Army personnel, at Old Dominion University in Norfolk, Virginia, which has close ties to the U.S. military. The attacker was killed.
These kinds of lone-wolf outbursts of retaliatory violence are the hardest to spot in advance and intercept, counter-terrorism experts say.
"Someone who is self-radicalized, a lone wolf, is the hardest to track," said Kenneth Gray, a former ‚ÄãFBI counter-terrorism agent who now teaches at the University of New Haven. "Normally there would be opportunities to track weapon transfers, foreign training, or transfers of funds. We apparently have none of that."
The latest incidents took place with the ‚ÄãU.S. engaged in what is already a deeply unpopular war among American voters, which was launched after President Donald Trump made drastic cuts to the Department of Homeland Security's intelligence unit.
Except for a security guard knocked down when the truck crashed through the entrance, everyone at the synagogue, including the children at its preschool, escaped unharmed, although dozens of law enforcement officers were treated for smoke inhalation. Ghazali, who became a U.S. citizen in 2016 and lived in nearby Dearborn Heights, was killed by synagogue security staff.
Shawn Brokos, ‚Äãwho was an FBI official in Pittsburgh when 11 people were killed in the Tree of Life synagogue shooting in 2018, credited Temple Israel for a "textbook reaction" that protected those inside.
The attack also showed the need for ‚Äãvigilance, she said, to be alert for someone who might be embracing "a grievance narrative."
"They're upset about current events," said Brokos, now the director of community security for the Jewish Federation of Greater Pittsburgh. "They're starting to access weapons. They're starting to make ‚Äåplans. These ‚ÅÝare all the things that are helpful for us to know so we can do these checks and share it with law enforcement."
Dearborn Heights Mayor Mo Baydoun said Ghazali's attack must be unequivocally condemned, and also noted Ghazali had been affected by the Iran war, in which more than 2,000 people have been killed , mostly in Iran and Lebanon.
"The tensions we see across the world too often find their way into our own neighborhoods, reminding us how deeply connected our shared safety is," Baydoun said in a statement.
Since the war began, security scares have affected airports in Kansas City and the Washington, D.C. suburbs; two men were charged with terrorism-related ‚Äãcrimes after throwing home-made bombs at an anti-Muslim rally ‚Äãin New York City; and a man who expressed ‚ÅÝpro-Iran views online killed four people after opening fire at a bar in Austin, Texas.
Trump has sought to defend his war to some lawmakers in Congress and, according to polls , many skeptical voters, including some of his "America First" base.
The Department of Homeland Security communicates significant threats to the American public through alerts under the National Terrorism Advisory ‚ÄãSystem. DHS issued such an alert in June 2025, saying brief hostilities between the U.S. and Israel and Iran had caused a "heightened threat environment" in the U.S.
That ‚Äãalert, which cited the risk ‚ÅÝof cyber attacks or violence from extremists already in the U.S., expired in September 2025.
There are no current NTAS advisories. And, last week, the White House halted a bulletin from DHS, the FBI and the National Counterterrorism Center warning state and local law enforcement agencies of a heightened threat to the U.S. because of the Iran war.
Since Trump's return to the White House last year with vows to slash the federal workforce, the DHS Intelligence and Analysis unit went from about ‚ÅÝ1,000 employees to ‚Äãsome 500-600, three former agency officials told Reuters. DHS did not respond to a request for comment.
Travis Nelson, director of Maryland's homeland ‚Äãsecurity office, said DHS has been focused on Trump's mass deportation efforts in its public communications rather than the possible threat posed by the Iran conflict.
"It's all about immigration enforcement," Nelson said. "We haven't seen anything from Homeland Security about the potential risk to the homeland as a ‚Äãresult of what's going on in the Middle East."

item.title: Five US Air Force refueling planes hit in Iranian strike on Saudi Arabia, WSJ reports
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Five U.S. Air Force refueling planes ‚Äãwere struck and ‚Äådamaged on the ground at Prince Sultan ‚Äãair base in ‚ÄãSaudi Arabia, the Wall ‚ÅÝStreet Journal reported ‚Äãon Friday, citing two ‚ÄãU.S. officials.
The planes, which were hit during an ‚ÄãIranian missile strike ‚Äãon the Saudi base in ‚Äårecent ‚ÅÝdays, were damaged but not fully destroyed and are ‚Äãbeing repaired, ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝJournal said, adding that no ‚Äãone was killed ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝthe strikes.
Reuters could not immediately verify ‚ÅÝthe ‚Äãreport.

item.title: US attacks Iran's Kharg Island, Trump says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ‚Äãon Friday Said ‚Äåon Friday that the United States carried ‚Äãout strikes against "every military ‚Äãtarget" on Iran's Kharg Island ‚ÅÝexport hub.
"For ‚Äãreasons of decency, I ‚Äãhave chosen NOT to wipe out the Oil ‚ÄãInfrastructure on ‚Äãthe Island," Trump wrote on ‚ÄåTruth ‚ÅÝSocial.
"However, should Iran, or anyone else, do anything to ‚Äãinterfere ‚Äãwith ‚ÅÝthe Free and Safe Passage ‚Äãof Ships through ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝStrait of Hormuz, I will immediately ‚ÅÝreconsider ‚Äãthis decision."

item.title: Ecuador declares curfew in coastal provinces for security operations
item.description:
QUITO, March 13 (Reuters) - Ecuador's President Daniel Noboa on Friday decreed a nighttime ‚Äãcurfew in four provinces of the Andean ‚Äåcountry, starting March 15, intended to facilitate military operations against criminal organizations with support from the ‚ÄãUnited States.
The measure covers the coastal ‚Äãprovinces of Guayas, El Oro, Santo ‚ÅÝDomingo, and Los Rios - the country's main ‚Äãdrug trafficking routes - and will run from ‚Äã11 p.m. to 5:00 a.m. local time.
Joint military operations between Ecuador and the U.S. started in ‚Äãearly March. A recent mission near the ‚ÄãColombian border resulted in the destruction of a drug ‚Äåtrafficking ‚ÅÝcamp, according to the Ecuadorean Ministry of Defense.
The site was operated by a group known as the Border Commandos, comprised ‚Äãof FARC ‚Äãdissidents, and ‚ÅÝhad the capacity to house 50 people, the ministry added.
Noboa ‚Äãhas made military repression against organized ‚Äãcrime ‚ÅÝa cornerstone of his administration. As part of his hardline stance he has criticized the ‚ÅÝgovernment ‚Äãof neighboring Colombia for ‚Äãfailing to combat drug trafficking, and implemented tariffs on Colombian imports.

item.title: China urges Afghanistan, Pakistan to resolve tensions via talks, not force
item.description:
SHANGHAI, March 14 (Reuters) - Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi told his Afghan counterpart Amir ‚ÄãKhan Muttaqi in a phone call ‚Äåthat disputes between Afghanistan and Pakistan should be resolved through dialogue and consultation, not ‚Äãforce, the foreign ministry said in ‚Äãa statement published on Friday.
Wang urged ‚ÅÝboth sides to remain calm and ‚Äãexercise restraint, hold face-to-face talks as soon ‚Äãas possible, seek an immediate ceasefire and address differences through dialogue, according to the statement.
The ‚Äãfurther use of force would only ‚Äãcomplicate the situation and intensify tensions, Wang said.
The ‚Äåministry's ‚ÅÝstatement came after Pakistan on Friday bombed the fuel depot of private airline Kam Air near Afghanistan's Kandahar airport, stepping ‚Äãup the ‚Äãneighbours' worst ‚ÅÝconflict in years, despite China's efforts to mediate.
Wang and ‚ÄãMuttaqi also exchanged views on the ‚Äãsituation ‚ÅÝin Iran , the statement said.
Wang told Muttaqi, according to the statement, that Beijing is willing ‚ÅÝto ‚Äãwork with the international ‚Äãcommunity, including Afghanistan, to bring peace to Iran.

item.title: Palestinian actor says he can't attend Oscars because of US travel ban
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - Palestinian actor Motaz Malhees said on Friday that a travel ban imposed by the Trump ‚Äãadministration is preventing him from attending this weekend's ‚ÄåAcademy Awards, whose nominees include a movie in which he has a starring role.
"The Voice of Hind Rajab," a film about a ‚Äãfive-year-old Palestinian girl killed by Israeli forces in Gaza ‚Äãin 2024, has been Oscar-nominated for best international ‚ÅÝfeature film.
Malhees, who plays the role of a call ‚Äãcenter operator attempting to help her, said he cannot ‚Äãattend the Academy Awards show, also known as the Oscars, because he has been barred from entering the United States.
"I am not allowed ‚Äãto enter the United States because of my Palestinian ‚Äãcitizenship," Malhees wrote on Instagram, adding that "it hurts" that he would not attend ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝOscars.
The U.S. State Department did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
In a December , opens new tab proclamation restricting entry of foreign nationals, U.S. President Donald Trump said he had "determined ‚Äãto fully restrict ‚Äãand limit ‚ÅÝthe entry of individuals using travel documents issued or endorsed by the Palestinian Authority."
The ‚Äãproclamation restricting entry of people from some ‚Äãcountries cited ‚ÅÝsecurity reasons.
The movie was spurred by an event in which five of Rajab's family members and two ambulance workers ‚ÅÝwho ‚Äãwent to save her were also ‚Äãkilled by Israeli fire. Israel says the incident is under review.

item.title: Eight convicted of terrorism-related charges for attack on Texas ICE facility
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - A jury convicted nine ‚Äãpeople on Friday for their involvement in a shooting at a U.S. ‚ÄåImmigration and Customs Enforcement detention center in Texas last year, the Justice Department said.
Eight of the nine defendants were found guilty of providing material support to terrorists, rioting, conspiring to use and ‚Äãcarry explosives, and using explosives during a riot, local media reported.
The conviction followed ‚Äãa 12-day trial in Texas.
The defendants were charged for their roles ‚ÅÝin a non-fatal shooting of a police officer at the immigration facility on ‚ÄãJuly 4 in Alvarado, Texas.
The defendants, who authorities said were dressed in black "military-style" clothing with head and ‚Äãface coverings when they carried out the attack on the facility, have been described the Trump administration as members of the antifa movement , which was designated a domestic terrorist organization , opens new tab by President Donald Trump ‚Äãlast year.
The case was the first time federal prosecutors filed terrorism charges targeting antifa, a largely ‚Äãunstructured, far-left movement whose followers broadly aim to confront those they view as authoritarian or racist.
Following ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝconviction, U.S. Attorney General Pam Bondi said this will not be the last case of its kind under the Trump administration.
ICE Director Todd M. Lyons welcomed the conviction, saying the summer attack by the defendants was violent in nature and "an abhorrent way ‚Äãfor antifa terrorists to 'protest.'"
The ‚Äãdefendants are Cameron ‚ÅÝArnold, Zachary Evetts, Benjamin Song, Savanna Batten, Bradford Morris, Maricela Rueda, Elizabeth Soto, Ines Soto and Daniel Rolando Sanchez-Estrada.
Lawyers for eight ‚Äãof the nine defendants did not immediately respond to a ‚Äãrequest for ‚ÅÝcomment
"We are thankful that the jury could see through the Government‚Äôs fear mongering antifa 'ambush' narrative," said Cody Cofer, a lawyer for Arnold, the lead defendant in the case.
"We are ‚ÅÝafraid ‚Äãthat our client‚Äôs guilty verdicts for Riot and ‚Äãrelated charges resulted from a compromise during deliberations or failure to consider our client‚Äôs individual conduct."
Cofer expressed an ‚Äãintent to appeal the case.

item.title: US says oil from strategic reserve to start reaching market next week
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - The Trump administration said on ‚ÄãFriday it expects early deliveries of ‚Äåoil from the U.S. strategic petroleum reserve to reach the market by the end ‚Äãof next week as part of ‚Äãan effort to combat the energy price ‚ÅÝspike since the start of the Iran ‚Äãwar.
The oil will be released in ‚Äãthe form of loans that companies will return with extra barrels as a premium, the U.S. ‚ÄãEnergy Department said in a statement. ‚ÄãThis system was meant to stabilize markets "at no cost ‚Äåto ‚ÅÝAmerican taxpayers," it said.
The Energy Department said it had requested companies to bid for 86 million barrels of crude ‚Äãoil, which ‚Äãwill be ‚ÅÝthe first from the release of 172 million barrels that Washington ‚Äãannounced this week.
The U.S. effort ‚Äãis ‚ÅÝpart of a broader release of 400 million barrels of oil agreed to by the ‚ÅÝ32-nation ‚ÄãInternational Energy Agency on ‚ÄãWednesday.

item.title: Barclays raises 2026 Brent forecast to $85 a barrel on Strait of Hormuz disruption
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Barclays on Friday raised its 2026 Brent crude forecast to $85 per barrel, ‚Äãciting ongoing supply disruptions linked to the ‚ÄåIran war that have sharply reduced oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz.
The bank said ‚Äãoil flows through the Strait of ‚ÄãHormuz route have fallen to a trickle ‚ÅÝand production shut-ins in Gulf countries ‚Äãhave climbed to more than 10 million barrels ‚Äãper day.
The revised forecast assumes the situation in the Strait of Hormuz normalizes within two to ‚Äãthree weeks, Barclays said in a research ‚Äãnote.
"If however, the market internalizes that it might take ‚Äåfour ‚ÅÝto six weeks for that, we could see 2026 Brent reprice to $100/b," the bank added.
Oil prices have risen despite efforts ‚Äãby the International Energy ‚ÄãAgency ‚ÅÝto release strategic reserves, as uncertainty around the duration of the ‚Äãconflict continues to support markets.
Brent futures ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝFriday settled at $103.14 a barrel, up $2.68, or 2.67%.
Barclays said the path of least resistance ‚ÅÝfor ‚Äãoil prices remains higher ‚Äãuntil there is a inflection point in the conflict.

item.title: Cyclone Vaianu lashes New Zealand's North Island, hundreds evacuated
item.description:
April 12 (Reuters) - Cyclone Vaianu made landfall in New Zealand's ‚ÄãNorth Island on Sunday, triggering floods, power outages and forcing hundreds to evacuate.
The cyclone crossed the coast ‚Äånear the Maketu Peninsula, packing destructive winds exceeding 130 kph (80 mph), heavy rain and large swells, national weather provider MetService said, describing Vaianu as a "life-threatening" system.
Authorities have placed several regions under emergency declarations and issued "red" level wind warnings, which are reserved for only the most extreme weather events.
The ‚Äãcyclone was tracking towards the fringes of the North Island, sparing Auckland, the country's most populous city, from the ‚Äãworst conditions, said Emergency Management Minister Mark Mitchell. But stronger winds and swells were still expected ‚ÅÝafter its afternoon landfall.
"It‚Äôs moved more to the fringes and more to the east, which means that we haven't quite ‚Äãseen the intensity that we had prepared for or that we thought we were going to get hit with. So that ‚Äãis good news," Mitchell said.
"In terms of what we're going to expect over the next 12 hours is we are going to see a bit more of intensification, as the cyclone starts to come down and get closer."
The combination of the afternoon's high tide and large swells from ‚Äãthe storm could trigger coastal inundation, Mitchell warned.
"The concerning time is really from 2 p.m. this afternoon (0800 GMT) onwards when ‚Äãwe've got high tides combined with those big swells," he said.
The cyclone has forced hundreds of residents to evacuate and knocked out electricity to ‚Äå5,000 ‚ÅÝhomes, with power restored to roughly 2,000, he said.
Authorities in the coastal Whakatane District reported a significant amount of damage as Vaianu passed through, with mandatory evacuations carried out at 270 properties.
New Zealand Defence Force members and heavy equipment have also been deployed to assist with evacuations.
MetService said it had recorded 130 kph wind gusts in some areas, 24-hour rainfall totals of more ‚Äãthan 100 mm (4 inches) in the ‚Äãcity of Whangarei and ‚ÅÝwave heights exceeding six metres (20 feet).
Air New Zealand (AIR.NZ) , opens new tab said in a statement that it had cancelled more than 90 turboprop flights, primarily out of regional North Island airports.
"Domestic jet and international ‚Äãservices are operating as scheduled, although there are some delays due to the weather conditions," the ‚Äãairline said.
Fire and ‚ÅÝEmergency New Zealand said it responded to more than 100 calls for assistance relating to wind damage and surface flooding.
Conditions will ease as Vaianu works its way down the North Island before exiting on Sunday evening at Hawke's Bay, MetService said.
"Things do improve ‚ÅÝfrom tonight ‚Äãand tomorrow, but at the moment this is still a life-threatening weather system," ‚Äãsaid Heather Keats, MetService head of weather news.
Vaianu has conjured up memories of 2023's Cyclone Gabrielle, which killed 11 and displaced thousands in one of New Zealand's ‚Äãbiggest natural disasters this century.

item.title: Vineyard Wind sues GE Vernova to block it from abandoning work on wind farm
item.description:
BOSTON, April 10 (Reuters) - Vineyard Wind has filed a lawsuit seeking to block a wind turbine-supplying unit of GE Vernova (GEV.N) , opens new tab from terminating agreements and abandoning work on a $4.5 ‚Äãbillion offshore wind farm in Massachusetts on the eve of completion.
Vineyard Wind, a ‚Äåjoint venture between Spain's Iberdrola (IBE.MC) , opens new tab and Denmark's Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners, filed a lawsuit , opens new tab this week in a state court in Boston accusing the GE Vernova subsidiary of wrongly trying to terminate their contract at the ‚Äãwind project's "most vulnerable stage."
It is one of five major East Coast offshore wind ‚Äãprojects that President Donald Trump's administration halted construction near the end of ‚ÅÝ2025, citing national security.
Federal judges following lawsuits allowed construction to resume on all five.
After resuming ‚Äãconstruction, Vineyard Wind's 806-megawatt project off the coast of Martha's Vineyard in February became the ‚Äãfirst large-scale offshore wind farm in the United States to begin initial operations.
But days later, GE Renewables US LLC, which designed, manufactured and installed Vineyard Wind's wind turbine generators, sent it a notice threatening to ‚Äãend their turbine supply agreement, the lawsuit said.
Vineyard Wind, in a complaint dated Wednesday, ‚Äãsaid GE did so citing a failure to pay amounts it was contractually due under their $1.3 billion ‚Äåcontract.
Vineyard Wind ‚ÅÝsaid it had exercised its right to withhold about $308 million as a result of GE's "admittedly poor performance," after one of its offshore blades in 2024 collapsed and fell into the waters off Nantucket, causing delays.
Vineyard Wind called GE's termination of the agreements improper and has asked ‚Äãa judge to ‚Äãblock GE from ceasing ‚ÅÝwork on the wind project, which "threatens to deprive hundreds of thousands of Massachusetts ratepayers of clean energy."
A judge has scheduled an April ‚Äã16 hearing to consider whether to issue a preliminary injunction.
GE Vernova ‚Äãin a ‚ÅÝstatement said it had elected to terminate its agreements due to Vineyard Wind withholding the money. The company said it did so after completing the installation of all 62 wind turbines ‚ÅÝat the ‚Äãwind farm.
"The company remains committed to the safety of ‚Äãthe wind farm and stands by our performance and our contractual obligations," GE Vernova said. "We will vigorously defend our ‚Äãposition through the appropriate legal process."

item.title: Iberdrola puts $1 billion solar stake sale on hold, sources say
item.description:
MADRID, April 10 (Reuters) - Iberdrola (IBE.MC) , opens new tab has put on hold for now the attempted sale of a minority stake in ‚Äãa roughly 1-gigawatt Spanish solar energy portfolio, according ‚Äåto two people familiar with the matter.
The Spanish company decided to explore the sale last year, seeking a valuation of around $1 billion for ‚Äãthe whole portfolio known as Project Julieta, one of ‚Äãthe sources said.
Yet the size of the valuation ‚ÅÝcomplicated talks, one of the people said, while the other ‚Äãpointed to waning interest from one unnamed bidder seen until ‚Äãrecently as the frontrunner.
Iberdrola said it doesn't comment on what it called market speculation.
