Spain's solar power capacity has increased rapidly in recent years, ‚Äãwhile electricity demand hasn't kept pace, hitting the prices ‚Äãthat producers get for their output and the value of their generating ‚Äåassets.
For ‚ÅÝIberdrola, asset rotation and partnerships - which include the disposal of non-core or mature assets and the sale of minority stakes - have been key elements of its strategy over recent ‚Äãyears.
The company uses ‚Äãproceeds from ‚ÅÝsuch deals to help fund new investments in core businesses, increasingly power grids. It set ‚Äãa target to raise 13.2 billion euros ‚Äãthrough 2028, 58% ‚ÅÝof which would come from partnerships and the rest from asset rotation. Three quarters of this has already been achieved.
It ‚ÅÝhas ‚Äãpartnered with the likes of Japan's Kansai ‚ÄãElectric Power , Norway's sovereign wealth fund and Abu Dhabi's state-owned renewable energy company Masdar .

item.title: Dutch government to appeal ruling in Bonaire climate case
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, April 10 (Reuters) - The Dutch state is appealing a court ruling that it ‚Äãmust set binding emissions targets ‚Äåto reach net zero by 2050 to protect the Caribbean island of ‚ÄãBonaire, a spokesperson for Climate ‚ÄãMinister Stientje van Veldhoven said ‚ÅÝon Friday.
Bonaire, a special municipality ‚Äãof the Netherlands located in the ‚Äãsouthern Caribbean and until 1954 a Dutch colony, is well-known as an eco-tourism ‚Äãdestination.
In its ruling, the court ‚Äãhad given the Netherlands 18 months to set ‚Äåup ‚ÅÝa legally binding plan to reduce greenhouse gas emissions to net zero by 2050 as agreed ‚Äãin international ‚Äãtreaties.
The ‚ÅÝcase had been brought by Greenpeace on behalf ‚Äãof Bonaire residents and was ‚Äãamong ‚ÅÝthe first to test national climate obligations set out in a ‚ÅÝlandmark ‚Äã2024 European climate ruling ‚Äãand last year's influential World Court advisory opinion .

item.title: Vietnam‚Äôs Vingroup signs pact with India's Maharashtra to explore $6.5 bln investments
item.description:
April 10 (Reuters) - Vietnam's largest conglomerate Vingroup (VIC.HM) , opens new tab on Friday signed a memorandum of understanding with India's Maharashtra's ‚Äãstate government to explore $6.5 billion in investments aimed at building a ‚Äåmulti-sector ecosystem in the financial capital.
The move will deepen Vingroup's footprint in India, where its electric vehicle unit VinFast operates a manufacturing facility in the ‚Äãstate of Tamil Nadu, and where it said it ‚Äãplans to build a $3 billion ecosystem in Telangana state.
As ‚ÅÝpart of the agreement, Vingroup will explore investments across ‚Äãsectors including urban development, electric mobility, renewable energy and ‚Äãpublic infrastructure in Maharashtra.
In urban development, the company is considering integrated townships spanning roughly 1,000 hectares near Mumbai at an estimated investment ‚Äãof about $5 billion, while in electric mobility it ‚Äãplans to deploy a fleet of 60,000 electric taxis at a $1.5 billion investment.
Vingroup did not ‚Äåprovide ‚ÅÝdetails on the investment disbursement timeline, but said that the plan is expected to create tens of thousands of jobs over the next three to five ‚Äãyears.
Maharashtra is ‚ÄãIndia's largest ‚ÅÝstate economy, accounting for about 14% of the country's GDP. Major domestic EV players such ‚Äãas Mahindra and Mahindra (MAHM.NS) , opens new tab and Tata Motors (TAMO.NS) , opens new tab ‚Äãhave ‚ÅÝa strong manufacturing presence in the state.
Vingroup's operations span industrials, real estate and green energy. The company has been expanding ‚ÅÝinto ‚Äãoverseas markets, with India identified as ‚Äãa key strategic priority.

item.title: Trump says US will blockade Strait of Hormuz after Iran peace talks fail
item.description:
MIAMI/ISLAMABAD, April 12 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said on Sunday the U.S. Navy would start ‚Äãblockading the Strait of Hormuz, raising the stakes after marathon talks with Iran failed to reach a deal to end the war , jeopardising a fragile two-week ‚Äåceasefire.
Trump also said in a post on Truth Social that the U.S. would take action against every vessel in international waters that had paid a toll to Iran, and begin destroying mines that he said the Iranians had dropped in the strait, a choke point for about 20% of global energy supplies that Iran has blocked.
"No one who pays an illegal toll will have safe passage on the high seas," Trump wrote, adding: "Any Iranian ‚Äãwho fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be BLOWN TO HELL!"
Iran's Revolutionary Guards responded with a statement warning that military vessels approaching the strait will ‚Äãbe considered a ceasefire breach and dealt with harshly and decisively, underlining the risk of a dangerous escalation.
Iran's Parliamentary Speaker Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf, ‚ÅÝwho led his country's delegation to the talks along with Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi, said Trump's new threats would have no effect on Iran.
"If you fight, we will fight, and if ‚Äãyou come forward with logic, we will deal with logic," he said, in comments carried by state media.
Six weeks of fighting has killed thousands , roiled the global economy and sent oil prices soaring as ‚ÄãIran prevented traffic through the strait.
In an interview with Fox News after his post about the strait, Trump said that he believed Iran would continue to negotiate and called the weekend discussions "very friendly".
"I do believe they're going to come to the table on this, because nobody can be so stupid as to say, ‚ÄòWe want nuclear weapons,‚Äô and they have no cards," Trump told Fox News from his golf course near Miami, ‚ÄãFlorida.
Trump also said that NATO allies, whom he has criticised for failing to back the war he launched along with Israel on February 28, wanted to help with the operation in ‚Äãthe strait.
There was no immediate comment from Washington's allies.
The weekend talks in Islamabad, which followed the announcement of a ceasefire last Tuesday, were the first direct U.S.-Iranian meeting in more than a decade and the ‚Äåhighest-level discussions ‚ÅÝsince the 1979 Islamic Revolution.
"The bad news is that we have not reached an agreement, and I think that's bad news for Iran much more than it's bad news for the United States of America," said Vice President JD Vance, who headed the U.S. delegation.
A U.S. official said Iran had rejected Washington's call for an end to all uranium enrichment, the dismantling of all major enrichment facilities and the transfer of highly enriched uranium. The two sides also failed to reach agreement on the U.S. demand that Iran cease funding for Hamas, Hezbollah and the Houthis as ‚Äãwell as fully open the strait, the official ‚Äãadded.
Qalibaf blamed the U.S. for not winning Tehran's ‚ÅÝtrust, despite his team offering "forward-looking initiatives". Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian, who discussed the talks in a call with Russian President Vladimir Putin, said Tehran wanted "a balanced and fair agreement".
"If the United States returns to the framework of international law, reaching an agreement is not far off," he ‚Äãtold Putin, Iranian state media reported.
Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency said "excessive" U.S. demands had hindered reaching a deal. Other Iranian ‚Äãmedia said there was ‚ÅÝagreement on a number of issues, but the strait and Iran's nuclear programme were the main sticking points.
Despite the stalemate, three supertankers fully laden with oil passed through the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday, shipping data showed, in what appeared to be the first vessels to exit the Gulf since the ceasefire deal.
Israel has continued bombing Tehran-backed Hezbollah militants in Lebanon, insisting - along with Washington - that that conflict ‚ÅÝwas not part ‚Äãof the Iran-U.S. ceasefire. Iran says the fighting in Lebanon must stop.
The Israeli military said it struck Hezbollah rocket ‚Äãlaunchers overnight into Sunday and black smoke could be seen rising in the southern suburbs of the Lebanese capital Beirut.
And in Israeli villages near the border, air raid sirens sounded, warning of incoming rocket fire from Lebanon.

item.title: Gaza aid flotilla aims to break Israeli blockade
item.description:
MADRID, April 12 (Reuters) - A second flotilla carrying humanitarian aid to Palestinians ‚Äåin Gaza was due to set sail on Sunday from the Spanish port of Barcelona, aiming to try to break the Israeli blockade.
Thirty-nine boats were due to leave the Mediterranean port city, a spokesperson for the flotilla ‚Äãsaid, and more vessels also laden with medical aid and other supplies are expected ‚Äãto join along the route towards Palestine .
Rough seas mean the flotilla will sail to ‚ÅÝanother port then head out to international waters later in the week, Thiago Avila, a ‚Äãmember of the flotilla's organising committee, told a press conference on Sunday.
The Israeli military halted a previous flotilla ‚Äãassembled by the same organisation last October as the boats attempted to reach blockaded Gaza, arresting Swedish activist Greta Thunberg and more than 450 other participants.
Israel, which controls all access to the Gaza Strip, ‚Äãdenies withholding supplies for its more than 2 million residents. Yet Palestinians and international aid ‚Äãbodies say supplies reaching the territory are still insufficient, despite a ceasefire reached in October which included guarantees of ‚Äåincreased ‚ÅÝaid.
Liam Cunningham, an actor who starred in the Game of Thrones television series who is supporting the flotilla but not taking part, told Reuters: "Every kilogram of aid that is on these ships is a failure because all these people on these ships giving up their time to help ‚Äãtheir fellow human beings are ‚Äãdoing what their ‚ÅÝgovernments are legally obliged to do."
The World Health Organization has said that even during armed conflicts, states are obligated under international humanitarian law to ‚Äãensure that people are able to reach medical care in safety.
‚ÄúThis is ‚Äãa mission ‚ÅÝthat aims to open a humanitarian corridor so the aid delivery organisations can arrive,‚Äù Saif Abukeshak, a Palestinian activist and member of the flotilla‚Äôs organising committee, told Reuters.
Swiss and Spanish activists on last year's ‚ÅÝflotilla said ‚Äãthey were subjected to inhumane conditions during their detention ‚Äãby Israeli forces - an allegation that was rejected by an Israeli foreign ministry spokesperson.

item.title: Hungary's Orban concedes landmark defeat to centre-right opposition
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - Hungary's veteran nationalist leader Viktor Orban conceded defeat on Sunday after a ‚Äãlandslide election victory by the upstart opposition Tisza party, in a setback for his allies in Russia and U.S. ‚ÄåPresident Donald Trump's White House.
Results based on 46% of votes counted showed the centre-right, pro-EU Tisza party of Peter Magyar , opens new tab winning 135 seats - or a crucial two-thirds majority - in the 199-member parliament, ahead of Orban's Fidesz party.
"The election results are not final yet, but the situation is understandable and clear," Orban said at the Fidesz campaign offices. "The election result is painful ‚Äãfor us, but clear."
Pollsters predicted a record voter turnout, with Hungarian television showing long queues outside some voting stations in ‚ÄãBudapest. Data at 1630 GMT, half an hour before polls were due to close, showed 77.8% of voters ‚ÅÝcasting their ballots, up from 67.8% four years earlier.
If the final results confirm the early readings, an end to Orban's period in ‚Äãgovernment after 16 years in power would have significant implications not only for Hungary, but for the European Union, Ukraine and beyond.
It would ‚Äãlikely spell an end to Hungary's adversarial role inside the EU, possibly opening the way for a 90 billion euro ($105 billion) loan to war-battered Ukraine blocked by Orban.
Defeat for Orban could also mean the eventual release of EU funds to Hungary that the bloc had suspended due to what Brussels said was ‚ÄãOrban's erosion of democratic standards.
Orban's exit would also deprive Russian President Vladimir Putin of his main ally in the EU and send shockwaves ‚Äãthrough Western right-wing circles, including the White House.
In Hungary, a Tisza victory could open the way for reforms that the party says would aim to ‚Äåcombat corruption ‚ÅÝand restore the independence of the judiciary and other institutions.
However, the extent of such reforms will depend on whether Tisza can secure the two-thirds constitutional majority it would need to reverse much of Orban's legacy.
Orban, a eurosceptic, carved out a model of an "illiberal democracy" seen as a blueprint by Trump's Make America Great Again (MAGA) movement and its admirers in Europe.
But many Hungarians have grown increasingly weary ‚Äãof Orban, 62, after three years ‚Äãof economic stagnation and soaring ‚ÅÝliving costs as well as reports of oligarchs close to the government amassing more wealth.
Tisza's leader Magyar appears to have successfully tapped into this frustration.
Casting his vote for Tisza in the Hungarian capital, Mihaly ‚ÄãBacsi, 27, said the country needed change.
"We need an improvement in public mood, there is too ‚Äãmuch tension in many ‚ÅÝareas and the current government only fuels these sentiments," he said.
Another voter, who gave her name as Zsuzsa, said she wanted continuity.
"I would really like if all the results that have been achieved in recent years remain - and I am terribly afraid of the war," she said, referring ‚ÅÝto the ‚Äãconflict raging in Ukraine, Hungary's eastern neighbour.
Orban sought to cast Sunday's election as ‚Äãa choice between "war and peace" . During campaigning, the government blanketed the country with signs warning that Magyar would drag Hungary into Russia's war with Ukraine, something he strongly denies.

item.title: Ben-Gvir visits Al-Aqsa Mosque compound
item.description:
JERUSALEM, April 12 (Reuters) - Israel's far-right police minister Itamar Ben-Gvir visited the flashpoint Al-Aqsa Mosque compound in Jerusalem on Sunday, saying ‚Äãhe was seeking greater access for Jewish worshippers and drawing condemnation ‚Äåfrom Jordan and the Palestinians.
The compound in Jerusalem's walled Old City is one of the most sensitive sites in the Middle East . Known to Jews as Temple Mount, it is ‚Äãthe most sacred site in Judaism and is Islam's third-holiest site.
Under ‚Äãa delicate, decades-old arrangement with Muslim authorities, it is administered by ‚ÅÝa Jordanian religious foundation and Jews can visit but may not pray ‚Äãthere.
Suggestions that Israel would alter the rules have sparked outrage among Muslims and ‚Äãignited violence in the past.
"Today, I feel like the owner here," National Security Minister Ben-Gvir said in a video filmed at the site and distributed by his office. "There is still more to ‚Äãdo, more to improve. I keep pushing the Prime Minister (Benjamin Netanyahu) to do ‚Äãmore and more ‚Äî we must keep rising higher and higher."
A statement from the Jordanian foreign ‚Äåministry ‚ÅÝsaid it considered Ben-Gvir's visit to be a violation of the status quo agreement at the site and "a desecration of its sanctity, a condemnable escalation and an unacceptable provocation".
The office of Palestinian president, Mahmoud Abbas, said such actions could ‚Äãfurther destabilize the ‚Äãregion.
Ben-Gvir's spokesman said ‚ÅÝthe minister was seeking greater access and prayer permits for Jewish visitors. He also said that Ben-Gvir had prayed at ‚Äãthe site.
There was no immediate comment from Netanyahu's office. ‚ÄãPrevious such ‚ÅÝvisits and statements by Ben-Gvir have prompted Netanyahu announcements saying that there is no change in Israel's policy of keeping the status quo.
Muslim, Christian and Jewish sites, ‚ÅÝincluding ‚ÄãAl-Aqsa had been largely closed , opens new tab to the public during the ‚ÄãIran war . There was no immediate sign of unrest on Sunday after Ben-Gvir's visit.

item.title: Nigerian airstrike hits market, 200 feared dead in northeast Yobe state
item.description:
MAIDUGURI, Nigeria, April 12 (Reuters) - At least 200 people are feared dead after Nigerian military jets struck a village market while pursuing Islamist militants in the northeast of the country on Saturday night, a councillor for the area and residents said on Sunday.
The Nigerian ‚ÄãAir Force, responding to reports of civilian casualties, said in a statement it had activated ‚Äãits Civilian Harm Accident and Investigation Cell "to immediately proceed to the location on a ‚ÅÝfact-finding mission on the allegation".
Earlier, the Air Force said it had killed Boko Haram militants in ‚Äãthe Jilli axis in Borno state.
The government of neighbouring Yobe state later said in a statement that an ‚Äãair strike on the area had been conducted near a market where shoppers and vendors had gathered.
"Some people from Geidam LGA (local government area) bordering Gubio LGA in Borno state who went to the Jilli weekly market were affected," said ‚ÄãBrigadier General Dahiru Abdulsalam, military adviser to the Yobe state government.
He gave no further details.
The strike occurred in a village in Yobe on the border with Borno, the heartland of a long-running insurgency ‚Äåthat has ‚ÅÝkilled thousands of people and displaced millions more.
Lawan Zanna Nur Geidam, the councillor and traditional head of Fuchimeram ward in Yobe's Geidam district, told Reuters that those injured were being taken to hospitals in Yobe and Borno.
"It's a very devastating incident at Jilli Market. As I'm speaking to you, over 200 ‚Äãpeople have lost their lives ‚Äãfrom the air strike ‚ÅÝat the market," he said in a telephone interview.
Three other residents and an official from an international humanitarian agency confirmed the strike and the estimated ‚Äãdeath toll.
The Yobe State Emergency Management Agency said it had received preliminary reports ‚Äãof an ‚ÅÝincident at Jilli Market "which reportedly resulted in casualties affecting some marketers" and activated emergency response.
Ahmed Ali, a 43-year-old resident who sells medical consumables at the market, said he had been injured in a blast.
"I became so scared ‚ÅÝand attempted ‚Äãto run away, but a friend dragged me and we ‚Äãall lay on the ground," he said from hospital.

item.title: South Africa coalition party elects Cape Town mayor as leader
item.description:
JOHANNESBURG, April 12 (Reuters) - South Africa's Democratic Alliance (DA) ‚Äåelected Cape Town Mayor Geordin Hill-Lewis as its leader on Sunday, as the ruling coalition's second-biggest party sought to capitalise on discontent to expand its power base in local polls this year.
The 39-year-old was widely considered the favourite ‚Äãto succeed Agriculture Minister John Steenhuisen, who has led the pro-business party since 2019 and ‚Äãis stepping down.
"If we work hard, we can win more towns and cities ‚ÅÝthan we've ever won before," Hill-Lewis said in his acceptance speech at a party conference near Johannesburg, while laying ‚Äãout broad ambitions for the next national election in 2029.
"I am not satisfied being a junior partner ‚Äãin a coalition government. Our ambition must be to lead the national government," he said.
Hill-Lewis has given few details about his plans but is not expected to depart significantly from the policies of his predecessor, who took the ‚ÄãDA into a coalition with the African National Congress (ANC) in 2024 while continuing to fight it ‚Äãon issues such as national health insurance and affirmative action, which the DA opposes.
Africa's most industrialised country must hold ‚Äålocal elections ‚ÅÝbefore November, and President Cyril Ramaphosa's ANC is expected to see its share of the vote slip again.
Local polls have traditionally gone worse for the ANC than the national vote, as voters furious at failures to deliver basic services like water and road repairs punish the party that has been ‚Äãin power since apartheid ‚Äãended in 1994.
The DA holds 22% of seats in the lower house of parliament, second to the ANC with 41%, and ‚Äãhas kept roughly the same vote share for the last decade.
The DA ‚Äãhas a reputation ‚ÅÝfor protecting the interests of South Africa's white minority - which it denies - and has not had a non-white leader since Mmusi Maimane resigned in 2019. Some analysts see that as a limiting factor for growth ‚ÅÝin a country ‚Äãthat is more than 90% non-white.
"It would've been so much ‚Äãeasier for them to appeal to a broader constituency if there was a dynamic person ... of another colour," author and political ‚Äãscientist Susan Booysen said.

item.title: Three killed as armed men attack Pakistan Coast Guards in Arabian Sea
item.description:
QUETTA, Pakistan, April 12 (Reuters) - Separatist insurgents killed three Pakistan Coast Guard personnel on Sunday in the first-ever attack on one of the maritime authority's patrol boats operating ‚Äãin the Arabian Sea, security officials said.
Intelligence and police officials ‚Äãsaid the boat was on a routine patrol in ‚ÅÝa coastal area close to the Pakistan-Iran border when the militants ‚Äãopened fire and killed three on board.
The incident adds to security ‚Äãchallenges in Balochistan, a flashpoint of insurgency where armed groups have frequently targeted security forces and infrastructure.
The banned separatist group Balochistan Liberation Army (BLA) claimed responsibility for ‚Äãthe attack. "Following the operations on land, the action in maritime ‚Äãboundaries marks a new development in the BLA‚Äôs military strategy," the BLA said ‚Äåin ‚ÅÝa statement.
Authorities have launched an investigation and security has been heightened in the area, said the intelligence and police sources.
Pakistan carried out airstrikes in February that sparked a week-long conflict ‚Äãwith Afghanistan. Islamabad blames ‚ÄãKabul for ‚ÅÝproviding safe haven to militant groups including the BLA to plan and coordinate their attacks. The Taliban ‚Äãgovernment has denied the accusation.
Gwadar, an important district ‚Äãwith a ‚ÅÝstrategic deep-sea port that is a key node in China's flagship "Belt and Road" infrastructure initiative, has seen repeated attacks on security forces ‚ÅÝand installations ‚Äãby Baloch separatist militants.
Authorities did not ‚Äãrespond to requests for comment about the incident.

item.title: Saudi Arabia summons Iraq envoy over drone threats from Iraqi territory
item.description:
April 12 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia said on ‚ÄãSunday it ‚Äåhad summoned Iraq's ambassador over ‚Äãwhat ‚Äãit described as threats ‚ÅÝtargeting the ‚Äãkingdom and ‚Äãother Gulf states from drones launched ‚Äãfrom Iraqi ‚Äãterritory.
The Saudi foreign ministry ‚Äåsaid ‚ÅÝin a statement that it had "emphasised ‚Äãthe ‚Äãimportance ‚ÅÝof Iraq dealing ‚Äãresponsibly with ‚Äãthese ‚ÅÝthreats and attacks".

item.title: Australia and US boost support for critical minerals with $3.5 billion
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 12 (Reuters) - Australia and the U.S. have committed more than A$5 billion ($3.5 billion) to back a range of critical mineral projects, nearly double the amount pledged when the two countries struck ‚Äãa co-operation agreement six months ago, Canberra said on Sunday.
The funding seeks to ‚Äåsupport Australian ventures to develop and refine metals vital to industries including defence, advanced manufacturing and the energy transition, a market that has long been dominated by China.
By doing so, they would support the re-industrialisation of America's ‚Äãhigh-tech manufacturing base, while helping to "counter China's export dominance and ensure Western supply-chain resilience," ‚Äãthe two nations said when they reached a framework deal last October .
In that ‚ÅÝdeal, Australia and the U.S. agreed to each provide at least $1 billion in investments towards ‚Äãan $8.5 billion pipeline of priority critical mineral projects across the two countries over the subsequent six months.
Australia ‚Äãhas a vast supply of critical minerals such as rare earths, but China has mastered the technically difficult and environmentally fraught refining process.
"Australia and the U.S. are delivering on the commitments made in the White House, with priority ‚Äãprojects in Australia that support production of rare earths and critical minerals," Resources Minister Madeleine ‚ÄãKing said in a statement.
"Australia is taking a global lead to diversify crucial supply chains for critical minerals and ‚Äårare ‚ÅÝearths, which are vital to support economic and national security for Australia and our trading partners."
The A$5 billion in funding to Australian critical mineral projects will be carried out through the Export Finance Australia (EFA) and U.S. Export-Import Bank (EXIM) agencies.
Among the largest investments is for a rare ‚Äãearths refinery project owned by Tronox ‚ÄãHoldings (TROX.N) , opens new tab where EFA ‚ÅÝand EXIM have issued letters of support and interest worth a combined A$849 million. Tronox, with operations in Western Australia and the U.S., would tap its ‚Äãexisting mining and processing capabilities to produce mixed rare earth carbonate containing ‚Äãboth light ‚ÅÝand heavy rare earth elements, King said.
The agencies also committed up to A$1 billion in combined backing for Ardea Resources' (ARL.AX) , opens new tab Kalgoorlie Nickel Project in Western Australia.
Other projects supported under the framework include Alcoa's (AA.N) , opens new tab Gallium Recovery ‚ÅÝProject and ‚ÄãArafura's (ARU.AX) , opens new tab Nolans Rare Earths Project along with graphite, magnesium ‚Äãand tungsten ventures.
Additional projects for minerals including vanadium and scandium have also received indications of support, King said.
($1 = 1.4164 Australian dollars)
(1 ‚ÄãAustralian dollar = $0.7060)

item.title: Israel approves appointment of new intelligence agency chief
item.description:
JERUSALEM, April 12 (Reuters) - Israel approved the appointment of Roman ‚ÄãGofman as the new Mossad ‚Äåintelligence agency head, a statement from the Israeli prime ‚Äãminister's office said on ‚ÄãSunday.
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin ‚ÅÝNetanyahu announced his intention ‚Äãto nominate Gofman in December ‚Äã2025 and a committee for senior appointments approved the decision on ‚ÄãSunday. Gofman will ‚Äãstart his role in June 2026, ‚Äåreplacing ‚ÅÝthe agency's current director David Barnea.
Gofman, 49, is a seasoned military general who ‚Äãrushed ‚Äãto Israel's ‚ÅÝsouthern border to rescue Israeli communities ‚Äãunder fire from a ‚ÄãHamas-led ‚ÅÝinfiltration on October 7, 2023. The incoming director ‚ÅÝcurrently ‚Äãserves as the ‚Äãprime minister's military secretary.

item.title: Iran's new supreme leader has severe and disfiguring wounds, sources say
item.description:
DUBAI, April 11 (Reuters) - Iran's new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei is still recovering from severe facial and leg injuries suffered in the airstrike that killed his father at the beginning of the war, three people close to his inner circle told Reuters.
Khamenei's face was disfigured in the attack on the supreme leader's compound in central Tehran and he suffered a significant injury to one or both legs, all three sources said.
The 56-year-old is nonetheless recovering from his wounds and remains mentally ‚Äãsharp, according to the people, who requested anonymity to discuss sensitive matters. He is taking part in meetings with senior officials via audio conferencing and is engaged in decision-making on major issues including the war and negotiations ‚Äåwith Washington, two of them said.
The question of whether Khamenei's health allows him to run state affairs comes during Iran's moment of gravest peril for decades, with high-stakes peace talks with the United States opening in the Pakistani capital Islamabad on Saturday.
The accounts of the people close to Khamenei's inner circle provide the most detailed description of the leader's condition for weeks. Reuters couldn't independently verify their descriptions.
Khamenei's whereabouts, condition and ability to rule still largely remain a mystery to the public, with no photo, video or audio recording of him published since the air attack and his subsequent appointment as his ‚Äãfather's replacement on March 8.
Iran's United Nations mission did not respond to Reuters questions about the extent of Khamenei's injuries or the reason he has not yet appeared in any images or recordings.
Khamenei was wounded on February 28, the first ‚Äãday of the war launched by the U.S. and Israel, in the attack that killed his father and predecessor Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who had ruled since 1989. Mojtaba Khamenei's wife, brother-in-law ‚ÅÝand sister-in-law were among other members of his family killed in the strike.
There has been no official Iranian statement on the extent of Khamenei's injuries. However, a newsreader on state television described him as a "janbaz", a term used for those badly wounded in war, ‚Äãafter he was named supreme leader.
The accounts of Khamenei's injuries tally with a statement made by U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth on March 13 when he said that Khamenei was "wounded and likely disfigured".
A source familiar with U.S. intelligence assessments told Reuters that Khamenei was ‚Äãbelieved to have lost a leg.
The CIA declined to comment on Khamenei's condition. The Israeli prime minister's office didn't respond to questions.
Alex Vatanka, senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, said that, regardless of the severity of his injuries, it was unlikely the new and inexperienced leader would be able to command the overarching power wielded by his father. While he is seen to represent continuity, it could take years for him to build up the same level of automatic authority, Vatanka added.
"Mojtaba will be one voice but it will not be the decisive one," he said. "He needs to prove himself ‚Äãas the credible, powerful, overriding voice. The regime as a whole has to make a decision in terms of where they are going to go."
One of the people close to Khamenei's circle said images of the supreme leader could be expected to be ‚Äãreleased within one or two months and that he might even appear in public then, although all three sources stressed he would only emerge when his health and the security situation allowed.
In Iran's theocratic system of rule, ultimate power is meant ‚Äåto be wielded ‚ÅÝby the supreme leader, a venerable Shi'ite Muslim cleric appointed by an assembly of 88 ayatollahs. The leader oversees the elected president while directly commanding parallel institutions including the Revolutionary Guards, a powerful political and military force.
Iran's first supreme leader, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, enjoyed unquestioned authority as the charismatic leader of the revolution and the most learned cleric of his day.
His successor, Ali Khamenei, was a less revered cleric but had served as Iran's president. He spent decades cementing his authority after his appointment in 1989, partly through promoting the power of the Revolutionary Guards.
His son Mojtaba does not command absolute power in the same way, senior Iranian sources have previously told Reuters. The Revolutionary Guards, who helped steer him into the top job after his father's assassination, have emerged as the dominant voice on strategic ‚Äãdecisions during the war. Iran's U.N. mission didn't respond to questions ‚Äãabout the power wielded by the Guards and the ‚ÅÝnew supreme leader.
As an influential figure in his father's office, Khamenei had previously spent years involved in exercising power at the top levels of the Islamic Republic, officials and insiders have said, building ties with senior Guards figures.
While he is widely seen as likely to continue his father's hardline approach due to his links to the Guards, we don't know much about his world view, said Vatanka ‚Äãat the Middle East Institute.
Khamenei's first communication with Iranians as supreme leader came on March 12, saying in a written statement read out by a television news presenter that the Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz should stay closed and ‚ÅÝwarning regional countries to shut U.S. bases.
His office has since issued a few other brief written statements from him, including on March 20 when he welcomed in the Persian new year, which he named the "year of resistance". Public statements of policy on Iran's war stance, its approach towards diplomacy, neighbours, ceasefire negotiations and domestic unrest, have been made by other senior officials.
Khamenei's absence is widely discussed on Iranian social media and in messaging app groups, when the country's patchy internet allows, with conspiracy theories widespread about his condition and ‚ÅÝwho is running the ‚Äãcountry.
One popular meme circulating online is a picture of an empty chair under a spotlight with the slogan "Where is Mojtaba?"
However, some government supporters, including a ‚Äãsenior member of the Basij militia, a volunteer paramilitary group run by the Revolutionary Guards, said that it was important for Khamenei to keep a low profile, given the threat posed by waves of U.S. and Israeli airstrikes that have already wiped out much of the country's leadership.
A lower-ranking Basij member agreed.
"Why should he ‚Äãappear in public? To become a target for these criminals?" Mohammad Hosseini, from the city of Qom, said in a text message.

item.title: Some of Swalwell's fellow Democrats urge him to quit Congress amid sexual assault allegations
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. Representative Eric Swalwell, a front-runner candidate for California governor, faced mounting calls to resign from Congress on Sunday as some of his fellow Democrats urged him to leave following accusations of ‚Äãsexual assault.
In separate TV interviews on Sunday morning talk shows, Democratic Representatives Pramila Jayapal, Ro Khanna ‚Äåand Eugene Vindman each said Swalwell should quit Congress.
Their comments came two days after The San Francisco Chronicle and CNN reported that a woman who previously worked in Swalwell's district office accused him of two nonconsensual sexual encounters.
CNN also reported that three other women ‚Äãmade sexual misconduct allegations against Swalwell, who represents a California district.
Swalwell has denied the accusations as "absolutely false" and ‚Äãvowed to fight them.
"What he did is sick and disgusting," Khanna told "Fox News Sunday" while ‚ÅÝcalling for law enforcement and House ethics investigations of Swalwell.
The Manhattan district attorney‚Äôs office on Saturday confirmed that it is ‚Äãinvestigating the sexual assault allegations.
Leading Democrats have previously called on Swalwell to drop out of the California governor's race. ‚ÄãAs pressure grows on him to also resign from Congress, some lawmakers have said they would back a motion to expel him from the House of Representatives if he does not leave on his own.
Representative Byron Donalds, a Republican from Florida appearing on NBC's "Meet ‚Äãthe Press", and Jayapal, a representative from Washington also appearing on "Meet the Press", each said on Sunday they would ‚Äãvote to expel Swalwell from Congress if such a vote came up.
In a social media post on Saturday, Representative Anna Paulina Luna, ‚Äåa ‚ÅÝFlorida Republican, wrote that she planned to file a motion to expel Swalwell from Congress. She told Fox News that she would file the disciplinary motion next week.
Another Democrat, Representative Sheila Cherfilus-McCormick , a Florida representative, also faces a potential expulsion vote after a House panel recently concluded that she violated ethics rules. A grand jury indicted her in November after ‚Äãshe was accused of stealing ‚Äãpandemic relief funds and ‚ÅÝdirecting that money to her 2021 congressional campaign.
Cherfilus-McCormick's office did not immediately return a request for comment. She has previously denied wrongdoing.
While Luna and other Republicans have called for ‚Äãexpelling Swalwell and Cherfilus-McCormick, Democrats have been calling for an expulsion of Representative Tony ‚ÄãGonzales, a Texas ‚ÅÝRepublican being investigated by the House over sexual misconduct allegations.
Gonzales' office did not immediately return a request for comment. Last month, he said he would not seek re-election later this year.
Donalds told "Meet the Press" that he wants to see Swalwell ‚ÅÝand Gonzales ‚Äãleave. "As far as I'm concerned both gentlemen need to go home," ‚ÄãDonalds said.
While calling on Swalwell to resign, Khanna told Fox that he also believes "there are other members (of Congress) and Republican members who should resign ‚Äãas well," such as Gonzales.

item.title: Hungary's opposition Tisza party leads last Median poll done before Sunday election
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - Hungary's centre-right Tisza party ‚Äãwas projected to ‚Äåwin 135 mandates in Hungary's 199-seat ‚Äãparliament, ahead ‚Äãof Prime Minister Viktor ‚ÅÝOrban's Fidesz ‚Äãparty, in a ‚Äãpoll carried out in the days before Sunday's ‚Äãelection by ‚Äãpollster Median.
According to the ‚Äåsurvey ‚ÅÝannounced on RTL Klub television and conducted between ‚ÄãApril 7 ‚Äãand ‚ÅÝ11, the far-right Mi ‚ÄãHazank (Our Homeland) ‚Äãparty ‚ÅÝwould not pass the 5% threshold ‚ÅÝto ‚Äãget ‚Äãinto parliament.

item.title: Iran's Guards will view military vessels approaching strait as ceasefire breach
item.description:
CAIRO, April 12 (Reuters) - Iran's Revolutionary Guards said ‚Äãon Sunday that ‚Äåany military vessels attempting to approach the ‚ÄãStrait of Hormuz ‚Äãwill be considered a ‚ÅÝviolation of ‚Äãthe two-week U.S. ceasefire ‚Äãand be dealt with harshly and decisively.
The strait ‚Äãis under ‚Äãthe control and 'smart management' of ‚ÄåIran's ‚ÅÝNavy, the Guards said in a statement reported by ‚ÄãIranian ‚Äãstate ‚ÅÝmedia, adding it is "open for ‚Äãthe safe passage ‚Äãof ‚ÅÝnon-military vessels in accordance with specific ‚ÅÝregulations".

item.title: Trump says it will take a little while for US to blockade the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: MIAMI, Florida, April 12 (Reuters) - Implementing a ‚Äånewly announced U.S. ‚Äãblockade ‚Äãof the Strait ‚ÅÝof ‚ÄãHormuz will ‚Äãtake a little while, ‚ÄãPresident ‚ÄãDonald Trump said ‚Äåin ‚ÅÝan interview with Fox ‚ÄãNews' "Sunday ‚ÄãMorning ‚ÅÝFutures with ‚ÄãMaria ‚ÄãBartiromo".

item.title: Peruvians vote in crowded presidential race as runoff looms
item.description:
LIMA, April 12 (Reuters) - Peruvians headed ‚Äãto the polls on Sunday to vote for a new president and members of Congress, casting ballots in a first-round field of ‚Äåmore than 30 presidential candidates after years of political turmoil that have eroded confidence in institutions and left voters deeply disillusioned.
With no clear frontrunner and all major candidates polling well below the 50% needed to win outright, a June 7 runoff appears likely. That could prolong uncertainty in the world's third-largest copper producer at a time of rising crime ‚Äãand intensifying competition for influence between the United States and China.
Voting stations opened at 7 a.m. local time (1200 GMT), with about 27 million people ‚Äãeligible to vote.
Early in the day, voters in parts of Lima complained that several polling stations had yet to open.
Margarita ‚ÅÝSandoval, 35, said she had waited in line for two hours without being able to enter her ballot station in Chorrillos, a southern district of ‚Äãthe capital.
‚ÄúI have to work and I can't vote," Sandoval said. "These elections are a disaster."
Others said the crowded race had made the decision difficult. ‚ÄúI only decided ‚Äãwho to vote for about a week ago,‚Äù said 33-year-old shop assistant Benjamin Alcantara.
Since 2018, Peru has cycled through eight presidents, fueling skepticism that any new administration will last a full five-year term following a dizzying turnover driven by impeachments, corruption scandals and weak governing coalitions that have paralyzed decision-making.
‚ÄúPeople really despise the current Congress," said Martin ‚ÄãCassinelli at the Atlantic Council. "They recognize them as responsible for the political chaos we've had over the last ten years,‚Äù he added.
Political distrust has fueled ‚Äãa crowded field spanning the ideological spectrum, including seasoned politicians, a far-right businessman and a television comedian.
Among the best known is conservative Keiko Fujimori , making her fourth presidential bid after reaching ‚Äåthe runoff ‚ÅÝin all three previous races.
Educated in the U.S. and leader of the powerful Popular Force party in Congress, Fujimori has framed herself as a guarantor of order and economic stability, appealing to voters alarmed by surging violent crime. Her candidacy remains polarizing, however, due to her family legacy and past legal troubles .
Ricardo Belmont, a former Lima mayor running for the center-left Civic Works Party, has shot up into second place after a late rise in support. He is followed by ‚Äãpopular comedian Carlos Alvarez, who is ‚Äãcampaigning on a tough-on-crime platform. Both ‚ÅÝare considered outsiders who have gained traction by tapping into a broader anti-establishment mood, according to analysts.
On the right, former Lima Mayor Rafael Lopez Aliaga, a wealthy businessman with socially conservative views, has campaigned on an ultra-conservative platform ‚Äãbut has seen support fluctuate.
Public insecurity has emerged as the dominant theme of the campaign. Homicides and extortion have ‚Äãclimbed in recent years, ‚ÅÝdriven in part by drug trafficking and illegal mining. Most leading candidates have proposed expanding the role of the armed forces in internal security.
The election also carries geopolitical implications. Peru's deepening economic relationship with China -- now its largest trading partner and a major investor in mining and infrastructure -- has raised concerns in Washington , which has ‚ÅÝstepped up ‚Äãdiplomatic and security engagement ahead of the vote.
Whoever advances to the runoff will face a fractured ‚ÄãCongress and newly reinstated Senate, which may complicate efforts to pass legislation and raise the risk of renewed impeachment battles.
Polls close at 5 p.m. (2200 GMT), with preliminary results from the country's election ‚Äãmonitor expected soon after.

item.title: China offers incentives to Taiwan following opposition leader's visit
item.description:
BEIJING, April 12 (Reuters) - China on Sunday unveiled 10 new incentive measures for Taiwan, including easing tourist curbs, allowing in "healthy" television ‚Äãdramas and facilitating food sales, following a visit by the island's opposition ‚Äåleader.
The move comes at the end of a trip to China by Cheng Li-wun, chairwoman of the Kuomintang (KMT), Taiwan's largest opposition party. She met Chinese President Xi Jinping and talked about ‚Äãthe need for peace and reconciliation.
The 10 measures, unveiled by the official ‚ÄãXinhua news agency, "explore" the establishment of a regular communication mechanism between ‚ÅÝthe KMT and China's Communist Party, the full resumption of flights between the ‚Äãtwo sides and permission for individuals from Shanghai and Fujian province to visit Taiwan.
A ‚Äãmechanism will be established to ease inspection standards for food and fishery products, but that has to be on the political foundation of "opposing Taiwan independence," Xinhua said.
Taiwanese TV dramas, documentaries and ‚Äãanimation will be allowed to be shown as long as they have "correct orientation, ‚Äãhealthy content, and high production quality," it added.
Taiwan's Mainland Affairs Council, which is in charge of ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝisland's China policy, said in a statement that Beijing's so-called "unilateral concessions" are merely poisoned pills packaged as "generous gift packages."
The Taiwanese government supports healthy and orderly cross-strait exchanges, but they should not be subject to political preconditions or objectives, it said.
In a ‚Äãstatement, the KMT welcomed ‚ÄãChina's announcement, saying ‚ÅÝit was a "gift" to the people of Taiwan.
China refuses to speak to Taiwan President Lai Ching-te, saying he is a "separatist." He ‚Äãrejects Beijing's sovereignty claims over the democratically governed island.
China and ‚ÄãTaiwan have ‚ÅÝblamed each other for the lack of a resumption of large-scale Chinese tourism to the island since the end of the COVID-19 pandemic.
Taiwan has also previously complained about ‚ÅÝChinese ‚Äãrestrictions on imports of certain agricultural and aquatic products, ‚Äãsaying China has in some cases used unjustified excuses to stop the spread of pests and diseases.

item.title: Irish police clear blockades as Dublin acts to soften fuel price blow
item.description:
DUBLIN, April 12 (Reuters) - Irish police on Sunday cleared tractors and trucks that had ‚Äåbeen blocking traffic and blockading oil infrastructure across the country during a wave of protests against surging fuel prices.
In a bid to ease the discontent, the government announced ‚Äã500 million euros ($586 million) worth of spending increases and tax ‚Äãcuts to soften the impact on consumers and businesses.
Protesters, angered ‚ÅÝby a more than 20% rise in diesel prices since the outbreak ‚Äãof the Iran war , had used vehicles to block an oil refinery, two ports, ‚Äãa fuel terminal and Irish roads.
The protests caused major transport disruption in Dublin and left about a third of Ireland's petrol stations without fuel, creating what Finance Minister ‚ÄãSimon Harris described as a "very dangerous moment" for the country.
Police removed vehicles ‚Äãblocking access to Ireland's only oil refinery on Saturday and cleared a blockade of ‚ÄåGalway Port, ‚ÅÝa major fuel depot, and removed roadblocks in Dublin on Sunday.
The government has refused to negotiate with the protesters, who included farmers, drivers and contractors, who complained that an earlier 250 million euro package to temporarily cut ‚Äãtaxes on petrol ‚Äãand diesel did ‚ÅÝnot go far enough.
But after talks with agricultural and transport industry groups, Dublin announced measures including a 10 ‚Äãcent per litre reduction for diesel and petrol and ‚Äãa delay ‚ÅÝin an increase in a carbon tax. A fuel subsidy support scheme will also be introduced for farming and fisheries, the government said.
A poll in ‚ÅÝthe ‚ÄãSunday Independent newspaper showed that 56% of ‚Äãvoters surveyed supported the protesters, but that most supporters of the two governing parties opposed them.
($1 = ‚Äã0.8533 euros)

item.title: Gas prices might stay high through midterms, Trump says
item.description:
MIAMI, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said on Sunday that the price of oil and gasoline ‚Äãmay remain high through November's midterm elections, a rare acknowledgement of the potential political fallout from his decision to attack Iran ‚Äåsix weeks ago.
"It could be, or the same, or maybe a little bit higher, but it should be around the same," Trump, who is in Miami for the weekend, told Fox News' "Sunday Morning Futures With Maria Bartiromo" when asked whether the cost of oil and gas would be lower by the fall.
The average price for regular gas at ‚ÄãU.S. service stations has exceeded $4 per gallon for most of April, according to data from GasBuddy. Trump's comments on Sunday came after ‚Äãweeks of asserting that the spike in prices is a short-term phenomenon, though his top advisers are cognizant of ‚ÅÝthe war's economic impacts, officials have said.
Earlier on Sunday, Trump announced on social media that the U.S. Navy would blockade the Strait of Hormuz and intercept ‚Äãany ship that paid a crossing fee to Iran, after marathon talks between the U.S. and Iran in Pakistan over the weekend did not yield ‚Äãa peace deal.
"No one who pays an illegal toll will have safe passage on the high seas," he wrote on Truth Social.
Any U.S. blockade is likely to add more uncertainty to the eventual resolution of the conflict, which is currently subject to a tenuous two-week ceasefire. The new tactic is in response to Iran's own closure of the ‚Äãstrait's critical shipping lanes, which has caused global oil prices to skyrocket about 50%.
The war began on February 28, when ‚Äãthe U.S. launched a joint bombing campaign with Israel against Iran. The scope quickly expanded as Iran and its allies attacked nearby countries, while Israel targeted Hezbollah with ‚Äåmassive strikes ‚ÅÝin Lebanon.
The war has buffeted global financial markets and caused thousands of civilian deaths, mostly in Iran and Lebanon.
Trump's political standing at home has suffered, with polls showing the war is unpopular among most Americans, who are frustrated by rising gasoline prices.
The president's approval rating has hit the lowest levels of his second term in office, raising concern among Republicans that his party is poised to lose control of Congress in the midterm elections. A Democratic majority ‚Äãin either chamber could launch investigations ‚Äãinto the Trump administration while ‚ÅÝblocking much of his legislative agenda.
U.S. Senator Mark Warner of Virginia, the ranking Democrat on the Senate Intelligence Committee, questioned the strategy behind Trump's planned blockade.
"I don't understand how blockading the strait is going to somehow push the ‚ÄãIranians into opening it," he told CNN's "State of the Union" on Sunday.
In a separate appearance on CBS's "Face the ‚ÄãNation," Warner said the ‚ÅÝblockade would not undermine Iranian control of the waterway.
"The Iranians have hundreds of speedboats where they can still mine the strait or put bombs against tankers in closing the strait," he said. "How is that going to ever bring down gas prices?"
Although Trump has repeatedly said that the war would be over soon, ‚ÅÝRepublican U.S. Senator ‚ÄãRon Johnson of Wisconsin told ABC News' "This Week" on Sunday that achieving U.S. aims ‚Äãin Iran "could take a long time."
"It's going to be a long-term project," said Johnson, who was not asked about Trump's proposed blockade. "I never thought this would be easy."

item.title: Australia PM Albanese to visit Brunei, Malaysia to shore up fuel supply
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 12 (Reuters) - Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese will visit Brunei and Malaysia this week, continuing diplomatic efforts to secure fuel supplies ‚Äãfrom Asian allies after disruptions caused by the Middle East ‚Äåconflict.
Albanese will travel to Bandar Seri Begawan and Kuala Lumpur from April 14 to 17, his office said in a statement on Sunday.
He is scheduled to ‚Äãmeet Brunei's ruler Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah and Malaysian Prime ‚ÄãMinister Anwar Ibrahim.
Foreign Minister Penny Wong would also meet her ‚ÅÝcounterparts in Brunei and Malaysia, the statement said.
"Engaging with critical ‚Äãregional partners such as Brunei Darussalam and Malaysia will help ensure Australia's ‚Äãenergy supply remains secure during times of uncertainty," Albanese said.
"We are taking every step to reinforce relationships and engage with key partners to keep our fuel ‚Äãsupply flowing."
The trip comes as Australia leans on its Asian allies ‚Äãto maintain fuel supplies amid disruptions caused by Iran's blockade of the Strait ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝHormuz, through which about one-fifth of the world's oil and gas transited before the conflict.
Australia, which imports about 80% of its fuel, has experienced localised shortages and soaring prices since the strait's closure.
Malaysia is ‚ÄãAustralia's third-largest source ‚Äãof refined fuel ‚ÅÝand supplies 10% of its fertiliser-grade urea imports, while Australia supplies 95% of Malaysia's imported natural ‚Äãgas.
Brunei supplies 9% of Australia's diesel imports and 11% ‚Äãof its ‚ÅÝfertiliser-grade urea imports.
The government has held talks on fuel with Brunei, China, Indonesia, Japan, Malaysia and South Korea, among others.
Albanese also travelled to ‚ÅÝSingapore ‚Äãlast week and met Prime Minister Lawrence Wong ‚Äãon Friday, with the two leaders agreeing to closer cooperation to ensure energy security.

item.title: Morning Bid: Back from the brink?
item.description:
April 10 - Everything Mike Dolan and the ROI team are excited to read, watch and listen to over the weekend.
From the Editor
Hello Morning Bid readers!
Markets returned from the long holiday weekend anything but rested, as U.S. President Donald Trump threatened civilizational destruction if Iran didn‚Äôt reopen the Strait of Hormuz before his deadline on Tuesday. But instead of hellfire came news of a two-week ceasefire, which was initially cheered by markets. The question now is whether that optimism will last as investors consider the wide gap ‚Äãbetween Washington and Tehran and the damage to energy supply chains caused by this conflict.
No one knows what will happen next, of course, but it seems likely that conditions will be less benign than many traders currently seem to believe .
The eleventh-hour ‚Äåceasefire announcement late on Tuesday sent both Brent and West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude prices tumbling from upwards of $110/bbl to under $100 a barrel .
This, in turn, allowed global stocks and bonds to stage a relief rally on Wednesday , as U.S. equity indexes finished sharply higher , Asian markets surged , and Europe‚Äôs STOXX 600 logged its best day in over 4 years . Government bonds rallied as well, while the dollar weakened against developed-market peers on hopes of a lasting end to the conflict.
However, cracks quickly emerged in the Pakistan-brokered ceasefire, pushing crude prices back towards $100/bbl on Thursday and stalling the global equity relief rally .
The deal appeared increasingly fragile after Israel on Wednesday staged its largest attack on Hezbollah during this conflict, with Washington and Tehran ‚Äãdisagreeing about whether strikes on the Lebanese militant group were meant to be included in the ceasefire agreement. Additionally, Iran attacked Saudi Arabia's East-West Pipeline ‚Äì its only crude oil export route since the war began ‚Äì shortly after the ceasefire was agreed .
For markets, the biggest ‚Äãquestion is what is ‚Äì or is not ‚Äì happening in the Strait of Hormuz, the critical artery for the global energy system. Trump had said the ceasefire is contingent on the narrow waterway‚Äôs reopening, yet the ‚ÅÝstrait remains effectively closed .
For Tehran, reopening appears to involve charging tolls for transiting ships, a demand that would likely result in higher global energy prices for years to come . This has all led Trump to write on Truth Social: ‚ÄúThat is not the agreement we have!‚Äù
Further peace talks are still ‚Äãset to go ahead in Islamabad this weekend, despite Iran suggesting they would be ‚Äúunreasonable‚Äù in light of the Israeli strikes in Lebanon and Trump issuing further military threats . Israel has said it would open separate talks with the Lebanese government aimed at ending the war there and disarming Hezbollah.
Markets appear somewhat optimistic ‚Äãabout the likelihood that fighting in Iran will not resume soon, with U.S. stock indexes advancing on Thursday and Asian equities rising on Friday , putting them on track for their best week in over three years. Wall Street was flat before the bell on Friday.
However, even if the parties agree on a lasting ceasefire, this will likely only bring partial relief to global energy markets. As a reminder, oil prices are still well above their levels before the conflict broke out, with Brent and WTI up around 34% and 48%, respectively, since the war began on February 28.
What's more, production capacity in the region is still depressed, with Saudi Arabia's oil output ‚Äãdown by 600,000 barrels per day and throughput on its East-West Pipeline by about 700,000 bpd, according to government sources .
While exporters in the Middle East could soon begin shipping oil trapped in the Gulf if the Strait of Hormuz is reopened, hopes of a rapid return to normal ‚Äãflows are almost certainly misplaced . This is particularly worrisome for Asia, where physical energy markets are likely to remain under stress for months even in the most optimistic scenarios.
All of this leaves the Federal Reserve in an increasingly unenviable position as higher global energy prices threaten to exacerbate already elevated inflation ‚Äì and potentially unanchor ‚Äåinflation expectations ‚Äì as ‚ÅÝAmericans eye higher prices at the pump .
On that front, this week‚Äôs macro releases included U.S. services PMIs that showed rising input costs ‚Äì an early signal of renewed inflationary pressure ‚Äì alongside PCE inflation figures that showed an expected uptick in U.S. price increases in February, before the war started.
At a global level, IMF chief Kristalina Georgieva warned again on Monday that ‚Äúall roads‚Äù now point toward higher prices and slower growth , a message unlikely to comfort policymakers wary of the spectre of stagflation.
Federal Reserve minutes released on Wednesday suggested more officials are leaning toward a rate hike in response to inflation risks, though most continue to see rate cuts as the base case later this year, with scope for deeper easing should the conflict begin to weigh on U.S. employment.
Both policymakers and investors will thus be eagerly awaiting today‚Äôs release of consumer price index (CPI) data for March, as investors shift their attention away from the ‚Äãbattlefield ‚Äì at least for now.
For more data-driven insights on markets and ‚Äãcommodities, check out Reuters Open Interest . You can learn:
This weekend, we're reading...
RON ‚ÄãBOUSSO, ROI Energy Columnist: This Foreign Affairs article , opens new tab offers a sharp analysis of China‚Äôs view on the Iran war and the growing risk that a volatile U.S. poses to Beijing‚Äôs economic and diplomatic interests.
ANDY ‚ÄãHOME, ROI Metals Columnist: A new International Energy ‚ÅÝAgency report , opens new tab is essential reading on rare earths that goes beyond the basics to spotlight an often-overlooked driver: explosive demand for rare earth permanent magnets.
JAMIE MCGEEVER, ROI Markets Columnist: "Shattered Assumptions And The Energy Quandary" , opens new tab is a deep dive by the always interesting Louis-Vincent Gave of Gavekal Research into the place global energy holds in the minds of investors - the risks and rewards, the upsides and downsides.
We're listening to...
CLYDE RUSSELL, ROI Asia Commodities and Energy Columnist: I appeared on Gulf Intelligence's Daily Energy Markets Podcast , opens new tab for a great discussion on the Iran war ceasefire, with heaps of scepticism over whether it will hold and result ‚ÅÝin a lasting ‚Äãdeal. We also explored why the real crisis lies in refined fuels, not crude prices.
And we're watching...
ANNA SZYMANSKI, ROI Editor‚Äëin‚ÄëCharge: Why hasn‚Äôt the Iran conflict triggered a classic flight ‚Äãto safety? I explored this question on the Reuters Econ World Podcast , opens new tab with host Carmel Crimmins and my ROI colleague Mike Dolan, discussing the recent moves in gold, Treasuries and haven currencies. We ask whether it may be time to rethink the entire concept of a safe haven.
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? ‚ÄãSign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the authors. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: What the Chicago Bears relocation fight can teach us about economics
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 10 (Reuters) - The battle to house the Chicago Bears‚Äô new stadium is pitting Midwestern U.S. states against each other. Taxpayers are apt to be the losers - and not just because sports stadiums rarely live up to their economic billing.
After more than half a century, the National Football League‚Äôs Chicago Bears are looking for a site to build ‚Äãa new stadium. The question of where has become a political football, with two states falling over themselves to pay for it.
The odds-on favorite destination was initially Arlington Heights, Illinois, a ‚Äåsuburb located northwest of Chicago. That cause was advanced last month by a committee of the Illinois General Assembly‚Äôs House of Representatives which approved a bill that would bestow tax breaks on qualified ‚Äúmegaprojects.‚Äù
Meanwhile, the other leading contender, Hammond, Indiana, won support from its own state legislature in the form of a $1 billion public funding package. Indiana Governor Mike Braun also signed a bill to create a Northwest Indiana stadium authority.
Players within the federal government have also gotten into the game. Democratic U.S. Representative Greg Casar of Texas and ‚ÄãIndependent Senator Bernie Sanders of Vermont have introduced a Home Team Act that would bar any sports team from relocating without first offering itself for sale to local buyers.
Casar contended that such a ‚Äãrestriction was imperative, saying that under current conditions, ‚ÄúBillionaire owners pit taxpayers against one another and then extort the government for billions of dollars.‚Äù
Leaving aside his demonization of the ‚ÅÝsuperrich, the congressman has a point about taxpayers often getting soaked to pay for athletic stadiums.
It is hardly news that tax-financed stadiums have a proven record of failing to deliver the economic benefits promised to voters.
My ‚Äãbook ‚ÄúUnwarranted Intrusions: The Case Against Government Intervention in the Marketplace,‚Äù published 20 years ago, summarized research showing that the presence of a stadium merely diverts spending from other entertainment and creates mostly low-paying jobs.
Proponents of tax subsidies for ‚Äãsports franchises often counter that having a major-league team enables a city to attract industrial facilities and corporate headquarters.
In reality, when considering a city as a potential site, corporations are primarily concerned with other factors important to their workforce, such as the quality of an area‚Äôs schools.
Another point to remember is that sports teams haven‚Äôt always needed enticements to move to ‚Äì or stay in ‚Äì a city. Professional baseball's first enclosed ballpark opened in Brooklyn in 1862, yet it took another 91 years before a local government first ‚Äãused taxpayer dollars to lure a team, when Milwaukee did so to attract the Boston Braves in 1953.
Governments‚Äô rush to splash tax breaks on sports teams has only amplified the problem over time, a reminder of the ‚Äãpower ‚Äì and danger ‚Äì of economic incentives.
If tax breaks for stadiums do not represent a worthwhile public investment in economic development, what kinds of government expenditures actually can move the needle?
The Information Technology and Innovation Foundation (ITIF), a well-respected nonpartisan ‚ÄåU.S. nonprofit concerned ‚ÅÝwith public policy, publishes estimates of economic multipliers, measures of the proportional increase in gross domestic product (GDP) that results from private investment or government spending.
According to ITIF estimates, in an advanced economy like the U.S., an investment of 1% of GDP in infrastructure increases economic output by approximately 1.5%. A comparable investment in education or human capital produces an increase of about 3%.
These investments take time to mature, of course. Infrastructure spending‚Äôs peak impact is typically reached after five to ten years, with the full benefits of education and human capital investment taking 20 years or more to show up, the ITIF notes.
A third category of productive public investment is scientific research and development. ITIF ‚Äãestimates that putting 1% of GDP into R&D raises ‚Äãannual growth by more than 1%. Peak impact ‚ÅÝis reached in seven or more years.
The International Monetary Fund largely backs up these conclusions. Its October 2025 Fiscal Monitor found that in advanced economies, a reallocation of 1% of GDP from low-impact government consumption to infrastructure can raise output by approximately 1.5% over 25 years, increasing to 3%‚Äì6% when redirected to human capital investments, namely, education ‚Äãor health.
Unfortunately, economic growth payoffs that occur over years or decades are less visible to voters than the splashy arrival of an NFL ‚Äãteam.
Legislators with reelection concerns are thus ‚ÅÝlikely to focus more on that type of short-term ‚Äúwin‚Äù rather than investments with more potential to boost growth over the long haul. The future prosperity of constituents not yet old enough to vote is typically not a politician‚Äôs chief concern.
Given these dynamics, legislators are likely to continue chasing sports franchises, even if all this does is transfer money from taxpayers to wealthy team owners. Not only could that result in wasted tax dollars but also in wasted potential from the more economically ‚ÅÝviable investments that ‚Äãaren‚Äôt being made.
(The views expressed here are those of Marty Fridson , the publisher of Income Securities Investor. He is a past governor of the ‚ÄãCFA Institute, consultant to the Federal Reserve Board of Governors, and Special Assistant to the Director for Deferred Compensation, Office of Management and the Budget, The City of New York.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab ‚Äãand X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Trading Day: Ceasefire sends stocks higher
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, April 9 (Reuters) - U.S. stocks rallied on Thursday, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq clocking their seventh daily gain, as investors shrugged off a rise in oil prices and pinned their hopes on the fragile U.S.-Iran ceasefire extending to Israel and Lebanon too.
In my column today I look beyond the market euphoria uncorked ‚Äãby Tuesday's announcement of the ceasefire, and outline why the economic, policy and geopolitical backdrop is still pretty sobering for investors.
If you ‚Äåhave more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Stagflation nation
Figures on Friday are expected to show annual headline U.S. CPI inflation last month was 3.3%, up sharply from 2.4% in February. That would be the highest in nearly two years. Core goods prices are running hot, and with oil 65% more expensive than it was a year ago, ‚Äãinflation hitting 4% is more likely than the Fed's 2% target.
Some of the early March survey data suggest the economy remains pretty resilient. But incoming hard ‚Äãdata isn't that robust, and the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow Q1 GDP estimate is now down to 1.3%. Plus, Q4 GDP was revised down on Thursday to 0.5%. Not great.
* AI disruption ‚Äåfears
U.S. software ‚ÅÝstocks tumbled on Thursday after Anthropic held back the wide release of a powerful AI model over concerns it could expose hidden cybersecurity vulnerabilities. Software stocks are down 25% this year, against a 4% decline for the wider tech sector.
Meanwhile, IMF Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva said Fund research shows AI could boost productivity by up to 0.8%, but also affect 60% of all jobs in developed economies. That is sobering, to put it mildly.
* Income incoming
The Q1 U.S. reporting season kicks into gear ‚Äãnext week, and the outlook is pretty ‚Äãrosy. The LSEG I/B/E/S consensus is ‚ÅÝfor a 14.4% rise in earnings, again led by tech - income is expected to rise 46%, and if consensus forecasts are met, that would mean tech accounting for 75% of the overall rise in dollar income.
A lot of optimism is ‚Äãbuilt into these forecasts. The Nasdaq is back above its pre-war level, and on Thursday clocked its seventh straight ‚Äãrise, a run not ‚ÅÝseen since August 2024. But remember, tech's valuation premium over the broader market has collapsed to a 7-year low. So maybe the bullishness is justified?
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want ‚Äãto receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , ‚Äãis committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Why investors can still believe in mean reversion
item.description:
LONDON, April 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Over the past century, U.S. small-cap stocks have outperformed the market by more than 2 percentage points a year. Companies that are cheap relative to book value have done slightly better since 1926. The latest Global Investment Returns Yearbook , opens new tab from UBS remains equivocal as to why these so-called investment factors have historically outperformed. Perhaps, suggest authors Elroy Dimson, Paul Marsh and Mike Staunton, it was due to ‚Äútransient anomalies,‚Äù that ‚Äãcease to work once they‚Äôve been spotted. These are not reassuring grounds for investing one‚Äôs wealth.
Veteran investor Jeremy Grantham, the co-founder of Boston asset manager GMO, has a more intellectually satisfying explanation for the value and ‚Äåsmall-cap ‚Äúanomalies‚Äù. In his recently published memoir, ‚Äú The Making of a Permabear: The Perils of Long-term Investing in a Short-term World , opens new tab ,‚Äù which I helped compile, Grantham explains how in the 1970s he was perhaps the first investor to construct a data series for small-cap stocks that went back to 1926. In the era before computers were widely used in the investment business, he built his index painfully by hand. It was a labour of love.
Grantham made some interesting discoveries. First, small caps had beaten the market since 1926 ‚Äì a finding that finance academics only later latched onto. Second, ‚Äútheir relative performance [against the market] moved in cycles, ‚Äãebbing and flowing.‚Äù After periods when small caps had badly lagged the market, they tended to catch up: ‚ÄúThe pendulum swung back and forth. Bursts of superior performance weren‚Äôt sustained indefinitely.‚Äù Third, small caps were riskier. Grantham noticed that they were ‚Äãalmost wiped out during the Great Depression, but over the following decades slowly regained their lost ground. Given that smaller companies are more vulnerable to economic dislocations, it made sense for their ‚ÅÝstocks to trade at a discount to the broader market which is dominated by larger more resilient firms.
Grantham also found that most of the extra returns from small caps came from reconstituting the index. After a firm did well and its market capitalisation ‚Äãgrew, it departed the series. And when a formerly large-cap firm fell on hard times, it joined the small-cap index. Rebalancing the portfolio worked thanks to mean reversion, he says. That‚Äôs to say, on balance, the losers‚Äô fortunes improved over time while the winners‚Äô advantage ‚Äãeroded.
‚ÄúIn a moderately mean-reverting world, any strategy of selling winners and buying losers should win,‚Äù Grantham writes. ‚ÄúThe fact that a company‚Äôs depressed profitability reverts to its average level seemed to me just the fortunes of war. Say a firm is operating in an industry that turns down. Its managers scramble around looking for ways to stop the rot‚Ä¶ they slash costs and eventually get something right‚Ä¶ and as profits recover the stock price does too. Conversely, when a firm is superprofitable its management becomes overconfident, it invests too much, costs rise faster than sales and margins decline.‚Äù
There was no special ‚Äúsmall-cap effect‚Äù, ‚Äãsays Grantham, just a process whereby the returns of winners and losers returned to the average, harvested through reshuffling the portfolio. Mean reversion also explains why value stocks have historically outperformed. Grantham realised that buying stocks that were cheap relative to their book value ‚Äãonly worked because on average corporate profitability reverts to the mean. ‚ÄúI looked at the data in the Value Line hard copy and saw with my naked eye that in most cases when things get very very profitable, they mean revert downwards. And after profits get wiped out, the ‚Äåreverse happens. The ‚ÅÝreason this happened is straightforward. Capital moves towards profits: excess returns attract competition and bad returns drive capital away.‚Äù
Later in his career, Grantham implemented his belief in mean reversion at the asset-class level. It turned out to be a fraught experience. In the late 1990s, a great stock market bubble formed in the United States: growth stocks were extremely overvalued, while value stocks had never looked cheaper. Grantham bet his firm that bubble would break. Many clients lost patience and left.
At the time, he was told he‚Äôd lost the plot. One client demanded he explain the firm‚Äôs relative underperformance, ‚Äúbut for God‚Äôs sake don‚Äôt give us any more of this nonsense about reversion to the mean.‚Äù Grantham held on and was vindicated. The bubble burst, growth companies collapsed and value stocks came roaring back. The experience imparted ‚Äãa painful lesson: ‚ÄúMean reversion is a useful and hitherto accurate ‚Äãrule but still a painful one‚Ä¶,‚Äù he wrote in one ‚ÅÝof his quarterly letters, ‚Äúyou will arrive back on trend eventually, but not necessarily with the same clients you left with.‚Äù
After the financial crisis of 2008, the value factor went into one of its periodic doldrums. Over the past decade, the valuation of the US stock market has entered bubble territory - and stayed there. Corporate profits ‚Äì formerly the most reliably mean-reverting data in finance, according to Grantham ‚Äì ‚Äãhave remained far above average. Mean reversion appears to have lost its gravitational powers.
Grantham frets that American corporations have become more less competitive. U.S. companies exert more political power, he says. ‚ÄãThey have used much of their profits ‚ÅÝto buy back shares rather than invest. A handful of large technology firms became super-profitable but did not attract competition because of their insuperable competitive strengths. ‚ÄúIf exceptional profit margins do not attract assets and increased competition, then the capitalistic system is broken,‚Äù writes Grantham.
Elsewhere, mean reversion is alive and kicking. Value stocks have outperformed in most markets since late 2020, according to the UBS Yearbook. Last year was a banner period for the value factor almost everywhere, except in the United States. How long can this American exceptionalism last? The current artificial intelligence frenzy ‚ÅÝcould signal a change. ‚ÄãFormerly asset-light giants like Alphabet (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and Meta Platforms (META.O) , opens new tab are now investing heavily in data centres. AI breakthroughs could lead to more direct competition between large technology ‚Äãfirms. It‚Äôs not clear that their competitive moats will remain intact.
‚ÄúHigher capex and more competition could drive mean reversion. We could be entering a new phase,‚Äù says Grantham. Even if that doesn‚Äôt happen, he believes that AI will accelerate the political pushback against large, powerful and obscenely profitable companies. One way or another, mean ‚Äãreversion is coming for the U.S. stock market. As ever, though, the timing remains uncertain!
Follow @Breakingviews , opens new tab on X.

item.title: AI struggles to model for angry Main Street mobs
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Artificial intelligence is getting stumped by angry and anxious humans. Facing the fastest-rising electricity bills in ‚Äãthe country, Maine‚Äôs House of Representatives advanced , opens new tab a proposal to ban new data centers larger than ‚Äå20 megawatts until late 2027. Local governments and utility commissions have mustered even stronger resistance. The opposition leaves Silicon Valley and Wall Street to rethink their ambitious plans.
The backlash is broadening and accelerating. States introduced more than 300 bills spanning , opens new tab ‚Äãmoratoriums, tax-incentive rollbacks and ratepayer protections in the first six weeks of 2026, according to lobbying ‚Äãfirm MultiState. Community-based pushback blocked or stalled some $160 billion of projects last year, ‚ÅÝThe New York Times reported , opens new tab last month, citing Data Center Watch, a research outfit backed by AI safety ‚Äãstartup 10a Labs.
When regulatory pressure squeezes developers, construction shifts to more receptive places like Texas, Indiana and Ohio. ‚ÄãThe flow creates new bottlenecks. The Lone Star State‚Äôs grid operator received 233 gigawatts of power requests , opens new tab as of December, nearly quadruple the previous year's amount.
The risk to ratepayers is quietly metastasizing. Utilities are booking guaranteed regulated returns on buildouts that ‚Äãmay prove speculative. Georgia Power‚Äôs plan , opens new tab to add 9.9 GW carries an estimated $50 billion to $60 billion cost to ‚Äãcustomers over decades. If demand disappoints , opens new tab , households face rate hikes well into the next decade.
Responses so far have failed to ‚Äåneutralize concerns. ‚ÅÝAmazon.com (AMZN.O) , opens new tab , Google, Meta Platforms (META.O) , opens new tab and Microsoft (MSFT.O) , opens new tab signed the White House's ratepayer protection pledge , opens new tab in March, but the commitments are voluntary and carry no penalties. Utility AEP Ohio forced data centers to cover 85% of claimed capacity, weeding out speculative projects and shrinking , opens new tab its waitlist by 57% to 13 GW. Expanding this rule nationwide risks ‚Äãconstraining investment and driving up ‚Äãpower prices.
Individual policy spats ‚ÅÝmask the bigger issue: AI resistance is entrenching itself across every level of government. Only one in four Americans say they think data centers deliver meaningful , opens new tab local ‚Äãjobs, while half are more concerned than excited , opens new tab about AI's increased use in ‚Äãdaily life, according ‚ÅÝto Pew Research Center. Lawmakers at every level now have a convenient boogeyman. AI is meant to help solve diffuse and multi-faceted problems, but the technology isn't equipped to handle this emotionally charged one.
Follow Sebastian Pellejero ‚ÅÝon LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: 7-Eleven IPO delay plays into Couche-Tard‚Äôs hands
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Seven & i's (3382.T) , opens new tab decision to push back the listing of 7-Eleven was almost certainly the right call. But time is running out for the ‚ÄãJapanese corner store empire to improve operations at its North American gas-and-goods subsidiary‚Äîa key plank in CEO Stephen ‚ÄåDacus' bid to revive growth and improve shareholder returns. The case is building for spurned Canadian suitor Alimentation Couche-Tard (ATD.TO) , opens new tab to take another run.
The planned spin-off of the $34 billion group's largest foreign business, spanning 13,000 shops across the U.S. and Canada, was whipped up last year to help fund 2 trillion yen ($12.6 ‚Äãbillion) in share buybacks through 2030. That was part of a broader drive led by Dacus to reassure shareholders ‚Äãdeprived of a juicy payout following the collapse of Couche-Tard's $46 billion buyout offer. After announcing the IPO ‚ÅÝdelay on Thursday, Seven & i insisted this programme will continue apace. That, however, didn't stop investors from dumping the stock, which ‚Äãhas now fallen 6% since the Nikkei published a report on the delay just before Thursday's market close.
