This reflects an uncomfortable reality ‚Äãfor the Seven & i boss who, per the Financial Times , opens new tab , said that any listing would be ‚Äúdriven strictly by value‚Äù, adding the delay was necessitated by slow progress in turning the American business and market conditions. Dacus took the helm last year with a mandate to convince investors that their best bet ‚Äãto secure sustained returns lay with management in Tokyo.
Progress has been dismal so far. 7-Eleven's same-store merchandise sales fell in three out ‚Äãof the last four quarters, with those in the December quarter down 0.6% year-on-year. To further complicate matters, the American chain also relies on ‚Äåpetrol ‚ÅÝsales, leaving it exposed to oil price shocks like those now roiling global markets. Since Couche-Tard walked away in July, Seven & i has delivered measly shareholder returns of 4.5%, versus 34.6% for Japan's benchmark Topix (.TOPX) , opens new tab index.
Such struggles are music to the ears of Couche-Tard chair Alain Bouchard, who dropped his 2,600 yen per share takeover bid due to what the Circle K owner described as a ‚Äãlack of engagement from Seven & i. ‚ÄãShares in the latter ‚ÅÝnow trade at a 20% discount to that offer price, leaving a wide gap for the Canadian rival to step through if it wants another crack at Japan‚Äôs largest-ever foreign acquisition.
Follow Hudson Lockett ‚Äãon Bluesky , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: Discount Tesla could be a painful reality check
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - It‚Äôs the irony of Elon Musk‚Äôs life that his ambitions make battery-powered cars seem pedestrian. Whether it‚Äôs rockets, robots, chips or chatbots, ‚Äãthe Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab tycoon has had his hands full of late. The distraction is evident in the $1.3 trillion ‚Äåelectric-vehicle pioneer's results. Both sales and analysts‚Äô estimates of future earnings are sliding alarmingly. That wasn‚Äôt supposed to matter, as Musk‚Äôs driverless revolution would render anything with a steering wheel obsolete. A new effort to build a cheap sports-utility vehicle, reported by Reuters on Thursday, sits awkwardly with that narrative. ‚ÄãThe discount vehicle could be an expensive reality check for Tesla investors.
Musk has promised to build a super-cheap ‚Äãcar in the past. After much talk of a brand-new manufacturing process that would slash costs in ‚ÅÝhalf, though, the plan seemed to disappear, replaced in part by cut-down versions of the best-selling Model 3 and Model ‚ÄãY. Instead, Tesla trumpeted the bizarre, no-steering-wheel Cybercab , with Musk decreeing that a ‚Äúregular $25,000 model‚Äù would be not only ‚Äúpointless‚Äù but ‚Äúcompletely at odds with ‚Äãwhat we believe.‚Äù The company's future, he implied, lay in building vehicles tuned to specifically serve as robotic chauffeurs.
This is a crucial thesis for Tesla‚Äôs extraordinary valuation. Even as analysts' estimates of future profit have declined precipitously, according to Visible Alpha, the company's shares have traded at ‚Äãan ever-higher multiple of that shrinking figure. First-quarter car sales of under 337,000 fell short of Wall Street expectations, and led ‚Äãto the widest gap between vehicles manufactured and delivered since the pandemic-ravaged first quarter of 2020. It‚Äôs also a far cry from the ‚Äånear-500,000 vehicles ‚ÅÝthat Tesla sold in the final three months of 2024.
Producing a cheaper conventional ride may shore up Tesla‚Äôs slipping market share as battery-powered rivals from China and Europe gain ground. The company has held expenses admirably steady even as it ramped up production of the dud Cybertruck: the average car cost it about $34,000 after adjusting for leases last quarter. If old efficiency ‚Äãtargets are still realistic, then ‚Äãa cheap vehicle is within ‚ÅÝreach. Pricey gasoline due to conflict in the Middle East gives drivers an extra incentive to make the switch . The new car will surely incorporate existing self-driving software, allowing Tesla to cling ‚Äãto the promise of future autonomy.
The problem is that the Middle East conflict is also ‚Äãscrambling supply chains, ‚ÅÝjust as the pandemic aftermath pushed Tesla's costs above $41,000 per vehicle in 2022. Even worse, though, is the tacit admission that Musk cannot single-handedly redefine what a car is. Regulations and customer acceptance take time. The expansion of robotaxis like Waymo is halting. ‚ÅÝThe driverless ‚Äãrevolution might be delayed. Tesla‚Äôs valuation isn‚Äôt yet pricing that in.
Follow Jonathan ‚ÄãGuilford on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Orban ousted after 16 years as Hungarians flock to pro-EU rival
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - Prime Minister Viktor Orban, a key opponent of European Union efforts to help Ukraine fend off Russia's invasion, lost power after 16 years on Sunday as Hungarians voted in record numbers for ‚Äãa pro-EU course spearheaded by centre-right rival Peter Magyar.
Orban, 62, had won endorsements from U.S. President Donald Trump and some top European conservatives, but early results showed ‚Äåhis nationalist Fidesz party losing to Peter Magyar 's pro-EU Tisza party due to Hungary's economic stagnation .
A fiery anti-Communist youth leader during the Cold War, Orban, the European Union's longest-serving leader, is a patriotic hero to supporters, but critics at home and abroad have accused him of taking Hungary on an authoritarian path.
Born in 1963 in a village west of Budapest, Orban trained as a lawyer, briefly studied political philosophy at Oxford, and even played semi-professional soccer before becoming ‚Äãprime minister for the first time in 1998 aged just 35.
Hungary joined NATO on Orban's watch but he lost power in 2002. After eight years in opposition, he won ‚Äãa landslide victory in 2010, enabling him to rewrite Hungary's constitution and pass major laws aimed at creating an "illiberal democracy".
His consolidation of executive power, new ‚ÅÝcurbs on NGO activities and media freedoms, and a weakening of judicial independence led to clashes with the European Union over democratic standards, culminating in a decision to suspend billions of euros in ‚Äãfunding for Hungary.
But all that came crashing down for Orban on Sunday night as preliminary results put Magyar on course for a parliamentary supermajority, enabling his centre-right party to unwind all of Orban's ‚Äãcontroversial reforms.
"What tonight's election result means for the fate of our country and nation and what the deeper or higher meaning of all this is, remains unclear. We do not know it yet. Time will tell," Orban told supporters, conceding defeat.
"But however it has turned out, we will keep serving our country and the Hungarian nation from opposition."
During Europe's 2015 migrant crisis, Orban cast himself as the guardian of Hungary's national identity and ‚ÄãChristian heritage, refusing to accept EU quotas for taking in asylum seekers, mostly Muslims from the Middle East and beyond. His government has gradually taken steps to erode LGBTQ+ rights .
His hard line on ‚Äãimmigration and his efforts to revive Hungary's declining birth rate earned him praise from other conservative leaders, including Trump.
Orban - who also racked up sweeping election wins in 2014, 2018 and 2022 - had secured endorsements this time ‚Äåfrom Italy's ‚ÅÝPrime Minister Giorgia Meloni, Marine Le Pen of France's National Rally, and Alice Weidel of the Alternative for Germany.
Trump has also endorsed Orban, saying U.S.-Hungary relations have reached "new heights" owing to their leadership after years of conflict under Democratic administrations in Washington.
Orban has maintained close ties with Russia, a key energy supplier , and China, whose firms are building major EV and battery plants in the landlocked central European country.
He has sought to frame the election as a choice between "war or peace" , suggesting Tisza wants to drag Hungary into the war raging in neighbouring Ukraine , which it strongly denies.
"For peace, Fidesz is the ‚Äãsafe choice," Orban said on the campaign trail in ‚ÄãFebruary. He has clashed frequently with Ukraine's ‚ÅÝPresident Volodymyr Zelenskiy and infuriated Hungary's EU partners by blocking a 90 billion euro aid package for Kyiv.
But surveys showed Hungarian voters were more concerned with domestic issues such as healthcare and the economy, which has stagnated for the past three years.
Hungary experienced the EU's worst inflationary surge following Russia's ‚ÄãFebruary 2022 invasion of Ukraine, which lifted food prices close to EU average levels, while Hungarian wages are still third-lowest in the 27-member ‚Äãbloc.
Despite generous pro-family policies, including ‚ÅÝcheap loans and tax benefits, Orban appears to have lost the support of younger voters as he has lurched to the right.
Polls before the election showed young voters particularly eager for change, with Orban shifting back and forth between trying to woo the key demographic and deriding their opposition to his leadership as a 'phony rebellion'.
"I know young people like to turn against their parents and this can cause political problems," ‚ÅÝsaid Orban, a ‚Äãfather of five and a grandfather.
Despite attending countless election rallies and keeping up a barrage of interviews and social ‚Äãmedia posts, he gave a rare glimpse late last year into the toll that the campaign may have taken after so many years at the helm.
"When I was a soldier (doing military service), they told us a soldier cannot be cold, he can only ‚Äãperceive the cold," he said. "I am the same. I am not tired. It is just that my strength is running out."

item.title: Once inspired by Orban, Hungary's Peter Magyar unseats him in landmark election
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - When Peter Magyar was a child, he taped a photo of Viktor Orban , then an anti-Communist firebrand, on his bedroom wall, thrilled by Hungary's first democratic elections in 1990.
Decades later, he ended Orban's 16-year rule as prime minister in an election that brought a record-high turnout and was expected to rattle Russia and send shockwaves through right-wing circles across the ‚ÄãWest, including U.S. President Donald Trump's White House.
Magyar's centre-right, pro-European Union Tisza party beat Orban's nationalist Fidesz party in Sunday's parliamentary election. Partial results showed Tisza ‚Äåwould win 137 seats, or a two-thirds majority, in the 199-seat parliament.
Only nine years old when communism collapsed, Magyar said he had decorated his walls with photos of leading political figures in his Budapest family home.
Orban, then a young lawyer, had become a hero of Hungary's pro-democracy movement when he publicly demanded in 1989 that Soviet troops leave the country.
"There was a surge of energy around the regime change that swept me up as ‚Äãa child," Magyar told the Fokuszcsoport podcast last year.
Magyar, whose family name literally means "Hungarian", burst into the limelight two years ago after his ex-wife, Orban's former justice minister ‚ÄãJudit Varga, resigned from all political roles after a sex-abuse case pardon that caused public uproar.
Magyar quickly distanced himself from the governing party and ‚ÅÝaccused it of corruption and spreading propaganda, saying he had become disillusioned with Fidesz.
Just four months after emerging from near-total obscurity with an interview at YouTube channel Partizan, Magyar‚Äôs new party ‚Äãwon 30% in the June 2024 European elections, finishing second to Fidesz and crushing the rest of the opposition.
Orban's defeat has significant implications not only for Hungary but for Europe and ‚Äãits populist far right.
Orban has sought to create what he calls an "illiberal democracy" since 2010, curbing media freedoms and NGO activities, and weakening the independence of the judiciary.
He has forged good relations with Russian President Vladimir Putin and also with U.S. President Donald Trump but has clashed repeatedly with the EU, which suspended billions of euros in funding due to concerns over Hungary's democratic standards.
By contrast, Magyar has pledged to rebuild Hungary's Western ‚Äãorientation and end its dependence on Russian energy by 2035 while striving for "pragmatic relations" with Moscow. He has also promised to unlock the frozen EU funds, which would help revive Hungary's ‚Äãstagnant economy.
"On the first day we need to pass anti-corruption measures and we need to submit our application to join the European Prosecutor's Office," Magyar said on Sunday morning after casting his vote.
But he has ‚Äåtrodden carefully ‚ÅÝduring the election campaign, keen not to scare away more conservative voters.
Unlike Orban, he does not reject in principle Ukraine's right to join the EU one day but Tisza's programme does not support fast-track entry for Kyiv. Like Fidesz, Tisza opposes EU quotas for taking in migrants and it would also keep in place a border fence built under Orban to keep out illegal migrants.
But analysts say tensions between Budapest and the EU - further aggravated by Orban's veto of a 90 billion euro aid package for Kyiv - could ease under Tisza.
"Orban has lost faith in the current form ‚Äãand direction of European integration, and is pursuing ‚Äãa policy of vetoes and obstruction," said ‚ÅÝBotond Feledy, a geopolitical analyst at Red Snow Consulting.
"Tisza has no objection in principle to integration and would pitch its battles at a practical level."
Magyar drew from Orban's playbook in this election, waging a grassroots campaign that took him into Fidesz's rural heartlands.
His rallies ‚Äãalways featured lots of national flags, in an Orban-style appeal to Hungarian voters' patriotism.
His consistent and clear messages, and skilful use of social ‚Äãmedia have all contributed ‚ÅÝto his rapid rise, said Gabor Toka, senior research fellow at the Vera and Donald Blinken Open Society Archives.
"Many people are also reassured by the story of someone who has irrevocably come into conflict with the system, and has no way back," he said, referring to Magyar's break with Orban.
Born in 1981 into a family of lawyers, Magyar also studied law. He married Varga in 2006 and ‚ÅÝwhen her career ‚Äãtook her to Brussels, Magyar joined Hungary's diplomatic corps and worked on EU legislation. After returning to Hungary, he ‚Äãjoined a state bank and then headed a student-loan agency.
Magyar and Varga, who divorced in 2023, have three sons.
Magyar describes himself as religious and says he enjoys cooking and playing soccer with his friends and sons.
Asked in December how ‚Äãhe had changed since going into politics, Magyar alluded to media reports that describe him as short-tempered, saying: "Now I count to 10."

item.title: Reactions to PM Orban's defeat in Hungary's election
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - Hungary's opposition centre-right Tisza party appeared on track to win Sunday's national election , partial results showed, ending 16 years of rule by Prime Minister Viktor Orban, a nationalist backed by U.S. President Donald Trump and Russia.
Here are some quotes ‚Äãfrom Hungarian politicians and foreign leaders on the election results.
"As ‚Äåwe promised, as we hoped, today, on April 12, 2026, Hungary and several million people made history again, exactly 23 years to the day after there was a referendum on joining the European Union in Hungary."
"The election results are not final yet but the ‚Äãsituation is understandable and clear. The election result is painful for us, but clear. The responsibility and possibility ‚Äãof governing was not given to us. I have congratulated the winner."
"Far-right authoritarian Viktor Orb√°n has lost the election. Trump ‚Äãsycophants and MAGA extremists in Congress are up next in November. Winter is coming."
"Hungary ‚Äãhas chosen Europe. Europe has always chosen Hungary. A country reclaims its European path. The Union grows stronger. Hungary has chosen Europe. Europe has always chosen Hungary. Together we are stronger. A country returns to its European path. The Union becomes stronger... Europe‚Äôs heart is beating stronger ‚Äãin Hungary tonight."
"I‚Äôve just spoken with Peter Magyar to congratulate him on his victory in Hungary! France welcomes ‚Äãwhat has been a victory in terms of people taking part in the democratic process, and a victory which shows the attachment of ‚Äåthe Hungarian ‚ÅÝpeople to the values of the European Union and for Hungary's role in Europe."
"The Hungarian people have decided. My heartfelt congratulations on your electoral success, dear @magyarpeterMP
"I am looking forward to working with you. Let‚Äôs join forces for a strong, secure and, above all, united Europe. Gratul√°lok, kedves Magyar P√©ter!"
"I congratulate Peter Magyar and Tisza on ‚Äãtheir victory in the Hungarian ‚Äãparliamentary elections - a result ‚ÅÝwith great importance to all of Europe. I look forward to close and constructive cooperation in pursuing peace and stability, democracy and the rule of law on our continent."
"In democratic elections, the Hungarian people have demonstrated their strong will to be an active ‚Äãmember of European ‚ÅÝUnion and NATO. The election result gives Hungary the opportunity to return to our community of values and security as a constructive actor."
"Hungarians have made a historic choice for a free and strong Hungary in a united Europe, rejecting forces that ‚ÅÝignore ‚Äãtheir interests. Congratulations to Peter Magyar on winning the election! I look forward ‚Äãto working together on what unites Estonia, Hungary and Europe."
"Big win for Hungary! Big win for Europe! Congratulations to @magyarpeterMP. There are a lot ‚Äãof things we could and should do for the sake of democracy, justice and peace."

item.title: US military says it will start blockade of all ships going to and from Iran on Monday
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. Central Command said it will begin implementing ‚Äãa blockade of all maritime ‚Äåtraffic entering and exiting Iranian ports on April 13 at 10 ‚Äãa.m. ET (1400 GMT), after ‚ÄãPresident Donald Trump said the U.S. ‚ÅÝNavy would start blockading ‚Äãthe Strait of Hormuz.
"The blockade ‚Äãwill be enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ‚Äãports and coastal areas, ‚Äãincluding all Iranian ports on the Arabian ‚ÄåGulf ‚ÅÝand Gulf of Oman. CENTCOM forces will not impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting ‚Äãthe Strait ‚Äãof ‚ÅÝHormuz to and from non-Iranian ports," CENTCOM wrote ‚Äãin a statement on ‚Äãsocial ‚ÅÝmedia.
Additional information will be provided to commercial mariners through a ‚ÅÝformal ‚Äãnotice before the ‚Äãblockade starts, CENTCOM added.

item.title: Oil bounces back above $100 after US, Iran talks end in stalemate
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 13 (Reuters) - Oil prices jumped above $100 ‚Äãa barrel on ‚ÄåMonday as the U.S. Navy prepared ‚Äãto blockade the ‚ÄãStrait of Hormuz after ‚ÅÝtalks between ‚Äãthe U.S. and Iran ‚Äãfailed to reach a deal to end ‚Äãthe war.
Brent crude ‚Äãfutures rose $7.11, or 7.47%, ‚Äåto $102.31 ‚ÅÝa barrel by 2204 GMT after settling 0.75% lower ‚Äãon ‚ÄãFriday.
U.S. ‚ÅÝWest Texas Intermediate was at $104.43 ‚Äãa barrel, ‚Äãup $7.86, ‚ÅÝor 8.14%, following a 1.33% loss ‚ÅÝin ‚Äãthe previous ‚Äãsession.

item.title: Spanish premier Sanchez heads for fourth China visit, risks annoying Trump
item.description:
MADRID, April 13 (Reuters) - Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez begins his fourth visit to China in as many years on Monday, underlining his ‚Äãdetermination to steer an independent course that has riled U.S. President Donald Trump .
Spain has been one of Europe's loudest proponents of ‚Äåexpanding trade and treating China as a strategic ally rather than the economic and geopolitical rival that Trump sees it as.
The repeat visit highlights Sanchez's determination not to align himself with Trump ‚Äî who has heavily criticised Spain for denying landing rights to U.S. forces in the war with Iran and for its relatively low defence spending - ‚Äãand threatened to punish uncooperative NATO military allies.
Sanchez's approach is popular with voters, but has unnerved firms and opposition politicians who fear that ‚Äãfriction with the U.S. administration could be costly for Spain.
A government source ‚ÅÝwho was not authorised to speak to the media said Tuesday's meeting with President Xi Jinping would focus on geopolitics, and that Spain ‚Äãsees China as a stabilising force.
Yet Ramon Gascon Alonso, Asia-Pacific coordinator of Spain's Exporters' and Investors' Club, said this focus could further harm trade burdened ‚Äãby Trump's tariffs on imports from the European Union, and "make our already precarious situation much worse".
"The United States is the leading foreign investor in Spain," he said. "We have a significant volume of trade with the United States in sectors that are central to our economy ‚Äì absolutely vital."
Sanchez stands out among European allies as an outspoken ‚Äãcritic of the U.S. and Israeli military campaigns.
A senior opposition source advised against any further criticism while Sanchez is in China, accusing Sanchez of ‚Äãtangling with Trump for domestic reasons and putting at risk NATO unity and the U.S. military presence in Spain.
Chinese ambassador to Spain Yao Jing said warm ‚Äårelations had ‚ÅÝencouraged Chinese companies to invest.
"Spain is more reasonable in dealing with China," he said. "It has its own judgment, its own interests, it wants access to Chinese markets. So we do business."
Chinese firms invested 643 million euros in Spain in 2025, up from 149 million a year earlier, data from the Economy Ministry shows, bringing the total to 9.7 billion euros between 2010 and 2025, primarily in extractive industry and ‚Äãthe energy sector.
Xi will this time ‚Äãfete Sanchez and his wife Begona ‚ÅÝwith a formal banquet, while he will also meet with China's premier, Li Qiang, and top legislator Zhao Leji during the three-day visit.
On day one, Sanchez will visit the headquarters of tech giant Xiaomi and host ‚Äãa forum for Spanish and Chinese energy, infrastructure, bioscience and technology firms. He will also meet with ‚Äãinternational relations specialists ‚ÅÝand make a speech at Beijing's Tsinghua University.
Madrid hopes the visit will help narrow a trade deficit that has more than doubled in four years to nearly $50 billion in 2025, looking to boost agricultural and manufacturing exports to offset high volumes of Chinese imports.
Still, Sanchez is not taking a business delegation ‚ÅÝwith him ‚Äãthis time, and no major deals are planned.
Officials do, however, hope to sign a regionalisation ‚Äãagreement for Spanish poultry exports affected by bird flu outbreaks. A similar deal for the pork industry is credited with staving off a major blow to one of Spain's most ‚Äãlucrative exports during a recent African swine fever outbreak.

item.title: Keiko Fujimori leads Peru's presidential vote with 16.6%, shows early exit poll from Ipsos Peru
item.description:
LIMA, April 12 (Reuters) - Right-leaning candidate Keiko Fujimori ‚Äãis leading Peru's ‚Äåpresidential vote with 16.6%, an early ‚Äãexit poll by ‚ÄãIpsos Peru showed on ‚ÅÝSunday.
Leftist candidate ‚ÄãRoberto Sanchez trailed with ‚Äã12.1%, and a third candidate, center-leftist Ricardo ‚ÄãBelmont, had 11.8% ‚Äãof the vote.
If no ‚Äåcandidate ‚ÅÝclears the 50% threshold needed to win outright, the ‚Äãtwo ‚Äãmost-voted ‚ÅÝcandidates would advance to a ‚Äãsecond round, ‚Äãscheduled ‚ÅÝfor June 7.

item.title: Cyclone Vaianu leaves North Island cleanup underway in New Zealand
item.description:
WELLINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - New Zealanders who evacuated ahead of a cyclone that battered the North Island on Sunday ‚Äãwere gradually being allowed to return home as ‚Äåthe storm moved away from the country.
Local media reported cleanup crews were clearing roads, removing fallen trees and checking ‚Äãfor possible landslides. Power companies continued to report ‚Äãunplanned outages, while some roads remained closed.
Julie Jukes, ‚ÅÝacting mayor of Whakatane, one of the areas ‚Äãhit by the cyclone, told Radio New Zealand it ‚Äãhad brought the worst weather she had ever seen, but "the main thing is that everybody was safe and it's only damage ‚Äãto property and trees, power and things like ‚Äãthat."
Prime Minister Christopher Luxon told Radio New Zealand the emergency system ‚Äåhad ‚ÅÝworked well, people had prepared for the storm and the response had been much better than in previous emergencies.
New Zealand forecaster MetService said in a post ‚Äãon X ‚Äãlate on ‚ÅÝSunday that Cyclone Vaianu had moved offshore and was east of the North Island.
By ‚ÄãMonday morning, the only warnings still in ‚Äãplace ‚ÅÝwere for large ocean swells off the coast of lower North Island.
New Zealand has been hit by a ‚ÅÝseries ‚Äãof severe weather events this ‚Äãyear, including heavy rain in January that triggered a landslide and killed ‚Äãsix people.

item.title: US to blockade Iran after talks fail to yield a¬Ýdeal
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - The U.S. military said it will begin a blockade of all maritime ‚Äãtraffic entering and exiting Iranian ports and coastal areas on Monday after weekend talks failed to reach a deal to end the war with Iran , jeopardizing a ‚Äåfragile two-week ceasefire.
The talks in Islamabad, which ran from Saturday into early Sunday, were the first direct U.S.-Iranian meeting in more than a decade and the highest-level discussions since Iran's 1979 Islamic Revolution. The negotiations came days after a ceasefire began on Tuesday, aimed at ending six weeks of fighting that has killed thousands of people across the Gulf, throttled vital supplies of energy and sparked fears of a wider regional conflict.
The U.S. Central ‚ÄãCommand said on Sunday that the U.S. blockade, starting at 10 a.m. ET (1400 GMT), would be "enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal ‚Äãareas, including all Iranian ports on the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman."
Vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports will ‚ÅÝnot be impeded, the U.S. military said. Additional information would be provided to commercial mariners through a formal notice prior to the start of the blockade, it said.
President Donald Trump on ‚ÄãSunday also said U.S. forces would intercept every vessel in international waters that had paid a toll to Iran.
"No one who pays an illegal toll will have safe passage on the high ‚Äãseas," Trump wrote on social media, adding: "Any Iranian who fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be BLOWN TO HELL!"
He added that the U.S. Navy will begin destroying mines that the Iranians had dropped in the Strait of Hormuz , a choke point for about 20% of global energy supplies.
While shipping data showed three supertankers fully laden with oil passed through the Strait on Saturday, tankers were steering clear of the waterway on Monday, ahead ‚Äãof the U.S. blockade.
After Trump's initial remarks on Sunday, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guards warned that military vessels approaching the strait will be considered a ceasefire breach and dealt with ‚Äãharshly and decisively, underlining the risk of a dangerous escalation.
A U.S. official said Iran rejected Washington's call for an end to all uranium enrichment, the dismantling of all major enrichment facilities and the transfer of ‚Äåhighly enriched ‚ÅÝuranium.
Iran also refused U.S. demands that Iran cease funding for Hamas, Hezbollah and the Houthis, as well as fully open the Strait of Hormuz, the official added.
Iranian media said there was agreement on a number of issues, but the strait and Iran's nuclear program were the main sticking points.
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi said Iran had "encountered maximalism, shifting goalposts, and blockade" when just inches away from an "Islamabad MoU."
"Zero lessons learned," he added. "Good will begets good will. Enmity begets enmity."
Six weeks of fighting has killed thousands , roiled the global economy and sent oil prices soaring as Iran prevented traffic through ‚Äãthe Strait.
The U.S. dollar and oil rose in ‚Äãearly trading on Monday after the failure ‚ÅÝof the talks, while stocks in Asia eased.
Trump told Fox News' "Sunday Briefing" program that oil and gasoline prices may remain high through November's midterm elections , a rare acknowledgement of the potential political fallout from the war.
Iran's Qalibaf posted a map of Washington-area gasoline prices on social media with the ‚Äãcomment: "Enjoy the current pump figures. With the so-called 'blockade'. Soon you'll be nostalgic for $4‚Äì$5 gas."
Trump told Fox News on Sunday that he believed ‚ÄãIran would continue to negotiate ‚ÅÝand called the Islamabad discussions "very friendly."
"I do believe they're going to come to the table on this, because nobody can be so stupid as to say, ‚ÄòWe want nuclear weapons,‚Äô and they have no cards," he said.
But several hours later, the U.S. president said he did not care whether a "desperate" Iran returned to the negotiating table.
"If they don't come back, I'm fine," Trump told journalists on Sunday night after ‚ÅÝhe returned ‚Äãto the Washington area from an overnight stay in Florida.
Qalibaf blamed the U.S. for not winning Tehran's trust, despite ‚Äãhis team offering "forward-looking initiatives," Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian, who discussed the talks in a call with Russian President Vladimir Putin, said Tehran wanted "a balanced and fair agreement."
"If the United States returns to the framework of international law, reaching an ‚Äãagreement is not far off," he told Putin, Iranian state media reported.

item.title: Hungary opposition's landslide win heralds reforms, thaw in EU ties
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Peter Magyar's landslide victory in Sunday's Hungarian election has handed his centre-right Tisza party a sweeping mandate that will give it a free ‚Äãhand to enact reforms, bolster the rule of law and potentially unlock billions in European Union funding.
Economists and political analysts say the incoming government's expected two-thirds supermajority ‚Äåwas the most EU- and market-friendly scenario - and before Sunday, one of the most improbable - and would likely trigger a strong rally in Hungarian assets on Monday.
A number of uncertainties remain, and wary diplomats and analysts say the new government must deliver on its promises before reaping the full benefits, but the markets for now look willing to give Budapest's new masters the benefit of the doubt.
"The result is a game-changer and will allow Magyar ‚Äãto govern with a free hand," said Mujtaba Rahman, a managing director at Eurasia Group. "Most importantly, he will be able to unwind Orban's autocracy and deliver on all of ‚Äãthe reforms the EU is demanding.
"That means at least 6.4 billion euros ($7.46 billion) from the resilience and recovery facility should flow quickly, shoring up ‚ÅÝthe real economy and further consolidating Tisza's win."
The election had long been anticipated as the most market-sensitive in Europe this year given the eurosceptic Orban's frequent clashes with ‚ÄãBrussels during his 16-year rule, over issues ranging from immigration to his closeness to Russia.
Orban had expressed confidence throughout the election campaign despite lagging in opinion polls, saying his goal was to protect ‚ÄãHungary's national identity and traditional Christian values within the EU, while denying any wrongdoing.
But the markets had been signalling for weeks that investors expected change. The share prices of companies linked to Orban fell sharply, while market volatility gauges indicated that big currency moves were likely after the election.
Magyar, addressing jubilant supporters chanting "Europe, Europe" after Orban conceded defeat, pledged to make Hungary a strong EU and NATO ally and rebuild ties marred by years ‚Äãof conflict.
"With the two-thirds majority allowing us to amend the constitution, we will restore the system of checks and balances," Magyar said.
"We will join the European Public Prosecutor's Office and guarantee the ‚Äãdemocratic functioning of our country. We will never again allow anyone to hold free Hungary captive or to abandon it."
One fundamental plank of Magyar's plan to kick-start Hungary's economy, which has been mired in near-stagnation ‚Äåfor the past ‚ÅÝthree years, was to unlock EU funds frozen as democratic standards eroded under Orban.
"A constitutional majority is a different story entirely," said Ian Bremmer at GZERO Media.
"That would give Magyar the power to rewrite the constitution, clear out Fidesz loyalists from captured institutions, fully access EU funding, and even adopt the euro ‚Äì a core campaign pledge."
On Sunday, Magyar called on Hungary's chief prosecutor, the head of the top court, the head of media authority and other officials to resign, saying the country's public institutions had been captured by Orban loyalists over the past 16 years.
Magyar has pledged a sweeping anti-corruption ‚Äãdrive as his party seeks to meet ‚ÅÝEU conditions, including stronger judicial independence and public procurement, to unlock the funds.
However, credit rating agencies such as S&P Global and Fitch Ratings, as well as some EU diplomats, are sceptical about whether any money still available under Hungary's pandemic recovery funding would be released.
Diplomats and analysts say comparisons ‚Äãwith Poland's 2023 election, when Prime Minister Donald Tusk's pro-EU cabinet secured a quick release of EU funding on promises to roll back ‚Äãhis nationalist predecessor's policies, ‚ÅÝmay be misguided.
"There is no willingness to give out the money only on a promise like the EU did to Tusk in Poland, who was not able to deliver on most promises," said an EU diplomat.
"Tisza would need to demonstrate that it can deliver. But if something is legally impossible, and that can be demonstrated, then the EU could figure out a way."
Analysts at Capital Economics say the release ‚ÅÝof EU funding ‚Äãcould help cut Hungary's budget deficit towards 3.5% to 4% of national output by the end of the decade ‚Äãand stabilise public debt - the EU's highest outside the euro zone.
"Overall, the election result marks a major turning point for Hungary's economy," Liam Peach said in a note.
"The durability of any positive market reaction will now depend on how quickly ‚ÄãTisza moves to rebuild relations with the EU, secure EU fund disbursements and signal a credible medium-term fiscal anchor."
($1 = 0.8573 euros)

item.title: US, Australia, Philippines hold second joint drills in South China Sea this year
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - The U.S. and Australia joined the Philippines for their second joint maritime exercises in ‚Äãthe South China Sea this year, amid tensions ‚Äåwith China in the disputed waterway.
The four-day drills from April 9 to 12 brought together warships, fighter jets ‚Äãand surveillance aircraft in a series of coordinated ‚Äãoperations to strengthen maritime defence capabilities, the ‚ÅÝPhilippine military said on Monday.
The exercises underscored the "deepening ‚Äãdefence cooperation among the three nations and their shared ‚Äãcommitment to regional security", it said in a statement.
The Philippines deployed its FA‚Äë50 fighter jets, while Australia contributed P‚Äë8A ‚ÄãPoseidon aircraft and the United States the USS ‚ÄãAshland, a dock landing ship.
The drills came ahead of the April ‚Äå20 ‚ÅÝopening of the annual large-scale war games called Balikatan - or "shoulder-to-shoulder" - between Manila and Washington, which for the first time will include Japan as a full ‚Äãparticipant rather ‚Äãthan an ‚ÅÝobserver, alongside Australia.
The Philippines and China have traded accusations over a series of ‚Äãincidents in the South China Sea, ‚Äãincluding a ‚ÅÝrecent encounter in which Beijing fired flares at a Philippine Coast Guard aircraft.
Beijing has routinely opposed Manila's joint ‚ÅÝmilitary ‚Äãactivities with its allied partners ‚Äãin the South China Sea, saying such drills heighten tensions in ‚Äãthe region.

item.title: Russia, Ukraine trade accusations of Orthodox Easter ceasefire violations
item.description:
KYIV/MOSCOW, April 12 (Reuters) - Russia and Ukraine accused each other on Sunday of breaching the 32-hour ceasefire in their four-year war, reporting ‚Äåmore than a thousand drone and shelling attacks just hours after the truce began on Saturday to mark Orthodox Easter.
The Russian defence ministry said it recorded 1,971 ceasefire violations overnight into Sunday.
The General Staff of Ukraine's military, in a late evening report issued as the end of the truce ‚Äãwas approaching, said it had compiled a total of 7,696 Russian violations over the course of the ‚Äãtruce.
According to the calendar of the Orthodox faith dominant in both countries, Easter is celebrated ‚ÅÝthis year on Sunday.
A soldier from Ukraine's 65th brigade fighting in Zaporizhzhia said Russian reconnaissance drones were still circling ‚Äãthe area despite the ceasefire, blocking efforts to recover the bodies of fallen troops.
"We wanted to evacuate our fallen comrades ‚Äãtoday, but (the Russians) are not letting us do so just yet," the soldier, known by the call sign Spider, said at a candle-lit military Easter service.
Both sides continued to report what they described as violations of the ceasefire throughout the day on Sunday. The ‚Äãtruce was due to end at midnight (2100 GMT).
The latest report by the Ukrainian General Staff said the 7,696 Russian ‚Äãviolations noted over the course of the truce included 1,355 artillery shelling incidents, 115 assault operations and 6,226 strikes by attack drones. ‚ÄåIt said ‚ÅÝno air strikes had been recorded.
Ukraine's State Emergencies Service said two civilians were wounded on Sunday by a Russian drone attack in northeastern Kharkiv region. Police in the region later said a Russian drone had struck a car, injuring one person.
Russia said a child was among civilians injured in a Ukrainian drone attack on the Kursk region. The ‚ÄãBelgorod region's governor, Vyacheslav Gladkov, ‚Äãreported two people killed by ‚ÅÝUkrainian shelling and several injured in other strikes through the border region.
The Russia-appointed head of the parts of southern Kherson region held by Russian forces, Vladimir Saldo, said Ukrainian troops ‚Äãhad shelled an administrative building and a private home in two villages, without causing ‚Äãinjuries.
Reuters could not ‚ÅÝindependently verify reports of military activity.
Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said on Sunday the truce was a "humanitarian gesture" from Russian President Vladimir Putin, but that Russian troops would resume fighting as soon as it ends.
"Putin has instructed the Russian military to ‚ÅÝremain ‚Äãon high alert in case of provocations," Peskov said.
Putin announced the temporary ‚ÄãOrthodox Easter ceasefire on April 9. Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskiy, whose previous ceasefire proposals were turned down by Moscow, agreed to the truce.

item.title: Businessman Rafael Lopez Aliaga leads Peru's presidential vote with 23.4%, official count shows
item.description:
LIMA, April 12 (Reuters) - Right-leaning candidate Rafael Lopez ‚ÄãAliaga is leading Peru's ‚Äåpresidential race with 23.4% of the votes, ‚Äãearly results ‚Äãof the official vote count ‚ÅÝshowed Sunday.
Center-left ‚Äãcandidate Jorge Nieto trailed ‚Äãwith 16.4% of votes, with 5% of ballots ‚Äãcounted.
A third candidate, ‚Äãright-wing Keiko Fujimori, had 16.1% ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝthe votes.
If no candidate clears the 50% threshold needed ‚Äãto ‚Äãwin ‚ÅÝoutright, the two most-voted candidates ‚Äãwould advance to ‚Äãa ‚ÅÝsecond round, scheduled for June 7.

item.title: Trump attacks Pope Leo, calling him 'weak' on crime and 'terrible'
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald ‚ÄåTrump issued a scathing attack on Pope Leo late on Sunday, calling him "weak" on ‚Äãcrime and "terrible" for foreign policy after ‚Äãthe religious leader criticized Trump's foreign ‚ÅÝand immigration policies.
"Pope Leo is WEAK ‚Äãon Crime and terrible for Foreign Policy," ‚ÄãTrump wrote in a lengthy post on Truth Social.
Leo, who is known for choosing his ‚Äãwords carefully, has emerged as an ‚Äãoutspoken critic of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran that ‚Äåbegan ‚ÅÝon February 28.
The pope said this month's threat from Trump to destroy Iranian civilization was " unacceptable ." He has also called ‚Äãfor " deep reflection " ‚Äãabout the ‚ÅÝway migrants are being treated in the U.S. under ‚Äãthe Trump administration.
"Leo should get his ‚Äãact ‚ÅÝtogether as Pope," Trump wrote in his post, later telling reporters he was ‚ÅÝnot ‚Äãa "big fan" of the ‚Äãpontiff.

item.title: Budapest turns into party zone after historic election that ends Orban era
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Budapest turned into a party zone well into the early hours on Monday with people dancing outside pubs and cars honking as supporters of the opposition Tisza party celebrated ‚Äãtheir sweeping victory in a pivotal election on Sunday.
The election, which brought a record ‚Äåturnout of 80% based on preliminary data, ended the 16-year rule of right-wing Prime Minister Viktor Orban and for Hungarians toasting and waving flags in the capital city this meant voting for the country's European orientation and ‚Äãa restoration of democracy, eroded by Orban and his government since 2010.
Budapest's famous Chain ‚ÄãBridge was illuminated in Hungary‚Äòs national colors and along the embankment by the ‚ÅÝDanube river tens of thousands partied, waving the national flag and European Union flags, and some ‚Äãtoasting with champagne in paper cups.
"I feel amazing! Really amazing! Like, I never thought this would ‚Äãactually happen. I've been praying for this for 16 years so I'm super happy," said Szilvia, a Tisza supporter, as people watched the victory speech from opposition leader Peter Magyar.
Chanting and singing broke out on the city's metro, ‚Äãwith people chanting "it's over." Other Tisza supporters celebrated at a rave outside the country's iconic parliament ‚Äãbuilding.
"It's a big celebration. I'm quite happy. I have been waiting for this for some time. And I ‚Äåhope that ‚ÅÝthe new leadership, new prime minister will be really a prime minister for all Hungarians, as he said," said Tisza supporter Sandor Zoletnik.
"Amazing!" "Can't describe it!" "We are so happy that Orban is finally gone." "We did not believe that it would change," a group of young people hugged and jumped, shouting.
"So, ‚Äãfinally, after at least ‚Äã16 years, we feel ‚ÅÝlike there is a hope. We feel like there is a hope that Hungary can start walking into the right direction," one of them ‚Äãsaid. Orban's Fidesz party had the lowest support in the young generation .
Fidesz, originally ‚Äãlaunched as ‚ÅÝan opposition youth movement in the late 1980s, is backed by just 8% of voters aged between 18 and 29, based on a survey by pollster Median, or 22% in the wider 18-39 age group, ‚ÅÝaccording ‚Äãto Zavecz Research.
Traffic stopped along some of the main boulevards ‚Äãas crowds in front of pubs overflowed and people waved national flags, stopping cars. Hundreds strolled along the bridges spanning ‚Äãthe Danube well after midnight.

item.title: Pope Leo to begin 10-day Africa tour on mission to spotlight continent's needs
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, April 13 (Reuters) - Pope Leo begins an ‚Äãambitious 10-day tour of four countries in Africa on Monday urging global leaders to address needs on the ‚Äåcontinent where more than a fifth of the world's Catholics live.
The first U.S. pope heads to Algeria for two days before continuing to Cameroon, Angola and Equatorial Guinea in a whirlwind tour to 11 cities and towns, traversing nearly 18,000 km (11,185 miles) over 18 flights.
The ‚Äãpope is making the visit with a mission "to help turn the world's attention to Africa", Cardinal Michael ‚ÄãCzerny, a senior Vatican official and close adviser to Leo, told Reuters.
The pope, who has emerged as ‚ÅÝan outspoken critic of the Iran war , has made only one big overseas trip since being elected last May, ‚Äãvisiting Turkey and Lebanon in November and December. He visited Monaco in March.
Leo, aged 70, relatively young and in good health ‚Äãfor a pope, is undertaking one of the most complicated tours arranged for a pontiff in decades.
More than 20% of the world's Catholics live in Africa, according to Vatican statistics. The three sub-Saharan nations the pope is visiting have populations where more than half identify as ‚ÄãCatholic.
Algeria, though, is an overwhelmingly Muslim country with under 10,000 Catholics among its population of some 48 million people. ‚ÄãThis is the first time it will host a Catholic pope.
Leo's tour is the ‚Äå24th ‚ÅÝby a pope to Africa since the late 1960s .
He is expected to touch on many topics in 25 planned speeches over 10 days, Vatican spokesperson Matteo Bruni told journalists on Friday, given that the four nations face diverse issues.
Likely topics include exploitation of natural resources, Catholic-Muslim dialogue, and dangers of political corruption, said Bruni.
Cameroon and Equatorial Guinea have presidents who have ‚Äãbeen in power for decades ‚Äãand have been accused ‚ÅÝof human rights abuses, which they deny .
The biggest event of the itinerary will likely come in Cameroon on Friday, when the Vatican said some 600,000 are expected for a Mass in ‚Äãthe coastal city of Douala.
Comfortable in several languages, Leo is expected to speak Italian, ‚ÄãEnglish, French, Portuguese ‚ÅÝand Spanish during the trip.
Landing in Algeria in mid-morning on Monday, Leo will address the country's political leaders before visiting the Great Mosque of Algiers in only his second visit to a mosque as pope.
He will travel on Tuesday to Annaba ‚ÅÝon Algeria's ‚Äãnortheast coast, for a visit to the ruins of the ancient ‚Äãtown of Hippo.
The site has special meaning to Leo, who is a member of the Augustinian religious order. The order is inspired by the teachings ‚Äãof fourth-century St. Augustine of Hippo, a major figure in the early Church.

item.title: Russia, Ukraine accuse each other of violating Orthodox Easter ceasefire
item.description:
MOSCOW/KYIV, April 11 (Reuters) - Russia and Ukraine on Saturday accused each other of violating ‚Äãa brief ceasefire in their four-year-old war hours into the truce put in place to mark Orthodox Easter.
Governors of two Russian ‚Äåborder regions said Ukrainian drones had attacked targets in the Kursk and Belgorod regions, injuring five people.
The General Staff of Ukraine's military said Russian forces had violated the terms of the 32-hour truce 469 times, including assault actions, shelling and drone strikes.
The ceasefire, announced by Russian President Vladimir Putin on Thursday, went into effect at 4 ‚Äãp.m. Moscow time (1300 GMT). His Ukrainian counterpart, Volodymyr Zelenskiy, said he would abide by it.
Reuters could not independently verify reports of military ‚Äãactivity.
The ceasefire was put in place as U.S.-led negotiations to reach a settlement have been halted amid the ‚ÅÝwar in the Middle East. U.S. and Iranian negotiators were meeting in Pakistan on Saturday to try to end their six-week-old war.
According to ‚Äãthe calendar of the Orthodox faith, which is dominant in both Russia and Ukraine, Easter is celebrated this year on Sunday.
Russian news agencies said ‚ÄãPutin attended services after midnight at Moscow's vast Christ the Saviour Cathedral.
In his Easter greeting, quoted by agencies, Putin described Easter as the "triumph of love, good and justice". He also expressed thanks to Orthodox Patriarch Kirill for his support of those fighting in Russia's military campaign, described as a "special military operation".
"I would like especially ‚Äãto note the effective assistance you provide to our heroes - the participants and veterans of the special military operation," he wrote.
Alexander Khinshtein, governor of ‚ÄãRussia's Kursk border region, wrote on the state-backed messenger service MAX that a Ukrainian drone had struck a petrol station in the town of Lgov, injuring ‚Äåthree people, ‚ÅÝincluding a child.
Khinshtein said the attack took place after the start of the truce.
In the adjacent Belgorod region, Governor Vyacheslav Gladkov said two people were injured in Ukrainian drone attacks.
Gladkov, writing on Telegram, said a man and woman were injured in attacks in Shebekino and Grayvoron, two small towns just inside the border. He also said Ukrainian forces had shelled Shebekino, damaging homes and other buildings.
A local Russia-installed official in a ‚Äãpart of southern Kherson region held ‚Äãby Moscow also reported on social ‚ÅÝmedia that a Ukrainian drone attack had injured one person.
The Ukrainian General Staff, in a late evening report on front-line activity, said it had counted 469 ceasefire infringements since 1300 GMT.
"Specifically, 22 enemy assault ‚Äãactions, 153 shelling incidents, 19 strikes by attack drones ... and 275 strikes by (First-Person View) drones."
During a similar ‚Äã30-hour Easter ceasefire ‚ÅÝagreed last year, each side accused the other of infringements.
Zelenskiy, in his nightly video address, made no reference to alleged ceasefire violations. He repeated that Ukraine would stick to the truce and wished it could be in effect longer.
"It would be right for the ceasefire to continue beyond this," Zelenskiy ‚ÅÝsaid.
"We have ‚Äãmade this proposal to Russia and if Russia once again chooses war instead of peace, ‚Äãit will show the world, and particularly the United States, who truly wants what."
Zelenskiy has proposed a halt to fighting on a number of occasions, but has been turned ‚Äãdown by Moscow, which says it is seeking an overall settlement.

item.title: Oil tankers steer clear of Hormuz ahead of US blockade
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 13 (Reuters) - Oil tankers are steering clear of the ‚ÄãStrait of Hormuz ahead of a U.S. blockade later on Monday following failed peace talks ‚Äåbetween the U.S. and Iran over the weekend, shipping data showed.
President Donald Trump said on Sunday the U.S. Navy would start blockading the Strait of Hormuz, raising the stakes after marathon talks with Iran failed to reach a deal to end the war, ‚Äãjeopardising a fragile two-week ceasefire.
U.S. Central Command said U.S. forces would begin implementing the blockade of ‚Äãall maritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports at 10 a.m. ET (1400 GMT) on ‚ÅÝMonday.
It would be "enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal areas, ‚Äãincluding all Iranian ports on the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman," it said in a statement on X.
U.S. ‚Äãforces would not impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports, and additional information would be provided to commercial mariners through a formal notice prior to the start of the blockade, it said.
Iran's ‚ÄãRevolutionary Guards said on Sunday that any military vessels attempting to approach the Strait of Hormuz would ‚Äãbe considered a violation of the ceasefire and be dealt with harshly and decisively.
Pakistan-flagged tankers Shalamar and Khairpur entered the ‚ÄåGulf on ‚ÅÝSunday, data from LSEG and Kpler showed.
The Aframax tanker Shalamar is heading to the United Arab Emirates on Monday to load Das crude while the Panamax-sized Khairpur is heading to Kuwait to load refined products, the data showed.
Pakistan National Shipping, which manages Shalamar, did not immediately respond to a request for comment outside of ‚Äãoffice hours.
Liberia-flagged very large crude ‚Äãcarrier (VLCC) Mombasa B, which ‚ÅÝalso transited the strait earlier on Sunday, is ballasting in the Gulf, the data showed.
Malta-flagged VLCC Agios Fanourios I, which tried to pass through the strait on ‚ÄãSunday to enter the Gulf to load Iraqi Basra crude for Vietnam, has ‚Äãsince turned ‚ÅÝback and is now anchored near the Gulf of Oman, the data showed. The tanker plans to head to Iraq.
Eastern Mediterranean Maritime, which manages the Agios Fanourios I, and CMB.TECH NV, the manager for the Mombasa B, did ‚ÅÝnot respond ‚Äãto requests for comment outside office hours.
Despite the stalemate, three supertankers ‚Äãfully laden with oil passed through the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday, shipping data showed. They appeared to be the first vessels to ‚Äãexit the Gulf since the ceasefire deal was struck last week.

item.title: US blockade of Iran will be major military endeavor, experts say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - A U.S. naval blockade of Iran is a major, open-ended military endeavor that could trigger fresh retaliation from Tehran and put tremendous strain on an already ‚Äãfragile ceasefire, experts say.
President Donald Trump, in a social media post after no deal emerged from peace talks this weekend in Islamabad, said the U.S. Navy "will begin ‚Äåthe process of BLOCKADING any and all Ships trying to enter, or leave, the Strait of Hormuz."
The U.S. military's Central Command later said the blockade will only apply to ships going to or from Iran, including all Iranian ports on the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman. It will take effect on Monday at 10 a.m. in Washington (1400 GMT), CENTCOM said.
Trump also said U.S. forces would interdict vessels that have paid tolls to Iran, ‚Äãeven if those ships are now in international waters. "No one who pays an illegal toll will have safe passage on the high seas," Trump wrote on Truth Social.
The ‚Äãultimate goal, Trump said, would be to pressure Iran to end its effective closure of the strait, a choke point for about 20% ‚ÅÝof the world's oil, to all but the countries that secure safe passage from Tehran.
If Trump's strategy succeeds, he would eliminate Iran's greatest point of leverage in negotiations with the ‚ÄãUnited States and clear the strait again for global trade, potentially lowering oil prices. But a blockade, experts say, is an act of war that requires an open-ended commitment of a significant number of ‚Äãwarships.
"Trump wants a quick fix. The reality is, this mission is difficult to execute alone and likely unsustainable over the medium to long-term," said Dana Stroul, a former senior Pentagon official during the Biden administration now at The Washington Institute for Near East Policy.
The U.S. military has not offered basic details yet about the blockade, including how many U.S. warships will enforce it, whether warplanes will be used ‚Äãand whether any Gulf allies will assist in the effort. Central Command declined to respond to requests for comment.
With enough warships, the U.S. Navy could set up a blockade that intimidates ‚Äãmany commercial tankers from trying to power through with Iranian oil, experts say.
But would the United States be prepared to board and seize ‚Äî or even damage or sink ‚Äî ships that try to break the blockade? What ‚Äåif they carry ‚ÅÝoil for China, a major power, or U.S. partners such as India or South Korea?
And what would Iran do? Retired Admiral Gary Roughead, a former chief of U.S. naval operations, cautioned that Iran could fire on ships in the Gulf or attack infrastructure of the Gulf states that host U.S. forces.
"I honestly believe that if we begin to do it, that Iran will have some kind of a reaction," Roughead said.
Iran's threats to shipping have caused global oil prices to skyrocket about 50% since the U.S. and Israel launched the war on February 28.
Trump said on ‚ÄãSunday that the price of oil and gasoline may ‚Äãremain high in the United States ‚ÅÝthrough November's U.S. midterm elections, which could see Trump's Republicans lose control of the U.S. Congress if there is a public backlash. The war has already been unpopular.
Frustrated by Iran's refusal to end the war on his terms, Trump on Sunday also floated the possibility of a ‚Äãresumption of U.S. strikes inside Iran, citing missile factories as one possibility.
U.S. Senator Mark Warner of Virginia, the ranking Democrat on the ‚ÄãSenate Intelligence Committee, questioned ‚ÅÝthe strategy, noting Iran could send speedboats to mine the strait or put bombs against tankers.
"How is that going to ever bring down gas prices?" Warner asked on CBS's "Face the Nation."
Thousands of U.S. military strikes have severely weakened Iran's military. But analysts say Tehran has emerged from the conflict as a vexing problem for Washington, with a more hardline leadership and a buried stockpile of highly enriched ‚ÅÝuranium.
Trump threatened ‚Äãon Sunday that "any Iranian who fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be BLOWN TO HELL!"
Iran's Revolutionary ‚ÄãGuards responded with a statement warning that military vessels approaching the strait will be considered a ceasefire breach and dealt with harshly and decisively, underlining the risk of a dangerous escalation.
Stroul said the crisis will require a long-term, ‚Äãinternational effort to resolve.
"Over the long run, this will need to be resolved through diplomacy and international political will," she said.

item.title: Trump returns to weary and failing playbook with Hormuz blockade threat
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, April 13 (Reuters) - Escalate to de-escalate is a well-worn playbook employed by U.S. President Donald Trump, and no doubt global crude oil markets are hoping his promise to blockade the Strait of Hormuz is just the latest example of this ploy.
The announcement that the U.S. ‚ÄãNavy will block vessels from Iranian ports using the narrow waterway raises the stakes massively in the six-week-old conflict.
Trump's threat was delivered with his usual bombast ‚Äåin a social media post on Sunday, and it effectively ended the surge in optimism that a ceasefire between the two parties would result in a re-opening of the vital waterway.
Trump's action follows the failure of weekend talks between the two parties in Pakistan, which ended with both sides seemingly far apart on key issues.
The latest escalation saw global benchmark Brent crude futures jump about 8% in early Asian trade on ‚ÄãMonday, climbing to $102.80 a barrel from a close of $95.20 on April 10.
Since the conflict started on February 28, the flow of crude oil, refined products and liquefied ‚Äãnatural gas through the Strait of Hormuz has slowed to a trickle from pre-war levels of roughly 20% of global supply.
What little traffic ‚ÅÝthat has gone through the strait since the start of April has mainly been from Iran, with data compiled by commodity analysts Kpler showing 15 vessels that loaded at Iran's ‚ÄãKharg Island terminal leaving between April 1 and 12.
Over the same period 12 tankers from other countries have made it through, with four from the United Arab Emirates, six from Saudi ‚ÄãArabia and two from Iraq, according to Kpler.
There are several ways to look at the threat to blockade vessels from using the Strait of Hormuz.
The first is that it's surprising this hasn't happened sooner.
By halting Iran's crude shipments the United States would put serious pressure on Tehran's revenue and also likely force China, the major buyer of Iranian oil, to become more involved, with the hope that Beijing would encourage ‚Äãits ally to make concessions in order to ensure vessels moved freely.
However, there are major challenges to stopping tankers from Iran.
It's unlikely the U.S. military would fire missiles or other ‚Äãweapons at them, given the risk of an environmental disaster.
This leaves the most likely option as trying to force them to change course through threats, and if that doesn't work, launching armed boarding parties ‚Äåto take ‚ÅÝphysical control of the ships.
Another way to look at Trump's threat is that it marks a return to escalation in the conflict, which makes the Iranian reaction key.
So far the hard-line clerical leaders in Iran have shown little sign of bending to U.S. demands, which range from ending its nuclear programme to fully re-opening the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran has largely done the opposite, responding with escalation of its own, such as attacking energy and military installations in its Gulf neighbours that host U.S. bases.
If the U.S. military does successfully interdict vessels from ‚ÄãIranian ports, it would be logical to expect ‚ÄãTehran would launch more attacks on ‚ÅÝits neighbours, and the most effective targets would be export facilities that bypass the Strait of Hormuz.
Already Iran has damaged Saudi Arabia's east-to-west pipeline to Yanbu on the Red Sea, as well as hitting facilities at the United Arab Emirates' Fujairah terminal on the Gulf of ‚ÄãOman.
Further attacks on these export pathways would inflict maximum damage on the already shaky crude oil markets, and likely turn what is ‚Äãalready a crisis into ‚ÅÝa full-blown emergency.
The hope in the crude market is that Trump is once again escalating to de-escalate, but there are limits as to how many times this can happen before the damage to the world economy becomes severe and long-lasting.
It's a common refrain among U.S. right-wing media and commentators that Trump is playing some sort of four-dimensional chess and will eventually outwit Iran.
But the reality ‚ÅÝis that Trump ‚Äãappears to be playing noughts and crosses, known as tic-tac-toe to Americans, and he hasn't yet worked out ‚Äãthat you cannot win.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) , your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, data-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI can ‚Äãhelp you keep up. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.

item.title: War-fuelled debt spiral will shape global power
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters Breakingviews) - The United Kingdom cut debt after the Napoleonic wars so it had the fiscal firepower to fight subsequent battles. But today‚Äôs superpower struggle between the United States and China, and the conflicts in Iran and Ukraine, are occurring when government borrowing across the world is already high.
Governments are spending more on defence and cutting their reliance on rivals in response to global threats. That is pushing ‚Äãup debt relative to GDP, limiting their ability to project power. Borrowing costs have also shot up. The yield on 10-year U.S. government bonds has almost trebled to 4.3% over the past five years.
Few countries ‚Äåare immune to this spiral. The United States, China, France, the United Kingdom and Japan already have gross government debt equivalent to more than a year‚Äôs worth of economic output. Russia has run down its National Wealth Fund to finance its war in Ukraine, while foreign governments have frozen around $300 billion of Moscow‚Äôs sovereign assets. Gulf Arab countries will also now have to dig deeper to defend themselves against Iran.
There is an old joke that the IMF stands for ‚Äúit‚Äôs mostly fiscal‚Äù. When the International Monetary Fund and World Bank hold their annual meetings this week in Washington, the interaction between ‚Äãgovernment borrowing and geopolitical tension will be high on the agenda.
Part of the problem is rising defence costs. President Donald Trump wants to increase U.S. spending by $500 billion to $1.5 trillion next year. Meanwhile, European members of NATO last ‚Äãyear committed to increasing their outlay to 5% of output in 2035 from the previous goal of 2%.
Governments are also increasingly alive to the risk of blackmail. Iran‚Äôs control of the Strait ‚ÅÝof Hormuz, through which a fifth of the world‚Äôs daily oil output previously passed, is the latest example. China‚Äôs domination of rare earths and Europe‚Äôs reliance on Russian gas before its invasion of Ukraine are others. Meanwhile, many countries are heavily dependent on ‚ÄãU.S. defence, technology and finance ‚Äî which gives Washington the ability to bully them.
The sensible response is for countries to cut their exposure to potential enemies. But that is costly as it interferes with the free movement of goods, services and capital. Governments either ‚Äãhave to subsidise the process or put in controls that limit economic growth. Borrowing will be higher or output lower than it would have been ‚Äî boosting debt ratios.
All borrowing is not created equal. While debt-to-output ratios and annual borrowing levels are a good starting point to assess a state‚Äôs fiscal strength, other factors are also important.
How a government spends money is vital. For example, defence spending can boost productivity , opens new tab if it is used for research and development. The United States is a well-known example. Ukraine is now building a thriving military technology sector as a result of innovation forced ‚Äãby the need to defend itself from Russia. By contrast, subsidising energy consumption ‚Äî as some governments are doing to offset the higher oil prices that the Iran war has unleashed ‚Äî does nothing to spur long-run growth.
Off-balance sheet liabilities also matter. For example, pension ‚Äãcommitments will show up in future debt figures. The damage will be a function of how rapidly a population is ageing and the generosity of retirement payments. China and Japan are in a bad place in terms of ageing, while France is burdened by an expensive pension ‚Äåsystem it cannot ‚ÅÝreform. By contrast, the United States gives relatively little money to retired citizens and its population is ageing more slowly.
Private-sector bad loans can also flip back onto governments‚Äô debt ratios ‚Äî either because states have to bail out banks as they did during the 2008 global financial crisis, or because sweeping problems under the carpet depresses growth. Moscow may be at risk because it has encouraged , opens new tab lenders to support its war effort. So may Beijing from its past promotion of housing investments. Washington could eventually find that leverage in areas such as private credit causes broader trouble.
How governments fund themselves also matters. If they rely mainly on domestic investors, like China, Japan and the euro zone do, they are in a stronger position. In extremis, they can use financial repression ‚Äî policies that lower government ‚Äãfinancing costs ‚Äî to force their people to fund them.
The United States ‚Äãis not so lucky. After years of running current ‚ÅÝaccount deficits, the value of its investments in the rest of the world is $27 trillion , opens new tab less than the value of what other countries have invested in America. This means Washington relies on the ‚Äú kindness of strangers ‚Äù, as Mark Carney, now Canada‚Äôs prime minister, put it when he was governor of the Bank of England.
Of course, the dollar‚Äôs status as the world‚Äôs main reserve currency gives the ‚ÄãUnited States a funding advantage. But Trump‚Äôs wars and U.S. economic bullying are undermining that privilege, creating an opportunity for the euro, yuan and crypto currencies.
If an ‚Äãeconomy is vigorous, a government can ‚ÅÝgrow out of high debt. On this yardstick, all the main economic blocs have their problems.
While the United States has a thriving technology sector, Trump‚Äôs erratic policies and immigration curbs are undermining its vitality. Although China has grown spectacularly, its export model is unsustainable, and political repression may eventually dim creativity and animal spirits. Meanwhile, the European Union has not invested enough in technology or integrated the markets of its 27 member states adequately.
Yet another factor which determines a government‚Äôs ability to carry heavy fiscal burdens is whether it can impose sacrifices ‚ÅÝon its people. ‚ÄãWestern states have little capacity to cut public spending or push up taxes. By contrast, China and Russia have high pain thresholds, in part because ‚Äãtheir autocratic governments find it easier to push through unpopular policies.
But even they have limits. For example, Russia is seemingly so keen to avoid conscription that it has used troops from North Korea and elsewhere to help fight its war in Ukraine.
Debt dynamics will be one of the factors that determine the superpower struggle ‚Äãbetween Washington and Beijing. On this score, both have challenges ‚Äî but China seems to have the edge.
Follow @Hugodixon , opens new tab on X

item.title: The Week in Breakingviews: Too big to fail again
item.description:
LONDON, April 12 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Welcome back! A week that started with Donald Trump threatening to destroy a ‚Äú whole civilization ‚Äù ended with peace talks in Pakistan and Iran charging tolls in the Strait of Hormuz. I‚Äôm taking a break from the conflict to write about another looming fight: over shadow banks. Let me know , opens new tab what else we should be paying attention to. If this newsletter was forwarded to you, sign up here to get it in ‚Äãyour inbox every weekend.
‚ÄúSimply stating the facts is enough to make clear how absurd things have become at Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena: shareholders must pick a new CEO who has never run a ‚Äåmainstream bank before, or the lender‚Äôs fired ex-boss.‚Äù
Read more: Rebel CEO is a rational vote in Italy‚Äôs bank drama .
Here‚Äôs a potted history of financial regulation over the past 15 years: After 2008, watchdogs tightened capital and liquidity rules to prevent big banks from ever threatening the global economy again. To the surprise and consternation of some ‚Äãbank CEOs, this effort broadly succeeded. Entire chunks of financial activity, including proprietary trading in bonds and equities and lending to riskier borrowers, migrated from regulated institutions into shadow banks, or what ‚ÅÝpolicymakers now prefer to call non-bank financial intermediaries.
The shift was expected, and deliberate. Regulators reasoned that non-banks which got into trouble would pose less of a threat to the economy and therefore be less prone to seeking politically toxic bailouts. Over the last ‚Äãdecade and a half, however, several things have changed. The first is that non-banks have grown much bigger: they now account for more than half of the roughly $500 trillion of financial assets in existence around the globe, according to the Financial Stability Board. The second ‚Äãis that they have moved closer to the heart of the financial system. Companies like Citadel Securities, Apollo Global Management (APO.N) , opens new tab and Stripe are not regulated banks, but still play key roles in making markets, allocating capital, and processing transactions.
Up to this point, bankers and regulators agree. Where they diverge is over what to do next. Many bank executives argue that some of the regulations introduced since 2008 should be modified, enabling them to grab back lost business or take advantage of new opportunities. JPMorgan (JPM.N) , opens new tab boss Jamie Dimon this week singled out the ‚ÄúGSIB surcharge‚Äù, which imposes an extra capital requirement on ‚Äãthe world‚Äôs biggest banks. The proposed charge, he wrote in his annual letter , opens new tab to shareholders, seems to punish ‚Äúour success, our strength, our consistency and our balanced business model. Frankly it‚Äôs not right, and it‚Äôs un-American.‚Äù It‚Äôs a rare post-2008 example of a top banker publicly ‚Äãmaking the case that big is better .
Regulators take a different view. They point to the relative stability of big banks in the last six turbulent years as a sign of success. As Klaas Knot, the former head of the Dutch central bank, told me this week, 2008 showed ‚Äåthat bank exposures, ‚ÅÝwhich may seem diversified in calm times, tend to become highly correlated in a crisis.
Watchdogs would therefore prefer to tighten up oversight of non-banks, to avoid remaking the drama of 2008 with a different set of villains. A working group organised by the Group of Thirty former regulators and central bankers, chaired by Knot and Agustin Carstens, the former head of the Bank for International Settlements, this week warned of a brewing ‚Äú perfect storm , opens new tab ‚Äù in the non-bank sector. Their report , opens new tab cites two specific concerns: the central role played by hedge funds and others in government debt markets, and the rapid growth of private credit providers in lending to companies. Both groups also tend to have extensive links to regulated banks.
The coming battle in financial regulation is therefore whether to raise the playing field for non-banks, or ‚Äãto lower it for traditional lenders. The shape of the next ‚Äãmeltdown will depend on who gets the upper hand.
The sums earmarked to build data centres around the world are so vast they can be hard to put into context. Nvidia (NVDA.O) , opens new tab boss Jensen Huang reckons it costs $60 billion to $80 billion to build a 1 gigawatt facility. With more than 100 GW of projects already in the planning stages, the implied outlay is at least $6.6 trillion. Is it possible to finance construction on such a ‚Äãscale? Jeffrey Goldfarb added up the numbers and reckons there‚Äôs $7.5 trillion available. Spending it at high speed is a different challenge, though. The U.S. interstate highway system, which cost a mere $500 ‚Äãbillion in today‚Äôs money, took three decades ‚ÅÝto complete.
Ransomware has become a large and lucrative global industry: half of all businesses with $10 million or more of turnover have suffered an attack on their computer systems in the past year. On The Big View , opens new tab this week I talked to Anja Shortland, professor of political economy at King‚Äôs College London and author of ‚ÄúWe Know You Can Pay a Million‚Äù, about why the business has boomed, and the fresh threats posed by new artificial intelligence and geopolitical conflicts.
Over in the Viewsroom , opens new tab , Aimee Donnellan and Jonathan Guilford talked to Neil Unmack about the ‚ÅÝdramatic corporate moves by ‚Äãglobal food groups, exemplified by Unilever (ULVR.L) , opens new tab and U.S. rival McCormick (MKC.N) , opens new tab creating a $65 billion mayonnaise-to-mustard giant.
The upsurge in Japanese takeovers has been one of the most ‚Äãstriking recent developments in corporate finance. After decades in the doldrums, Japanese companies have started prioritising returns to shareholders, under pressure from the government, activist investors, and hostile suitors. Now a seemingly benign directive from Japan‚Äôs Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry threatens to derail the M&A bonanza. Hudson Lockett explains why an instruction ‚Äãfor companies to prioritise ‚Äúcorporate value‚Äù may be something of a poison pill.
Want to receive The Week in Breakingviews in your inbox every Saturday? Sign up for the newsletter here .
Follow Peter Thal Larsen on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: US Democrats cheer defeat of Trump ally Orban in Hungary
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. Democrats celebrated the defeat of ‚ÄãHungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban on Sunday, while President Donald Trump's allies and Republican peers ‚Äåoffered a more mixed response to the loss of the leader that Trump had endorsed.
Trump had backed Orban leading up to the vote, even speaking briefly last week at a campaign rally in Hungary, when U.S. Vice President JD Vance telephoned ‚Äãhis boss upon taking the stage.
But Orban lost power after 16 years as Hungarians voted ‚Äãin record numbers for a pro-EU course spearheaded by center-right rival Peter Magyar.
U.S. ‚ÅÝlawmakers from both major parties congratulated Magyar on his victory.
Some Democrats framed Orban's loss as a ‚Äãharbinger of things to come for the November midterm elections in the United States.
"Pay attention, Donald Trump. ‚ÄãWannabe dictators wear out their welcome," Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer said.
"Far-right authoritarian Viktor Orban has lost the election. Trump sycophants and MAGA extremists in Congress are up next in November," said U.S. House of Representatives Minority Leader Hakeem ‚ÄãJeffries.
Republicans such as U.S. Senator Roger Wicker viewed the Hungarian election result as a repudiation of Russian ‚ÄãPresident Vladimir Putin, with whom Orban had cultivated ties over the years.
Wicker, a Mississippi Republican who chairs the Senate ‚ÄåArmed ‚ÅÝServices Committee, said the results showed that Hungary's population rejected "the malign influence of Vladimir Putin" and "decided their own future."
Trump himself did not mention the Hungarian elections on Sunday, even as he opined on topics through social media posts, a television interview and a brief gathering with reporters.
But a few of his ‚Äãpro-Orban allies, such as technology ‚Äãtycoon Elon Musk, ‚ÅÝlamented the result.
"Soros Organization has taken over Hungary," Musk wrote on his X social media platform.
Billionaire financier and major Democratic donor George Soros, a Hungarian immigrant ‚Äãto the United States, has long been vilified by many conservatives.
Orban has been ‚Äãat loggerheads ‚ÅÝwith the European Union over a range of issues, including Russia's war in Ukraine .
His self-described "illiberal democracy" has echoed policies touted by Trump, including a hard line against immigration, hostility toward global institutions, and attacks on the media ‚ÅÝand ‚Äãuniversities.
Orban was the first European leader to endorse Trump during his ‚Äã2016 presidential bid.
Trump said last week his administration stood ready "to use the full economic might of the United States to strengthen ‚ÄãHungary's economy" if Orban won.

item.title: Spanish premier urges China to take bigger role in multipolar order
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 13 (Reuters) - Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez on Monday said China should take on a more substantial role with issues including climate change, security, defense ‚Äãand the fight against inequality, adding that Europe will also have to ‚Äåredouble its efforts as the U.S. withdraws from leadership roles on many fronts.
Sanchez, who is making his fourth visit to China in four years, spoke at Beijing's Tsinghua University.
His visit comes as ‚Äãmany Western governments seek to maintain engagement with Beijing despite lingering security ‚Äãand trade tensions. It follows visits to China earlier this year ‚ÅÝby the prime ministers of Britain, Ireland, Canada and Finland.
Spain has been one ‚Äãof Europe's loudest proponents of expanding trade and treating China as a strategic ally ‚Äãrather than an economic and geopolitical rival.
Sanchez said China could do more in terms of fighting climate change, promoting global health, controlling the development of responsible artificial intelligence as well as nuclear ‚Äãweapons.
"For example, by demanding as it is doing, that international law be respected ‚Äãand that the conflicts in Lebanon, Iran, Gaza and the West Bank and Ukraine cease," he ‚Äåsaid.
"Europe ‚ÅÝwill also have to redouble its efforts, especially now that the United States has decided to withdraw from many of these fronts."
Sanchez is expected to meet Chinese President Xi Jinping on Tuesday where they are expected to focus on geopolitics.
China accounted ‚Äãfor 74% of Spain's ‚Äãtotal trade deficit, ‚ÅÝSanchez said, adding that cooperation was important to build a "balanced, globalised economy that generates shared prosperity."
Madrid hopes Sanchez's visit will ‚Äãnarrow Spain's trade deficit, which more than doubled in four ‚Äãyears to ‚ÅÝnearly $50 billion in 2025. It is looking to boost agricultural and manufacturing exports to offset high volumes of China's imports.
