China's official news agency Xinhua on Monday said Sanchez's visit ‚ÅÝwas ‚Äãset to further consolidate bilateral ties and pointed to ‚Äãa broader pathway for steady engagement between China and Europe at a time of growing global uncertainty.

item.title: US, Indonesia discussing agreement allowing US military overflight in Indonesian airspace, Indonesian ministry says
item.description:
JAKARTA, April 13 (Reuters) - Indonesia and ‚Äåthe United States are discussing ‚Äãan ‚Äãagreement to give U.S. ‚ÅÝmilitary aircraft ‚Äãaccess to ‚ÄãIndonesian airspace, Indonesia's defence ministry said ‚Äãon Monday, ‚Äãadding that no deal ‚Äåhas ‚ÅÝyet been reached.
U.S. Defence Secretary Pete ‚ÄãHegseth ‚Äãis ‚ÅÝset to meet ‚Äãwith his ‚ÄãIndonesian ‚ÅÝcounterpart Sjafrie Sjamsoeddin later ‚ÅÝon ‚ÄãMonday.

item.title: South Korea and Poland to upgrade ties as Tusk calls Seoul key ally after US
item.description:
SEOUL, April 13 (Reuters) - South Korean President Lee Jae Myung and Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk agreed on Monday to upgrade ties to a comprehensive strategic partnership, with the leaders ‚Äãplacing defence cooperation at the centre of the relationship.
In remarks made before ‚Äãtalks between the leaders at the presidential Blue House, Lee said ‚ÅÝthe countries would further expand defence industry cooperation under a $44.2 billion framework pact signed ‚Äãin 2022.
"K2 tanks, K9 self‚Äëpropelled howitzers, FA‚Äë50 light-attack aircraft, and Chunmoo multiple rocket ‚Äãlaunchers ‚Äî bearing South Korea's technology and pride ‚Äî are now safeguarding Poland‚Äôs territory and its people across its vast lands," Lee said.
The South Korean leader said that the partnership extended beyond arms ‚Äãsales to include joint production, technology transfers and training.
Tusk described South Korea ‚Äãas Poland's "most important ally after the United States, especially in the defence industry" and said he ‚Äåwould ‚ÅÝpersonally oversee expanded defence cooperation between the countries.
He said the upgraded partnership meant the two countries would take on a shared responsibility and should play a role in contributing to global peace and international stability.
The two leaders also affirmed ‚Äãexpanded cooperation across a ‚Äãwide range of ‚ÅÝfields, including energy supply chains, infrastructure, science and technology, advanced industries, space and people‚Äëto‚Äëpeople exchanges.
South Korea has become one ‚Äãof Poland's leading arms suppliers in recent years as Warsaw ‚Äãmoves to ‚ÅÝrapidly modernise its military following Russia's invasion of Ukraine.
In 2022, South Korea and Poland signed a defence framework agreement for South Korean companies to supply Poland with ‚ÅÝarms as ‚Äãwell as jointly produce military equipment on Polish ‚Äãsoil.
Since then, defence companies including Hanwha Aerospace (012450.KS) , opens new tab and Hyundai Rotem (064350.KS) , opens new tab have signed follow-up, multi-billion-dollar contracts to ‚Äãsupply equipment such as tanks and missile launchers.

item.title: Australia appoints woman to lead its army for the first time
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 13 (Reuters) - Australia on Monday said a woman would lead its army for the first time in history, as part of a reshuffle of ‚Äãthe country's defence force leadership.
Lieutenant General Susan Coyle, the current chief ‚Äåof joint capabilities, will become chief of army in July, the government said in a statement. She will replace Lieutenant General Simon Stuart.
Coyle's appointment comes as Australia's military seeks to ‚Äãboost the number of female officers in its ranks. It ‚Äãfaces a wave of allegations of systematic sexual harassment and discrimination.
"From ‚ÅÝJuly, we will have the first ever female chief of army ‚Äãin the Australian Army's 125-year history," Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said in a ‚Äãstatement.
Defence Minister Richard Marles called Coyle's appointment a "deeply historic moment."
"As Susan said to me, you cannot be what you cannot see," he said.
"Susan's achievement will be deeply significant ‚Äãto women who are serving in the Australian Defence Force today and ‚Äãwomen who are thinking about serving in the Australian Defence Force in the future."
Coyle, ‚Äå55, ‚ÅÝenlisted in the military in 1987 and has held a number of senior command roles. She will be the first woman to lead any service branch of the military, Marles said.
Women currently make up around 21% of ‚Äãthe Australian Defence ‚ÄãForce (ADF) and 18.5% ‚ÅÝof senior leadership roles. The ADF has set a target of 25% of overall participation for women by 2030.
Last October, ‚Äãa class action lawsuit was filed against the ADF alleging ‚Äãit failed ‚ÅÝto protect thousands of female officers from systematic sexual assault, harassment and discrimination.
The government on Monday also appointed Vice Admiral Mark Hammond, the current chief ‚ÅÝof ‚Äãthe navy, as the head of the ADF, succeeding ‚ÄãAdmiral David Johnston.
The current deputy chief of navy, Rear Admiral Matthew Buckley, will replace Hammond as ‚Äãhead of the branch.

item.title: Haiti declares three days of national mourning for 25 killed in stampede
item.description:
PORT-AU-PRINCE, April 12 (Reuters) - Haiti declared three ‚Äãdays of national mourning ‚Äåon Sunday, a day after a deadly stampede killed 25 people ‚Äãduring an annual celebration thronged ‚Äãby students and visitors at ‚ÅÝits Laferriere Citadel tourist ‚Äãattraction.
In a national address, Prime Minister ‚ÄãAlix Didier Fils-Aim√© said mourning would begin from Tuesday, and the government ‚Äãwould cover the funeral ‚Äãexpenses for disaster victims.
Earlier in the day, ‚ÄåEmmanuel ‚ÅÝPierre, the national head of the civil protection authority, told Reuters authorities had revised down ‚Äãthe ‚Äãdeath toll ‚ÅÝto 25 from an initial tally of ‚Äã30.
The early-19th-century fortress built ‚Äãshortly ‚ÅÝafter Haiti's independence from France is a UNESCO World Heritage ‚ÅÝsite.

item.title: Slovakia's Fico ready to cooperate with Hungary's new prime minister
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico congratulated Hungary's incoming leader, Peter Magyar, on Monday, offering "intensive cooperation", while expressing gratitude ‚Äãto outgoing Prime Minister Viktor Orban after a major election upset .
Fico, ‚Äåa key ally who had backed Orban before voting started, said Slovakia was ready for the close ties and joint action in protecting energy interests remained a goal.
Veteran nationalist ‚ÄãOrban lost power in Sunday's election after 16 years in ‚Äãoffice, with Magyar and his upstart centre-right Tisza party winning ‚ÅÝa comfortable majority of seats in parliament.
"With full respect, I take note ‚Äãof the decision of the citizens of Hungary ... and am ready for ‚Äãintensive cooperation with the new Hungarian prime minister, whom I congratulate on the election result," Fico said in a statement.
Under Fico, who returned to office in 2023, Slovakia ‚Äãhas been a close ally of Hungary, with both neighbours maintaining warm ‚Äãties with Moscow, opposing European Union sanctions and keeping up purchases of Russian oil ‚Äåand ‚ÅÝgas.
Both have sought to restore Russian oil flows through the Druzhba pipeline, which has been out of action since late January after damage in Ukraine that Kyiv said was caused by a Russian strike.
Fico said he ‚Äãbelieved Slovakia, Hungary and ‚Äãcentral Europe ‚ÅÝwere still interested in resuming its operation, adding that joint action with Hungary in protecting energy interests remained unchanged ‚Äãas a government goal.
Last week, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy ‚Äãsaid pipeline ‚ÅÝrepairs would be completed in spring. Fico and Orban have had tense relations with Zelenskiy despite Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
In a separate statement ‚ÅÝon Facebook, ‚ÄãFico said Slovak-Hungarian relations have never been ‚Äãat such a high level, and he praised Orban for exemplary cooperation and protection of ‚Äãsovereignty and national interests.

item.title: Millions of people in Sudan surviving on one meal a day as food crisis deepens, NGOs say
item.description:
GENEVA, April 13 (Reuters) - Millions of people in Sudan are surviving on just one meal a day, as the country's food crisis deepens and threatens to spread, according to ‚Äãa report published on Monday by a group of non-governmental organisations.
Sudan's war between ‚Äåthe Sudanese army and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces, which enters its third year on Wednesday, has caused widespread hunger and displaced millions of people amid one of the world's largest humanitarian crises.
"In the two areas ‚Äãworst hit by the conflict - North Darfur and South Kordofan - millions of families can ‚Äãonly access one meal a day," the report by Action Against Hunger, ‚ÅÝCARE International, International Rescue Committee, Mercy Corps, and the Norwegian Refugee Council found.
"Often, they miss ‚Äãmeals for entire days," the report stated, adding that many people have resorted to eating leaves ‚Äãand animal feed to survive.
The army-aligned Sudanese government denies the existence of famine, while the RSF denies responsibility for such conditions in areas under its control.
Some 61.7% of Sudan‚Äôs population ‚Äì 28.9 million people ‚Äì is acutely ‚Äãfood-insecure, according to the 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan.
The United Nations has reported widespread ‚Äãatrocities and waves of ethnically charged violence. In November, the global hunger monitor confirmed, for the first time, famine ‚Äåconditions ‚ÅÝin al-Fashir, as well as Kadugli.
In February, the U.N.-backed Integrated Food Security Phase Classification found that famine thresholds for acute malnutrition have been surpassed in Um Baru, where the rate of acutely malnourished children under 5 was nearly double the famine threshold, and Kernoi.
The report, based on interviews ‚Äãwith farmers, traders, and ‚Äãhumanitarian actors in Sudan, ‚ÅÝdetails how the war in Sudan is driving communities towards famine conditions - due to disruptions to farming as well as the use of starvation ‚Äãas a weapon of war - including deliberate destruction of farms and ‚Äãmarkets.
Communal kitchens ‚ÅÝare increasingly unable to meet rising needs, while major donor funding cuts are impeding aid agencies' abilities to respond, the report said.
Women and girls have been disproportionately affected, as they face a high risk ‚ÅÝof rape ‚Äãand harassment when going to fields, visiting markets or ‚Äãcollecting water, the report said. Female-headed households are three times more likely to experience food insecurity than male-headed households, it ‚Äãadded.

item.title: Taiwan government should lead engagement with China on new measures, senior official says
item.description:
TAIPEI, April 13 (Reuters) - Taiwan's engagement with China on looser controls proposed by Beijing for trade ‚Äåand tourism should be led by the government rather than private party-to-party contacts, a senior Taiwanese security official said on Monday.
China on Sunday unveiled 10 new incentive measures for Taiwan, including easing tourist curbs, allowing in "healthy" television ‚Äãdramas and facilitating food sales, following a visit by the island's opposition leader.
But China refuses ‚Äãto speak with Taiwan President Lai Ching-te's administration, saying he is a "separatist", ‚ÅÝand has rebuffed multiple offers of talks.
Tsai Ming-yen, head of Taiwan's National Security Bureau, said only ‚Äãthe government should be assessing, planning and driving relevant exchanges.
"Having the government take charge of the ‚Äãrelevant planning can therefore also better help avoid unnecessary risks and long-term consequences," he told reporters at parliament.
China's Taiwan Affairs Office did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The Kuomintang (KMT), Taiwan's largest opposition party whose leader ‚ÄãCheng Li-wun met Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing last week, has welcomed the measures, saying ‚Äãthey can help with peace and bring economic opportunities.
Hou Yu-ih, the KMT's 2024 presidential candidate and mayor of Taipei's ‚Äåsister ‚ÅÝcity New Taipei, told reporters on Monday that exchanges must be initiated on the basis of equality, dignity, and mutual respect, starting with less sensitive areas like culture.
"I believe that the leaders on both sides of the strait must take the responsibility of avoiding war. Peace is the ‚Äãultimate goal," he said. "Engagement ‚Äãis about communication and ‚ÅÝminimising conflict as much as possible."
Tsai said such "goodwill measures" from China were historically brought up ahead of elections, concentrated on certain specific ‚Äãcounties and cities, or specific companies, industries, or individuals.
"However, this has already ‚Äãbecome a ‚ÅÝtool the Chinese communists use to interfere in Taiwan's elections," he added.
Taiwan will hold key local elections in November.
Ahead of the last presidential elections in early 2024, Taiwan complained that China had used ‚ÅÝa series ‚Äãof trade measures to try to sway electors, including ‚Äãreviewing tariff concessions on agricultural and fishery products, machinery, auto parts and textiles.
Taiwan's government rejects Beijing's territorial claims, saying only the ‚Äãisland's people can decide their future.

item.title: Taiwan president to visit Eswatini, last diplomatic ally in Africa
item.description:
TAIPEI, April 13 (Reuters) - Taiwan President Lai Ching-te will visit Eswatini next week, his office said on Monday, the ‚Äãisland's last remaining diplomatic ally in Africa.
Taiwan, which ‚ÄåChina claims as its own territory with no right to state-to-state relations, now has formal ties with only 12 ‚Äãcountries, almost all small, less-developed nations in Latin ‚ÄãAmerica, the Caribbean and the Pacific, like Belize ‚ÅÝand Tuvalu.
Lai will be in Eswatini from April ‚Äã22-26, his spokesperson Karen Kuo told reporters, for the ‚Äã40th anniversary of King Mswati III's accession and his 58th birthday.
Lai is flying directly to Eswatini, which is almost entirely surrounded ‚Äãby South Africa, and does not require a ‚Äãlayover, unlike visits to Latin America, which require transits via the ‚ÄåUnited ‚ÅÝStates that routinely anger China.
This will be Lai's first trip outside of Taiwan since November 2024, when he visited the Marshall Islands, Tuvalu, and Palau, and ‚Äãtransited through Hawaii ‚Äãand the ‚ÅÝU.S. territory of Guam.
The last time a Taiwanese president visited Eswatini, formerly known ‚Äãas Swaziland and home to around 1.3 ‚Äãmillion people, ‚ÅÝwas in 2023, when Tsai Ing-wen made the journey.
Taiwan has provided large amounts of aid to the small ‚ÅÝsouthern ‚ÄãAfrican nation, an absolute monarchy. ‚ÄãIn 2021, it sent antiviral medication to help King Mswati III recover ‚Äãfrom COVID.

item.title: TotalEnergies discovers hydrocarbons in Congo near existing production facilities
item.description:
PARIS, April 13 - French oil major ‚ÄãTotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab has found ‚Äåhydrocarbons as part of a drilling campaign to ‚Äãexpand its currently ‚Äãoperational Moho field offshore ‚ÅÝRepublic of Congo, ‚Äãit said on Monday.
The ‚Äãwell encountered a column of 160 metres of oil ‚Äãand gas. Total ‚Äãsaid the Moho G discovery ‚Äåand ‚ÅÝits previous Moho H find represent recoverable resources of close ‚Äãto ‚Äã100 ‚ÅÝmillion barrels.
Oil majors have expressed renewed ‚Äãinterest in West ‚ÄãAfrica ‚ÅÝin a bid to replenish resources from ‚ÅÝdeclining ‚Äãfields elsewhere.

item.title: Baker Hughes sells Waygate unit to Hexagon for about $1.45 billion
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Oilfield services provider Baker ‚ÄãHughes (BKR.O) , opens new tab sold its Waygate Technologies ‚Äåunit to Swedish industrial technology group Hexagon (HEXAb.ST) , opens new tab for about $1.45 billion in ‚Äãcash, the two companies ‚Äãsaid on Monday.
The deal is expected ‚ÅÝto close in the ‚Äãsecond half of 2026, the ‚Äãcompanies added.
Hexagon said it plans to finance the acquisition using cash and existing ‚Äãdebt capacity.
Last year, Baker ‚ÄãHughes agreed to buy Chart Industries (GTLS.N) , opens new tab in ‚Äåa $13.6 ‚ÅÝbillion all-cash deal to leverage its industrial and energy technology portfolio to drive growth and ‚Äãexpand its ‚Äãpresence ‚ÅÝin the natural gas and LNG sectors.
Baker Hughes ‚ÄãCEO Lorenzo Simonelli has ‚Äãbeen ‚ÅÝreshaping the company to adapt to the global energy transition , shedding ‚ÅÝnon-core ‚Äãassets while investing ‚Äãin cleaner energy solutions.

item.title: Malaysia detains two tankers suspected of illegally transferring¬Ýdiesel
item.description:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 13 (Reuters) - Malaysia's Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA) said it has detained two ‚Äãtankers for allegedly conducting an illegal ship-to-ship transfer of ‚Äåabout 700,000 litres of diesel off the island of Penang over the weekend.
Malaysia has been cracking down on fuel smuggling amid mounting shortages and supply disruptions ‚Äãthroughout the region caused by the war in the ‚ÄãMiddle East.
($1 = 3.9700 ringgit)
(This story has been corrected to change the number to 700,000, from 700,00, in paragraph 1)

item.title: China model gains appeal in Vietnam as police expand power
item.description:
HANOI, April 13 (Reuters) - Vietnam is increasingly edging closer to China's model of governance, tightening state control while embracing Chinese technology and regulation as its most powerful leader in decades heads to Beijing this week, ‚Äãaccording to internal documents, public policy plans and sources.
The two Communist neighbours have swung between conflict and cooperation over centuries. Now, Vietnam is leaning more openly toward Beijing, ‚Äåas China-friendly security figures rise in Hanoi under party chief To Lam, a former public security boss.
Lam will meet China's leader Xi Jinping on Wednesday on his first overseas trip since becoming state president on April 7, a move that formally unites two of Vietnam's most powerful roles, echoing Xi's own concentration of authority and breaking from Vietnam's traditional emphasis on collective leadership.
"Vietnam-China relations have entered a new stage, marked by higher political trust, more substantive defence and security cooperation, deeper and ‚Äãmore practical cooperation across sectors," Lam said in a joint statement with Xi after they last met in April 2025.
This week's visit is expected to yield dozens of cooperation agreements, people ‚Äãbriefed on the plans said. While such documents are often non-binding, the relationship is becoming more tangible: China's exports to Vietnam are at record highs , and Chinese ‚ÅÝinvestment in manufacturing south of the border is booming.
The people spoke on condition of anonymity due to the sensitive nature of the topic.
Vietnam is still hedging geopolitically, analysts say, keeping doors open to Washington ‚Äãand others. But at home, it is moving closer to China's governance model - particularly control-driven regulation despite Western misgivings, underscoring how China's influence is deepening as Lam reshapes the state.
Vietnam has "a dual approach of actively ‚Äãlearning from the Chinese model while selectively resisting its influence," said Nguyen Khac Giang, a visiting fellow at Singapore's ISEAS Yusof Ishak Institute.
Alexander Vuving of the Asia-Pacific Center for Security Studies in the U.S., said that closer ties with China without adequate guardrails "will have a negative impact not only on Vietnam's security, prosperity, and autonomy, but also on its relations with the U.S. and the West."
Vietnam's foreign ministry did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Technology has emerged ‚Äãas one of the clearest markers of warming ties.
Vietnam has dropped earlier concerns about the use of Chinese equipment in its 5G network , while the country's largest internet provider FPT (FPT.HM) , opens new tab announced investments in an undersea cable ‚Äãto be built by a Chinese vendor that the U.S. considers linked to sanctioned telecom giant Huawei. A telecom company under Vietnam's public security ministry is in talks with Chinese companies for additional 5G deals.
Chinese firms are simultaneously exploring ‚Äåinvestments in Vietnamese ‚ÅÝdata centres, a strategic asset, according to people familiar with the discussions.
"Chinese interest in Vietnam's data-centre market has increased noticeably over the past 18-24 months," said Mickael Driol, head of investment advisory firm Mekong Partners. He said much of that is driven by manufacturers who moved operations to Vietnam from China.
Hanoi is prioritising state control in data regulation, similar to China. Western tech companies and the U.S. government have repeatedly raised concerns over data protection rules drafted by Vietnam's security ministry that limit cross-border data transfers.
Draft documents seen by Reuters show Vietnam plans to establish state-run data-trading exchanges overseen by the public security ministry - mirroring China's centralised ‚Äãdata model and expanding the state's ability to deploy ‚Äãinformation for surveillance and strategic goals. In ‚ÅÝWestern markets, such platforms are typically privately run.
Vietnam is also expanding a national electronic identification system, enabling authorities to identify individuals through AI camera networks that are being rolled out nationwide - another parallel with China's surveillance architecture.
"The police's rising power (in Vietnam) may partly explain a growing interest in Chinese-style social control tactics," said ‚ÄãGiang.
Unencumbered by public opinion that has grown less critical of China, the Vietnamese Communist Party is also advancing a more China-style economic model centred ‚Äãon subsidies , public investment and large ‚ÅÝinfrastructure projects, sometimes in direct cooperation with Beijing on sensitive projects including high-speed rail links .
The shift has been reinforced by TikTok's popularity in Vietnam, where positive narratives about China often dominate, and by Hanoi's increasingly muted criticism of Beijing's actions in the disputed South China Sea.
Vietnam remains more open than China to foreign investors and still depends heavily on external capital. But China's share of total investments is rising, and Chinese brands are gaining popularity at home.
China's influence ‚ÅÝis also visible ‚Äãin finance. Vietnam relies on unconventional monetary policy tools such as lending mandates to banks reminiscent of China's policy, maintains ‚Äãtight foreign ownership caps in key sectors, and is grappling with a property bubble that echoes China's experience.
Now Hanoi is considering deeper intervention in equity markets. Proposed measures include a government-backed stabilisation fund to buy stocks during downturns - an idea explicitly modelled on China.
"China ‚Äãcreated one and succeeded in reassuring investors," said an internal security ministry document reviewed by Reuters.

item.title: Two killed in Ukraine despite Easter ceasefire, governor says
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Two people were killed and one injured by Russian troops in Ukraine's frontline eastern Donetsk region ‚Äãbefore the expiry of a ceasefire to mark Orthodox ‚ÄåEaster , a local official said on Monday,
One person was killed in the city of Kramatorsk and one in Druzhkivka ‚Äãon Sunday, regional governor Vadym Filashkin said on ‚Äãthe Telegram messaging app, without giving more details.
Both ‚ÅÝRussia and Ukraine have accused each other of breaching ‚Äãthe 32-hour ceasefire from Saturday afternoon to midnight (2100 GMT) ‚Äãon Sunday.
Ukraine's Coordination Headquarters for the Treatment of Prisoners of War, citing a spokesperson of the 14th Army Corps, said on ‚ÄãMonday that during "the so-called 'Easter ceasefire'" Russian servicemen also ‚Äãshot dead four Ukrainian prisoners of war in the Kharkiv region.
It ‚Äådid ‚ÅÝnot give more details, adding that Russia was preparing a campaign to deny it.
There was no immediate comment from Russia and Reuters could not verify the ‚Äãinformation independently.
The General ‚ÄãStaff of ‚ÅÝUkraine's military said on Monday it had compiled a total of 10,721 Russian ‚Äãviolations over the course of the truce.
It ‚Äãsaid the ‚ÅÝviolations included 1,567 artillery shelling incidents, 119 assault operations and 9,035 strikes by attack drones. It said no ‚ÅÝair ‚Äãstrikes had been recorded.
The Russian ‚Äãdefence ministry has said it recorded 1,971 ceasefire violations by Ukraine overnight ‚Äãinto Sunday.

item.title: Kuwait raises May crude prices for Asia, pricing document shows
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Kuwait has raised ‚Äãthe official selling ‚Äåprice for Kuwait Export ‚ÄãCrude to ‚ÄãAsia in May ‚ÅÝto $17 a ‚Äãbarrel above ‚Äãthe average of Oman/Dubai quotes, a ‚Äãprice document ‚Äãreviewed by Reuters ‚Äåshowed ‚ÅÝon Monday.
The producer increased the May Kuwait ‚ÄãSuper ‚ÄãLight ‚ÅÝcrude OSP to $17 ‚Äãa barrel ‚Äãabove ‚ÅÝOman/Dubai quotes.

item.title: What the Trump tax breaks giveth, the gasoline pump taketh away
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 10 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump in February touted this year's tax refunds as "substantially greater than ever before," thanks to new individual tax breaks approved in ‚Äã2025, telling taxpayers that they should think of him when the funds hit their bank accounts.
"Don't spend all of this money in one place!" he wrote on the Truth Social media platform.
But that's what's happening, as ‚Äåthe gasoline pump eats most of the larger tax refunds that people are seeing from new tax breaks on tips, Social Security retirement payments, overtime pay, car loan interest and state and local tax bills that were part of last year's Republican-backed tax-cut legislation.
Despite a tenuous ceasefire in the U.S. war with Iran, oil prices held close to $100 a barrel on Friday ahead of talks between U.S. and Iranian officials in Islamabad as the Strait of Hormuz remained closed . But even if the waterway, which handles about 20% of the world's oil supply, reopens soon, fuel prices could keep rising for months , according to ‚Äãthe U.S. Energy Information Administration, which predicts global benchmark Brent crude prices will average $96 a barrel this year.
Destruction of energy infrastructure across the Gulf region will limit future output, according to Rystad Energy , opens new tab , which estimates rebuilding costs of at least $25 billion. ‚ÄãThat figure could rise further as attacks continue.
The drop in energy supply potentially means months more of pain at the pump, and economists say Americans with lower incomes, who spend more of ‚ÅÝtheir pay on gasoline, will see the smallest gains from the tax breaks.
U.S. consumer prices jumped by the most in nearly four years in March, rising 0.9%, thanks mostly to a record one-month increase in gasoline prices on the back of the spike in ‚Äãglobal oil prices and the persistent pass-through of higher tariffs, Bureau of Labor Statistics data showed on Friday. Higher costs for diesel, fertilizer, aluminum and other inputs affected by the Middle East conflict, food inflation and other price hikes could eat what's left of those ‚Äãrefunds.
As of April 3, the average individual refund for the 2025 tax year was up $346, or 11.1%, from the prior-year average to $3,462, according to the latest Internal Revenue Service filing data , opens new tab .
These figures could shift higher ahead of the April 15 tax filing deadline on Wednesday, with estimates clocking in at $560 from Morgan Stanley, $611 from the conservative-leaning Tax Foundation and $1,000 from the U.S. Treasury. But some of that relief may come in the form of reduced income withholding or lower quarterly tax payments by individuals.
Economists at the Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research estimate that war-driven price spikes have ‚Äãpushed up Americans' average annual gasoline costs for this year by $857. The March 23 estimate was made when Brent futures were trading at $99 a barrel - about $2 higher than on Friday - and assumed that the Strait of Hormuz, which is still largely shut, would reopen around ‚ÄãApril 10.
Democrats on the Joint Economic Committee in Congress estimate that Americans paid an additional $8.4 billion for gasoline during the first month of the Iran war, based on price data from motorist advocacy group AAA, vehicle fuel economy data from Edmunds.com and federal gasoline consumption data. ‚ÄåThat figure is more ‚ÅÝthan a quarter of the $30.7 billion increase in total IRS individual refunds through April 3.
"Gas prices are probably the most salient price in the economy," said Neale Mahoney, a professor of economics at Stanford University. "The impacts may be modest from a macro perspective, but for the sort of kitchen-table economics of a family, and things that they're paying attention to, they can be big."
A family that was expecting a bigger refund and planning a bigger summer vacation or kitchen remodel this year may pull back, as they dig deeper into their wallets to fill up their tanks. Grocery bills also are expected to rise as higher diesel, fertilizer, jet fuel and aluminum prices work their way through the economy.
What was meant to be a mild boost to the economy is now turning into more of a cushion against ‚Äãa deeper slowdown, but analysts are starting to mark down ‚ÄãU.S. consumption and GDP forecasts by a few tenths of ‚ÅÝa percentage point. Morgan Stanley now expects 2026 consumption growth to slow to 1.7% from 2.1% in 2025, with durable goods taking most of the hit, while Oxford Economics has slashed its global GDP growth forecast for 2026 to 2.6% from 3.0%, well below the pace of recent years.
Most of the tax breaks in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act passed by the ‚ÄãRepublican-controlled Congress last year were retroactive to the start of 2025, so most of the first-year benefits will come through claimed income deductions.
Several of these breaks, including those on tips, overtime, ‚ÄãSocial Security and auto loan interest, ‚ÅÝare available regardless of whether taxpayers itemize their returns or take the standard deduction, which was also increased by $1,150 for individual filers for the 2025 tax year. This change would yield a refund increase of $138 for those in the 12% tax bracket with incomes of $11,926 to $48,475.
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent last week called the overtime deduction "a home run," with 25% of filers claiming it.
But one of the biggest breaks, a $30,000 increase in the deduction for state and local taxes paid, still requires itemization, largely putting it out of reach for non-homeowners who don't have mortgages ‚ÅÝor pay property ‚Äãtaxes.
It's not until tax returns reach the market-income range of $71,659 to $126,348 - higher than that of 60% of earners - that taxpayers will still have money left over ‚Äãfrom the new tax breaks after their higher fuel bills, according to data from the conservative-leaning Tax Foundation. Market income includes adjusted gross income, tax-exempt interest, employer-sponsored health and pension benefits and other items.
But Tax Foundation data shows that the cuts for the top 0.01% of returns above $2.24 million in income still yield a ‚Äãbigger tax savings than for those earning up to $37,486.

item.title: Poland‚Äôs premier hails Hungary vote as blow to authoritarian rule
item.description:
WARSAW, April 13 (Reuters) - Hungary's election shows that Europe is not on an inevitable path toward authoritarian rule, ‚ÄãPolish Prime Minister Donald Tusk said on Monday, ‚Äåafter the center-right Tisza party ended Viktor Orban's 16‚Äëyear grip on power.
"Everyone feared there was a trend toward authoritarian, corrupt ‚Äãregimes," Tusk said while on an official ‚Äãvisit to South Korea , according to Polish state-owned ‚ÅÝnews agency PAP.
"That's not the case. First Warsaw, ‚Äãthen Bucharest, Chisinau, now Budapest."
In 2025, centrist Nicusor Dan won ‚Äãa presidential election in Romania and Moldova's pro-European ruling party won a resounding victory over its Russia-aligned rival.
Tusk had repeatedly criticized ‚ÄãOrban's government for its close ties with Moscow.
"I'm ‚Äãglad that this part of Europe is showing that we ‚Äåare ‚ÅÝnot doomed to corrupt and authoritarian governments, because that's what Victor Orban's government unfortunately became after many years in power."
Neighbors Poland and Hungary are linked ‚Äãby their long, ‚Äãshared history, ‚ÅÝclose trade ties and cooperation within the European Union and NATO.
Tusk said he ‚Äãhad spoken with Tisza leader Peter ‚ÄãMagyar ‚ÅÝto congratulate him on his victory.
"We briefly discussed his visit to Warsaw," Tusk said.
"As you know, he long ‚ÅÝago ‚Äãchose Warsaw as his first ‚Äãvisit for quite obvious reasons. I think our relationship will be ‚Äãabsolutely exceptional."

item.title: Pope says he will continue to speak out against war after Trump attack
item.description:
ABOARD PAPAL PLANE, April 13 (Reuters) - Pope Leo told Reuters on Monday that he plans ‚Äãto continue speaking out against war after U.S. President Donald Trump's direct ‚Äåattack on the leader of the 1.4-billion-member Church.
In comments aboard the papal flight to Algiers, where the first U.S. pope is starting a 10-day tour to ‚Äãfour African countries, the pontiff also said the Christian message ‚Äãwas being "abused".
"I don't want to get into a debate with ‚ÅÝhim," Leo told Reuters as he greeted journalists on the plane. "I don't ‚Äãthink that the message of the Gospel is meant to be abused ‚Äãin the way that some people are doing."
"I will continue to speak out loudly against war, looking to promote peace, promoting dialogue and multilateral relationships among the ‚Äãstates to look for just solutions to problems," he said, speaking ‚Äãon English.
"Too many people are suffering in the world today," said Leo. "Too many innocent ‚Äåpeople ‚ÅÝare being killed. And I think someone has to stand up and say there's a better way."
"The message of the church, my message, the message of the Gospel: Blessed are the Peacemakers. I do ‚Äãnot look at ‚Äãmy role as ‚ÅÝbeing political, a politician," he said.
Leo, originally from Chicago, has emerged as an outspoken critic of the ‚ÄãU.S.-Israeli war on Iran in recent weeks and decried ‚Äãthe "madness of ‚ÅÝwar" on in a peace appeal on Saturday.
Trump, in an apparent response to the pope's criticisms of both the conflict and the White House's ‚ÅÝhard-line ‚Äãimmigration policies, said late on Sunday that ‚ÄãLeo was "terrible".
"Pope Leo is WEAK on Crime, and terrible for Foreign Policy," Trump wrote in ‚Äãa post on Truth Social.

item.title: Philippines warns of 'sabotage' after cyanide seizure in disputed South China Sea atoll
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - The Philippines discovered cyanide on Chinese boats operating around a ‚Äådisputed atoll in the South China Sea, security officials from the country said on Monday.
Authorities said laboratory tests confirmed the presence of the highly toxic substance in bottles seized by the Philippine navy in operations at Second ‚ÄãThomas Shoal last year.
Officials warned the cyanide could have had serious consequences for marine life ‚Äãand weakened the reef supporting a warship that Manila grounded on the ‚ÅÝatoll to reinforce its maritime claim.
"We wish to underscore that the use of cyanide in ‚ÄãAyungin Shoal is a form of sabotage that seeks to kill local fish populations, depriving ‚ÄãNavy personnel of a vital food source," Cornelio Valencia, National Security Council spokesperson, told a press conference using the Philippines' name for the atoll. Valencia added that cyanide could damage the reef and "ultimately compromise" the warship's stability.
The ‚ÄãChinese Embassy in Manila did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The Philippines has ‚Äãaccused China of disrupting resupply missions to troops on the vessel, including a June 17, 2024, incident that ‚Äåturned ‚ÅÝviolent and resulted in a Filipino sailor losing a finger. China has denied allegations of aggressive conduct during such encounters and accused the Philippines of trespassing in its waters.
The June confrontation later led to a provisional understanding for resupply missions to the grounded ship.
China and the ‚ÄãPhilippines held high-level talks ‚Äãlast month over the ‚ÅÝSouth China Sea, exploring preliminary steps towards oil and gas cooperation , and confidence-building measures at sea, including communication between their coast guards.
The Philippine foreign ministry ‚Äãsaid in a statement on Monday the scope of the coast ‚Äãguard cooperation ‚ÅÝwould be limited, and "does not contemplate cooperation in sensitive operational areas," adding that there had been no discussions on joint patrols.
China claims nearly all the South China Sea, including areas claimed by ‚ÅÝBrunei, Malaysia, ‚Äãthe Philippines and Vietnam.
A 2016 ruling by an international ‚Äãarbitral tribunal found Beijing's sweeping claims had no basis under international law, a decision China rejects. More than $3 trillion ‚Äãin annual ship-borne commerce travels through the key waterway.

item.title: Israelis oppose Iran ceasefire, divided over whether to respect it, poll says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, April 13 (Reuters) - Nearly two-thirds of Israelis oppose the Iran ceasefire but the public is divided over whether Israel should respect the two-week truce or resume attacks ‚Äãon Iran, according to a poll from the Hebrew University of Jerusalem.
The ‚Äåpoll was the first national survey of Israelis conducted after the U.S. and Iran agreed last week to a ceasefire brokered by Pakistan, the survey's authors said. The two sides failed to ‚Äãreach a broader deal to end the war in weekend talks in Islamabad.
The ‚Äãceasefire has halted U.S. and Israeli airstrikes on Iran. But it ‚ÅÝhas not ended a parallel war between Israel and the Iranian-backed Hezbollah in Lebanon, ‚Äãwhere the Israeli military has continued deadly bombardment that has killed many civilians. Hezbollah ‚Äãhas continued to fire rockets at Israeli towns in the country's north.
On Lebanon, more than 61 percent of Israelis believe the truce should not extend to the fighting with Hezbollah, a core demand ‚Äãby Iran in talks with the U.S., according to the poll, conducted by researchers ‚Äãat Hebrew University's Agam Labs.
Asked what Israel should do about Iran, 39 percent said Israel should ‚Äåcontinue ‚ÅÝattacks, 41 percent said their country should respect the ceasefire, and 19 percent said they weren't sure, the poll said.
The poll was based on a sample of 1,312 Israelis interviewed from April 9-10, with a margin of error of 3.2 percent.
With the fate ‚Äãof the Iran ceasefire ‚Äãunclear, Israel is digging ‚ÅÝin for a long, drawn-out conflict across the Middle East, with Israeli officials concluding that their enemies in Iran, Lebanon, Gaza ‚Äãand beyond cannot be eliminated outright.
The public's perception of Israel's military ‚Äãsuccess in ‚ÅÝIran holds high stakes for Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, who faces an election due by October that most public opinion polls show he will lose.
According to the Hebrew University ‚ÅÝpoll, Netanyahu's ‚Äãstanding among Israelis has decreased since the start ‚Äãof the Iran war, with 34 percent of Israelis preferring him as premier now versus 40 percent at ‚Äãthe start of the conflict.

item.title: Exclusive: Chilean copper miner Codelco, contractors fined after deadly mine collapse
item.description:
SANTIAGO, April 13 (Reuters) - Chilean state copper miner Codelco was fined by labor authorities after last year's deadly collapse at its El Teniente mine, while three contractors whose workers were killed or injured were sanctioned more heavily, according to inspection records obtained by Reuters through public-records requests.
The previously unreported sanctions were imposed in the months ‚Äãfollowing the July 31 underground seismic event that triggered a rock burst at El Teniente , the world's largest underground copper mine, killing six contract workers and injuring others.
Reuters obtained the files from ‚ÄåChile's labor ministry through open-records requests. In Chile, such fines are notified directly to employers and can be challenged or reduced administratively, but are not typically disclosed in public announcements.
At the time of the accident, then-Labor Minister Giorgio Boccardo said that his office and the mining regulator, Sernageomin, would investigate the causes of the incident and whether any labor-safety rules had been breached.
The quake, measured at about magnitude 4.3, led to a halt in all underground operations at the sprawling mine complex amid rescue efforts and safety inspections.
The collapse ‚Äãcarried a significant production cost for Codelco. The company has said the shutdown of underground operations at El Teniente and a slow restart cut copper output by tens of thousands of metric tons, disrupting shipments at ‚Äãa time of tight global supply.
The disaster also underscored the geotechnical risks facing Chile's aging underground mines .
The records show the three contractors were fined the ‚ÅÝequivalent of about $87,000 combined, versus roughly $20,000 for Codelco, a gap that reflects Chile's split-liability framework for subcontracted work.
While the principal company - Codelco - can be penalized for overarching safety failures, contractors remain directly responsible as employers for accident reporting, ‚Äãrisk assessment, worker assignment and other compliance duties, under Chile's labor code.
In one sanction, labor inspectors said Codelco lacked a complete, written procedure showing how seismic warnings were used to decide whether work should stop or be restricted.
After the ‚Äãaccident, regulators also found that Codelco violated labor rules when workers were found entering, or preparing to enter, underground areas while the mine-wide suspension remained in force, according to a separate sanction record.
Under Chilean labor rules, some serious or fatal-accident violations can be fined at up to 150 UTM, an inflation-linked Chilean tax unit, or about $11,000 at current values, per infraction. The regulator levied a 340 UTM penalty, roughly $26,000 in today's terms, on a company after a fatal construction-site collapse in a 2007 case.
Labor advocates and worker-safety specialists ‚Äãhave questioned whether such small penalties carry enough deterrent force for major employers.
"It is essential to raise the size of fines in order to effectively deter companies from violating mining safety regulations," a Chilean House of Representatives investigative ‚Äãcommission report said in 2011 after a mining disaster.
Proposals since then to hike up fines for serious or fatal workplace accidents have not gotten off the ground.
Since the collapse, Codelco has said it tightened safety procedures for restarting work at ‚ÄåEl Teniente, ‚ÅÝadding safety briefings at the start of shifts, improving communications underground, stepping up checks on workers' locations and reviewing protective equipment.
It later said an independent panel led by a former Anglo American CEO was examining what caused the accident and whether broader management or workplace problems played a role.
In a statement to Reuters, Codelco said that its seismic alert response system was active the day of the accident, and that it had appealed the fine by the labor ministry.
It added that there was an "ongoing legal proceeding related to the oversight of worker entry during the work stoppage" and that it was awaiting a decision from authorities.
Codelco said in August that El Teniente mine manager Andres Music would leave ‚Äãhis role, and in February announced the departure of three ‚Äãsenior executives after an internal audit found inconsistencies and ‚ÅÝconcealment following a separate rock burst at the mine several years earlier.
Among the three contracting firms fined, Strabag (STRV.VI) , opens new tab subsidiary Zublin was fined for failing to report a worker's death within 24 hours. Inspectors found that although the company knew about the fatality within two hours, it didn't notify labor authorities until the following evening.
Immediate reporting ‚Äãis critical to implement emergency safety measures to protect remaining workers, the report said.
The Austrian company did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
A unit of ‚ÄãChilean construction firm SalfaCorp (SALFACORP.SN) , opens new tab was also ‚ÅÝsanctioned after one of its workers died in the Andesita sector of the mine. Inspectors said the company failed to immediately report the fatal accident to authorities, in addition to other offenses.
The records also said Salfa's risk assessment did not adequately account for seismic hazards and that the company failed to do enough to protect workers' lives.
SalfaCorp did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Chile's labor regulator also fined Constructora Gardilcic, the unlisted contractor whose workers were killed and injured in ‚ÅÝthe Recursos Norte ‚Äãarea of the mine.
Inspectors said the company reported the accident late, delayed filing injury reports and showed weak safety planning.
Authorities also concluded Gardilcic ‚Äãdid not adequately account for the risk of violent rock bursts outside designated danger zones and put some workers in jobs they were not cleared to do.
Gardilcic did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Codelco has said the areas hardest hit by the accident ‚Äãwill remain under tight restrictions while criminal , regulatory and technical investigations continue.
The company has pledged a gradual, regulator-approved restart, leaving uncertainty over when the entire mine can resume normal operations.

item.title: Argentina credit stress deepens as inflation and utility costs hit households
item.description:
BUENOS AIRES, April 13 (Reuters) - Gonzalo Martinez, a 37-year-old teacher, hopes that one day when he opens his bank ‚Äåaccount, he'll have no more debt looming over him.
Martinez is one of a growing number of Argentines falling behind on bank loans as shrinking purchasing power - the result of rising inflation and lower government subsidies - pushes household finances to the brink.
Data from Argentina's central bank (BCRA) ‚Äãshowed that household loan delinquencies rose to 10.6% in January 2026, up from 9.3% in December, and ‚Äãjust 2.8% in December 2023, when President Javier Milei took office.
Milei has overseen deep "chainsaw" ‚ÅÝcuts to public spending in order to rein in inflation, achieving Argentina's first budget surplus in more than ‚Äãa decade. The austerity measures have been met with street protests, including from public university students and retirees who have seen ‚Äãschool funding and pensions shrink.
Analysts told Reuters the rise in loan defaults reflects shrinking real incomes, as wage negotiations have lagged behind inflation and the government has slashed subsidies for public services such as electricity, gas and transportation.
‚ÄúIncreases in public service tariffs have ‚Äãbeen compressing disposable income and, as a result, families‚Äô ability to settle their debts,‚Äù said Pablo Besmedrisnik, economist and ‚Äãdirector of consultancy VDC.
Martinez, who lives in Buenos Aires, said he has accumulated credit card debt as his salary hasn't adjusted to inflation.
"I ‚Äåexpected ‚ÅÝwhat I‚Äôd be paying on the card to represent a smaller share of my income, but it hasn‚Äôt," he said.
Although annual inflation has dropped significantly during Milei‚Äôs presidency - from 211.4% in 2023 to 117.8% in 2024 and ending 2025 at 31.5% - analysts said the improvement has failed to restore purchasing power.
Monthly inflation has edged up , accelerating from 1.5% in ‚ÄãMay 2025 to 3% ‚Äãin March 2026, according to a ‚ÅÝcentral bank market expectations survey released on Wednesday.
‚ÄúCredit delinquency reflects the income crisis facing families,‚Äù said Pablo Moldovan, economist and director at consultancy C-P Consultora.
Nahuel, a 37-year-old public ‚Äãsector employee who preferred not to disclose his last name, told Reuters he took ‚Äãa loan during ‚ÅÝhis vacation and then needed another loan to pay it off.
He currently has five overdue loans.
Analysts expect delinquencies to rise further as higher global energy prices boost inflation.
‚ÄúThere is no sign of a change in trend,‚Äù said Moldovan.
Several consultancies and economists ‚ÅÝwarned that ‚Äãdelinquency levels on loans issued outside the formal banking system - such ‚Äãas from companies that offer private loans - could be two to three times higher than the figures reported by the central bank.

item.title: UK will not back blockade of Strait of Hormuz, PM Starmer says
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said on ‚ÄãMonday that whatever the pressure, Britain would not be dragged into ‚Äåthe Iran war nor be involved in a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.
"We're not supporting the blockade," he told BBC Radio 5 Live, adding that it was ‚Äãvital to get the Strait reopened.
"It is, in my view, vital ‚Äãthat we get the Strait open and fully open, and ‚ÅÝthat's where we've put all of our efforts in the last ‚Äãfew and we'll continue to do so," Starmer said.
Britain had minesweepers in the ‚Äãregion, he said, and while he could not discuss operational matters, the military capability was "focused from our point of view on getting the Strait full open".
The U.S. military said it ‚Äãwould begin a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and exiting ‚ÄãIranian ports and coastal areas on Monday, after weekend talks failed to reach a deal ‚Äåto ‚ÅÝend the war with Iran.
The U.S. Central Command said that the U.S. blockade, starting at 10 a.m. ET on Monday (1400 GMT), would be "enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal ‚Äãareas, including all ‚ÄãIranian ports on ‚ÅÝthe Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman."
Vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports will ‚Äãnot be impeded, the U.S. military said.
President Donald Trump ‚Äãsaid on ‚ÅÝSunday U.S. forces would also intercept every vessel in international waters that had paid a toll to Iran.
"No one who pays an illegal toll will ‚ÅÝhave safe ‚Äãpassage on the high seas," Trump wrote ‚Äãon social media, adding: "Any Iranian who fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be ‚ÄãBLOWN TO HELL!"

item.title: Iran's Hormuz gamble ushers in a tense new normal for Gulf energy
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - The Iran war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz have shattered a status quo that prevailed among Middle East oil and gas producers for decades. Even if the current, shaky ceasefire holds, the uneasy ‚Äúnew normal‚Äù has likely set the stage for yet another round of conflict.
Tehran has now demonstrated both its ability and willingness to seal off the critical waterway and strike vital ‚Äãinfrastructure across the region, fundamentally altering the risk calculus for its neighbours and jeopardizing Gulf countries‚Äô long‚Äëterm oil and gas strategies.
The six‚Äëweek war laid bare deep tensions between the Islamic Republic and its ‚Äåkey regional neighbours Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Bahrain and Iraq - all close U.S. allies. They had long avoided direct confrontation with Tehran under a tacit understanding that war would devastate their shared economic interests. But that entente cordiale has now been blown apart.
At the heart of the crisis lies the Strait of Hormuz . Iran closed the waterway for the first time in history, trapping nearly a fifth of the world‚Äôs oil and gas supplies inside the Gulf, delivering a devastating shock to the region and the global economy. Even if shipping ultimately resumes, ‚Äãthe unprecedented act marks a historic rupture.
Iran has signalled that it wants to retain leverage over the strait under any future peace deal, floating the idea of controlling traffic and charging vessels a transit fee. U.S. President Donald ‚ÄãTrump has urged Tehran to fully reopen the waterway, and on Sunday said the U.S. Navy would immediately start blockading the strait, raising the stakes after marathon talks with Iran failed to ‚ÅÝreach a deal to end the war .
Yet even a formal reopening would do little to erase the lesson the war has taught Iran‚Äôs neighbours. The threat of Iran closing the strait - now proven feasible with limited military effort - is a genie ‚Äãthat cannot be put back into the bottle.
The war has sharply exposed how vulnerable the region‚Äôs energy infrastructure is.
Fighting between the U.S., Israel and Iran quickly spilled across borders as Iranian missiles and drones struck dozens of targets in neighbouring ‚Äãcountries, including major oil and gas facilities across the Gulf.
Around 11 million barrels per day of oil production and Qatar‚Äôs entire LNG output were forced offline, while refineries, export terminals and other critical installations , opens new tab suffered extensive damage.
For Saudi Arabia, the world‚Äôs largest crude exporter and de facto leader of OPEC, the implications are especially troubling. Even the alternative export route it painstakingly developed in recent decades to bypass Hormuz proved vulnerable.
The kingdom‚Äôs East‚ÄëWest Pipeline, designed to divert roughly 7 million barrels per day from the eastern oil heartland to the Red Sea port of Yanbu, ‚Äãwas hit within hours of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire announcement.
Saudi Arabia, which exported around 8 million bpd of oil before the war, said the attacks had cut its oil production capacity by around 600,000 bpd and reduced throughput on the East‚ÄëWest Pipeline by about ‚Äã700,000 bpd.
At the same time, the UAE‚Äôs oil export pipeline to the port of Fujairah , which sits outside the strait, was also struck repeatedly. And for Qatar and Kuwait, the strait remains the sole export route.
While this situation might look like a checkmate for Tehran, ‚Äåthe Gulf is ‚ÅÝmore apt to see it as an intolerable status quo that will need to be remedied.
This new reality strikes at the core of the Gulf‚Äôs economic model ‚Äì both now and in the future.
For starters, the region faces years of reconstruction and instability that will undermine national development plans, even if the elevated geopolitical risk premium keeps energy prices higher.
The long-term threat is even greater.
The war has jolted many nations - particularly in Asia - into a painful reassessment of energy import dependence. For the Gulf, that comes at the worst possible moment: producers were already facing the need to maximise exports in the face of potentially diminishing demand as major importers shift away from fossil fuels. The conflict will only accelerate it.
For Gulf producers who ‚Äãexpect uninterrupted exports of oil, liquefied natural gas, refined ‚Äãproducts, chemicals and fertilizer to fund their economies for ‚ÅÝdecades to come, uncertainty over Hormuz is simply unsustainable, not only geopolitically but economically.
Consequently, the key regional powers, Saudi Arabia and the UAE - both of which harbour international ambitions economically and geopolitically - are unlikely to accept any strategic reality in which Iran can constrain their goals over the long term. That, in turn, points to a heightened risk of future confrontation.
"The strait ‚Äãmust be open - fully, unconditionally and without restriction. Energy security and global economic stability depend on it. The weaponization of this vital waterway, in any form, cannot stand," ‚ÄãSultan Al Jaber, the CEO of ‚ÅÝUAE state oil giant ADNOC, said last week .
The terms of whatever "permanent" peace deal the parties eventually agree to will matter less than the fact that this war has broken a long‚Äëstanding taboo: targeting vital economic infrastructure in the Gulf is no longer off limits.
That shift alone makes the regional balance of power more volatile, casting a long shadow over the Middle East for years to come.
The rally in global equities and the decline in oil prices following last week's ceasefire announcement suggest investors believe the Middle ‚ÅÝEast will soon ‚Äãreturn to business as usual.
They are almost certainly wrong. The most likely outcome is not a restoration of the old order but a new ‚Äãnormal - one in which Gulf powers seek to ensure Iran can never again wield the Hormuz trump card.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Ron Bousso , a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid ‚Äãdaily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Hungarian voters strengthen Europe‚Äôs liberal case
item.description:
BERLIN, April 13 (Reuters Breakingviews) - The defeat of Viktor Orban in the Hungarian parliamentary election didn‚Äôt come as a total shock ‚Äì weeks of polls had showed his Fidesz party trailing by more than 10 percentage points ‚Äãbehind opponent Peter Magyar‚Äôs Tisza. The surprise came from the magnitude , opens new tab of the latter‚Äôs victory, and the determined rebuke given by Hungarian ‚Äåvoters not only to the man who had dominated Hungary‚Äôs political life for nearly 30 years, but to his allies, U.S. President Donald Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin.
In the short term - outside Hungary, that is - the loudest sighs of relief will be heard in European capitals, and in Ukraine. In Brussels, there is now the clear prospect ‚Äãof a more functional European Union. The type of ‚Äúilliberal democracy‚Äù that Orban had promoted put him in frontal opposition to the rest ‚Äãof Europe, which had sanctioned what it considered breaches of the rule of law by cutting off funds to ‚ÅÝBudapest. Of these, 19 billion euros remain frozen pending the repeal of authoritarian legislation. And Orban‚Äôs Putin-friendly democracy, in its latest incarnation, had been ‚Äãblocking a 90 billion euros loan to Ukraine that is vital to the country‚Äôs survival after the U.S. cut all support last year.
It was not ‚Äãobvious before the vote that Magyar would obtain the two-thirds of parliamentary seats necessary to repeal the laws the EU most strongly objected to - on the judiciary‚Äôs independence, the media, public finances and even the Church. Hungarian voters proved the fears wrong - with 138 seats out of 199, Magyar will have all the space ne needs to de-Orbanise ‚ÄãHungary. He has a definite mandate to implement the pro-European, moderate program he advocated throughout his campaign.
Donald Trump, whose support for Orban has been ‚Äãfrequent and vocal, to the point that he sent his Vice President JD Vance to Budapest a few days before the vote, has lost one his rare friends ‚Äåin Europe. ‚ÅÝAnd Vladimir Putin, with whom Orban had signed a 12-point cooperation agreement , opens new tab only five days ago, will not only lose an ally but see Europe more effective in its support of Ukraine. Hungarian voters also sent him an instructive message: in Europe, even autocrats leave the stage when voters show them the exit sign.
Magyar will not have an easy job - starting with finding the coffers near-empty, after the previous government reached half its deficit target , opens new tab in the ‚Äãfirst two months of the year. But ‚Äãhe will be able to ‚ÅÝcount on the benevolence of his EU counterparts, providing he takes action to undo Orban‚Äôs contentious reforms. At the very least international investors will start eyeing differently a country where corruption and nepotism kept growing in the ‚Äãlast 15 years. And if the new prime minister-elect acts fast, 19 billion euros of frozen EU money ‚Äãwill go a long ‚ÅÝway to jump-start a Hungarian recovery.
Follow Pierre Briancon on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: What does a US naval blockade of Iran mean for oil flows?
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - The U.S. military said it would block shipping traffic in and out of Iran's ports starting ‚Äãat 10 a.m. ET (1400 GMT) on Monday, a move that would prevent roughly two million barrels of Iranian oil a day ‚Äåfrom entering the world's markets, further tightening global supply.
Here are details on the planned blockade and its implications for oil markets.
After weekend peace talks in Islamabad between negotiators from the U.S. and Iran ended without a deal, President Donald Trump said the U.S. Navy "will begin the process of BLOCKADING any and all ships trying to enter, ‚Äãor leave, the Strait of Hormuz."
The U.S. military's Central Command later said the blockade would only apply to ships going to or from Iran, ‚Äãincluding all Iranian ports on the Gulf and Gulf of Oman. U.S. forces would not impede freedom of navigation ‚ÅÝfor vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports and additional information would be provided, it said.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards responded to Trump ‚Äãby warning that military vessels approaching the strait would be considered a ceasefire breach and dealt with harshly and decisively.
Retired Admiral Gary Roughead, a former chief ‚Äãof U.S. naval operations, cautioned that Iran could fire on ships in the Gulf or attack the infrastructure of Gulf states that host U.S. forces.
Blocking Iranian shipments would disconnect a significant source of oil from the world's markets. Iran exported 1.84 million barrels per day (bpd) of crude in March and has shipped 1.71 ‚Äãmillion bpd thus far in April, compared with a full-year average of 1.68 million bpd in 2025, according to Kpler data.
However, a surge in Iranian ‚Äãoutput before the war started on February 28 has led to near-record levels of Iranian oil loaded on ships, with more than 180 million barrels floating as of ‚Äåearlier this ‚ÅÝmonth, according to Kpler data.
Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, which has been severely curtailed by an Iranian blockade since the start of the war, remains nearly halted despite last week's two-week ceasefire agreement between Washington and Tehran.
Oil tankers were steering clear of the strait on Monday.
On Sunday, two Pakistan-flagged tankers, Shalamar and Khairpur, entered the Gulf to load cargoes from the United Arab Emirates and Kuwait; a third ship, ‚Äãthe Liberia-flagged very large crude carrier (VLCC) Mombasa ‚ÄãB, also transited the strait ‚ÅÝearlier on Sunday and was ballasting in the Gulf.
Another VLCC, the Malta-flagged Agios Fanourios I, which tried to pass through the strait on Sunday to load Iraqi crude destined for Vietnam, turned back and was anchored near the Gulf ‚Äãof Oman.
On Saturday, three fully loaded supertankers passed through the Strait of Hormuz in what appeared to be the ‚Äãfirst vessels to ‚ÅÝexit the Gulf since the U.S.-Iran ceasefire deal.
Some 187 laden tankers carrying 172 million barrels of crude oil and refined products were inside the Gulf as of last Tuesday, according to Kpler.
Before the war, most Iranian oil exports were shipped to China, the top global crude importer. Last month, the ‚ÅÝU.S. unveiled ‚Äãa sanctions waiver that has enabled other buyers, including India, to import Iranian oil.
India is set ‚Äãto receive its first crude shipment from Iran in seven years this week, ship tracking data from LSEG and Kpler showed on Wednesday.
Before the war, roughly 20% of global oil and natural ‚Äãgas exports were shipped through the Strait of Hormuz, with most cargoes headed to Asia, the largest importing region.

item.title: Trains through Angola's Lobito critical mineral corridor suspended by floods
item.description:
LUANDA, April 12 (Reuters) - A railway through Angola's Lobito corridor - a vital source of critical ‚Äãminerals like copper and cobalt - was ‚Äåforced to suspend operations on Sunday after nearby rivers burst their banks, causing flooding.
The Lobito ‚ÄãAtlantic Railway said heavy rains flooded ‚Äãbridges over the Halo River, between ‚ÅÝCubal and Caimbambo stations, as well as ‚Äãa bridge over the Cavaco River, near ‚ÄãBenguela.
"As a result, rail traffic on the affected sections is suspended indefinitely," it said in a ‚Äãstatement.
Angola in 2022 granted the Lobito ‚ÄãAtlantic Railway - a consortium of Trafigura, Mota-Engil and ‚ÄåVecturis ‚ÅÝSA - a 30-year concession to operate the rail link and provide a quick route for copper and cobalt exports from ‚Äãthe Democratic ‚ÄãRepublic of ‚ÅÝCongo to the Lobito port on the Atlantic coast.
The trains ‚Äãalso move sulphur in the opposite ‚Äãdirection ‚ÅÝto DRC mines as well as agricultural commodities and industrial products from the port.
Climate ‚ÅÝchange ‚Äãis worsening floods across ‚Äãthe southern Africa region, frequently disrupting transport.

item.title: Turkey concerned about any new Iran-US rules for Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
ANKARA, April 13 (Reuters) - Turkey's foreign minister raised concerns on Monday about Iran or the United States proposing ‚Äãany new regulations for transiting the Strait of Hormuz, and ‚Äåsaid he saw difficulties around proposals to re-open the waterway with an international force.
Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan - who is in close touch with the U.S., Iran and ‚Äãmediator Pakistan - said in comments reported by state-owned Anadolu Agency that ‚ÄãHormuz should be opened through diplomacy, after weekend U.S.-Iran talks failed ‚ÅÝto reach a deal to end the war.
Negotiators should use "convincing methods" ‚Äãin talks with Iran and Hormuz should be opened as soon as ‚Äãpossible, he said, adding that "nobody wants" the strait to become part of the war.
"There are many difficulties to intervening here with an international armed force. Especially as the ‚Äãwar continues, how much will it narrow, expand? We see many countries ‚Äãare not keen on this," Fidan said.
"The problem is whether there will be proposals ‚Äåon ‚ÅÝnew regulations from now on, particularly from Iran," which could decide to be "more active" on the strait, he added.
In retaliation for U.S.-Israeli attacks, Iran effectively closed Hormuz, through which about a fifth of global energy supplies ‚Äãtransit, sending energy ‚Äãprices soaring.
Starting on ‚ÅÝMonday, the U.S. military said it would begin a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports ‚Äãand coastal areas. Washington has sought help to re-open ‚Äãthe strait ‚ÅÝfrom allies, who have not expressed interest .
Fidan said the U.S. and Iran were "sincere" in their ceasefire talks, but added that they could encounter problems if they ‚ÅÝapproached ‚Äãthe issue of Iranian nuclear enrichment from ‚Äãan "all-or-nothing" perspective.
A source said Fidan spoke on Sunday with U.S. and Pakistani officials involved in the ‚Äãtalks.

item.title: Israeli fire kills three in Gaza following new ceasefire talks
item.description:
CAIRO/GAZA, April 13 (Reuters) - An Israeli airstrike killed at ‚Äãleast three Palestinians in the Gaza Strip on Monday, health officials said, as mediators met leaders from ‚ÄåHamas in an effort to shore up a U.S.-brokered ceasefire deal.
Medics said the strike had hit a group of men outside a school in Deir al-Balah in the central Gaza Strip. There was no immediate comment by the Israeli military.
At Al-Aqsa Hospital in Deir al-Balah, the bodies ‚Äãof those killed lay on the ground in white shrouds outside the morgue as relatives and friends arrived ‚Äãto bid them farewell. Some kissed the victims' foreheads before holding special prayers.
‚ÄúThis isn‚Äôt a truce; ‚ÅÝit‚Äôs a trap for our young men. Every day there are martyrs, every single day. How long can this continue?‚Äù ‚Äãsaid Umm Hussam Abu El-Rous, a female relative of one of the victims.
‚ÄúIsn't it unjust that a three-year-old child is afraid ‚Äãof seeing his (dead) father? He says, 'My father went to bring me something from the shop,'" she added.
The ceasefire that began last October halted two years of full-blown war but left Israeli troops in control of a depopulated zone demarcated by yellow-painted blocks that comprises well over half of ‚ÄãGaza, with Hamas in power in a narrow coastal strip and Israeli airstrikes continuing .
More than 750 Palestinians have been killed since ‚Äãthe deal took effect, while militants have killed four Israeli soldiers. Israel and Hamas have traded blame for ceasefire violations.
Palestinians say Israeli forces ‚Äåhave ‚ÅÝbeen moving some of the yellow concrete markers westward. Israel denies this.
The violence comes as leaders from Hamas and other Palestinian factions have been meeting since Saturday in Cairo with mediators from Egypt, Turkey, and Qatar to discuss implementing the second phase of the Gaza deal.
Under a plan put forward by U.S. President Donald Trump's Board of Peace , Hamas would be ‚Äãrequired to lay down its arms ‚Äãin stages over eight months ‚ÅÝafter a U.S.-backed committee of Palestinian technocrats takes control in Gaza.
However, Hamas' disarmament has been a major obstacle to progress on Trump's ceasefire deal and plan for Gaza , which have also ‚Äãbeen put under strain by the war in Iran.
Two officials close to the latest talks ‚Äãsaid Hamas told ‚ÅÝmediators that discussions on disarmament could only move forward after Israel fully implements the first phase of Trump's October deal, which includes a complete ceasefire in Gaza.
Israeli military officials have said they are preparing for a swift return to full-scale war if Hamas does ‚ÅÝnot ‚Äãlay down its weapons.
The Gaza war began on October 7, 2023, with a ‚ÄãHamas-led attack on Israel that killed 1,200 people, according to Israeli tallies.
Israel's ensuing two-year campaign killed more than 72,000 Palestinians, according to Gazan health authorities, ‚Äãand left the territory mostly in ruins.

item.title: Canada's Carney set to win majority government in special elections
item.description:
OTTAWA, April 13 (Reuters) - Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney is likely to secure a parliamentary majority for his Liberal government Monday, a win that would give him the ability to push ‚Äãthrough the legislative agenda he says is needed in an increasingly uncertain and divided geopolitical world.
Three special elections are being held Monday in Ontario and Quebec, ‚Äåwith two in districts - known as ridings - that have long voted Liberal. Winning just one of those seats will give Carney a majority. Polls show they are expected to pick up at least two.
Carney has said that would help him deal more effectively with the trade war with the U.S. started by President Donald Trump. Carney‚Äôs Liberals currently have 171 seats in the 343-seat House of Commons.
With a parliamentary majority, Carney will have a ‚Äãfreer hand to govern - and solidify his grip on leading Canada until at least 2029, when national elections are due to be held next.
‚ÄúHe will be able to pass ‚Äãlegislation without having to go to the opposition to secure enough votes,‚Äù said Andrew McDougall, assistant professor in Canadian politics at the University ‚ÅÝof Toronto. The Liberals have relied on selective support from the Conservatives to pass economic and trade-related legislation in the last year.
McDougall said a majority government would also allow Carney ‚Äãto decide the timing of the next election. Minority governments are at risk of a snap election if they lose a confidence motion and typically last less than two years.
After five opposition ‚Äãlegislators in five months defected to Carney‚Äôs Liberals, he should also have a slightly more comfortable margin than a single seat. Only the governments led by John A. Macdonald, Canada's first prime minister, and Jean Chretien have seen more politicians defect to the ruling party.
On Wednesday, longtime Conservative politician Marilyn Gladu switched parties to join Carney‚Äôs government, saying Canada needs ‚Äúa serious leader who can address the uncertainty that has arrived due ‚Äãto the unjustified American tariffs.‚Äù
Gladu, a former chemical engineer who has previously drawn criticism for promoting unproven scientific treatments during the COVID-19 pandemic, opposing a ban on conversion therapy and ‚Äãsuggesting the military be used to end Indigenous-led protests against oil pipelines, thanked Carney for inviting her into ‚Äúthe large Liberal tent.‚Äù
Carney said Thursday that the core values of the Liberal Party, like inclusivity, ‚Äådiversity, and ‚ÅÝsafeguarding protections within Canada's Charter of Rights and Freedoms, have not changed and that being part of the government caucus means supporting those principles.
Yves-Francois Blanchet, leader of the separatist Bloc Quebecois party, however, said ‚Äúthe ideological differences between the Liberals and Conservatives are getting thinner and thinner by the minute.‚Äù
The Bloc Quebecois is in an extremely tight race with the Liberals in Terrebonne, Quebec. The Liberals won it by just one vote in the last federal election, but the result was overturned by Canada's Supreme Court because of a misprint on a voter's envelope.
The two other seats ‚Äãare in Ontario, including one vacated by former ‚ÄãDeputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland and ‚ÅÝanother previously held by former Liberal lawmaker Bill Blair, who resigned after he was appointed ambassador to the United Kingdom. The Liberals are expected to hold both seats.
‚ÄúWhat we‚Äôre seeing right now is a change of focus for the Liberal party,‚Äù said Laura Stephenson, chair of ‚Äãthe political science department at the University of Western Ontario.
She said that while Carney‚Äôs predecessor, Justin Trudeau, shifted the party to ‚Äãthe left and prioritized issues ‚ÅÝlike reconciliation with Indigenous peoples, rights for minority groups and immigration, there are more pressing matters for Carney, who has been a more centrist leader.
‚ÄúHe is focused on helping Canada survive the economic turmoil, not remaking society,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúWhen we‚Äôre in tough times like this, there are different calculations being made.‚Äù
Recent polling from Nanos shows more than half of Canadians prefer Carney as their ‚ÅÝprime minister, ‚Äãwith just 23% picking Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre. Before Carney became leader of the Liberal Party last year, ‚ÄãPoilievre had been projected to win the next election by more than 20 points.
‚ÄúCarney has done a fairly good job showing Canadians he can handle Trump,‚Äù said McDougall of the University of Toronto. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs shown Canadians he‚Äôs a ‚Äãcompetent manager of the economy and the country,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúAnd so far Canadians have not been overly impressed by the alternatives.‚Äù

item.title: Keiko Fujimori holds lead in Peru's election with 50% of ballots counted
item.description:
LIMA, April 13 (Reuters) - Delays at polling stations ‚Äãand lingering uncertainty over who will reach ‚Äåa runoff marked a second day of voting and vote counting in Peru on Monday, with conservative ‚ÄãKeiko Fujimori clinging to a narrow lead ‚Äãin a crowded presidential race.
Logistical problems tied ‚ÅÝto the distribution of voting materials prompted ‚Äãofficials to extend voting into Monday for tens ‚Äãof thousands of voters who were unable to cast ballots on Sunday after the opening of some polling ‚Äãstations was delayed.
The official count from Peru's ‚Äãelectoral authority, ONPE, showed in early morning hours former congresswoman ‚ÄåFujimori ‚ÅÝleading with about 17% of the vote, followed by right‚Äëwing former Lima mayor Rafael Lopez Aliaga on roughly 15%, and center‚Äëleft candidate ‚ÄãJorge Nieto ‚Äãin third ‚ÅÝplace with around 13%, with 50% of votes counted.
Exit polls on ‚ÄãSunday had placed Fujimori ‚Äì daughter of ‚Äãformer ‚ÅÝPresident Alberto Fujimori, who was imprisoned for human rights abuses ‚Äì in the lead, although Lopez Aliaga ‚ÅÝbriefly ‚Äãmoved ahead earlier in ‚Äãthe official count, underscoring how tight and fluid the race ‚Äãremains.

item.title: Cameroon separatists declare three-day ceasefire for papal visit
item.description:
DAKAR, April 13 (Reuters) - A separatist alliance in Cameroon's English-speaking regions said it would observe a three-day "safe travel passage" later this week to allow ‚Äãcivilians and visitors to move freely during a visit by Pope ‚ÄåLeo.
The pope left on Monday for a tour of four African countries - Algeria, Cameroon, Angola and Equatorial Guinea - aiming to urge global leaders to address the continent's challenges. Africa is home to ‚Äãmore than a fifth of the world's Catholics.
A simmering conflict between secessionist ‚Äãmilitias and government troops has killed thousands in the English-speaking west ‚ÅÝof Cameroon since 2017.
The alliance, which it said brings together the Ambazonia ‚ÄãGoverning Council (AGovC), Ambazonian Prisoners of Conscience and several separatist movements, said the arrangement ‚Äãwould run from April 15 to April 17 across what it described as Ambazonian territory. The pope is expected in the Anglophone city of Bamenda on April 16 as part of ‚Äãhis whirlwind 10-day tour.
In a statement sent to Reuters, the group said ‚Äãits forces had been instructed to ensure a secure environment for civilians, pilgrims, visiting dignitaries, the ‚ÄåHoly ‚ÅÝFather and his entourage.
The alliance described the move as a humanitarian and security measure aimed at safeguarding civilian life and allowing people to participate in the visit without fear, stressing that the papal trip was spiritual in nature.
"This decision ‚Äãreflects a deliberate ‚Äãcommitment to responsibility, ‚ÅÝrestraint, and respect for human dignity, even in the context of ongoing conflict," said Lucas Asu, spokesperson of AGovC, which ‚Äãruns one of the largest militias in western Cameroon ‚Äî the ‚ÄãAmbazonia Defence ‚ÅÝForces.
Cameroon's government in the capital, Yaounde, did not immediately respond to the announcement. Calls to a government spokesperson were not answered.
Separatist leaders also urged all parties to ‚ÅÝrespect ‚Äãthe safe passage period.
However, previous ceasefires and humanitarian ‚Äãpauses announced by Anglophone separatists have not always been observed by all armed factions operating in the ‚ÄãCentral African nation's restive regions.

item.title: South Korean president's Holocaust remarks spark outcry from Israel and controversy at home
item.description:
SEOUL, April 13 (Reuters) - South Korean President ‚ÄãLee Jae Myung has sparked a diplomatic row with Israel and criticism at home after ‚Äåcomparing Israeli military actions against Palestinians to the Holocaust in a post on social media platform X.
The controversy began on Friday after Lee said "wartime killings" by the Israel Defense Forces were "no different from the Jewish massacre" by the Nazis in World War Two, ‚Äãand reposted footage with a caption that said it showed Israeli troops had tortured and thrown a ‚ÄãPalestinian from the roof of a building.
Israel's Foreign Ministry said in a post on ‚ÅÝX on Saturday that Lee "for some strange reason, chose to dig up a story from 2024." It said ‚Äãthe incident occurred during an IDF operation against what it called "terrorists" and had been thoroughly investigated.
The ministry accused ‚ÄãLee, who had said that he needed to verify the footage, of the "trivialization of the massacre of Jews on the eve of Holocaust Remembrance Day in Israel," saying his remarks were "unacceptable and warrant strong condemnation."
Israel marks Yom HaShoah on Monday remembering the ‚Äãsix million Jews murdered by the Nazis.
The Israeli military said in 2024 it was investigating the incident in the ‚Äãvideos and described the actions as serious and not in keeping with its values.
