Friday's comments are a rare instance of Lee discussing ‚Äåinternational ‚ÅÝpolitics on social media and come as his government navigates a surge in energy prices following U.S. and Israeli strikes against Iran. Tehran has closed the Strait of Hormuz to tanker traffic and South Korea is one of the world's largest importers of oil and gas.
Lee did not mention the Iran war in his posts but ‚Äãsaid that South Koreans were ‚Äãtoday feeling "immense pain and ‚ÅÝnational hardship."
The president later on Saturday said it was "disappointing" that Israel criticised his comments and that it was natural to feel sorry if someone was suffering.
South Korea's foreign ministry ‚Äãlater said it was regrettable Israel "misunderstood" Lee's remarks, which were about universal human ‚Äãrights.
Lee's comments also ‚ÅÝproved controversial at home.
South Korea's conservative party hit out at Lee for failing to speak more prudently and said he was showing double standards for his silence on human rights abuses in North Korea, while Lee's Democratic Party praised him ‚ÅÝfor speaking ‚Äãout on the universal value of human dignity.
The mainstream Joongang Ilbo ‚Äãnewspaper said on Monday Lee would be well advised to recognise the weight of a president's remarks and the risk of misunderstanding from unfiltered ‚Äãcomments on social media, especially in sensitive global disputes.

item.title: UK and France to co-host talks this week on possible defensive naval mission for Hormuz
item.description:
PARIS/LONDON April 13 (Reuters) - Britain and France will this week co-host ‚Äãtalks aimed at restoring freedom of navigation in ‚Äåthe Strait of Hormuz, including discussions on what President Emmanuel Macron described as a possible strictly defensive naval mission.
The talks are intended ‚Äãto bring together Britain, France and other countries ‚Äãwilling to join a peaceful multinational mission aimed at ‚ÅÝrestoring freedom of navigation in the strait.
"This strictly defensive ‚Äãmission, which will be separate from the warring parties, is ‚Äãintended to be deployed as soon as the situation allows," Macron said in a post on X.
Tensions in the Gulf have intensified after ‚Äãthe United States said it would begin a blockade of ‚Äãmaritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports, while Iran has effectively shut ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝStrait, a route that carries about a fifth of the world's oil consumption.
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said the continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz was deeply damaging ‚Äãglobal shipping ‚Äãand was adding ‚ÅÝto cost‚Äëof‚Äëliving pressures.
"This week the UK and France will co-host a summit to advance ‚Äãwork on a coordinated, independent, multinational plan to ‚Äãsafeguard ‚ÅÝinternational shipping when the conflict ends," Starmer posted on X.
Britain has previously convened representatives of more than 40 countries that share ‚ÅÝthe ‚Äãaim of restoring freedom of navigation. ‚ÄãThe United States was not involved in those talks.

item.title: ASEAN foreign ministers urge US and Iran to push for permanent resolution
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - ASEAN foreign ministers called on Monday for the United States ‚Äãand Iran to continue negotiating a permanent ‚Äåend to their conflict, and to implement the ceasefire fully and effectively.
The ministers, who met virtually to discuss the war ‚Äãin the Middle East, also called for the restoration of ‚Äãsafe, unimpeded and continuous passage of vessels and ‚ÅÝaircraft in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Kremlin says it hopes for pragmatic ties with new Hungarian leadership after Orban defeat
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 13 (Reuters) - The Kremlin said on Monday it hoped to continue its "highly pragmatic ties" with ‚ÄãHungary's new political leadership after Prime Minister ‚ÄåViktor Orban, a close Russian partner, was beaten at the ballot box by upstart rival Peter Magyar.
Orban, Hungary's veteran nationalist leader, lost power to Magyar ‚Äãand his centre-right Tisza party in an ‚Äãelection on Sunday after 16 years in office - ‚ÅÝa setback for his allies in Russia and ‚ÄãU.S. President Donald Trump's White House.
"Hungary has made its ‚Äãchoice, and we respect that choice," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters.
"We look forward to continuing our highly pragmatic engagement ‚Äãwith Hungary‚Äôs new leadership. We noted Magyar‚Äôs statement ‚Äãregarding his willingness to engage in dialogue. Naturally, this will be ‚Äåbeneficial ‚ÅÝfor both Moscow and Budapest."
Orban, who last visited Moscow in November, blocked a 90-billion euro ($105 billion) loan to Ukraine after accusing it of sabotaging Russian oil ‚Äãdeliveries to ‚Äãhis country, ‚ÅÝsomething Kyiv denied, and delayed and sometimes opposed EU sanctions on Russia.
Hungary has ‚Äãalso continued to buy oil and gas ‚Äãfrom ‚ÅÝRussia despite Moscow's war in Ukraine - which Orban said he did not want his country to be dragged ‚ÅÝinto - ‚Äãand Moscow is building a new ‚Äãnuclear power plant for Hungary.
($1 = 0.8559 euros)

item.title: Hungarians look to changed future after pro-EU Magyar's election landslide
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Hungarians were waking up to a political earthquake on Monday, after a landslide victory for the centre-right ‚Äãopposition reverberated everywhere from Washington to Kyiv, sending local markets surging and turning Budapest into a party zone .
The ballot's implications stretched ‚Äåfar beyond the borders of the landlocked country of under 10 million people, as outgoing nationalist Prime Minister Viktor Orban was a key ally of both U.S. President Donald Trump and Russia's Vladimir Putin, and had frustrated European efforts to aid war-torn Ukraine.
Supporters of election winner Peter Magyar's Tisza party hope he can unblock billions in European Union ‚Äãfunds frozen over concerns about democratic standards, bolster the rule of law and hold those they accuse of wrongdoing under the ‚Äãprevious government to account thanks to his two-thirds supermajority.
"It is an immense honour that you have empowered us ‚ÅÝto form a government with the most votes ever received, and to work for the next four years for a free, European, functioning, and ‚Äãhumane Hungary," Magyar posted on X on Monday.
The Hungarian forint surged 2.5% to a more than four-year high against the euro on Monday, while ‚Äãthe Budapest stock exchange gained almost 3% in anticipation of the EU funds starting to flow.
EU leaders, Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskiy and Trump's Democrat opponents in the United States have all warmly welcomed Magyar's victory. Orban has blocked a 90-billion-euro ($105 billion) loan to war-battered Ukraine.
German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said on Monday that decision-making in the EU, ‚Äãincluding with regard to Russia, would become easier after Magyar's win.
The Kremlin said on Monday Russia respected the choice of Hungarian voters and would continue ‚Äãwhat it called "pragmatic ties" with the new government in Budapest.
In Budapest many residents were recovering from wild celebrations that saw ‚Äåthe streets ‚ÅÝof the capital thronged with revellers well into the early hours.
Among those letting themselves go was Zsolt Hegedus, a leading candidate for health minister, whose dance moves on stage following Magyar's speech went viral online.
Tisza supporters spoke on Monday morning of their optimism about the end of Orban's adversarial relations with Brussels.
"We are part of the European Union and we want to stay within the European Union," said network engineer Gyula Ferenc Teleki.
Magyar ‚Äãhas pledged a sweeping anti-corruption drive, ‚Äãincluding stronger judicial independence and ‚ÅÝtougher public procurement rules to help secure the release of the EU funds.
On Sunday, Magyar called on Hungary's chief prosecutor, the heads of the top court and the media authority, and other officials to resign, saying ‚Äãthe country's public institutions had been captured by Orban loyalists over the past 16 years.
"I cannot believe...," said ‚ÄãPeter, who declined ‚ÅÝto give his surname, his voice cracking with emotion as he spoke about the election result.
"We have so many problems because the Hungarian state is not in good condition, but I think we have many funds from the EU, they will be coming, and maybe the new government has a better ‚ÅÝway to ‚Äãrule this country."
Some conservatives, including a former Polish minister granted asylum in Hungary by Orban, ‚Äãlamented the election result.
"Conservative Poland and Europe have lost a very important bastion," said Marcin Romanowski, who served in Poland's previous nationalist Law and Justice government. He is wanted by ‚ÄãPolish prosecutors on charges of abuse of power.

item.title: Ghana says France is open to engaging on slavery reparations
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Ghana said France was open to having discussions with a coalition of countries that are calling for reparations for transatlantic slavery, following a meeting ‚Äãlast week with President Emmanuel Macron.
Ghana's President John Dramani Mahama, accompanied ‚Äåby foreign minister Samuel Okudzeto Ablakwa and other officials, held talks with Macron in Paris on Wednesday last week.
Ablakwa said on X after the meeting that Macron has indicated ‚ÄãFrance was open to discussions on reparations, including the return of ‚Äãlooted artefacts, addressing global economic inequities and dismantling structural racism.
An official ‚ÅÝfrom √âlys√©e Palace said on Sunday that both countries discussed France's efforts ‚Äãto return culturally significant objects and human remains, as well as the legal ‚Äãframeworks around these restitutions.
The official did not mention the additional measures cited by Ablakwa.
The meeting followed the United Nations' adoption last month of a Ghana-led resolution recognising slavery as the "gravest crime ‚Äãagainst humanity" and calling for reparations. France, along with other European countries, abstained.
France's ‚Äãrepresentative at the U.N. said the abstention was due to concerns that the resolution appeared ‚Äåto "establish ‚ÅÝa hierarchy among crimes against humanity".
Ablakwa said that despite that abstention, Macron had said France was willing to have an "open and honest dialogue" on the matter.
In 2001, France recognised transatlantic slavery as a crime against humanity. But, like most ‚ÄãEuropean nations, it ‚Äãhas not formally ‚ÅÝapologised for its involvement or committed to reparations.
From the 15th to the 19th century, at least 12.5 million Africans were ‚Äãkidnapped and forcibly transported by mostly European ships and ‚Äãsold into ‚ÅÝslavery. France trafficked an estimated 1.3 million people, according to the Slave Voyages database.
Last year, Macron said he would set up a commission to examine France's past with ‚ÅÝHaiti.
Longstanding ‚Äãcalls for reparations have gained momentum worldwide but so ‚Äãhas a backlash , with critics arguing modern states should not be held accountable for historical wrongs.

item.title: Police fire tear gas as workers' protest in India's Noida turns violent
item.description:
NOIDA, India, April 13 (Reuters) - Police lobbed tear gas shells and used "minimum force" on Monday to quell a factory workers' protest in the ‚ÄãIndian national capital's suburb of Noida, which turned violent on its ‚Äåfourth day, with vehicles torched and stones pelted in parts of the satellite city.
Noida is among the largest planned industrial townships of Asia and houses thousands of ‚Äãindustrial units.
Living costs across the world have risen as the ‚ÄãU.S.-Israeli war in Iran curbed fuel supplies. Similar protests in ‚ÅÝthe auto-making state of Haryana last week led to the government ordering ‚Äãa 35% hike in minimum wages.
Vinay Mahoti, 30, who hails from the ‚Äãeastern state of Bihar and works at a hosiery company in Noida, said he initially protested inside his manufacturing unit but later joined workers from other companies who took ‚Äãto the streets.
"Duty hours should be fixed, overtime hours should be ‚Äãpaid, and companies...should adhere to the guidelines laid by the federal government," he said, ‚Äålisting ‚ÅÝhis demands.
Visuals from news agency ANI, in which Reuters has a minority stake, showed dozens of protesters marching on the street and chanting slogans, while security personnel in anti-riot gear looked on.
Other visuals showed an ‚Äãoverturned vehicle with ‚Äãflames rising out ‚ÅÝof it, and protesters hurling stones and trying to break through barricades.
Local police said "minimum force" was being used ‚Äãto maintain law and order, while Narendra Kashyap, ‚Äãa lawmaker ‚ÅÝin Uttar Pradesh state, where Noida is located, called on protesters to discuss their demands with the government.
"Senior police and administrative officials are making ‚ÅÝpersistent efforts ‚Äãto counsel the workers and urge them ‚Äãto maintain peace and restraint," Gautam Budh Nagar police said in a statement.
($1 = 93.3200 Indian ‚Äãrupees)

item.title: Venezuela hopes to lure back international miners, but it's a risky business
item.description:
PUERTO ORDAZ, Venezuela, April 13 (Reuters) - Venezuela's effort to attract foreign investors to develop its gold, iron and bauxite seams has enthusiastic backing from the Trump administration but faces big challenges, including armed groups with deep interests in the chaotic, largely illegal prospecting industry that has developed over the past 20 years, according to nine miners, residents and community activists who spoke ‚Äãto Reuters.
The sources in the sprawling southeastern state of Bolivar said they were skeptical international companies will be able to meaningfully invest without major improvements to security in the state, where local criminals operate alongside Colombian rebels and state security forces ‚Äåhave been accused of colluding with criminals to prop up illegal gold operations.
"The (crime) syndicates control the mines. They‚Äôre the ones who set the rules and enforce the law in many of the mines where we work. Depending on the situation, they impose punishments and can be very violent," said Ines Garcia, a 51‚Äëyear‚Äëold informal miner in El Callao municipality. "You look after yourself, because even talking is a risk.‚Äù
The administration of U.S. President Donald Trump has supported moves by Venezuela's acting president, Delcy Rodriguez, to attract investors since she took power in January, including a mining law passed in April by the ruling party-controlled national assembly, headed by Rodriguez's brother Jorge, which allows foreign and private companies or ‚Äãconsortiums to extract gold and strategic minerals.
Venezuela's oil-dependent economy has for years been battered by hyperinflation, a sustained brain drain, international sanctions, deteriorated oil infrastructure and deep corruption . It desperately needs income to fund Rodriguez's promises to improve public services and wages after Washington removed former president ‚ÄãNicolas Maduro in January. Mining investments would offer another source of taxes, royalties and employment.
U.S. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said during a March visit the law will create opportunities for companies and that Rodriguez will ensure ‚ÅÝtheir security. The U.S. has already issued a license authorizing certain transactions involving Venezuelan-origin gold, including with state-owned mining company Minerven.
‚ÄúYou have guarantees, you have legal certainty, political security, stability and peace of mind so your investments can be developed fully ‚Äî not only in the hydrocarbons sector, where there ‚Äãare many opportunities, but also in the mining sector,‚Äù Rodriguez told visiting investors at a March event. The government has provided no details about its security plans for Bolivar.
Canada-listed Gold Reserve, which has said it intends to resume mining in Venezuela and which was part of the Burgum delegation, was ‚Äãafterward granted a U.S. license allowing it 30 days to negotiate with the government. Gold Reserve did not respond to Reuters questions about whether it was seeking the return of its Brisas mine, which was seized by the government in 2009.
Swiss commodities trader Trafigura is already working with Venezuela's state gold miner Minerven on a responsible sourcing program announced last month and said the work is in compliance with a license issued by the U.S. Treasury. Mining companies Hartree, Peabody Energy, Ivanhoe and TechMet, which were named in local press as having been part of the Burgum delegation, did not respond to questions from Reuters.
Though many international miners have wide experience grappling with security ‚Äãissues, for people who live and work in the so-called Orinoco Mining Arc, a quartet of municipalities in Bolivar designated by Rodriguez's predecessor as strategic for mining development, corporate interest feels like the cart before the horse.
‚ÄúFor real investment to take place it is essential to resolve supply‚Äëchain transparency, ‚Äãsecurity ‚Äî because this is a hot zone with armed groups present ‚Äî and oversight of the socio‚Äëenvironmental impact of operations,‚Äù said an engineer who worked for Minerven for a decade and asked to remain anonymous for fear of reprisals.
Colombia's National Liberation Army (ELN) rebels and former members of the now-demobilized Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (FARC) are ‚Äåactive in Bolivar, the ‚ÅÝsources said, as are local criminal organizations the Tren de Guayana and 3R, along with unnamed groups run by gang leaders who go by the aliases ‚ÄòJuancho‚Äô and ‚ÄòFabio.‚Äô
Criminals have supplied mining machinery, fuel and weapons in the region, as well as extracted gold, for years, said Pedro Yepez, 61, a two-decade veteran of mining in El Callao and Sifontes municipalities. "There is no way they can do that without the government‚Äôs complicity and permission,‚Äù he said.
A community activist who has monitored human rights violations in the area for decades and asked not to be identified out of safety concerns also said armed groups control the mines and operate in complicity with the government. Other residents and community leaders echoed his concerns, with several telling Reuters that criminals regularly extort businesses as the government looks the other way.
The Venezuelan communications ministry, which handles all press queries for the government, did not respond to detailed ‚Äãquestions about the allegations of complicity by the military, security plans for ‚Äãthe region or contacts with potential investors.
The military's 'Operation Roraima,' meant to ‚ÅÝcombat illegal mining in Bolivar, began in 2023 and has continued in the years since, with top leaders publicly celebrating the destruction of illegal mining camps and equipment, including by posting photos of controlled explosions to social media.
The U.S. Department of the Interior did not respond to detailed questions about security concerns in the region.
Both the military and armed groups have been accused of involvement in killings and ‚Äãdisappearances, along with other rights violations in the region, a UN Human Rights Council fact‚Äëfinding mission said in a 2022 report, which also expressed deep concern over worker exploitation, child labor, human trafficking, violations of ‚ÄãIndigenous rights and environmental destruction.
There has been ‚ÅÝa sustained increase in gold flows from Venezuela since Maduro created the Arc in 2016, driven primarily by informal and illegal mining, the Organization for Economic Co‚Äëoperation and Development said in a separate 2021 report. The report highlighted human rights violations, environmental destruction and military collusion with armed groups related to the industry.
The new mining law would provide "a veneer of legality" to "severe environmental degradation and ongoing human rights violations," 16 local non-governmental groups said in an open letter published in late March, adding they were concerned about continued military control.
"Military presence in mines has historically been associated with human rights violations, corruption and the creation of hybrid ‚ÅÝgovernance structures,‚Äù the ‚Äãletter said.
Even if foreign mining companies were able to establish themselves in Bolivar, some activists and residents said they still worry about the impact on communities and what foreign presence ‚Äãwould mean for the livelihoods of small-scale and informal miners, including the region's Indigenous groups.
‚ÄúYou have to put the term ‚Äòbenefit‚Äô in quotation marks ‚Äî who benefits? Because the same state that negotiates and seeks investors forgets all the problems that exist," said Italo Pizarro, an activist from Sifontes municipality, who said Indigenous communities could face particular risks.
A Bolivar geologist with more than ‚Äã40 years of experience in the sector said it would be difficult to oust tens of thousands of informal miners who work in the region, as well as criminals who live off extortion: "This won‚Äôt be simple."

item.title: Morning Bid: Blockade takes its toll
item.description:
April 13 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , Editor-at-Large, Finance and Markets
Oil prices zoomed back above $100 a barrel and global stocks were shaky on Monday as U.S. President Donald Trump sought to blockade traffic to and from Iranian ‚Äãports in the critical Strait of Hormuz after peace talks in Islamabad failed over the weekend.
It‚Äôs not clear whether the failure of ‚Äåthe talks jeopardises the two-week ceasefire announced last week , but Trump‚Äôs attempt to up the ante by sending the U.S. Navy into the Gulf is already starting to unwind some of last week‚Äôs relief rally.
I‚Äôll get into that and more below.
But first, listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast , where I break down the latest twist in the U.S.-Iran ‚Äãwar - and why markets should care about Viktor Orban's election defeat in Hungary.
And finally, don't forget to mark April 23 in your calendar, when ‚ÄãI'll be joining my ROI colleague Jamie McGeever for a timely webinar discussion on rethinking safe-haven assets in uncertain times. Sign ‚ÅÝup here , opens new tab .
Both Brent and WTI crude leapt back above $100 a barrel on Monday as the U.S. Navy prepared its Hormuz blockade, set to come ‚Äãinto effect at 10 a.m. EDT, though the benchmarks remain below last week‚Äôs highs before the ceasefire announcement.
Wall Street futures were down almost 1% before the bell and ‚ÄãEuropean shares slipped , while major Asian indexes closed lower. The dollar advanced against major currencies in early trading but later pared some of those gains.
Brent crude is up some 40% since the conflict started. Perhaps just as worrying for American consumers was President Trump‚Äôs admission on Sunday that gas prices may stay elevated through the midterm elections in November - or rise even more. That ‚Äãacknowledgement suggests the pressure of domestic politics alone is unlikely to secure an early end to the Middle East conflict.
The war's inflation implications became clear last ‚ÄãFriday as U.S. consumer prices increased by the most in nearly four years in March, leaving annual inflation at 3.3% , with gasoline prices accounting for most of the monthly rise.
Elsewhere, ‚ÄåHungary‚Äôs nationalist ‚ÅÝleader Viktor Orban was voted out of office after 16 years in power in a landslide weekend election that‚Äôs set to give winner Peter Magyar‚Äôs party a two-thirds majority in parliament. That will allow Magyar to seek warmer ties with the EU and enact constitutional reforms.
The Hungarian forint surged , as did the country‚Äôs bonds, with some 18 billion euros of frozen EU funds now potentially back on tap.
Finally, the first-quarter U.S. earnings season will kick off in earnest today with Goldman Sachs‚Äô update, while ‚Äãthe IMF and World Bank‚Äôs Spring Meetings ‚Äãare set to begin in Washington.
Chart ‚ÅÝof the day
An estimated 10% of the S&P 500 will have reported first-quarter results by this Friday , with a flood of earnings due in the following weeks. Aside from banks, major company results this week include Netflix, Johnson & Johnson and PepsiCo.
Overall ‚ÄãS&P 500 company earnings are expected to rise by about 14% compared to the year-ago period, according to analyst ‚Äãestimates compiled by LSEG ‚ÅÝIBES as of Friday. Instead of cowering at the prospect of a year-long oil shock, analysts have actually marked up estimates for full-year S&P 500 earnings growth. Whether that gets sideswiped by companies' own guidance now remains to be seen.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. March existing home sales (10 a.m. EDT)
* Fed's Stephen Miran speaks
* World Bank ‚ÅÝand IMF ‚ÄãSpring Meetings begin in Washington
* OPEC Monthly Oil Market Report
* U.S. corporate earnings: Goldman Sachs
Want to ‚Äãreceive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of ‚Äãthe author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Germany plans $1.9 billion of fuel price relief to tackle energy crisis
item.description:
BERLIN, April 13 (Reuters) - Germany's coalition government has agreed fuel price relief for consumers and businesses worth ‚Äå1.6 billion euros ($1.9 billion), ending a dispute over how to respond to an oil price surge triggered by the Iran war .
The energy tax on diesel and petrol will be cut by about 0.17 euros per litre for two months, the conservative CDU party and its ‚Äãcentre-left SPD coalition partners said on Monday.
The Iran war has caused the biggest disruption to global energy supplies ‚Äãon record, with plans for a U.S. blockade of Iranian ports and coastal areas further driving ‚ÅÝup crude prices .
"This war is the real cause of the problems we are experiencing in our own ‚Äãcountry as well," Chancellor Friedrich Merz said at a press conference.
He said the coalition was doing everything possible to ‚Äãcushion the impact of the conflict, which has been put on hold by a fragile ceasefire, and urged oil companies to pass the tax cut on in full. "We expect the oil industry to pass along these relief measures directly and fully to consumers," Merz ‚Äãsaid.
Economists and industry groups were sceptical.
Marcel Fratzscher at economic research institute DIW Berlin said a large share of the ‚Äãtax break could "end up in oil companies' bank accounts" and criticised the measures for failing to encourage fuel-saving.
Germany's filling station operators ‚Äåechoed that ‚ÅÝconcern, calling on the government to impose price controls on oil majors or risk them marking up prices to pocket part of the relief.
"The government needs to be tough on oil majors," a spokesperson told the Rheinische Post newspaper.
The coalition also agreed to let companies pay a 1,000 euro relief bonus per employee, exempt from payroll taxes ‚Äãand social security contributions.
Weekend talks ‚Äãappeared to defuse a dispute ‚ÅÝthat flared on Friday when Economy Minister Katherina Reiche, a Merz ally, criticised proposals from Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil of the SPD to impose a windfall tax on oil companies.
A ‚Äãsource close to Merz told Reuters that Reiche's remarks undermined a push by the ‚Äãchancellor to resolve ‚ÅÝcoalition disagreements quietly.
The government is under pressure to act as Europe's largest economy struggles with weak growth and disruptions from global trade tensions.
Merz said on Monday that Germany would oppose a planned 2027 tightening of European Union CO2 levies on ‚ÅÝhybrid ‚Äãvehicles and would argue in Brussels for a more "technology-open" approach, including recognition ‚Äãof cars running on renewable fuels.
The coalition is also preparing broader income tax cuts for lower- and middle-income groups from January 2027.
($1 = 0.8556 ‚Äãeuros)

item.title: Japan risks summer power crunch due to Middle East LNG disruptions -IEEJ analyst
item.description:
TOKYO, April 13 (Reuters) - Japan may face a power supply crunch if the Middle East crisis drags on and liquefied natural gas shipments remain disrupted, just as ‚Äãair-conditioning demand rises during the Japanese summer, an analyst at a leading ‚Äåenergy think tank said on Monday.
Takafumi Yanagisawa, executive analyst at the Institute of Energy Economics, Japan (IEEJ), said that a prolonged conflict could also delay the start of additional supplies expected by Japan ‚Äãfrom 2028 from new projects in Qatar and the United Arab Emirates.

item.title: Nigeria seeks IMF, World Bank support as Iran shock hits reforms
item.description:
ABUJA, April 13 (Reuters) - Nigeria will seek stronger international financial support at this week's IMF-World Bank ‚ÄåSpring Meetings as the Iran war lifts fuel costs at home and complicates reforms, its finance minister said on Monday.
Surging crude prices had some clear benefits for Africa's ‚Äãtop oil producer, boosting foreign exchange earnings, Wale Edun said in ‚Äãa statement ahead of this week's meetings in Washington.
"But the ‚ÅÝshock comes at a critical transition point, intensifying inflationary pressures and ‚Äãraising living costs for households," he added.
Petrol prices have surged more than ‚Äã50% to 1,330 naira ($0.9788) per litre - and diesel more than 70% to 1,550 naira a litre - since the start of the conflict, hurting people and businesses, Edun said.
The jolting ‚Äãchange threatened to derail reforms launched in 2023 to stabilise the economy ‚Äãand revive growth.
President Bola Tinubu has rolled out Nigeria's most ambitious economic overhaul in ‚Äådecades ‚ÅÝby ending costly fuel and energy subsidies, devaluing the currency and changing the tax system.
At this week's meetings, Edun, who chairs the G24 group of developing countries, said he planned to push for lower borrowing costs, ‚Äãfairer global financial conditions ‚Äãand more ‚ÅÝsupport for countries pursuing reforms.
Nigeria's benchmark Bonny Light crude grade climbed from about $70‚Äì$73 a barrel before the conflict ‚Äãto current levels above $120, the government said.
Inflation eased sharply ‚Äãto 15.06% ‚ÅÝin February from around 33% in December 2024, but remains high compared with regional peers and has come under renewed pressure since the conflict began, ‚ÅÝthe ‚ÄãWorld Bank said last week.
Edun said the ‚Äãgovernment would focus on attracting private investment, creating jobs and sustaining growth, while shielding vulnerable households ‚Äãfrom rising prices.
($1 = 1,358.8200 naira)

item.title: Philippines' Marcos suspends taxes on fuel products
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr suspended taxes on kerosene and liquefied petroleum ‚Äãgas on Monday to help consumers cope with ‚Äårising fuel costs.
Congress had granted Marcos emergency powers to adjust fuel taxes, one of several measures the Philippines has ‚Äãundertaken to cope with a sharp surge ‚Äãin oil prices driven by the Middle East ‚ÅÝconflict.
Marcos said the temporary tax reduction cuts LPG prices by 3.36 ‚ÄãPhilippine pesos ($0.0559) per kilogram and almost 37 Philippine pesos ($0.6154) per tank ‚Äãand 5.60 Philippine pesos ($0.0931) per liter, lowering costs for households that rely on it for lighting and cooking.
Marcos did not ‚Äãsay how long the suspension will last, but ‚Äãan interagency committee tasked with leading the government's response to ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝenergy crisis will meet on April 14 to discuss whether to suspend, reduce, or maintain excise taxes on gasoline and diesel.
Diesel and gasoline prices in the ‚ÄãPhilippines have ‚Äãmore than ‚ÅÝdoubled since the start of the Middle East conflict on February 28, as ‚Äãthe country, which relies heavily on imported ‚Äãfuel, ‚ÅÝfaces supply pressures amid a global price surge.
The Philippines' central bank warned last week of "spillover effects" from the Middle ‚ÅÝEast ‚Äãconflict after headline inflation in ‚ÄãMarch hit 4.1% on an annual basis.
($1 = 60.1270 Philippine pesos)

item.title: EU should reconsider its plans to ban imports of Russian gas, Eni CEO says
item.description:
MILAN, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Union should reconsider its plans to ‚Äãprogressively ban imports of Russian ‚Äågas from the start of 2027, Claudio Descalzi, the CEO of Italian ‚Äãenergy group Eni (ENI.MI) , opens new tab said.
In ‚Äãan address to an event organised ‚ÅÝby the co-ruling League ‚Äãparty on Sunday, Descalzi was quoted ‚Äãby Italian news agencies as saying it was unclear how the bloc could ‚Äãreplace 20 billion cubic ‚Äãmetres of gas from Russia, which provides ‚Äåvital ‚ÅÝflexibility to the output of power stations.
Russia is facing a European ban on Russian LNG imports ‚Äãon short-term ‚Äãcontracts ‚ÅÝfrom April 25 and on long-term contracts from ‚ÄãJanuary 1, 2027.
Descalzi said ‚Äãthat ‚ÅÝin terms of the disruption of energy supplies, the conflict in ‚ÅÝIran ‚Äãis "the most important ‚Äãevent of the last 40 years."

item.title: India's HPCL issues rare tender seeking tanker to load Russian LPG, document shows
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - India's state-run Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab has issued a rare ‚Äãtender seeking a liquefied petroleum gas tanker ‚Äåto immediately load propane and butane from Russia's Ust-Luga port, a tender document seen by Reuters ‚Äãon Monday showed.
The vessel, to be loaded ‚Äãwith 12,000 metric tons of butane and ‚ÅÝ8,000 tons of propane for discharge ‚Äãon India's west coast, must not be under ‚Äãsanctions and must have no links to Iran, the document said.
HPCL did not immediately respond to a ‚Äãrequest for comment.
Indian state refiners are seeking ‚Äãto buy LPG, used as cooking gas, from more diversified ‚Äåsources ‚ÅÝincluding Russia as India faces its worst LPG supply crisis in decades.
Supplies from the Middle East have been disrupted by the ‚Äãclosure of the ‚ÄãStrait of ‚ÅÝHormuz following the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
Indian authorities have previously said they ‚Äãare purchasing LPG from countries including ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝU.S., Norway, Canada, and Russia.
India consumed 33.15 million tons of LPG last year, with imports ‚ÅÝaccounting ‚Äãfor about 60% of ‚Äãdemand. About 90% of those imports came from the Middle ‚ÄãEast.

item.title: CPC raises oil exports in March by 48% from February, sources say
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 13 (Reuters) - The Caspian Pipeline Consortium (CPC), which supplies oil from Kazakhstan and Russia, increased crude exports by 48% in March, two industry ‚Äãsources said on Monday, buoyed by a recovery ‚Äãin output at the Tengiz oilfield.
Chevron-led (CVX.N) , opens new tab Tengiz is the ‚ÅÝlargest single field in Kazakhstan, a country that produces ‚Äã2% of the world's daily supply of crude oil, mainly ‚Äãexported via CPC to the Russian port of Novorossiysk.
The field, operated by Tengizchevroil, went offline in January after power outages were reported ‚Äãat the site near the Caspian Sea in western ‚ÄãKazakhstan.
Tengizchevroil's general director, William Lacobie, said last week that the field had returned ‚Äåto ‚ÅÝfull production.
The sources said that CPC, which pumps oil via the Tengiz-to-Novorossiysk pipeline, increased oil exports last month to 6.196 million metric tons, or 1.58 million barrels per day. ‚ÄãThat compared with ‚ÄãFebruary's 3.79 ‚ÅÝmillion tons, or 1.07 million bpd.
CPC declined to comment.
Challenges facing CPC have included Ukrainian ‚Äãdrone attacks on the Russian terminal. It had ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝhalt its operations for a few days after Ukraine attacked its facilities at the end of November, effectively destroying one of ‚ÅÝthree ‚Äãfloating moorings.
Kazakhstan's energy ministry said on ‚ÄãTuesday that oil exports via CPC were stable after Russia's military had accused Ukraine ‚Äãof damaging its loading facilities.

item.title: Germany details fuel relief, tax cuts, EU auto policy response
item.description:
BERLIN, April 13 - Germany's coalition government on Monday announced a package of measures to cut fuel costs, allow a tax-free worker bonus, set a timeline for overhauling the statutory health insurance system and ‚Äãpush changes to EU car emissions rules.
The following are key points from the coalition ‚Äåpaper:
- Cut Germany's energy tax on diesel and petrol by about 0.17 euros per litre for two months, relief worth around 1.6 billion euros ($1.87 billion). Cost to be offset by competition- or tax-based measures targeting oil ‚Äãcompanies.
- Tougher antitrust: expand powers of Germany's federal Bundeskartellamt competition watchdog to collect data ‚Äãacross the motor-fuel supply chain and pursue remedies, including clawing back excess profits after sector ‚ÅÝprobes, to ensure wholesale price drops are passed on to consumers.
- Longer term: expand domestic energy ‚Äãsupply, including by tapping selected German gas fields, accelerating renewables and strengthening cross-border power grid links.
- 2026: Employers may pay a one-off, tax- and social-contribution-free bonus of 1,000 euros. Lost tax revenue to be offset by a tobacco tax hike this year.
- 2027: Major overhaul of income tax to permanently reduce the burden ‚Äãon lower- and middle-income households.
Germany's statutory health insurance system, covering most residents and backed ‚Äãby payroll contributions from employers and employees, faces a funding gap that could reach 40 billion euros in 2030.
- ‚ÄåGoal: curb ‚ÅÝspending growth, align outlay with revenue and stabilise contribution rates, with all stakeholders required to contribute.
- Cabinet to approve a draft law on April 29, with passage targeted before the summer parliamentary recess.
The European Commission has opened a new lawmaking process. Germany is responding ‚Äãto the Commission's draft:
- ‚ÄãGermany supports a "technology‚Äëneutral" approach, ‚ÅÝwhich would still allow new combustion‚Äëengine cars to be registered after 2035 if they meet EU rules.
- Germany will seek to pause the tougher ‚Äã2027 formula for plug‚Äëin hybrids ("Utility Factor"). It will push for vehicles that ‚Äãrun exclusively ‚ÅÝon renewable fuels, including advanced biofuels, to be credited as zero‚Äëemission without delay.
- Germany rejects the Commission's proposed bonus compliance credits for small electric vehicles and any size-based preferential treatment.
- "Banking and borrowing": Germany backs letting carmakers ‚ÅÝcarry ‚Äãover excess CO2 reductions or bring some forward in the ‚Äã2025‚Äì2029 and 2030‚Äì2034 periods, with three‚Äëyear compliance periods for the 2030 and 2035 targets to allow flexible compliance and avoid ‚Äãpenalties.
($1 = 0.8550 euros)

item.title: India's top solar state has renewable projects of about 60 GW awaiting transmission links
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - India's top solar energy-generating state of Rajasthan has clean energy projects of capacity amounting to ‚Äãabout 60 gigawatt (GW) awaiting transmission links as planners struggle ‚Äåto keep pace with a rapid build-out, a regulatory filing showed.
The problem underscores a critical challenge for India in its effort to nearly double its ‚Äãnon-fossil based power generation to 500 GW by 2030 ‚Äãas the systems carry electricity to other states from ‚ÅÝrenewable‚Äërich regions such as Rajasthan.
Planner the Central Transmission Utility of India ‚ÄãLtd (CTUIL), told the national power regulator it had been unable to ‚Äãprovide a transmission system for about 60 GW of projects.
The western desert state has 179 GW of renewable energy potential, with more than 85% of ‚Äãprojects clustered in its four districts of Barmer, Bikaner, Jaisalmer ‚Äãand Jodhpur, the April 10 filing showed.
Applications for grid connectivity totalling about 130 ‚ÄåGW ‚ÅÝhave been received in Rajasthan, while transmission systems for only about 73 GW have been planned or are being set up, it added.
"CTUIL is facing challenges and difficulties in identification of corresponding transmission ‚Äãsystem for 60 ‚ÄãGW applications," ‚ÅÝthe planner said.
The issue was highlighted after electricity regulators told Saurya Urja Company of Rajasthan Ltd, which ‚Äãis developing a 400-megawatt solar park in Bikaner, ‚Äãit ‚ÅÝcould withdraw its connectivity application and recover bank guarantees if needed.
The ruling by the Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (CERC) came in response to challenges ‚ÅÝin ‚Äãplanning transmission the company faced.
The regulator also ‚Äãasked the transmission planner to advise project applicants about the transmission delays, allowing them ‚Äãto withdraw connectivity applications.

item.title: Europe's biggest pension investor eyes private markets boost
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - Europe's biggest pension investor, APG, will increase its allocation to private markets to ‚Äãjust over 30% and sees current credit market flux as a potential buying opportunity, its chief investment ‚Äåofficer for private investments told Reuters.
APG invests around 600 billion euros ($702.00 billion) for clients including ABP, the Netherlands' biggest pension fund. Around 26% of its assets are currently in private markets but it would add more after ongoing changes to investment rules in the Netherlands, Patrick ‚ÄãKanters said.
The new rules under the Future Pensions Act, introduced in phases since 2023, free Netherlands-based funds ‚Äãfrom committing to a defined retirement payout for workers and allow more risk to be taken, ‚ÅÝincluding reducing the money kept in lower-yielding but liquid government debt.
With people living longer and having more jobs over ‚Äãtheir lifetime, the new system gives younger workers their own pot of money which can potentially grow much more quickly.
Currently, ‚ÄãAPG has around 10% of its total assets in real estate; 5-6% in infrastructure, which would rise to 10% over time; 8% in private equity, up from 6% historically; and a sub-1% allocation to natural capital assets such as forestry.
It also has a "rather small" holding of ‚Äã1.5% in private debt which could rise to between 2% and 4% over time, depending on the client. Based on ‚Äãits current assets, that could mean its allocation rising closer to 24 billion euros from around 9 billion euros.
Large Dutch funds are ‚Äåbeginning to ‚ÅÝtransfer clients' money to the new pots this year and all Dutch pension funds have until January 1, 2028 to complete the transition.
The move comes as the broader market faces increased volatility after several U.S. retail-focused funds were hit by a surge in redemption requests amid concerns around falling returns and the impact of AI on software firms.
"Some sub-markets are ‚Äãcorrecting, and that can indeed ‚Äãprovide opportunities going forward," Kanters ‚ÅÝsaid in an interview this month. "For these types of investments, you need to have a very long investment horizon."
For APG, the focus was on investing where capital was scarce, structures ‚Äãwere robust and underwriting discipline was strong, including in real assets and infrastructure‚Äërelated financing, ‚ÄãKanters said.
"Ultimately, manager ‚ÅÝquality, deal structuring, and downside protection matter more to us than making thematic sector calls."
APG's existing private debt investments cover areas including real asset credit, speciality finance, structured credit, direct lending and non-performing loans. Around 60% is in Europe against a market ‚ÅÝaverage allocation ‚Äãof around 30%.
"The U.S. remains the largest and most established private debt ‚Äãmarket globally. For a long‚Äëterm investor like us looking to build a larger and diversified portfolio, it is difficult to ignore that depth and breadth," ‚ÄãKanters said.
Asia also presented attractive returns and high‚Äëquality managers, he said.
($1 = 0.8547 euros)

item.title: Islamist militants raid Nigerian army base, kill commander and six soldiers
item.description:
MAIDUGURI, Nigeria, April 13 (Reuters) - Islamist militants riding motorbikes stormed an army base near Nigeria's northeastern border ‚Äãwith Chad, killing the commander and six ‚Äåother soldiers late on Sunday, two military sources said.
The assault was by members of Boko Haram and its splinter ‚Äãgroup Islamic State West Africa Province, the sources ‚Äãtold Reuters, and came days after a string ‚ÅÝof coordinated attacks on military positions in other parts ‚Äãof Borno State.
Insurgents have ramped up attacks on overstretched ‚Äãbases and looted weapons across the region, piling pressure on the government and President Bola Tinubu who has vowed to end ‚Äãyears of violence.
Those killed late on Sunday included ‚Äãthe base commander who was weeks from retirement and six ‚Äåother ‚ÅÝsoldiers whose vehicle struck an improvised mine while heading to reinforce troops during the attack, the sources said.
At least 12 militants were killed in a gunfight ‚Äãduring the attack ‚Äãon the ‚ÅÝ242 Battalion barracks in Monguno, the sources added, requesting anonymity because they were ‚Äãnot authorised to speak to the ‚Äãmedia.
Militants have ‚ÅÝwaged a 17-year insurgency seeking to carve out an Islamic state in northeast Nigeria, killing thousands and ‚ÅÝdisplacing ‚Äãat least 2 million people, aid ‚Äãgroups say, despite major military campaigns.

item.title: Social media platforms need to stop never-ending scrolling, UK's Starmer says
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - Social media platforms such as Instagram and TikTok need to take action to stop young users wasting hours ‚Äãmindlessly scrolling never-ending videos, British Prime Minister Keir ‚ÄåStarmer said on Monday.
Britain, like other countries , is considering restricting access to social media for children and it is testing bans, curfews and app time ‚Äãlimits to see how they impact sleep, family life ‚Äãand schoolwork.
"We're consulting on whether there should be a ‚ÅÝban for under 16s," Starmer told BBC Radio. "But I think ‚Äãequally important, the addictive scrolling mechanisms are really problematic to ‚Äãmy mind. They need to go."
Social media companies had designed algorithms that were intended to encourage addictive behaviour, and parents were asking the government ‚Äãto intervene, Starmer said.
Australia in December became the world's first ‚Äãcountry to ban social media for children under 16, with Greece and ‚ÄåIndonesia ‚ÅÝalso enacting similar bans.
More than 45,000 people had already responded to its consultation on children's online safety, the UK government said, adding that there was still time to contribute before a ‚Äãdeadline of May ‚Äã26.
"We want ‚ÅÝto hear from mums and dads who are worried about the amount of time their ‚Äãchildren spend online and what they are viewing," ‚ÄãTechnology Secretary ‚ÅÝLiz Kendall said on Monday.
"We want to hear from teenagers who know better than anyone what it is like to grow ‚ÅÝup ‚Äãin the age of social media. ‚ÄãAnd we want to hear from families about their views on curfews, AI ‚Äãchatbots and addictive features."

item.title: US military to enforce blockade in Gulf of Oman, Arabian Sea, note to seafarers
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - The U.S. military will enforce a ‚Äãblockade in the Gulf of Oman ‚Äåand Arabian Sea east of the Strait of Hormuz and it will apply to all vessel ‚Äãtraffic regardless of flag, the U.S. Central ‚ÄãCommand said in a note to ‚ÅÝseafarers seen by Reuters on Monday.
The note ‚Äãsaid the blockade would come into effect at ‚Äã1400 GMT on Monday.
"Any vessel entering or departing the blockaded area without authorization is subject to interception, ‚Äãdiversion, and capture," the note said.
"The ‚Äãblockade will not impede neutral transit passage through the ‚ÄåStrait ‚ÅÝof Hormuz to or from non-Iranian destinations."
The blockade "encompasses the entirety of the Iranian coastline to include but not limited to ports and ‚Äãoil terminals", ‚Äãthe note ‚ÅÝsaid, adding that humanitarian shipments including food, medical supplies, and other ‚Äãessential goods would be permitted, subject ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝinspection.
Tehran has threatened to retaliate against ports of its Gulf neighbours, after weekend talks failed to ‚ÅÝreach ‚Äãa deal to end the ‚Äãwar, leaving a ceasefire in jeopardy.

item.title: Iran in touch with India on ships passage through Strait of Hormuz, Iranian envoy says
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - Iran has "good contact" ‚Äãwith the Indian ‚Äågovernment on passage of the South ‚ÄãAsian nation's ships ‚Äãthrough the Strait of ‚ÅÝHormuz and ‚Äãwants to help New ‚ÄãDelhi, Iranian Ambassador to India Mohammad Fathali ‚Äãsaid on Monday.
The ‚ÄãU.S.-Israeli war on Iran, ‚Äåwhich ‚ÅÝbegan in February, has restricted shipping through the key ‚Äãroute - ‚Äãa ‚ÅÝconduit for 40% of India's ‚Äãcrude imports - ‚Äãimpacting ‚ÅÝtrade and squeezing oil supplies.

item.title: Russia calls for joint food reserves with BRICS to counter Middle East crisis risks
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 13 (Reuters) - Russia, the world's largest wheat exporter, should create joint food reserves with ‚Äãfellow BRICS members and former Soviet neighbours to counter the risks to global food security ‚Äåstemming from the conflict in the Middle East , a senior Russian security official said on Monday.
About half of the world's food is grown using fertilizer, while one-third of global fertilizer trade used to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow ‚Äãshipping lane along Iran's coast that has been largely closed since the conflict began.
"To ensure food ‚Äãsecurity, it is highly important to expand cooperation with friendly countries, primarily the ‚ÅÝmember states of the Eurasian Economic Union and BRICS, including through the creation of joint food reserves," ‚ÄãAlexander Maslennikov, deputy secretary of Russia's Security Council, was quoted as saying by domestic news agencies.
Chaired by ‚ÄãPresident Vladimir Putin, the Security Council includes top officials and helps shape Kremlin decisions on major national security issues. Putin is due to meet BRICS member Indonesia's President Prabowo Subianto in the Kremlin on Monday with food security likely to feature ‚Äãon the agenda.
Maslennikov said the Middle East crisis posed serious risks to global food security. If the ‚Äãglobal fertilizer shortage persists until early summer, yields of major crops could fall by half, he said, fuelling the sharpest ‚Äårise ‚ÅÝin world food inflation in recent years.
He added that the number of hungry people worldwide could rise to a record 673 million.
The World Bank, International Monetary Fund and the U.N. World Food Programme warned last week that sharp increases in oil, natural gas and fertilizer prices triggered by the war in the Middle East ‚Äãwill inevitably cause rising ‚Äãfood prices and food ‚ÅÝinsecurity.
Russia is a major producer and exporter of fertilizer but lacks the capacity to significantly increase output this year. It is also seeking to raise agricultural ‚Äãexports by half by 2030.
Maslennikov said the current situation, while posing risks to ‚ÄãRussia's own ‚ÅÝfood security, also created long-term opportunities for the country's agricultural producers.
"Russia is in a strong position to increase food exports to the countries of the Middle East, as well as to Asia, Africa and Latin America," he ‚ÅÝsaid.
Egypt, ‚Äãa BRICS member, is the largest importer of Russian wheat, ‚Äãwhile Russia also exports food to China and India, the bloc's two biggest economies. The Eurasian Economic Union, led by Russia, also ‚Äãincludes grain exporter Kazakhstan, as well as Belarus, Armenia and Kyrgyzstan.

item.title: Turkey says NATO should reset ties with Trump at next summit, prepare for future
item.description:
ANKARA, April 13 (Reuters) - Turkey said on Monday that NATO allies should use their July summit in Ankara to reset ties with U.S. ‚ÄãPresident Donald Trump and prepare for a potential reduction of ‚ÄåU.S. involvement in the alliance.
Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said that Turkey believed Trump would attend the NATO leaders' summit on July 7-8 due to his "personal ‚Äãrespect" for President Tayyip Erdogan, but added he understood Trump ‚Äãwas otherwise reluctant come to the meeting.
Trump has criticised NATO ‚ÅÝfor years and last week threatened to pull the United States ‚Äãout of the alliance over European members' refusal to send ships to ‚Äãunblock the Strait of Hormuz near Iran. That compounded friction within the bloc over his earlier plans to acquire Greenland.
Fidan told the state-owned Anadolu news ‚Äãagency that allies had long considered Trump's criticisms to be ‚Äãrhetoric, but were now planning around the possibility of reduced U.S. involvement and ramping ‚Äåup ‚ÅÝtheir own defensive capacities.
"NATO countries need to turn this Ankara Summit into an opportunity to put ties with the United States on a systematic basis," he said.
"If there will be a U.S. withdrawal ‚Äãfrom some NATO ‚Äãmechanisms, there needs ‚ÅÝto be a plan and programme to phase this out so nobody is left in the open," ‚Äãhe added.
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte has said ‚Äãhe understood ‚ÅÝTrump's frustrations with the alliance, but that the "large majority of European nations" had been helpful to Washington's war effort in Iran.
A senior White ‚ÅÝHouse ‚Äãofficial told Reuters last week that Trump, ‚Äãas part of frustration with NATO, had also considered the option of removing some U.S. ‚Äãtroops from Europe.

item.title: Southport girls' murders should have been prevented, UK inquiry says
item.description:
LIVERPOOL, England, April 13 (Reuters) - The murders of three young girls at a Taylor Swift-themed dance event in Britain in 2024 should have been prevented, ‚Äãbut there was a "fundamental failure" by state bodies to recognise ‚Äåthe risk the killer posed, a public inquiry concluded on Monday.
Teenager Axel Rudakubana launched the frenzied knife attack at the summer vacation event in Southport, northern England, ‚Äãon July 29, 2024, in what Prime Minister Keir Starmer ‚Äãcalled "a devastating moment" in British history.
He was jailed for at ‚ÅÝleast 52 years after he admitted the killings shortly before his trial ‚Äãlast year.
Inquiry chair Adrian Fulford said there was a failure by ‚Äãagencies ‚Äì including the police, the counter-radicalisation scheme Prevent and social services ‚Äì to "take ownership of the risk" Rudakubana posed.
"This failure lies at the heart of why (Rudakubana) was able to ‚Äãmount the attack, despite so many warning signs of his capacity ‚Äãfor fatal violence," he added.
Rudakubana was referred to Prevent three times, firstly in December ‚Äå2019 ‚ÅÝafter taking a knife to school and searching online for school shootings, but no further action was taken.
Rudakubana attended his previous school later in December 2019 with a hockey stick and a knife, which Fulford ‚Äãdescribed as "a watershed ‚Äãevent".
"If appropriate ‚ÅÝarrangements and reasonable resources had been in place to address the risk that (Rudakubana) posed to others from December ‚Äã2019 onwards, it is highly likely that the ‚Äãtragedy ... would ‚ÅÝnot have occurred," Fulford said.
He also said Rudakubana's parents "bear significant responsibility" for failing to alert the authorities to the risk their son posed.
Rudakubana, then ‚ÅÝ17, ‚Äãkilled Bebe King, six, Elsie Dot Stancombe, ‚Äãseven, and Alice Dasilva Aguiar, nine, and wounded another 10 in an attack which was ‚Äãfollowed by days of nationwide rioting .

item.title: EU member states must coordinate on energy prices amid Iran conflict, von der Leyen says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Union's member states must coordinate on energy prices amid a ‚Äã22 billion euro ($25.70 billion) increase in fossil fuel ‚Äåbills since the start of the Iran war, EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said on Monday.
"We're also looking into ... coordination ‚Äãof member states' gas storage filling to avoid ‚Äãthat many member states go to the market ‚ÅÝat the same time," von der Leyen told reporters ‚Äãin Brussels.
"And we will coordinate oil stock releases, to achieve ‚Äãthe largest possible effect, and we will ensure that member states' emergency measures will not impact the single market."
The EU Commission is planning ‚Äãto publish proposals for energy price measures on April ‚Äã22, to be discussed by EU leaders at their informal summit ‚Äånext ‚ÅÝweek.
Separately the EU's executive arm will present an electrification strategy before the summer, von der Leyen said as she stressed the need for structural measures to lower energy prices ‚Äãas well.
"We ‚Äãare paying ‚ÅÝa very high price for our global dependency on fossil fuels, and the grim reality ‚Äãfor our continent is fossil fuel energy ‚Äãwill ‚ÅÝremain the most expensive option in the years to come," von der Leyen said.
"Our strategy to decarbonize has not only ‚ÅÝbeen ‚Äãconfirmed in the last years, but ‚Äãis growing in importance day by day," she added.
($1 = 0.8559 euros)

item.title: Physical oil in Europe hits record high near $150 a barrel as Hormuz crisis worsens
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 - European crude oil prices climbed to a record high near $150 a barrel on Monday as the U.S. plan to blockade ‚Äãthe Strait of Hormuz added to concern about ‚Äãtight supplies.
The price of Brent crude futures ‚ÅÝfor June delivery LCOc1 , opens new tab rose 6% to more than $100 a barrel as ‚Äãthe U.S. Navy prepared to block ships to and ‚Äãfrom Iran via the Strait of Hormuz in a move that could restrict Iranian oil exports after Washington and Tehran failed to reach ‚Äãa deal to end the war.
This is far ‚Äãshort of Brent's all-time high of $147 a barrel set in 2008.
However, ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝprice of physical crude cargoes for immediate delivery is significantly higher as the effective closure of Hormuz has sent buyers in Europe and Asia scrambling ‚Äãto secure supplies. ‚ÄãThe outright ‚ÅÝprice of North Sea Forties crude reached $148.87 a barrel on Monday, LSEG data ‚Äãshowed, exceeding its 2008 peak.
Repsol CEO ‚ÄãJosu ‚ÅÝJon Imaz commented on the premium at which physical cargoes are trading to the prices fetched on ‚ÅÝfinancial markets ‚Äãat an event on Monday.
"Physical ‚Äãtransactions are under a lot of strain," he said.

item.title: How the AI boom derailed clean‚Äëair efforts in one of America's most polluted cities
item.description:
April 10 (Reuters) - Barbara Johnson has been fighting coal pollution for decades in her mostly Black neighborhood of North St. Louis as an organizer with Metropolitan Congregations United ‚Äì one of many activist groups campaigning for cleaner air in a city that has some of the country‚Äôs dirtiest.
Until recently, Johnson had reason to believe things would improve: tougher federal soot standards adopted in 2024 under the Biden administration were scheduled to go into effect in 2027, requiring plants to ‚Äãslash emissions or shut down. That would have forced one of the area‚Äôs biggest polluters - Ameren‚Äôs Labadie Energy Center power plant - to cut its soot emissions in half to stay in business.
Johnson‚Äôs hopes vanished in February, however, when President Donald Trump‚Äôs administration scrapped the standards before they took effect as part of broader efforts to ‚Äåensure the nation‚Äôs grid can meet surging demand from data centers. Now she wonders if she‚Äôll ever get to see the changes she‚Äôs been fighting for since her youth.
"You take two steps forward and four steps back," said Johnson, 75. "I am used to that backwards trend but how many generations will it take to make those positive changes stick?"
Trump‚Äôs rollbacks in support of AI mark a reversal in U.S. environmental policy and a painful truth for America's clean air activists: After years pushing coal toward the exits, the rise of power-hungry data centers has nudged the country's most polluting power source back to the stage.
Trump last year issued an executive order entitled "Reinvigorating America‚Äôs Beautiful Clean Coal Industry" , opens new tab that said coal-fired power was crucial to meeting the rise in U.S. electricity demand driven by the construction of artificial intelligence data processing centers. He has since provided funding to keep old plants running, issued orders to delay plant retirements, and rolled ‚Äãback environmental regulations on mercury and other toxins to free plants from costly upgrades.
"Ensuring affordable baseload power, including coal, is essential for keeping the lights on and heating American homes," the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency said in an emailed statement about the regulatory rollbacks. "EPA is committed to ensuring clean air for all Americans regardless of race, gender, creed, or background."
The U.S. ‚ÄãDepartment of Energy estimates artificial intelligence and data‚Äëcenter growth will create 50 gigawatts of new electricity demand by 2030 ‚Äì a nearly 4% increase over the 1,300 gigawatts produced by all U.S. power plants in 2025.
Reuters interviewed 20 air quality activists and health advocates for this story and found ‚ÅÝall had identified the AI boom - and the policies supporting it - as the biggest potential threat to U.S. air quality due to its need for power, including from dirty sources like coal.
Over the past decade, the number of U.S. coal plants providing energy to the grid and other industrial operations dropped to about 200 from nearly 400 in 2015, according to EPA data examined by Reuters. But ‚Äãthat pace has slowed fast .
In 2025 only four plants producing 2.6 gigawatts were retired, compared with 94 producing 15 gigawatts in 2015, as the DOE issued emergency orders keeping them online, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration.
A coalition of farmers, environmentalists and homeowners have united to resist data-center expansion out of concern for its impacts, ranging from higher power bills to reduced water supplies - a potential liability for Republicans ‚Äãin the November midterm elections.
Trump has since secured voluntary agreements from big tech companies to pay up for their power needs and shield American consumers from higher bills, but his administration has not announced steps to address the health effects of higher pollution from expanded power generation.
St. Louis will be among the U.S. cities most impacted by the regulatory rollbacks, mainly because of its already poor air quality and the close proximity of the huge Labadie plant, according to the interviews, and government data reviewed by Reuters.
Last year, metro St. Louis residents had "good" air to breathe during only one-third of the days of the year, according to the standards used by the EPA‚Äôs Air Quality Index. That ranked St. Louis 475th in air quality out of 501 small and large U.S. metro areas.
The Labadie Energy Center is a significant contributor, according to EPA data and recent scientific studies , opens new tab .
The plant, a sprawling facility that sits around 40 miles ‚Äãto the city‚Äôs west, produces the highest combined total of sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides among U.S. coal plants, and also emits soot at a rate that is two to three times that of nearly every other U.S. coal plant, according to EPA data.
That pollution drives an estimated economic burden of up to $5.5 billion each year, with about $820 million of those costs borne by St. Louis area ‚Äãresidents, according to a Reuters analysis of the EPA‚Äôs Co-Benefits Risk Assessment (COBRA) tool.
COBRA estimates health costs such as emergency room visits and measures how much people, collectively, are willing to pay for cleaner air because it lowers the risk of premature death.
Reuters showed the analysis to two outside experts - Bryan Hubbel, a senior fellow at non-profit research group Resources for the Future and John Graham, a senior scientist at the environmental research ‚Äågroup Clean Air Task Force - ‚ÅÝwho both agreed with the figures.
Labadie‚Äôs owner, St. Louis-based utility Ameren Corp , did not contest the Reuters analysis of the EPA data.
Ameren said the plant operates within the existing federal pollution limits. Labadie will keep running for at least another decade to ensure reliable power supplies for all customers, Ameren said.
"Our employees live here, raise families here and rely on the same energy as our neighbors," Craig Giesman, Ameren's director of environmental services, said in a statement. "That is just one of many reasons we remain focused on operating responsibly, protecting public health and providing reliable energy, especially when it‚Äôs needed most."
The EPA declined to comment on Reuters‚Äô analysis of the COBRA data, but pointed out the agency is seeking to update its cost-benefit modeling tools.
A scientific study led by researchers at the University of Washington and published in the Journal of the International Society for Environmental Epidemiology last year said St. Louis would be the city most impacted by delaying tougher soot standards on U.S. coal plants.
Biden‚Äôs regulation would have forced Labadie to slash its soot emissions by more than half to continue to operate. Those soot limits would have yielded net public health benefits of up to $3 billion , opens new tab nationwide by 2037, according to the EPA‚Äôs 2023 cost-benefit ‚Äãanalysis.
The EPA under Trump has since reversed course. The agency told Reuters the Biden administration‚Äôs estimates ‚Äãwere overblown and that existing standards provide "an ample margin of safety to protect public ‚ÅÝhealth."
St. Louis clean air activists see it differently.
"Our region continues to be a sacrifice zone," said Darnell Tingle, director of United Congregations of Metro-East, another activist network. "We are trying to prepare for these data centers, and negate their harm to our communities."
The predominantly Black neighborhoods of North St. Louis already have some of the city‚Äôs worst air quality. Tiny particles of soot pollution small enough to penetrate the brain and lungs exceed federal safety limits there regularly, according to a Reuters analysis of data tracked by the EPA, thanks to industrial sources along with pollution from nearby highways and rail operations.
Some ‚Äã78% of African Americans live within 30 miles of a coal-fired power plant, according to the NAACP, compared to 56% of non-Hispanic whites. Soot pollution from power plants, meanwhile, kills African Americans at a rate that is 25% higher than the national average, according to a 2019 ‚Äãstudy in the Environmental Science & Technology journal.
"The logic is that ‚ÅÝwe need cheap electricity in the U.S. But if you look at the rise in healthcare costs for residents in the St. Louis area, this isn‚Äôt cheap," said Patricia Schuba, who runs a local environmental group that monitors Labadie and three other coal plants.
Tougher pollution limits had forced Ameren to upgrade Labadie. About a decade ago, Ameren installed state-of-the-art soot pollution controls for two of Labadie‚Äôs four coal-fired boilers in order to comply with Obama-era soot limits.
At a minimum, older controls on the remaining boilers would have needed retrofitting to meet Biden‚Äëera limits, Ameren told the EPA in a March 2025 letter seeking an exemption. Ameren declined to answer questions about how much upgrading the plant would have cost.
Meanwhile, data‚Äëcenter developers are breaking ground on major projects around St. Louis, pushing up regional electricity demand.
Ameren has said it has signed service contracts for an additional ‚ÅÝ2.3 gigawatts of potential ‚Äãpeak demand from data centers ‚Äì roughly the output of the Labadie plant ‚Äì and that more requests are coming. One of the biggest upcoming data center projects is a 1,000-acre development by Amazon Web Services proposed for rural Montgomery County, about ‚Äã55 miles from Labadie. The power would be supplied by Ameren.
Amazon declined to comment.
The data center industry‚Äôs trade group - the Data Center Coalition - said its member companies were among the top purchasers of clean energy but that utilities, regulators and grid operators are ultimately responsible for the kinds of power generation being used by consumers.
"While the data center industry is leaning in to support the development of the 21st-century electrical grid, it‚Äôs important to recognize that resource planning and generation procurement decisions are ‚Äãmade by utilities, grid operators, and policymakers, not large load customers like data centers," said Lucas Fykes, Senior Director of Energy Policy and Regulatory Counsel at the coalition.

item.title: China scientists rush to climate-proof potatoes
item.description:
By Florence Lo
Filed Nov. 18, 2024, 5:06 p.m. GMT
Photography and reporting by¬ÝFlorence Lo
Reporting by Mei Mei Chu and ¬ÝXiaoyu Yin
Filed November 27, 2024, 11:00 p.m.¬ÝGMT
In a research facility in the northwest of Beijing, molecular biologist Li Jieping and his team harvest a cluster of seven unusually small potatoes, one as tiny as a quail‚Äôs egg, from a potted plant.
Grown under conditions that simulate predictions of higher temperatures at the end of the century, the potatoes provide an ominous sign of future food security.
At just 136 grams (4.8 oz), the tubers weigh less than half that of a typical potato in China, where the most popular varieties are often twice the size of a baseball.
China is the world‚Äôs biggest producer of potatoes, which are crucial to global food security because of their high yield relative to other staple crops.
But they are particularly vulnerable to heat, and climate change, driven by fossil fuel emissions, is pushing temperatures to dangerous new heights while also worsening drought and flooding.
With an urgent need to protect food supplies, Li, a researcher at the International Potato Center (CIP) in Beijing, is leading a three-year study into the effects of higher temperatures on the vegetable. His team is focusing on China‚Äôs two most common varieties.
‚ÄúI worry about what will happen in the future,‚Äù Li said. ‚ÄúFarmers will harvest fewer potato tubers, it will influence food security.‚Äù
Li‚Äôs team grew their crop over three months in a walk-in chamber set at 3 degrees Celsius above the current average temperature in northern Hebei and Inner Mongolia, the higher altitude provinces where potatoes are usually grown in China.
Their research, published in the journal Climate Smart Agriculture this month, found the higher temperatures accelerated tuber growth by 10 days, but cut potato yields by more than half.
Under current climate policies, the world is facing as much as 3.1 C of warming above pre-industrial levels by 2100, according to a United Nations report released in October.
In Inner Mongolia, dozens of workers clutching white sacks rush to gather potatoes dug up from the soil before the next downpour.
‚ÄúThe biggest challenge for potatoes this year is the heavy rain,‚Äù said manager Wang Shiyi. ‚ÄúIt has caused various diseases... and greatly slowed down the harvest progress.‚Äù
Meanwhile, seed potato producer Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company has invested in aeroponic systems where plants are grown in the air under controlled conditions.
Farmers are increasingly demanding potato varieties that are higher-yielding and less susceptible to disease, particularly late blight, which caused the Irish Potato Famine of the mid-19th century and thrives in warm and humid conditions.
‚ÄúSome new and more aggressive (late blight) strains have begun to appear, and they are more resistant to traditional prevention and control methods,‚Äù said general manager Li Xuemin, explaining the Inner Mongolia-based company‚Äôs strategy.
The research by CIP, which is headquartered in Lima, is part of a collaborative effort with the Chinese government to help farmers adapt to the warmer, wetter conditions.
In the greenhouse outside Li's lab, workers swab pollen on white potato flowers to develop heat-tolerant varieties.
Li says Chinese farmers will need to make changes within the next decade, planting during spring instead of the start of summer, or moving to even higher altitudes to escape the heat.
‚ÄúFarmers have to start preparing for climate change,‚Äù Li said. ‚ÄúIf we don‚Äôt find a solution, they will make less money from lower yields and the price of potatoes may rise.‚Äù
A Chinese flag flutters next to sacks of newly harvested potatoes, at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers load a lorry with sacks of newly harvested potatoes at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Sprinklers on a centre pivot irrigation system water growing potato plants, at a farm under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company, in Old Barag Banner, Inner Mongolia, China, June 17, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato tubers dug up by harvesters lie on the ground, at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Researcher Li Yafei rinses a potato plant, before separating the tubers and weighing them, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 14, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Lab workers sort vessels with potato plantlets as they prepare them for propagation, in a tissue culture laboratory at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, April 17, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato plantlets propagated in a laboratory lie on the potting mix as workers transplant them in a greenhouse, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
A lab worker holds potato plantlets propagated in a laboratory to transplant them in a greenhouse, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Researcher Li Yafei sets up a device to measure the photosynthesis rates of potato plants grown inside a heat chamber, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, April 2, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato plants grow in a pot at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 14, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
A worker adjusts the hose as she waters potato plantlets transplanted from a laboratory, in a greenhouse at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Researchers transport a cart of potato plantlets to a greenhouse, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
A worker places clasps on potato plants to help them grow vertically, at a greenhouse under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company, in Yakeshi, Inner Mongolia, China, June 15, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato flowers grow in a greenhouse at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers pack potato plants into containers to transplant them to the field, in a greenhouse at a farm under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company, in Yakeshi, Inner Mongolia, China, June 17, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers sort sacks of seed potatoes to be distributed to other facilities, in a warehouse at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, April 2, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers rest on mats placed outside a greenhouse during their lunch break, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers sit in a bus for a lunch break Workers sit in a bus for a lunch break, after their work to transplant greenhouse-grown potato plants to the field was disrupted by the rain, at a farm under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company in Yakeshi, Inner Mongolia, China, June 16, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
The Wider Image
Photography: Florence Lo
Reporting: Mei Mei Chu, Florence Lo, Xiaoyu Yin
Writing: Mei Mei Chu
Photo editing: Maye-E Wong and Marta Montana Gomez
Text editing: Kate Mayberry
Design: Marta Montana Gomez
LICENSE THIS STORY
Amid Israel‚Äôs military assault, the world‚Äôs hunger monitor has struggled to access key data to determine whether conditions in Gaza constitute a famine.
OnlyFans is among the world's most successful social media platforms ‚Äì but a secretive one. Reuters traces its journey from an obscure, porn-free site to an adults-only phenomenon turbocharged by erotic performers and influencers.
It‚Äôs a simple but brutal equation: The number of people going hungry or otherwise struggling around the world is rising, while the amount of money the world‚Äôs wealthiest nations are contributing toward helping them is dropping.
Multiple OnlyFans accounts featured suspected child sex abuse, investigator reports.
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the world‚Äôs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: Buffett‚Äôs Berkshire Hathaway operates the dirtiest set of coal-fired power plants in the US
item.description:
In his letter to investors last year, Berkshire Hathaway Chairman Warren Buffett urged readers to come ‚Äúinhale the air, drink the water‚Äù and attend the company‚Äôs annual shareholder meeting in Omaha, his hometown.
The letter, a document pored over by shareholders, analysts and press every year for insights into the legendary investor‚Äôs thinking, playfully sought to link the success of his conglomerate with the setting of the heartland city, in eastern Nebraska along the Iowa border.
One detail omitted: Federal emissions data show that Omaha‚Äôs air quality ranks in the bottom third of U.S. cities, fouled in part by coal-fired power plants owned by Berkshire in neighboring Iowa. A modeling tool of the Environmental Protection Agency, or EPA, shows that pollution from the plants each year causes up to seven premature deaths, $104 million in healthcare expenses and 1,800 lost school days in the metropolitan area of Omaha, with nearly 1 million people.
The calculations, performed by Reuters using the EPA‚Äôs modeling data for 2023, were reviewed and confirmed by three outside emissions scientists consulted for this report. The scientists are all independent research experts with no affiliation with the news agency or the electricity industry.
The air over Omaha, and other areas near Berkshire‚Äôs dozen coal-fired power plants nationwide, casts a pall over the environmental record of a company that touts itself at the vanguard of clean energy. Despite investments to date of $41 billion in renewable energies, mostly wind and solar power, Berkshire operates the dirtiest corporate coal fleet in the U.S. by at least one important measure, a Reuters investigation shows.
Berkshire‚Äôs coal plant fleet, operated by three utility companies it has acquired in recent decades, emits more nitrogen oxides than any other coal fleet in the country, federal emissions data show. Reuters analyzed the most recent full calendar year of EPA emissions data available for coal plants, from 2023.
Known as NOx, nitrogen oxides are poisonous gases and key components of smog and haze. They help form lung-damaging ozone and tiny soot particles that can cause respiratory illness and premature death in communities up to hundreds of miles away.
