Sunday‚Äôs post, depicting Trump in a white robe with an apparently healing hand on a prone man's head, could create a ‚ÅÝrift between Trump and the religious right, whose support was critical to his victory in the 2024 election.
In the painting-like image, Trump holds a glowing orb ‚Äãin one hand and uses his other hand to touch a seemingly sick man on the forehead. The Statue of Liberty, fireworks, a fighter jet and eagles ‚Äãcould be seen in the background.
The post was removed on Monday morning. The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Brilyn Hollyhand, who served as the co-chair of the Republican National Committee Youth Advisory Council, wrote on X: ‚ÄúThis is gross blasphemy. Faith is not a prop. You don‚Äôt need to portray yourself as a savior when your record ‚Äãshould speak for itself.‚Äù
Riley Gaines, a former collegiate swimmer and outspoken critic of transgender athletes in women‚Äôs sports who has appeared with Trump at rallies, wrote ‚Äãon X she could not understand why Trump posted the image.
‚ÄúDoes he actually think this?‚Äù she wrote. ‚ÄúEither way, two things are true. 1) a little humility would serve him well ‚Äå2) God ‚ÅÝshall not be mocked.‚Äù
Trump, who does not attend church regularly, won large majorities of Christian voters in the 2024 election. He made gains among Catholic voters, who backed him by a 56% to 42% margin after splitting more evenly in previous elections, according to an analysis by Ryan Burge, a political science professor at Washington University and a former pastor.
After Trump narrowly survived an assassination attempt in July 2024, some evangelical supporters said it was evidence he had been blessed by God.
Last ‚Äãyear, after the death of Pope ‚ÄãFrancis, Trump posted an image showing himself ‚ÅÝas pope , prompting outrage from many Catholics.
In recent weeks, Leo has become one of the most prominent critics of the war in Iran, even making an unusual direct appeal to Trump and urging him to find an ‚Äúoff-ramp.‚Äù
Leo has also said that ‚ÄãJesus cannot be used to justify war and that God rejects the prayers of those who start conflicts. Those ‚Äãremarks were widely seen ‚ÅÝas a rebuke to Trump officials like U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth, who has cited scripture to justify the use of "overwhelming violence" against enemies and likened the rescue of a U.S. airman inside Iran to the resurrection of Jesus Christ.
Trump also feuded at times with Leo‚Äôs predecessor, Francis, who publicly opposed Trump‚Äôs deportation campaign as un-Christian.
At least eight ‚ÅÝmembers of ‚ÄãTrump's cabinet are Catholic, including Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio.
Bishop Robert ‚ÄãBarron, who serves on a Trump-created religious liberty commission, said on X that the president owed Leo an apology for his "inappropriate" statements on social media. But he also praised Trump in the same ‚Äãpost for his outreach to Catholics.

item.title: Protracted Hormuz crisis could trigger agrifood catastrophe, UN food agency says
item.description:
ROME, April 13 (Reuters) - A prolonged crisis in ‚Äãthe Strait of ‚ÄåHormuz could trigger a global agrifood ‚Äãcatastrophe by disrupting ‚Äãfertiliser and energy exports, ‚ÅÝdriving up ‚Äãfood prices and ‚Äãsqueezing crop yields, the U.N. Food and Agriculture ‚ÄãOrganization said ‚Äãon Monday.
FAO Chief Economist Maximo ‚ÄåTorero ‚ÅÝsaid poorer countries were most exposed because planting ‚Äãcalendars meant ‚Äãdelays ‚ÅÝin access to key ‚Äãinputs could quickly ‚Äãtranslate ‚ÅÝinto lower output, higher inflation ‚ÅÝand ‚Äãslower global ‚Äãgrowth.

item.title: Emerging economies in focus at IMF World Bank meetings
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 - Global financial policy makers gather in Washington for the International Monetary Fund and World Bank Group Spring Meetings this week against the backdrop of yet another global economic shock - this time from the Iran war .
The latest economic strife - marked by soaring borrowing costs, energy prices and inflation prospects - will amplify ‚Äãpressure on a number of emerging market nations , from Sri Lanka and Egypt to Pakistan, that had been just emerging from the ‚Äåother crises of recent years.
Here is a selection of those and other countries expected to be in focus:
IMF funding is key for Ukraine, which struck a two-year $8.1 billion programme with the Fund in February.
One major external obstacle to keeping IMF funding on track may have been removed: Sunday's election ouster of Hungarian Premier Viktor Orban is expected to pave the way for 90 ‚Äãbillion euros of European Union funding to Kyiv, a precondition for the IMF programme.
However, Ukraine also still needs to pursue an ambitious domestic reform ‚Äãprogramme ranging from raising revenue to tackling corruption and greater exchange rate flexibility.
"The key question domestically is if we see more, ‚ÅÝand we do need to see more, efforts by the Ukrainians to pass these pieces of reform, and they seem like they're going there," Roger Mark ‚Äãat asset manager Ninety One said.
The West African nation's economic outlook is still murky due to the discovery of billions in undisclosed debt, which prompted the IMF to halt ‚Äãa $1.8 billion loan programme in 2024.
Talks on a new programme are ongoing, but correcting the debt burden would likely require a painful and lengthy fiscal consolidation , which may not be easy for local authorities.
"Without the large fiscal effort, the Fund may ask Senegal for some sort of debt treatment, which has been firmly rejected by the authorities," JPMorgan analysts said in a ‚Äãnote, adding they expected talks between Dakar and the IMF to continue. "Our base case of a new IMF arrangement remains tricky, with a muddle-through scenario as ‚Äãanother option."
Mozambique has been in talks with the IMF since mid-2025 over a new lending programme. The country has said it wants to restructure its debt, though it has yet to ‚Äåoutline any ‚ÅÝdetails. In a surprise move, it also repaid dues to the IMF early in March - though that was interpreted as a sign it wanted to secure fresh funds down the line.
"Mozambique's recent payment is a bold attempt to acquire further credit from the IMF," Christian Franken at Oxford Economics said. "Indeed, we expect such a loan agreement to be finalised in Q2 2026."
Gabon's authorities formally requested a Fund programme in March to support their reform agenda. Years of political instability have left Gabon, the ‚Äãsecond-largest CEMAC economy, in an economic crunch with dwindling reserves.
Talks on ‚Äãthe contours of a programme are ‚ÅÝexpected to continue during the Spring meetings.
In the past two years, Egypt has unlocked billions in IMF funding and foreign direct investment from Gulf states. But heavy exposure to energy imports, GCC remittances and tourism makes Egypt vulnerable to the fallout ‚Äãfrom the Iran war.
Some expect Egypt to ask for an augmentation of its IMF programme which includes an $8‚ÄØ billion ‚ÄãExtended Fund Facility and ‚ÅÝa $1.3 billion Resilience and Sustainability Facility. The Fund is due to review the programme for the next financing tranche in June, but analysts expect delays could push that to later in the year.
Venezuela's $5 billion in special drawing rights - the IMF's own currency - has been frozen since 2021 as its government was not recognised by a majority of IMF ‚ÅÝBoard Member countries - a ‚Äãstatus that has yet to change under the leadership of President Delcy Rodriguez.
But with the ‚ÄãFund consulting on the matter, this could soon change. A data blackout, which has stymied engagement with multilateral lenders, is also being faded out, with Caracas releasing batches of macroeconomic data in recent weeks. Some analysts ‚Äãsay this could be seen as a precursor to a formal IMF mission visiting Caracas in coming months.

item.title: Hungarian election winner Magyar outlines his party's plans, views
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 - Hungarian election winner Peter Magyar vowed on Monday to change the constitution in a drive to restore democratic standards, following a landslide victory he said showed the country wanted to be anchored in Europe after 16 years of nationalist rule.
Hungary's veteran Prime Minister Viktor Orban ‚Äãand his nationalist Fidesz party lost power to the upstart centre-right Tisza party in Sunday's election, marking a setback ‚Äåfor Orban's allies in Russia and U.S. President Donald Trump's White House.
Following is a rundown of some key quotes from Magyar's news conference in Budapest.
"According to the rules, the result of the election becomes final by May 4 the latest, but this could happen earlier and the President... must convene the first session ‚Äãof the new parliament 30 days after the election at the latest."
"I call on President Tamas Sulyok to convene the inaugural ‚Äãsession as soon as possible, and not wait until May 12."
"The Hungarian people yesterday, exactly 23 years ‚ÅÝafter the referendum on our EU membership, confirmed Hungary's place in Europe."
"I would like to see a strong Central Europe within a strong ‚ÄãEU."
"We will have a lot of tasks... the first, to adopt the functional measures, to start Hungary's accession to the European Public Prosecutor's ‚ÄãOffice... to amend the fundamental law (constitution), and to write into it that in the future in Hungary anyone can only serve as prime minister for two terms, which is eight years."
"We will fundamentally restore, we will do everything to restore the rule of law, plural democracy, and the system of checks and balances."
"I ‚Äãwould like to underline that we will not use anti-democratic measures to restore the rule of law, but luckily our two-thirds mandate allows ‚Äãus to do a lot."
"A government's job is to ensure the independence of the judiciary, the independence of the investigative authorities, to provide all possible financial ‚Äåand other ‚ÅÝresources to carry out their tasks."
"We don't need this president," Magyar said, referring to Sulyok, saying he was only signing off on all papers that were proposed to him.
"I call on him to leave, if he doesn't, we will find a solution."
Sulyok became Hungary's ceremonial head of state with the backing of Orban's Fidesz party.
"Hungary is in a difficult financial and economic situation. The National Bank is primarily responsible for ‚Äãensuring exchange rate stability. I myself ‚Äãbelieve... Governor Mihaly Varga is ‚ÅÝdoing his job in line with the central bank law and I think that in a country which is in a difficult economic situation, cooperation with the national bank is important... of course while respecting the ‚Äãindependence of the central bank.
"So I will try to work this way, and if we see that ‚Äãthe central bank ‚ÅÝgovernor and the bank itself work in line with their legal mandate and are not, for example, trying to obstruct the financial policy of the new government... then we will be able to work together. We do not need yet more chaos... to harm investors' confidence in Hungary."
"I ‚ÅÝhave clearly ‚Äãstated several times already last year that everyone has the right to assemble in ‚ÄãHungary.
"We have clearly stated that, according to Tisza and the many millions of Hungarians who support Tisza, everyone can live with, and love, whomever they want, as long as ‚Äãthey do not violate the laws and do not harm others."

item.title: Nigeria plans July start for gas deliveries to Abuja with AKK pipeline
item.description:
LAGOS, April 13 (Reuters) - Nigeria hopes to begin delivering natural gas to the capital Abuja by July through its long-delayed Ajaokuta‚ÄìKaduna‚ÄìKano (AKK) gas ‚Äãpipeline , the upstream oil regulator said, marking a key milestone ‚Äåfor the country's gas development plans.
"We're hoping that by July, gas will be delivered to Abuja through the AKK gas pipeline," a spokesperson for the ‚ÄãNigerian Upstream Petroleum Regulatory Commission told the regulator's in-house magazine.
The ‚Äã614-kilometre (382-mile) pipeline is designed to deliver more than 2.2 billion ‚ÅÝcubic feet of gas per day and is a core ‚Äãpart of Nigeria's strategy to shift its energy mix towards gas, supply ‚Äãpower plants and industries in the north, and reduce reliance on diesel and fuel oil.
Nigeria holds Africa's largest gas reserves, estimated at over 210 trillion cubic ‚Äãfeet, but much of the country's gas infrastructure remains underdeveloped, ‚Äãmaking the AKK pipeline a critical test of its gas-led growth ambitions.
The $2.8 billion project, ‚Äåfirst ‚ÅÝconceived in 2008, has missed several delivery targets, including earlier deadlines of 2023 and the final quarter of 2025.
Construction began in 2020 but was slowed by funding pressures and engineering challenges, most notably the ‚Äãcrossing of the ‚ÄãRiver Niger. That ‚ÅÝsection, widely regarded as the project's most technically demanding, required drilling beneath the riverbed using horizontal directional ‚Äãdrilling, often compared to a scaled-down version of ‚Äãthe Eurotunnel.
An ‚ÅÝenergy lawyer involved in the project told Reuters that work was now at an advanced stage, with the pipeline more than 90% complete.
Gas ‚ÅÝtransported ‚Äãthrough the AKK pipeline will be sourced ‚Äãfrom Nigeria's southern producing areas largely through its interconnection with the East-West Obiafu-Obrikom-Oben (OB3) gas ‚Äãpipeline, according to industry officials.

item.title: Botswana signs energy and mineral exploration deals with Oman
item.description:
GABORONE, April 13 (Reuters) - Botswana's president has clinched multiple ‚Äãagreements with Oman, including on mineral exploration, oil storage infrastructure and renewable ‚Äåpower, during a visit to the Gulf nation designed to strengthen economic ties, the presidency said on Monday.
Botswana has been seeking to engage Gulf states as part of its strategy to diversify beyond ‚Äãdiamond mining and into critical minerals such as copper, gold, graphite, and ‚Äãiron ore.
President Duma Boko, who is on a three-day visit, presided over ‚ÅÝthe signing of deals with the Sultan of Oman, Haitham bin Tariq, the ‚Äãpresidency said.
They included a joint mineral exploration deal under which Botswana will seek to increase mining ‚Äãexploration , targeting unexplored areas covering roughly 70% of its territory.
Revenue from diamond sales, which typically account for about a third of Botswana's national revenue, has fallen as the global diamond market has been ‚Äãweakened by economic uncertainty and the rise in popularity of lab-grown stones.
Another deal was for the development of a 500-megawatt solar power plant that ‚Äåfollows ‚ÅÝon from an announcement on March 20 that NAQAA Sustainable Energy LLC, a subsidiary of Oman's state-owned O-Green, had been selected to design, finance, construct, and operate the plant in northwest Botswana.
It will have a minimum operational life of 25 years as Botswana ‚Äãseeks to increase ‚Äãthe contribution of renewable ‚ÅÝenergy in its power supply from 8% now to 50% by 2030.
The presidency's statement said the plant would position Botswana "to secure ‚Äãits energy future and unlock long-term economic value".
It did not provide ‚Äãany financial ‚ÅÝdetails.
State-owned Botswana Oil Limited also signed a joint development agreement with Oman's energy investment company, OQ S.A.O.C to build oil storage infrastructure in Walvis Bay, Namibia, and in Botswana.
Oman ‚ÅÝis also ‚Äãtrying to diversify its economy, which is dependent ‚Äãon oil, although its revenues have increased as a result of the oil price surge linked to the Middle Eastern ‚Äãconflict that began at the end of February.

item.title: Colombia to control hippo population through euthanasia
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 13 (Reuters) - Colombia will launch a plan in the second half of 2026 to control its invasive hippopotamus ‚Äãpopulation, including euthanizing an initial 80 animals, Environment Minister ‚ÄåIrene Velez said on Monday.
The South American country is home to some 200 hippos in the central region near the Magdalena River. If ‚Äãcontrol measures are not adopted, the population could increase ‚Äãto as many as 1,000 by 2035, Velez said.
"We ‚ÅÝmust act to reduce the hippopotamus population. These actions ‚Äãare essential to protect our ecosystems and our native species," Velez ‚Äãtold journalists, noting that population growth threatens species like river turtles and manatees and causes water pollution. "We believe there may be approximately 80 individuals ‚Äãthat could be subject to the measure (of euthanasia)."
Colombia's first ‚Äãfour hippos were illegally imported in the 1980s by the late drug trafficker ‚ÄåPablo ‚ÅÝEscobar, who established a zoo on one of his properties. The population has since grown largely unchecked, despite some efforts by provincial environmental authorities.
The program announced on Monday has a ‚Äãbudget of 7.2 ‚Äãbillion pesos ($1.98 ‚ÅÝmillion) and includes measures like confinement and relocation. Though Colombia began talks months ago with eight ‚Äãgovernments including India, Mexico, Philippines, Ecuador, Peru ‚Äãand South ‚ÅÝAfrica to possibly transfer some animals to zoos or sanctuaries in those countries, the necessary authorizations have not yet been obtained, ‚ÅÝVelez ‚Äãsaid.
The Colombia hippos have genetic defects ‚Äãdue to inbreeding, which has reduced some institutions‚Äô interest in them.

item.title: Dominican Republic rains force 30,000 to evacuate, 3 reported dead
item.description:
SANTO DOMINGO, April 13 (Reuters) - Some 30,000 people have evacuated their homes in the Dominican Republic over recent days due to torrential rains ‚Äãfrom weather systems that have hovered over the Caribbean ‚Äånation hurling rain onto much of its northern region for more than a month.
Local media reported three people had died, including a seven-year-old girl who ‚Äãtried to cross a river, a 19-year-old man who ‚Äãtried to cross on horseback, and an infant girl ‚ÅÝin the capital, where the wall of her house collapsed due ‚Äãto the rains.
"The government's priority at this time is to save ‚Äãlives, protect property and preserve infrastructure," President Luis Abinader's office said in a statement on Monday, urging people to stay away from vulnerable areas.
Abinader declared ‚Äãa national emergency in five provinces as well as in ‚Äãthe capital.
A day earlier, the country's Emergency Operations Center (COE) Director Juan Manuel Mendez ‚Äåtold ‚ÅÝa press conference 30,500 people had been evacuated, 14 communities were left isolated, 6,100 homes were flooded and highways and bridges were damaged.
"The rains will continue," meteorological institute chief Gloria Ceballos said on ‚ÄãMonday, noting these ‚Äãshould ease ‚ÅÝslightly before intensifying again on the weekend.
"We have a frontal system that remains stationary in the northeast," ‚Äãshe said. "The soil is already saturated. We have ‚Äãhad ‚ÅÝpractically 45 days of it raining every day in some parts of the territory."
In the nearby U.S. territory of Puerto Rico, authorities warned ‚ÅÝthat ‚Äãheavy rain and thunderstorms could trigger urban ‚Äãflooding later on Monday.

item.title: Opposition candidate concedes defeat to Wadagni in Benin presidential race
item.description:
COTONOU, April 13 (Reuters) - The sole ‚Äãopposition candidate in Benin's presidential election, Paul Hounkpe, has conceded defeat to Finance ‚ÄåMinister Romuald Wadagni following the vote in the West African country, state television reported on Monday.
A 49-year-old former Deloitte executive, Wadagni is the chosen successor of outgoing President Patrice Talon and had the backing of the ruling ‚Äãcoalition in Sunday's election, which took place four months after the government was nearly ‚Äãtoppled in a coup attempt .
The opposition candidate, Paul Hounkpe of the Cowry Forces ‚ÅÝfor an Emerging Benin party, acknowledged his defeat in a statement on Monday before provisional ‚Äãresults were announced, according to a social media post by the state television network.
Wadagni was ‚Äãthe overwhelming favourite after the main opposition party, The Democrats, failed to get enough lawmakers to sponsor a candidate of its own and did not back Hounkpe.
Wadagni will come under ‚Äãpressure to bolster the country's defences in the north, which faces a growing threat from jihadist ‚Äãgroups based in the central Sahel.
The disgruntled soldiers who staged the December coup attempt said they were ‚Äåpartly ‚ÅÝmotivated by deteriorating security conditions there.
"A stable transition in Benin is important not only for the country itself but also for the broader West Africa region, given the political instability caused by successive coups in the Sahel," said Heni Nsaibia, senior West Africa analyst for Armed Conflict Location & ‚ÄãEvent Data (ACLED), a conflict ‚Äãmonitoring group.
"It is ‚ÅÝtherefore especially necessary to focus efforts on stabilizing the north, preventing further upheaval, and demonstrating a model of stability for the broader region."
Wadagni ‚Äãhas also vowed to deliver on bread-and-butter issues like expanding access to ‚Äãclean water, ‚ÅÝexpanding social security programmes and guaranteeing emergency healthcare regardless of ability to pay.
On the campaign trail, Hounkpe said high-profile tourism projects and impressive GDP growth under Talon and Wadagni had not improved ‚ÅÝthe lives ‚Äãof most people.
He has also decried what he described ‚Äãas a climate of fear as political space for the opposition shrinks, with the ruling coalition holding every seat in ‚Äãthe National Assembly.

item.title: Former Brazil intelligence chief arrested by ICE in the US, Brazilian media says
item.description: SAO PAULO, April 13 (Reuters) - Former ‚ÄãBrazilian intelligence ‚Äåchief Alexandre Ramagem has ‚Äãbeen ‚Äãarrested by ICE ‚ÅÝin the ‚ÄãUnited States, ‚Äãthe Brazilian media reported on ‚ÄãMonday ‚Äãciting Brazil's federal police, ‚Äåafter ‚ÅÝfleeing Brazil in September following ‚Äãhis ‚Äãconviction ‚ÅÝfor plotting ‚Äãa coup ‚Äãwith ‚ÅÝformer President Jair ‚ÅÝBolsonaro.

item.title: Goldman Sachs nears another precarious peak
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 13 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Climbing the mountain is one thing. For David Solomon, staying on top might be the real challenge. On Monday, the boss of ‚ÄãGoldman Sachs (GS.N) , opens new tab announced that revenue , opens new tab for the first three months of 2026 had reached within 3% of ‚Äåa quarterly peak set five years ago. Back then, an ill-advised foray into retail banking and a drop-off in initial public offerings led to a quick descent. This time around, rising global conflict and uncertainty, paired with increasing risk in trading and lending, could arrest the Wall ‚ÄãStreet titan‚Äôs rise.
For now, many of the firm‚Äôs businesses are riding high. Revenue from dispensing deal advice rose ‚Äã89% year-over-year, as mergers and acquisitions stage a massive comeback . Equity traders turned in the best ‚ÅÝquarter in Goldman‚Äôs history. All told, the bank‚Äôs revenue hit $17.2 billion, its highest three-month tally since a record of $17.7 billion ‚Äãin the first quarter of 2021. That was right before rising inflation halted the Federal Reserve‚Äôs easy-money policy, and with ‚Äãit, the frenetic pace of initial public offerings and buyouts. Solomon‚Äôs push into retail banking also proved a misstep, from which he has since retreated.
Profitability still lags: earnings rose 19% from a year ago, to $5.6 billion, but still well below a $6.8 billion peak. That has to ‚Äãdo with mix. Five years ago, investment banking, generally Goldman's most profitable business, generated $3.6 billion in sales versus $2.8 billion now.
A ‚Äãlower reliance on investment banking ‚Äî notoriously vulnerable to market whipsaws ‚Äî could be a good thing. Indeed, Goldman's share price has more than doubled ‚Äåover ‚ÅÝthe past half-decade. Executives repeat the common Wall Street refrain that the deal pipeline remains strong. In a nod to variability, though, they added that raging Gulf conflict is weighing on corporate confidence, a necessary ingredient for massive transactions.
These days, Goldman generates more revenue from asset management, which rose to $3.3 billion from $735 million five years ago, and lending. The firm says ‚Äãthese are more "durable" than investment banking. ‚ÄãThat could be the ‚ÅÝcase, but they are still far from risk-free.
Goldman‚Äôs reckoning of how much it could lose from its market activities on an average day rose 40% from a year ago. Provisions ‚Äãfor loan losses remain small, at just 2% of overall revenue, but nearly tripled in ‚Äãits corporate and ‚ÅÝinvestment bank. History suggests that lending is as subject to booms and busts as anything else. The view from the summit, of course, tends to make any problems look small.
Follow Stephen Gandel on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: IEA is ready to further tap global oil reserves if needed, chief says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The head of the International Energy Agency, ‚ÄåFatih Birol, said on Monday he hopes another oil stockpile release is not needed but "we stand ready to act" if ‚Äãthe energy shock resulting from the war ‚Äãwith Iran requires it.
The 32-member IEA agreed last ‚ÅÝmonth to release 400 million barrels of oil ‚Äãfrom reserves, the largest coordinated release ever, in a ‚Äãbid to calm oil markets. The U.S., the world's largest oil and gas producer, agreed to release 172 million barrels from ‚Äãits Strategic Petroleum Reserve.
"I hope, very much hope, ‚Äãwe don't need to do it but if it is ‚Äåneeded ‚ÅÝwe are ready to act," Birol said.
Birol reiterated at an Atlantic Council event that the war has resulted in the worst global energy disruption ever and ‚Äãsaid that more ‚Äãthan 80 ‚ÅÝoil and gas facilities including production, terminals and refineries across the Middle East have been ‚Äãdamaged by war with Iran. Benchmark oil ‚Äãprices ‚ÅÝare trading near $100 a barrel.
Due to the vast extent of the production shut-ins and closure of the Strait ‚ÅÝof ‚ÄãHormuz, the oil releases are "not ‚Äãa solution," Birol said, "it's just reducing the pain."

item.title: Russia‚Äôs Rosneft diverts oil to Tuapse refinery from drone-hit Novorossiysk port, sources say
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Rosneft is diverting oil supplies to the Tuapse refinery from the Black Sea port of Novorossiysk after the terminal, which handles roughly 14% of Russia's crude exports, was heavily ‚Äãdamaged in a drone attack, three sources said on Monday.
A Ukrainian drone attack ‚Äåsparked a large fire at the Sheskharis oil terminal in the Novorossiysk port last week, damaging two of its main berths and forcing oil exports from the port to halt, according to the sources, who ‚Äãwere familiar with the matter but were not authorised to speak to the ‚Äãmedia.
The development underscores Russia's challenges in exporting oil as the rising number of ‚ÅÝUkrainian drone attacks deals damage to its energy infrastructure, putting additional pressure on the ‚ÄãRussian pipeline system and increasing the risk of disruptions to crude oil output.
Russian oil producer Rosneft, ‚Äãone of the country's largest exporters shipping crude via Novorossiysk, diverted a significant volume of oil to its Tuapse refinery, two of the sources said.
Runs at the refinery, which is located close to the Novorossiysk ‚Äãport, are expected to increase to nearly 800,000 metric tons this month, they added, from ‚Äãusual 500,000-600,000 tons per month.
Tuapse processed about 500,000 tons in March, the sources said. The refinery is focused ‚Äåon fuel ‚ÅÝexports and ramping up its capacity makes sense amid higher margins, they added.
Rosneft did not respond to a request for comment.
Other exporters transporting oil via the port have begun diverting crude volumes to alternative destinations, including other export routes and domestic refineries, the sources added, saying ‚Äãthey do not expect ‚Äãa quick resolution.
They ‚ÅÝare likely to redirect oil to Baltic ports that have resumed loadings after the attacks or also to the domestic market, the sources ‚Äãsaid.
Novorossiysk, which can export about 700,000 barrels per day (bpd) of crude, resumed ‚Äãlimited oil ‚ÅÝloadings last week, but traders said the flows were minimal and the port was not operating anywhere near full capacity.
The loadings resumed for berth number two, which accommodates smaller tankers, while the ‚ÅÝtwo main ‚Äãoil export berths remained shut.
Moscow ramped up oil loadings in ‚Äãearly April and exported more crude on average than in March, Reuters reported last week, helping to offset part ‚Äãof the losses caused by disruptions in the Black Sea.

item.title: EU launches operations of critical minerals procurement platform
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Union on Monday launched the critical minerals section of its energy and materials procurement platform, which aims ‚Äãto give more power to regional buyers and cut dependence on ‚Äådominant producer China by aggregating their purchases.
The platform is part of the bloc's RESourceEU strategy , announced in December to develop its supply chains for rare earths and other ‚Äãstrategic minerals needed for the energy transition and defence applications. China ‚Äãcontrols up to 90% of output in that sector.
The EU's Energy and Raw Materials Platform , opens new tab opened to submissions from buyers on ‚ÄãMonday in the first phase of a multi-month process, an EU spokesperson ‚Äãtold Reuters.
The platform connects buyers with suppliers, but leaves the two sides to finalise trades.
The first round of connections between buyers and sellers will start on April 13, ‚Äãthe spokesperson said, and will focus on "immediately and soon-to-be-available rare earths, battery ‚Äãand defence raw materials". The results will be announced in September.
Last year, the EU chose ‚ÄåPriceWaterhouseCoopers ‚ÅÝand a Slovak software company to develop its 9 million euro platform, which has separate mechanisms for strategic raw materials, hydrogen, and energy products such as natural gas and biomethane.
The platform kicked off with hydrogen, which started submissions ‚Äãin November and announced ‚Äãresults , opens new tab on April ‚ÅÝ1, with 273 match-ups.
The REsourceEU plan aims to accelerate the bloc's Critical Raw Materials Act, passed in 2023. ‚ÄãThe EU wants to speed up the development of ‚Äãits own ‚ÅÝsupply chains so as not to rely on any single country for more than 65% of its demand.
Under the CRMA, the bloc has set ambitious ‚ÅÝ2030 targets ‚Äãfor domestic production of critical minerals required ‚Äãfor its green transition - 10% of annual needs mined, 25% recycled and 40% processed domestically ‚Äãby the end of the decade.

item.title: South Africa agrees new climate loan, critical minerals cooperation on Germany visit
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Germany has agreed to give South Africa ‚Äåa new 200 million euro ($234 million) concessional climate loan, and the two countries will deepen cooperation in ‚Äãother areas like critical minerals, South Africa's ‚Äãforeign minister said on Monday.
The loan will ‚ÅÝsupport investment in South Africa's power grid ‚Äãand renewable energy capacity, Minister Ronald Lamola said ‚Äãafter talks with German counterpart Johann Wadephul in Berlin.
He said German and European Union funding for green hydrogen and ‚Äãbattery value chain cooperation had also been ‚Äãextended by more than 270 million euros.
Lamola thanked Wadephul ‚Äåfor ‚ÅÝGerman support despite South Africa's strained relationship with the United States during President Donald Trump's second term in office.
Trump has excluded South Africa from meetings ‚Äãof the ‚ÄãGroup of ‚ÅÝ20 nations this year and criticised its foreign policy and domestic ‚Äãrace laws. He boycotted a G20 summit in ‚ÄãJohannesburg ‚ÅÝin November.
Lamola said: "We feel we are part of it (the G20) because of the support that we ‚ÅÝhave ‚Äãreceived from Germany and from ‚Äãother G20 members."
($1 = 0.8547 euros)
